Question Text: Which statement best defines machine learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Letting computers learn from data instead of being explicitly programmed | Storing very large datasets so that software can search through them faster | Automating routine work so that humans are removed from all decisions | Hand-coding in advance every single rule a program will ever apply
Correct Answer: Letting computers learn from data instead of being explicitly programmed

Question Text: In Arthur Samuel's checkers program, how did the system improve its play over time?
Options (A|B|C|D): It simply memorized a fixed list of board positions known to win | It searched every possible continuation of the game to perfect depth | It learned by playing many games against itself and tracking outcomes | It was corrected by hand by a human expert after each move it made
Correct Answer: It learned by playing many games against itself and tracking outcomes

Question Text: Why is machine learning well suited to tasks such as speech recognition or web search ranking?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every rule needed to hand-code these systems is already fully known | These tasks are too complex to capture fully with explicit rules | These tasks can be carried out without making use of any data at all | These tasks involve only a very small number of possible outputs
Correct Answer: These tasks are too complex to capture fully with explicit rules

Question Text: Which of the following is an example of machine learning in action?
Options (A|B|C|D): A streaming service recommending movies similar to ones you watched | Computing the sales tax on a purchase with a fixed written formula | Saving a brand-new contact in your phone by typing in the details | Typing out a text message with predictive word suggestions disabled
Correct Answer: A streaming service recommending movies similar to ones you watched

Question Text: Why is machine learning often viewed as a key step toward building more capable AI systems?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because every AI system that exists today is already fully intelligent | Because machine learning removes essentially all need for computation | Because symbolic, rule-based programming has now been abandoned entirely | Because learning algorithms let systems improve from experience
Correct Answer: Because learning algorithms let systems improve from experience

Question Text: For which kind of problem is machine learning especially appropriate?
Options (A|B|C|D): When no data exists at all and gathering examples is impossible | When perfect certainty is required and no prediction is ever needed | When the input-to-output mapping is hard to specify but examples exist | When the whole task can be solved by a few short hand-coded rules
Correct Answer: When the input-to-output mapping is hard to specify but examples exist

Question Text: Why do industries outside of software present large opportunities for machine learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only companies outside of software ever collect any usable data | Many sectors hold untapped problems where data can add value | Software companies can no longer benefit at all from using ML | Machine learning is mainly suited to academic research settings
Correct Answer: Many sectors hold untapped problems where data can add value

Question Text: Which task is the clearest sign that machine learning may outperform explicit programming?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sorting a long list of numbers from smallest to largest | Diagnosing disease by finding patterns in many X-ray images | Computing a shortest path when all of the rules are known | Looking up an amount owed in a fixed, published tax table
Correct Answer: Diagnosing disease by finding patterns in many X-ray images

Question Text: Which statement best describes why the present is a strong time to learn machine learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): The field is shrinking quickly, so there is very little competition | Most ML systems are already fully automated and need no people at all | Demand for ML skills is high and its economic impact is broad | Only a small handful of industries still make any practical use of ML
Correct Answer: Demand for ML skills is high and its economic impact is broad

Question Text: What common mistake should you avoid when deciding whether to apply machine learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Thinking carefully about how the model will be deployed early on | Assuming good tools alone suffice without knowing how to apply them | Collecting labeled examples before you begin to train any model | Trying out more than one approach before committing to a method
Correct Answer: Assuming good tools alone suffice without knowing how to apply them

Question Text: What is the key distinction between supervised and unsupervised learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Supervised learning is outdated while unsupervised learning is the modern choice | Supervised learning handles only images while unsupervised handles only text | Supervised learning is always linear while unsupervised is always nonlinear | Supervised learning uses labeled outputs while unsupervised does not
Correct Answer: Supervised learning uses labeled outputs while unsupervised does not

Question Text: Which question is most naturally framed as a supervised learning problem?
Options (A|B|C|D): Which of these news articles appear most similar to one another? | Is this incoming email message spam or not spam? | What previously hidden structure is present within this dataset? | How should these unlabeled customers be grouped into segments?
Correct Answer: Is this incoming email message spam or not spam?

Question Text: Which question is most naturally framed as an unsupervised learning problem?
Options (A|B|C|D): How can these unlabeled data points be grouped by similarity? | Which one of two categories does this tumor most likely belong to? | What price should this house sell for, given past labeled sales? | Will this particular user end up clicking on the displayed ad?
Correct Answer: How can these unlabeled data points be grouped by similarity?

Question Text: Why is unsupervised learning described as 'unsupervised'?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it can operate only on data that is randomly generated | Because the input examples it receives contain no features at all | Because the algorithm gets no correct answer for any example | Because it needs no human involvement of any kind whatsoever
Correct Answer: Because the algorithm gets no correct answer for any example

Question Text: Which pairing of a task with its learning type is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Spam filtering—unsupervised; market segmentation—supervised learning | Speech recognition—unsupervised; anomaly detection—supervised learning | Housing-price prediction—unsupervised; news clustering—supervised learning | Breast-tumor diagnosis—supervised; customer grouping—unsupervised
Correct Answer: Breast-tumor diagnosis—supervised; customer grouping—unsupervised

Question Text: What characterizes a regression problem?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model happens to rely on more than one input feature | The goal is to predict a number from many possible values | The output is only an ordered ranking and nothing more | The output is selected from a small fixed set of categories
Correct Answer: The goal is to predict a number from many possible values

Question Text: What characterizes a classification problem?
Options (A|B|C|D): The training data is restricted to just a single input feature | The model is required to take images as its only form of input | The model predicts one label from a limited set of categories | The model predicts any real-valued number along a continuum
Correct Answer: The model predicts one label from a limited set of categories

Question Text: Why is predicting a home's selling price treated as regression rather than classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the data about houses is essentially always unlabeled | Because prices span many numeric values, not a few categories | Because the model used must take the form of a straight line | Because houses are physical, visual objects out in the world
Correct Answer: Because prices span many numeric values, not a few categories

Question Text: Why can outputs written as 0 and 1 still describe classification rather than regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because classification can never make any use of text-based labels at all | Because regression problems are restricted to using negative numbers only | Because any use of numbers at all automatically makes a task regression | Because what truly matters is that the outputs stand for categories
Correct Answer: Because what truly matters is that the outputs stand for categories

Question Text: Which of the following is the best example of a classification problem?
Options (A|B|C|D): Predicting the exact final selling price of a home | Labeling a tumor as either benign or malignant | Forecasting tomorrow's high temperature in degrees | Estimating the height of a given person in centimeters
Correct Answer: Labeling a tumor as either benign or malignant

Question Text: What is the goal of a clustering algorithm?
Options (A|B|C|D): To predict a known output label for each example | To split every dataset into exactly two classes | To delete outlier points and clean the dataset | To group similar unlabeled examples together
Correct Answer: To group similar unlabeled examples together

Question Text: Why is automatically grouping news articles by story (as in Google News) an example of clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because each individual article arrives with a manual topic label | Because it first translates every article before grouping it | Because it forecasts future stock prices from the article text | Because related articles are grouped without given labels
Correct Answer: Because related articles are grouped without given labels

Question Text: When clustering is applied to DNA microarray data to study a population, what is the algorithm trying to uncover?
Options (A|B|C|D): A doctor-supplied diagnosis for each person | The precise expression value of every single gene | A health ranking from healthiest to least healthy | Types of individuals that emerge from the data
Correct Answer: Types of individuals that emerge from the data

Question Text: Why is market segmentation, grouping customers into segments, framed as an unsupervised learning problem?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the segments are discovered from data, not given as labels | Because the underlying marketing data can never be numeric values at all | Because unsupervised methods completely ignore how customers behave | Because every valid customer segment is required to be strictly binary
Correct Answer: Because the segments are discovered from data, not given as labels

Question Text: Which statement best captures an important caveat about the groups a clustering algorithm produces?
Options (A|B|C|D): It needs labeled training examples in order to function correctly | Its groups are structure it found, not guaranteed ground truth | It always reveals the one true number of groups that are present | It is simply regression carried out without any target values given
Correct Answer: Its groups are structure it found, not guaranteed ground truth

Question Text: What is anomaly detection primarily used for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Detecting unusual events such as fraudulent charges | Transcribing long recordings of speech into written text | Grouping many similar news stories into shared topics | Predicting a house's selling price from its floor area
Correct Answer: Detecting unusual events such as fraudulent charges

Question Text: At a high level, how does anomaly detection differ from clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Clustering predicts numbers, anomaly detection predicts categories | Clustering needs labels, but anomaly detection needs none at all | Clustering finds normal structure; anomaly detection flags oddities | They are one identical method known by two different names
Correct Answer: Clustering finds normal structure; anomaly detection flags oddities

Question Text: Why can anomaly detection be valuable in finance?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because every single transaction is already labeled by default | Because only supervised regression is able to detect fraud | Because financial data contains no real patterns at all | Because rare, unusual transactions may signal fraud
Correct Answer: Because rare, unusual transactions may signal fraud

Question Text: Which scenario is the best fit for anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Choosing the single best slope for a regression line | Estimating the resale value of a family home accurately | Flagging account activity unlike its normal behavior | Sorting many customer reviews into positive and negative
Correct Answer: Flagging account activity unlike its normal behavior

Question Text: Which common misunderstanding about anomaly detection should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That it just means finding numerically large values in the data | That any rare label is an anomaly, not behavior unlike the norm | That it can only ever be applied to image-classification problems | That it always requires building a three-dimensional cost surface
Correct Answer: That any rare label is an anomaly, not behavior unlike the norm

Question Text: What is the main purpose of dimensionality reduction?
Options (A|B|C|D): To add extra features so the model grows more complex | To assign a correct label to each unlabeled example | To guarantee that predictions become perfectly accurate | To compress data into fewer dimensions, losing little
Correct Answer: To compress data into fewer dimensions, losing little

Question Text: Why is dimensionality reduction considered a form of unsupervised learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it relies on reinforcement-style reward signals | Because its goal is always to predict discrete categories | Because it can only be run after a classification step | Because it works on inputs without needing output labels
Correct Answer: Because it works on inputs without needing output labels

Question Text: Which outcome best reflects successful dimensionality reduction?
Options (A|B|C|D): More dimensions, with added redundancy in the features | A perfectly correct predicted label for every example | Fewer dimensions, with the key information preserved | Complete removal of any need for data preprocessing
Correct Answer: Fewer dimensions, with the key information preserved

Question Text: What is a key practical reason dimensionality reduction can matter?
Options (A|B|C|D): It keeps the feature count exactly identical to before | It recasts every single learning problem as a regression task | It always raises model accuracy no matter the situation | It simplifies large data while keeping useful structure
Correct Answer: It simplifies large data while keeping useful structure

Question Text: Which misunderstanding about dimensionality reduction should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That it shrinks the representation deliberately, not arbitrarily | That it can only ever be applied to image datasets in practice | That it just means dropping columns chosen completely at random | That it counts as supervised because it changes the model's inputs
Correct Answer: That it shrinks the representation deliberately, not arbitrarily

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Training Set, Features, Targets, and Predictions
Question Text: In supervised learning, what is a training set?
Options (A|B|C|D): The list of model parameters obtained after training | A dataset of inputs paired with their correct outputs | A collection containing solely unlabeled raw input data | A separate dataset reserved only for final deployment
Correct Answer: A dataset of inputs paired with their correct outputs

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Training Set, Features, Targets, and Predictions
Question Text: In standard supervised-learning notation, what does x represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The target output value | The count of training examples | The prediction the model makes | The input feature or features
Correct Answer: The input feature or features

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Training Set, Features, Targets, and Predictions
Question Text: In standard supervised-learning notation, what does y represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model's estimated output | The value of the cost function | The actual target output value | The model's learning rate
Correct Answer: The actual target output value

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Training Set, Features, Targets, and Predictions
Question Text: In standard supervised-learning notation, what does y-hat represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single special training example | The true output recorded in the training set | The number of input features used | The model's predicted estimate of y
Correct Answer: The model's predicted estimate of y

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Training Set, Features, Targets, and Predictions
Question Text: In standard supervised-learning dataset notation, what does m denote?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total number of training examples | The size of the model's prediction error | The number of distinct output classes | The number of layers inside the model
Correct Answer: The total number of training examples

Question Text: In a task that predicts house price from house size, what does linear regression learn?
Options (A|B|C|D): A list of fixed hand-written pricing rules | A set of discrete categories for home types | A grouping of houses into neighborhood clusters | A straight-line map from input to output
Correct Answer: A straight-line map from input to output

Question Text: Why is linear regression classified as a regression model rather than a classification model?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it needs no labeled training data | Because it always relies on multiple features | Because it sorts inputs into discrete classes | Because it outputs continuous numeric values
Correct Answer: Because it outputs continuous numeric values

Question Text: What does the term 'univariate linear regression' mean?
Options (A|B|C|D): A linear model fit without any labels | A linear regression with a single input | A regression model with a single output class | A linear model trained on a single example
Correct Answer: A linear regression with a single input

Question Text: When first studying regression, why is it common to begin with a straight-line model before fitting more complex curves?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because nonlinear models lack cost functions | Because curved models cannot fit real data | Because it is simple and builds intuition | Because straight lines are always most accurate
Correct Answer: Because it is simple and builds intuition

Question Text: Which expression is the model form for linear regression with one input variable?
Options (A|B|C|D): f(x) = w + x + b^2 | f(x) = wx + b | f(x) = (y-hat) + x | f(x) = x / y
Correct Answer: f(x) = wx + b

Question Text: In the model f(x) = wx + b, what are w and b together called?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model's parameters | The model's predictions | The training examples | The target output labels
Correct Answer: The model's parameters

Question Text: In the linear model f(x) = wx + b, what does the weight w control?
Options (A|B|C|D): The count of the training examples | The slope, or tilt, of the line | The stored set of target values | The recorded prediction error value
Correct Answer: The slope, or tilt, of the line

Question Text: In the linear model f(x) = wx + b, what does the bias b represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total number of features | The overall size of the dataset | The y-intercept of the line | A value held at zero in training
Correct Answer: The y-intercept of the line

Question Text: If w = 0 in the model f(x) = wx + b, what kind of prediction function results?
Options (A|B|C|D): A flat, constant horizontal line | A vertical line through the origin | A two-output binary classifier | An upward-opening parabola
Correct Answer: A flat, constant horizontal line

Question Text: Why are the parameters w and b central to how a machine learning model learns?
Options (A|B|C|D): They take the place of the training data | They are tuned in training to fit data | They only have an effect in clustering | They are fixed before any data is seen
Correct Answer: They are tuned in training to fit data

Question Text: What is the purpose of the cost function in linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It assigns a discrete class label to inputs | It gauges how well predictions match targets | It selects how many examples to train on | It rescales every input feature automatically
Correct Answer: It gauges how well predictions match targets

Question Text: In the squared error cost function, what quantity is squared for each example?
Options (A|B|C|D): The value of the input feature x used | The gap between the prediction and target | The value of the model's bias parameter b | The count of examples in the training set
Correct Answer: The gap between the prediction and target

Question Text: Why does the squared error cost function average the errors over the training set instead of just summing them?
Options (A|B|C|D): So that the model becomes nonlinear | So that labels turn into input features | So cost does not grow with example count | So that predictions are no longer needed
Correct Answer: So cost does not grow with example count

Question Text: Why is the squared error cost commonly scaled by 1/(2m) rather than 1/m, where m is the number of examples?
Options (A|B|C|D): The 1/2 makes later derivatives cleaner | The 1/2 is needed only for classification | The 1/2 guarantees zero training error | The 1/2 changes which model class is used
Correct Answer: The 1/2 makes later derivatives cleaner

Question Text: Why is squared error a sensible loss choice for linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is valid only when the bias b is zero | It ignores all of the smaller errors | It forces the predictions to be categorical | It penalizes large errors heavily and works well
Correct Answer: It penalizes large errors heavily and works well

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cost Function Intuition and Model Fit
Question Text: What does a smaller value of the cost function generally indicate?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model is using a larger feature set | Predictions sit closer to the training targets | The training set contains fewer examples | The target labels have become categorical
Correct Answer: Predictions sit closer to the training targets

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cost Function Intuition and Model Fit
Question Text: For the dataset (1,1), (2,2), (3,3) fit with f(x) = wx (no bias), why does the cost J(w) equal 0 when w = 1?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the slope of the line is negative | Because the learning rate is set to zero | Because each prediction equals its target | Because there is no training data to fit
Correct Answer: Because each prediction equals its target

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cost Function Intuition and Model Fit
Question Text: On a scatter plot with a fitted regression line, what does the vertical gap between a data point and the line represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total size of the training set | The learning rate used in training | The number of input features used | The prediction error for that point
Correct Answer: The prediction error for that point

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cost Function Intuition and Model Fit
Question Text: Why does minimizing the squared error cost tend to improve how well the model fits the data?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the cost is unrelated to prediction quality | Because a lower cost makes the slope steeper | Because lower cost means smaller squared errors | Because minimizing cost automatically adds data
Correct Answer: Because lower cost means smaller squared errors

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cost Function Intuition and Model Fit
Question Text: What key intuition emerges from comparing the cost produced by different values of the weight w?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each value of w gives a different line and cost | One value of w can stand for many models | The cost is independent of the training data | The weight w changes the cost but not the line
Correct Answer: Each value of w gives a different line and cost

Question Text: For linear regression with squared-error cost, when both the weight w and bias b are allowed to vary, what shape does the cost function J(w,b) trace out over the (w,b) plane?
Options (A|B|C|D): A flat tilted plane with one constant slope all over | A rugged surface with many separate isolated valleys | A staircase built from discrete flat horizontal steps | A convex bowl with a single lowest point at its base
Correct Answer: A convex bowl with a single lowest point at its base

Question Text: On a contour plot of the cost function J(w,b), what does a single contour line represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Predicted outputs for one fixed input value | The decision boundary of a trained classifier | Training examples that share the same label | Parameter pairs (w,b) that yield the same cost
Correct Answer: Parameter pairs (w,b) that yield the same cost

Question Text: Why is a contour plot a useful way to study the cost function J(w,b)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It can only be drawn when the minimum cost equals zero | It removes the need to evaluate cost on the data | It shows model predictions without using any parameters | It depicts a 3D cost surface in 2D using equal-cost curves
Correct Answer: It depicts a 3D cost surface in 2D using equal-cost curves

Question Text: On a contour plot of J(w,b) drawn as concentric ellipses, where does the parameter pair giving the minimum cost lie?
Options (A|B|C|D): On the largest outermost ellipse | At the center of the smallest ellipse | Only along the line where b equals 0 | At any point where w is largest
Correct Answer: At the center of the smallest ellipse

Question Text: What relationship is illustrated by plotting candidate regression lines alongside their positions on the cost contour plot?
Options (A|B|C|D): The quality of the line's fit is unrelated to the cost | Better-fitting lines map to parameters nearer the cost minimum | Only perfectly horizontal lines can minimize the cost | Every line through the data attains an identical cost value
Correct Answer: Better-fitting lines map to parameters nearer the cost minimum

Question Text: What is the central objective of the gradient descent algorithm?
Options (A|B|C|D): To automatically assign labels to unlabeled examples | To convert a regression problem into a classification one | To iteratively adjust parameters so as to reduce the cost | To reach a perfect, zero-error model in a single step
Correct Answer: To iteratively adjust parameters so as to reduce the cost

Question Text: In the common analogy of standing on a hill and trying to reach the valley, how does gradient descent behave?
Options (A|B|C|D): It steps uphill to gather more information first | It jumps to random spots until it lands in a valley | It moves only sideways, keeping its height unchanged | It repeatedly steps in the steepest downhill direction
Correct Answer: It repeatedly steps in the steepest downhill direction

Question Text: What is meant by a local minimum of a cost function in gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Another name for the set of training examples that are used | The single point holding the highest cost in the whole space | A point lower than its neighbors but not the lowest overall | Any parameter values at which the total cost turns negative
Correct Answer: A point lower than its neighbors but not the lowest overall

Question Text: Why can the choice of starting parameter values affect the outcome of gradient descent on some cost surfaces?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because gradient descent does not actually rely on any parameters | Because parameter initialization only ever matters in supervised tasks | Because different starting values can reach different local minima | Because the cost function completely ignores how it is initialized
Correct Answer: Because different starting values can reach different local minima

Question Text: Why is gradient descent regarded as important well beyond fitting linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It applies only in the case where a model has one single free parameter | It removes any need to define a cost function when fitting a model | It serves solely to draw the cost contour plots used for inspection | It can optimize many functions, including large deep neural networks
Correct Answer: It can optimize many functions, including large deep neural networks

Question Text: At a given parameter value, what information does the derivative term provide to gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Whether the dataset's examples are labeled or not | The total number of training examples available | The exact location of the global minimum of the cost | The direction and local steepness for updating the parameter
Correct Answer: The direction and local steepness for updating the parameter

Question Text: In the update w := w - alpha * (dJ/dw), if the derivative dJ/dw at the current point is positive, how does w change (assuming alpha > 0)?
Options (A|B|C|D): w stays fixed regardless of alpha | w decreases | w increases | w jumps directly to its target value
Correct Answer: w decreases

Question Text: Using the same update w := w - alpha * (dJ/dw) with alpha > 0, if the derivative dJ/dw at the current point is negative, how does w change?
Options (A|B|C|D): w is set to zero automatically | w is converted into a class label | w decreases even further | w increases
Correct Answer: w increases

Question Text: Why is drawing the tangent line to the cost curve helpful for building intuition about the derivative?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes computing the cost function itself wholly unnecessary | Its slope approximates how the cost is changing at that point | It carries meaning only for classification, never for regression | It maps out the model's entire future training path exactly
Correct Answer: Its slope approximates how the cost is changing at that point

Question Text: What is the key intuition behind subtracting the derivative term when updating a parameter in gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): It moves the parameter opposite the uphill slope, which lowers cost | It guarantees the updated parameter value always turns out negative | It forces the underlying model to collapse into a purely linear one | It makes the derivative term grow steadily larger on every step
Correct Answer: It moves the parameter opposite the uphill slope, which lowers cost

Question Text: In the gradient descent update rule, what does the learning rate alpha control?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of output classes to predict | The mathematical form of the cost function | How large each parameter update step is | The number of input features in the model
Correct Answer: How large each parameter update step is

Question Text: What is the likely effect of choosing a learning rate alpha that is too small?
Options (A|B|C|D): The derivative becomes zero at every point | Gradient descent fails right away on the first step | The cost is forced to increase on each step | Gradient descent still works but converges very slowly
Correct Answer: Gradient descent still works but converges very slowly

Question Text: What can go wrong if the learning rate alpha is set too large?
Options (A|B|C|D): The derivative vanishes after just one update | It may overshoot the minimum and fail to converge | The model automatically becomes more accurate | The algorithm silently switches to unsupervised learning
Correct Answer: It may overshoot the minimum and fail to converge

Question Text: With a fixed learning rate alpha, why can gradient descent still settle into a minimum rather than stepping past it forever?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the model gradually stops paying any attention at all to the data | Because every local minimum just happens to share the exact same cost | Because the value of alpha quietly shrinks toward zero all on its own | Because the derivative shrinks near the minimum, so the steps get smaller
Correct Answer: Because the derivative shrinks near the minimum, so the steps get smaller

Question Text: If gradient descent arrives at a point where the derivative dJ/dw is exactly zero, what does the basic update rule do next?
Options (A|B|C|D): It leaves the parameter unchanged | It replaces the current training set | It makes the parameter oscillate widely | It drives the cost down below zero
Correct Answer: It leaves the parameter unchanged

Question Text: Why is the standard form of gradient descent called "batch" gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): It trains many separate models in parallel | Each step uses the entire training set at once | It applies only to batches of image inputs | It updates one training example per step
Correct Answer: Each step uses the entire training set at once

Question Text: When computing the gradient in batch gradient descent for linear regression, what is the sum taken over?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the single largest-error example | Every training example in the dataset | Only features with the largest values | Only the unlabeled examples present
Correct Answer: Every training example in the dataset

Question Text: What is one implication of computing each gradient descent update using the full training set?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each update reflects the whole dataset | No cost function is needed to train | The parameter b can no longer change | The algorithm ignores the target values
Correct Answer: Each update reflects the whole dataset

Question Text: How does batch gradient descent differ from variants that use subsets of the data, such as mini-batch or stochastic gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): It computes updates without using any derivatives | It is identical in practice with no real difference | It uses all examples per step; the others use smaller subsets | It works only for classification, not regression tasks
Correct Answer: It uses all examples per step; the others use smaller subsets

Question Text: Which feature of the update rule justifies the name "batch" gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each derivative sums over i=1 to m, the whole set | "Batch" is just another word for vectorization | Parameters are updated in alphabetical order | The fitted line is plotted in separate batches
Correct Answer: Each derivative sums over i=1 to m, the whole set

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Linear Regression’s Cost Function Has a Global Minimum
Question Text: Why does squared-error linear regression avoid the multiple-local-minima problem that can affect more complex cost surfaces?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it holds w and b fixed at zero | Because it never relies on any derivatives | Because its cost function is convex and bowl-shaped | Because its cost ignores the training data
Correct Answer: Because its cost function is convex and bowl-shaped

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Linear Regression’s Cost Function Has a Global Minimum
Question Text: What does it mean for the squared-error cost function to be "convex"?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always evaluates to zero at initialization | It is bowl-shaped with a single global minimum | It depends on exactly one input feature | Its prediction must be a linear function
Correct Answer: It is bowl-shaped with a single global minimum

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Linear Regression’s Cost Function Has a Global Minimum
Question Text: What practical benefit does the convexity of the squared-error cost provide when training linear regression with gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Training the model no longer requires using any data at all | Gradient descent converges to the answer in a single update step | The particular choice of cost function stops mattering at all | With a suitable learning rate, it reaches the global minimum
Correct Answer: With a suitable learning rate, it reaches the global minimum

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Linear Regression’s Cost Function Has a Global Minimum
Question Text: Which statement best distinguishes the linear regression cost surface from a general "hilly" surface with many valleys?
Options (A|B|C|D): The hilly case alone is supervised, while regression is unsupervised | Only the hilly surface ever makes any real use of gradient steps | Its squared-error surface is a single convex bowl, not many valleys | Linear regression actually has no free parameters left to optimize
Correct Answer: Its squared-error surface is a single convex bowl, not many valleys

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Linear Regression’s Cost Function Has a Global Minimum
Question Text: Which belief about gradient descent applied to squared-error linear regression is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That its updates are computed from derivatives | That it tends to get stuck in poor local minima | That it depends on a chosen learning rate | That its goal is to minimize the cost function
Correct Answer: That it tends to get stuck in poor local minima

Question Text: What is the key change when moving from univariate to multiple linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): The training set no longer needs labels | It uses several input features instead of one | The model no longer has any parameters | The predicted output stops being numeric
Correct Answer: It uses several input features instead of one

Question Text: In multiple linear regression, what does the symbol n typically represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of input features | The number of training examples | The number of gradient descent steps | The number of output categories
Correct Answer: The number of input features

Question Text: In multiple-feature notation, what does x^(i) represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The ith feature across all examples | The target value of the ith example | The vector of all features for example i | The ith gradient descent update
Correct Answer: The vector of all features for example i

Question Text: Why might multiple linear regression predict housing prices better than single-variable regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It can use more relevant inputs like size, floors, and age | It mathematically guarantees perfectly accurate predictions | It quietly reframes the prediction task as a clustering problem | It completely removes the need to train on any data at all
Correct Answer: It can use more relevant inputs like size, floors, and age

Question Text: Which model form correctly expresses multiple linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): f(x) = w1*x1 + w2*x2 + ... + wn*xn + b | f(x) = x / y, a ratio of inputs | f(x) = category(x), a class label | f(x) = b, a constant with no inputs

Question Text: Why is vector notation introduced for multiple linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): To eliminate the need for features | To write the same model more compactly | To turn the model into a classifier | To drop the bias term from the model
Correct Answer: To write the same model more compactly

Question Text: In f(x) = w·x + b, what does the dot product w·x compute?
Options (A|B|C|D): The output class that scores the highest | The feature count multiplied by the bias | The gap between the target and the prediction | The sum of paired products of weights and features
Correct Answer: The sum of paired products of weights and features

Question Text: If w and x are vectors, what is the role of b in f(x) = w·x + b?
Options (A|B|C|D): It counts the dimensions of vector w | It converts the input x into a matrix | It stores the current loss value | It is added as a separate scalar offset
Correct Answer: It is added as a separate scalar offset

Question Text: Which statement correctly describes the notation used for multiple linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): Vectors show up only within unsupervised learning settings, never here | The bias b also becomes a vector whenever the input x is a vector | x^(i) can be a vector, unlike the scalar x in the univariate case | Dot product notation is useful only for drawing cost contour plots
Correct Answer: x^(i) can be a vector, unlike the scalar x in the univariate case

Question Text: Regarding terminology, which point about naming this model is commonly clarified to avoid confusion?
Options (A|B|C|D): Dot products taken between two vectors are simply not permitted in machine learning | Only one single input feature is ever truly capable of carrying meaningful signal | Vectors written in this notation are never once allowed to be laid out as rows | The model with multiple features is "multiple," not "multivariate," regression
Correct Answer: The model with multiple features is "multiple," not "multivariate," regression

Question Text: Why is vectorization especially useful when writing machine learning code?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes the underlying model less mathematical | It guarantees a model with higher predictive accuracy | It usually makes the code shorter and faster to run | It removes the need to collect training data
Correct Answer: It usually makes the code shorter and faster to run

Question Text: What is the most efficient way to compute the dot product of a weight vector w and a feature vector x in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sort the two vectors and then multiply them pairwise | Convert both vectors to strings before combining them | Use a vectorized NumPy operation such as np.dot(w, x) | Write out each multiplication term by hand and sum them
Correct Answer: Use a vectorized NumPy operation such as np.dot(w, x)

Question Text: Why can vectorized code run much faster than an equivalent for-loop implementation?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because libraries and hardware run many operations in parallel | Because it quietly cuts down the amount of training data used | Because it manages to avoid using any model parameters at all | Because it swaps the learning objective for a much simpler one
Correct Answer: Because libraries and hardware run many operations in parallel

Question Text: Which statement about vectorization is a misunderstanding that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is purely a cosmetic rewrite with no effect on speed | It can improve efficiency without changing the mathematical result | It is most beneficial when there are many features to process | It lets code take advantage of optimized linear algebra libraries
Correct Answer: It is purely a cosmetic rewrite with no effect on speed

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gradient Descent for Multiple Linear Regression
Question Text: How does the gradient descent update change when moving from one feature to multiple features?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each weight w_j gets its own update along with the bias b | The model becomes unsupervised and needs no labels | Only the bias b is updated while the weights stay fixed | The cost function is no longer needed during training
Correct Answer: Each weight w_j gets its own update along with the bias b

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gradient Descent for Multiple Linear Regression
Question Text: What stays conceptually the same when extending gradient descent to multiple linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): The need for labeled training examples disappears | The number of parameters is always exactly one | The model output must now become categorical | Repeatedly updating parameters to reduce a cost function
Correct Answer: Repeatedly updating parameters to reduce a cost function

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gradient Descent for Multiple Linear Regression
Question Text: Why is vectorization especially helpful for gradient descent in multiple linear regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because many parameters and features must be updated efficiently | Because it prevents the model from overfitting on its own | Because it converts the regression task into clustering | Because linear regression otherwise has no closed-form solution
Correct Answer: Because many parameters and features must be updated efficiently

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gradient Descent for Multiple Linear Regression
Question Text: During training of a multiple linear regression model, what quantities are actually being updated?
Options (A|B|C|D): The algebraic formula of the cost function | The weight vector w and the bias term b | The labeled training examples in the dataset | The names assigned to each input feature
Correct Answer: The weight vector w and the bias term b

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gradient Descent for Multiple Linear Regression
Question Text: Which belief is a common conceptual mistake when reasoning about the multi-feature gradient descent update?
Options (A|B|C|D): The update is applied iteratively until the cost converges | Assuming there is still only one weight left to optimize | Vector notation can simplify how the update is written | Each input feature has its own weight that must be learned
Correct Answer: Assuming there is still only one weight left to optimize

Question Text: What is the normal equation method used for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Choosing how many input features to keep automatically | Solving for linear regression parameters without iterating | Grouping unlabeled data points into clusters | Training an arbitrary deep neural network architecture
Correct Answer: Solving for linear regression parameters without iterating

Question Text: What is a key limitation of the normal equation compared with gradient descent?
Options (A|B|C|D): It cannot be applied to linear regression problems | It requires all labels to be removed before solving | It always produces worse predictions than gradient descent | It does not generalize to most other learning algorithms
Correct Answer: It does not generalize to most other learning algorithms

Question Text: Why might gradient descent still be preferred even though the normal equation exists?
Options (A|B|C|D): It works across far more models and scales better | It removes the need to define any cost function | It only ever needs a single training example to run | It avoids every matrix computation entirely and forever
Correct Answer: It works across far more models and scales better

Question Text: What might happen inside a mature machine learning library when it fits a linear regression model?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is unable to solve for the parameters on its own | It may use the normal equation behind the scenes | It always restricts itself to batch gradient descent | It converts the regression problem into classification
Correct Answer: It may use the normal equation behind the scenes

Question Text: Which statement about the normal equation is a misunderstanding that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is a specialized alternative mainly for linear regression | Libraries may sometimes call it internally to fit a model | It skips iterative updates for that specific problem | It is the main general-purpose training method for modern models
Correct Answer: It is the main general-purpose training method for modern models

Question Text: What is one reason neural networks are attractive relative to simpler models like decision trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): They outperform every other model on every possible task | They never require any optimization during training | They can be scaled up and combined into larger systems | They make labeled training data completely unnecessary
Correct Answer: They can be scaled up and combined into larger systems

Question Text: What broader point applies when comparing neural networks with other model families such as decision trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): Once neural networks exist, every simpler model is never useful for anything | Model families have trade-offs; neural nets excel in larger composite systems | Decision trees and neural networks are exactly identical models in theory | Neural networks fully remove any need for feature design in every single case
Correct Answer: Model families have trade-offs; neural nets excel in larger composite systems

Question Text: Why might neural networks be favored in systems that combine multiple learned components?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because decision trees are simply unable to produce any predictions at all | Because combining several components removes the need for any training | Because neural networks can be trained well without using any data at all | Because neural network modules can be connected together more naturally
Correct Answer: Because neural network modules can be connected together more naturally

Question Text: Which belief is a misunderstanding to avoid when comparing different types of models?
Options (A|B|C|D): One model class is universally best regardless of task and scale | Neural networks can be powerful for complex large-scale problems | Some methods combine into larger systems more easily than others | Which method works best depends on the specific application
Correct Answer: One model class is universally best regardless of task and scale

Question Text: What practical theme does the comparison between neural networks and decision trees illustrate?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only supervised learning models are ever genuinely worth considering here | Model architecture stops mattering entirely once you collect enough data | Model choice depends on how well a method fits the problem and scales | The newest model available is always going to be the very best choice
Correct Answer: Model choice depends on how well a method fits the problem and scales

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Inference vs. Training in Neural Networks
Question Text: What does inference mean in the context of a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): Running a trained network to produce an output from an input | Adjusting the weights using labeled training examples | Selecting which optimizer to use during fitting | Removing hidden layers to shrink the model
Correct Answer: Running a trained network to produce an output from an input

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Inference vs. Training in Neural Networks
Question Text: What does training a neural network refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): Running forward propagation just once on a single fresh input | Learning the weight values from data so predictions are useful | Drawing out the full architecture diagram before writing any code | Converting the model's output tensors into plain NumPy arrays first
Correct Answer: Learning the weight values from data so predictions are useful

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Inference vs. Training in Neural Networks
Question Text: In a TensorFlow model, forward propagation corresponds most directly to which operation?
Options (A|B|C|D): Setting every single weight value by hand on each forward pass | Randomly reinitializing all of the training labels before each pass | Inference that computes activations through layers to the output | Partitioning the whole training dataset into unlabeled clusters
Correct Answer: Inference that computes activations through layers to the output

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Inference vs. Training in Neural Networks
Question Text: Why is it useful to keep inference (using a model) and training (fitting a model) conceptually distinct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the two terms actually mean exactly the same thing in practice | Because inference applies only to regression tasks, not classification | Because using a model and learning one are related but separate steps | Because training somehow makes the model stop computing activations
Correct Answer: Because using a model and learning one are related but separate steps

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Inference vs. Training in Neural Networks
Question Text: Which common confusion about inference and training should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Recognizing that training sets weights while inference applies them | Understanding that both steps appear in typical workflows | Believing that computing a prediction is the same as fitting the weights | Noting that forward propagation is the core of inference
Correct Answer: Believing that computing a prediction is the same as fitting the weights

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Neural Networks Work Well with Large Data
Question Text: Why have neural networks grown sharply more important in recent years?
Options (A|B|C|D): They removed the need for any optimization step during the training | They require far less underlying mathematics than older methods did | Their accuracy tends to keep rising as more data becomes available | They now operate exclusively on image data and nothing else at all
Correct Answer: Their accuracy tends to keep rising as more data becomes available

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Neural Networks Work Well with Large Data
Question Text: How do neural networks typically contrast with many traditional learning algorithms as data grows?
Options (A|B|C|D): Neural nets help only on very small datasets | Traditional methods always improve faster with more data | Traditional methods are unsupervised; neural nets are supervised | Neural nets keep gaining where many older methods plateau early
Correct Answer: Neural nets keep gaining where many older methods plateau early

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Neural Networks Work Well with Large Data
Question Text: What broader change made neural networks far more useful in practice?
Options (A|B|C|D): CPUs were abandoned across machine learning | Labels vanished from nearly all modern datasets | The volume of available digital data grew dramatically | Neural networks stopped relying on trainable parameters
Correct Answer: The volume of available digital data grew dramatically

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Neural Networks Work Well with Large Data
Question Text: Given plenty of data, why might a larger neural network beat a smaller one?
Options (A|B|C|D): Smaller networks are incapable of doing inference | Greater capacity lets it exploit more of the information | Larger models automatically guarantee zero error | Bigger networks skip the training step entirely
Correct Answer: Greater capacity lets it exploit more of the information

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Neural Networks Work Well with Large Data
Question Text: Which misunderstanding about data and neural networks should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That their strength is closely tied to access to ample useful data | That model size can influence final performance | That neural nets are powerful regardless of data scale or quality | That performance trends can vary across algorithms
Correct Answer: That neural nets are powerful regardless of data scale or quality

Question Text: Why are biological neurons mentioned when introducing artificial neural networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): To argue that trained neural networks reason exactly the way a human does | To claim that artificial neurons replicate real brain cells in detail | To give loose intuition for units that take inputs and emit an output | To show that biology knowledge is required in order to write the code
Correct Answer: To give loose intuition for units that take inputs and emit an output

Question Text: In the simplified view, what does a single artificial neuron do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It takes numeric inputs, computes, and outputs a number | It stores an entire dataset inside itself | It labels examples without any training | It serves as a replacement for matrix multiplication
Correct Answer: It takes numeric inputs, computes, and outputs a number

Question Text: What major caveat applies to the biological inspiration behind neural networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): Biological and artificial neurons are mathematically identical | Knowing dendrite behavior is required to build networks | The brain is still so poorly understood that the analogy is loose | Deep learning is essentially a subfield of biology
Correct Answer: The brain is still so poorly understood that the analogy is loose

Question Text: Despite the brain being poorly understood, why does the biological analogy still help?
Options (A|B|C|D): It gives an intuitive starting point, though engineering now leads practice | It shows that every good model must copy the wiring of the brain exactly | It removes the need for experimentation when designing a network architecture | It proves that any sufficiently large neural network gains consciousness
Correct Answer: It gives an intuitive starting point, though engineering now leads practice

Question Text: Which misunderstanding about the brain analogy should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Recognizing modern networks work despite being simplified | Treating biology as motivation rather than a literal blueprint | Understanding that the brain remains poorly understood | Assuming deep learning succeeds by modeling neuroscience in detail
Correct Answer: Assuming deep learning succeeds by modeling neuroscience in detail

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: What is the core idea of viewing neural networks as learned feature engineering?
Options (A|B|C|D): They function correctly only when every input feature is strictly binary | They remove the need to supply the network with any input features at all | They memorize the training labels directly without any real computation | They learn useful internal features instead of using hand-designed ones
Correct Answer: They learn useful internal features instead of using hand-designed ones

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: How do hidden layers support the idea of learned feature engineering?
Options (A|B|C|D): They take over the job of the output layer and remove it from the network | They store the entire training set unchanged and look up answers later | They force every activation in the layer to settle on one single value | They learn transformations of inputs that become more useful for the task
Correct Answer: They learn transformations of inputs that become more useful for the task

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: Why is the learned-feature-engineering view helpful for intuition?
Options (A|B|C|D): It implies a network never needs the designer to make architecture choices | It explains how complex predictions get built from simpler learned features | It shows that input features are basically irrelevant in machine learning | It proves that every hidden unit ends up with a clear human-readable meaning
Correct Answer: It explains how complex predictions get built from simpler learned features

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: What is an important caution about features that a network learns internally?
Options (A|B|C|D): Learning good features makes the iterative training process unnecessary | Learned features can help even when humans cannot easily interpret them | Every neuron reliably maps to a single clearly named real-world concept | Feature learning takes place only inside the final output layer of the net
Correct Answer: Learned features can help even when humans cannot easily interpret them

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: Which misunderstanding about feature learning in neural networks should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That learned internal representations help make neural networks powerful | That successive hidden layers can transform the network inputs progressively | That learned features free humans from caring about inputs or data quality | That a network can reduce some of the manual feature design work on its own
Correct Answer: That learned features free humans from caring about inputs or data quality

Question Text: What was the original motivation behind the invention of neural networks decades ago?
Options (A|B|C|D): To design faster graphics processing hardware | To build software that imitates how the brain learns | To replace linear regression with a richer formula | To automate the collection of large digital datasets
Correct Answer: To build software that imitates how the brain learns

Question Text: Which part of a biological neuron sends electrical impulses outward to other neurons downstream?
Options (A|B|C|D): The axon | The dendrites | The nucleus | The cell body
Correct Answer: The axon

Question Text: Which statement best describes the relationship between modern artificial neural networks and the human brain?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each artificial neuron is more intricate than a biological one | They are identical to the brain in both function and architecture | They reproduce biological learning so well they replace neuroscience | They are vastly oversimplified and resemble brain learning only loosely
Correct Answer: They are vastly oversimplified and resemble brain learning only loosely

Question Text: In the simplified mathematical model of a neuron, what basic operation does the neuron perform?
Options (A|B|C|D): It takes numeric inputs, computes on them, and outputs a number | It emits random electrical impulses to simulate thinking | It acts as linear regression but holds no parameters | It stores a fixed label and returns that label unchanged
Correct Answer: It takes numeric inputs, computes on them, and outputs a number

Question Text: Why do neuroscientists keep making fundamental discoveries even though deep learning is already successful?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because we still understand very little about how the brain works | Because the brain's learning rules are now completely mapped | Because deep learning rests on perfectly accurate brain models | Because biological neurons are simpler to program than artificial ones
Correct Answer: Because we still understand very little about how the brain works

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: Compared with manual feature engineering (such as hand-combining house size and lot dimensions for price prediction), what is a neural network's main advantage?
Options (A|B|C|D): It relies on fewer mathematical formulas than manual work | It is restricted to fitting purely linear functions | It can learn useful features from the data on its own | It needs the user to hand-combine features into better ones
Correct Answer: It can learn useful features from the data on its own

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: In a face-recognition neural network, what do the earliest hidden layers typically learn to detect?
Options (A|B|C|D): Distinct face parts such as an eye or an ear | Very short lines or oriented edges in the image | Whole face shapes like a complete head | The identity of the specific person shown
Correct Answer: Very short lines or oriented edges in the image

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: In a face-recognition network where early layers detect edges and middle layers detect face parts, what does a still-later hidden layer typically learn to do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Convert pixel intensities into a tabular spreadsheet | Combine face parts into larger, coarser face shapes | Detect only simple edges and short line segments | Identify the dominant color of the input image
Correct Answer: Combine face parts into larger, coarser face shapes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: If the same neural network architecture is trained on pictures of cars instead of faces, what happens to the features its layers learn?
Options (A|B|C|D): It stops working because it was built only for faces | It learns edges, then car parts, then whole car shapes | It still detects eyes and ears in the first layer | It needs the programmer to redefine each hidden layer
Correct Answer: It learns edges, then car parts, then whole car shapes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: What role do the "activations" passed from one layer to the next play in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are learned features that ease the task for later layers | They are the raw pixel intensities of the original image | They are the single final prediction returned to the user | They are values used to hand-compute the cost function
Correct Answer: They are learned features that ease the task for later layers

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Input Layer, Hidden Layer, and Output Layer
Question Text: In neural network terminology, what is another name for the input layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): Layer 0 | The hidden layer | Layer 1 | The activation function
Correct Answer: Layer 0

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Input Layer, Hidden Layer, and Output Layer
Question Text: Why is a middle layer of a neural network called a "hidden layer"?
Options (A|B|C|D): It stays invisible to the processor while the training program is executing | Programmers are not permitted to view the source code that computes its values | Its activations never appear in the training data, which lists only x and y | Its only purpose is to encrypt the feature values flowing through the network
Correct Answer: Its activations never appear in the training data, which lists only x and y

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Input Layer, Hidden Layer, and Output Layer
Question Text: What does the output layer of a neural network typically provide?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total count of neurons across the network | The model's final prediction or estimated probability | The raw input feature vector x for the network | The learned features feeding the hidden layers
Correct Answer: The model's final prediction or estimated probability

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Input Layer, Hidden Layer, and Output Layer
Question Text: By the usual convention (which excludes the input layer from the count), how many layers does a network with one input layer, one hidden layer, and one output layer have?
Options (A|B|C|D): Two layers | Three layers | Zero layers | One layer
Correct Answer: Two layers

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Input Layer, Hidden Layer, and Output Layer
Question Text: Which statement best describes a "layer" in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): The physical processor hardware on which the learning algorithm is executed | A group of neurons that take similar inputs and together output a vector | The ordered list of target labels that is attached to the training examples | A single neuron that receives many different inputs all at the same time
Correct Answer: A group of neurons that take similar inputs and together output a vector

Question Text: In the context of a single neuron, what does the term "activation" refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The act of powering on the computer that runs it | The number of training iterations completed so far | How strongly the neuron sends output to neurons downstream | The total number of parameters w and b in the neuron
Correct Answer: How strongly the neuron sends output to neurons downstream

Question Text: What is the name of the function g (such as the sigmoid) that produces a neuron's activation value?
Options (A|B|C|D): The prediction function | The loss function | The cost function | The activation function
Correct Answer: The activation function

Question Text: In a multi-layer neural network, which notation represents the output activation vector of the second layer?
Which statement about activation functions is true?
Options (A|B|C|D): The sigmoid is the only activation function any neural network can use | Activation functions are applied only to units within the input layer | Functions other than sigmoid can be used to make a network more powerful | Activation functions are what hand-engineer the input features of the data
Correct Answer: Functions other than sigmoid can be used to make a network more powerful

Question Text: An output-layer neuron produces an activation of 0.84. To turn this into a binary prediction of 1 or 0, what should you do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Threshold the activation at 0.5 | Multiply the activation by two | Round the activation to the nearest ten | Switch the activation function to ReLU
Correct Answer: Threshold the activation at 0.5

Question Text: Which two design choices primarily define a neural network's architecture?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of hidden layers and the units per layer | The programming language and the source of the data | The number of training examples and the target accuracy | The GPU model and the size of the dataset used
Correct Answer: The number of hidden layers and the units per layer

Question Text: In the activation notation a^[l]_j, what does the superscript [l] in square brackets indicate?
Options (A|B|C|D): The index of the current training example | The position of the neuron within the layer | The exponent that the activation a is raised to | The layer of the network the activation belongs to
Correct Answer: The layer of the network the activation belongs to

Question Text: What is a "multilayer perceptron"?
Options (A|B|C|D): A neural net with hidden and output layers | A hardware device built for big data jobs | A structure found in the biological brain | A single-step linear regression model
Correct Answer: A neural net with hidden and output layers

Question Text: By convention, which layer's activation is written as a^[0] so the layer numbering stays consistent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The first hidden layer of the network | The single output unit of the network | The final layer producing the output | The input feature vector x of the network
Correct Answer: The input feature vector x of the network

Question Text: What determines the activation values a computed by a hidden layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): The single final output probability that the whole network ends up producing | That layer's own parameters w and b and the previous layer's activations | A fixed preference value that the user sets by hand before any training | Only the original input feature vector x that was first fed to the network
Correct Answer: That layer's own parameters w and b and the previous layer's activations

Question Text: What is the core purpose of the forward propagation algorithm in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): To produce predictions or inferences from an input | To attach the correct labels to the training images | To compute the gradients of the cost function | To update the network's weights w and biases b
Correct Answer: To produce predictions or inferences from an input

Question Text: During forward propagation, what serves as the input to Layer 2?
Options (A|B|C|D): The ground-truth label y for the example | A freshly generated set of random numbers | The output activation vector of Layer 1 | The original input feature vector x
Correct Answer: The output activation vector of Layer 1

Question Text: In what order are the layers evaluated during forward propagation?
Options (A|B|C|D): From the input layer through the hidden layers to the output layer | From the output layer working backward toward the original input layer | Every layer simultaneously computes all of its outputs in one step | From the leaf nodes upward through the branches to a single root node
Correct Answer: From the input layer through the hidden layers to the output layer

Question Text: In a handwritten-digit classifier, what does the final forward-propagation output a^[3] represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The intensity value of the 64th image pixel | The total of all weights in the third layer | The predicted probability the image is that digit | A fixed vector consisting of zeros and ones
Correct Answer: The predicted probability the image is that digit

Question Text: Why is computing activations through forward propagation often called "inference"?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it applies learned parameters to predict an output for a new input | Because it does away with any need for a label y during the whole process | Because it works backward to recover the full training set from the weights | Because a person still has to step in and finalize each predicted answer
Correct Answer: Because it applies learned parameters to predict an output for a new input

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: When a hidden layer uses sigmoid activations, what is each neuron in that layer effectively computing?
Options (A|B|C|D): A small logistic regression unit | A linear regression line with no sigmoid | A plain sum of its incoming inputs | A number drawn at random each time
Correct Answer: A small logistic regression unit

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: If a hidden layer contains three neurons, how many separate sets of parameters (w and b) does that layer hold?
Options (A|B|C|D): Zero, since only the output layer has them | Three sets, one for each neuron | Four sets, one per input feature | One shared set for the whole layer
Correct Answer: Three sets, one for each neuron

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: How is the activation a_1 of a hidden unit related to the logistic (sigmoid) function g?
Options (A|B|C|D): a_1 is a weight used inside the function g | a_1 is the derivative of the function g | a_1 is the raw input passed into g | a_1 is the value of g applied to (w · x + b)
Correct Answer: a_1 is the value of g applied to (w · x + b)

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: What does it mean to call a neural network layer "dense"?
Options (A|B|C|D): The layer relies on a linear activation function | Every unit connects to all outputs of the previous layer | The layer is unusually expensive to compute | The layer contains a very large number of units
Correct Answer: Every unit connects to all outputs of the previous layer

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: In a binary demand-prediction network, how does the output layer differ from the hidden layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): It contains strictly more neurons than the preceding hidden layer does | It carries no parameters w or b and simply forwards the activations along | It does not pass its result through the sigmoid activation function at all | It has a single unit mapping the hidden activations to one probability
Correct Answer: It has a single unit mapping the hidden activations to one probability

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks

Correct Answer: A fully white pixel

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: A binary digit classifier has two hidden layers with 25 and 15 units. How many units are in its output layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): One unit giving the chance of 1 vs 0 | 40 units, the sum of the hidden units | 64 units, matching the input pixels | 15 units, matching the last hidden layer
Correct Answer: One unit giving the chance of 1 vs 0

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: What is the goal of a multiclass handwritten-digit recognition system?
Options (A|B|C|D): To rotate images to a standard angle | To tell apart only the digits 0 and 1 | To recognize each digit from 0 to 9 | To count how many pixels an image has
Correct Answer: To recognize each digit from 0 to 9

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: In a network that classifies digits 0 through 9, how many output units does the final Softmax layer have?
In the Keras call Dense(units=3, activation="sigmoid"), what does the argument units=3 specify?
Options (A|B|C|D): The learning rate used for that layer | The number of neurons in that layer | The number of training examples fed in | The total number of layers in the network
Correct Answer: The number of neurons in that layer

Question Text: Which of these is the most widely used framework for building and training deep learning models?
Options (A|B|C|D): Pandas | NumPy | TensorFlow | Matplotlib
Correct Answer: TensorFlow

Question Text: Given a Keras Dense layer object named Layer1 and an input x, how do you compute that layer's output activations a1 in code?
Options (A|B|C|D): a1 = Dense(x) | a1 = sigmoid(x) | a1 = Layer1(x) | a1 = Layer1.weights()
Correct Answer: a1 = Layer1(x)

Question Text: In neural network terminology, what does the term "dense" layer refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The raw input feature vector that is passed into the network | An activation function that squashes values into the range (0,1) | A training process that converges unusually slowly over time | A layer where every unit connects to all previous activations
Correct Answer: A layer where every unit connects to all previous activations

Question Text: In a Keras Dense layer, what does the setting activation="sigmoid" specify?
Options (A|B|C|D): The scheme used to initialize the layer's weight values | The optimizer used to carry out the gradient descent step | The function applied to each unit to produce its activation | The loss function that the overall model tries to minimize
Correct Answer: The function applied to each unit to produce its activation

Question Text: Why do NumPy and TensorFlow sometimes represent the same data in slightly different ways?
Options (A|B|C|D): NumPy is only ever able to represent one-dimensional vectors of numbers | They were built years apart by different teams using different conventions | NumPy stores numbers in binary while TensorFlow stores them in decimal form | A single team built both at the same time with fully identical conventions
Correct Answer: They were built years apart by different teams using different conventions

Question Text: What is the name of the core data structure TensorFlow uses to represent matrices and multidimensional arrays?
Options (A|B|C|D): Series | Column vector | Tensor | DataFrame
Correct Answer: Tensor

Question Text: In Python, how does a 1D vector such as [200, 17] differ from a 2D matrix such as [[200, 17]]?
Options (A|B|C|D): The 2D matrix is structurally unable to hold any numeric values at all | They are completely identical to one another once they are stored in code | The 1D vector runs faster to process but loses some numerical accuracy | The 1D vector has no row/column shape; the 2D matrix has set dimensions
Correct Answer: The 1D vector has no row/column shape; the 2D matrix has set dimensions

Question Text: Which call converts a TensorFlow tensor back into an ordinary NumPy array?
Options (A|B|C|D): tensor.as_array() | tensor.to_numpy() | tensor.numpy() | tensor.convert()
Correct Answer: tensor.numpy()

Question Text: Which Python library is the standard tool for linear algebra and array computation that existed before TensorFlow?
Options (A|B|C|D): Keras | Scikit-learn | NumPy | PyTorch
Correct Answer: NumPy

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: What does the Sequential model in TensorFlow/Keras do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It converts incoming NumPy arrays into TensorFlow tensors | It computes the value of the cost function for a single example | It trains the assembled network by running gradient descent | It chains multiple layers together in order to form one network
Correct Answer: It chains multiple layers together in order to form one network

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: When building a digit classifier, what is the main advantage of using a Sequential model instead of wiring the layers together by hand?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes any need to define a dedicated output layer at all | It lets TensorFlow pass data between the layers automatically | It is the only available way to make use of the sigmoid function | It makes the resulting network slower to run but more accurate
Correct Answer: It lets TensorFlow pass data between the layers automatically

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: What are the three general steps for specifying and training a model in TensorFlow?
