Options (A|B|C|D): Initialize weights, take the derivative, update | Define the model, compile it, then fit it | Receive input, process it, return output | Scale the features, train, then test
Correct Answer: Define the model, compile it, then fit it

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: When modeling whether a batch of coffee is roasted well, why might you use a Sequential model with two layers?
Options (A|B|C|D): A hidden layer can extract features and the output layer gives a probability | Splitting into two layers lets the model avoid working with tensors entirely | Each layer is restricted to processing only one single input feature at a time | Two separate layers are required so that the coffee ends up roasted twice over
Correct Answer: A hidden layer can extract features and the output layer gives a probability

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: Which method generates predictions on new inputs from a trained Sequential model?
Options (A|B|C|D): model.forward() | model.compile() | model.predict() | model.fit()
Correct Answer: model.predict()

Question Text: In a single neural network layer, how is the activation of one unit computed by hand?
Options (A|B|C|D): Dot the weights w with the input a_in, add bias b, then apply the activation | Use a for loop to count how many of the input pixels are nonzero | Add together all of the weight values that appear anywhere in that layer | Take the arithmetic average of all of the input values to the layer
Correct Answer: Dot the weights w with the input a_in, add bias b, then apply the activation

Question Text: If you implement forward propagation for a layer of 3 units in plain Python, what would you use to hold the layer's final activations?
Options (A|B|C|D): A text string built up from the pixel values | A single floating-point number value | A Python list of the raw pixel intensities | A NumPy array holding the 3 activation values
Correct Answer: A NumPy array holding the 3 activation values

Question Text: When computing a unit's activation by hand, what does the quantity z represent before the activation function is applied?
Options (A|B|C|D): The weighted sum of the inputs plus the bias | The cost incurred by the prediction error | The final predicted output value of the unit | The learning rate used during model training
Correct Answer: The weighted sum of the inputs plus the bias

Question Text: Why is it worthwhile to learn how to implement forward propagation from scratch?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is a strict requirement listed for every machine learning job opening | It demonstrates why TensorFlow should essentially never be used | It builds intuition for what libraries do inside and helps with debugging | It is needed in order to design graphics processing unit hardware
Correct Answer: It builds intuition for what libraries do inside and helps with debugging

Question Text: In the code for a single layer's forward pass, what does a_in represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The final output produced by the entire network | The activation vector coming from the previous layer | The true target label y for the current example | The count of neurons present in the current layer
Correct Answer: The activation vector coming from the previous layer

Question Text: A dense layer's weight matrix W is stored so that each column holds the weights for one unit. How can the number of units in the layer be read directly from W?
Options (A|B|C|D): It equals the number of rows of W | It cannot be recovered from W alone | It equals the sum of all entries of W | It equals the number of columns of W
Correct Answer: It equals the number of columns of W

Question Text: In a from-scratch Python implementation of a single dense layer, what is the role of the for loop that iterates over the units?
Options (A|B|C|D): It computes the activation of each unit in the layer in turn | It steps through the training examples one at a time | It accumulates the total cost J across the dataset | It searches for the global minimum of the gradient
Correct Answer: It computes the activation of each unit in the layer in turn

Question Text: Inside the loop 'for j in range(units)', the statement 'w = W[:,j]' is executed. What does this statement do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It extracts row j of W, the inputs to unit j | It returns the dot product across all columns | It extracts column j of W, the weights for unit j | It zeros out every weight stored in W
Correct Answer: It extracts column j of W, the weights for unit j

Question Text: Just before the unit-by-unit loop in a dense layer, the line 'a = np.zeros(units)' runs. Why is this line needed?
Options (A|B|C|D): To preallocate an output array sized to hold each of the unit activations | To reset all of the model parameters that were already learned back to zero | To fill in any missing or invalid entries in the input feature vector | To shrink the learning rate down to zero just before the updates begin
Correct Answer: To preallocate an output array sized to hold each of the unit activations

Question Text: A general dense-layer function is given the input activations 'a_in' together with the layer's weights W and bias b. What does the function return?
Options (A|B|C|D): The final scalar cost J for the network | A scatter plot visualizing the input data | The unchanged weight matrix W it was passed | The layer's output activation vector 'a_out'
Correct Answer: The layer's output activation vector 'a_out'

Question Text: What practice has most enabled researchers to scale up to very large neural networks over the past decade?
Options (A|B|C|D): Restricting the models to use only biological neuron designs | Writing efficient vectorized implementations of the network | Avoiding matrix operations in favor of plain scalar loops | Training the models on far smaller amounts of input data
Correct Answer: Writing efficient vectorized implementations of the network

Question Text: What is the result of applying the sigmoid function element-wise to a matrix Z?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each entry of Z is passed through sigmoid individually | Every negative entry of Z is clipped so it becomes zero | The matrix Z is collapsed down to a single scalar value | The matrix Z is multiplied by its own transpose Z^T
Correct Answer: Each entry of Z is passed through sigmoid individually

Question Text: In a vectorized implementation of forward propagation, what replaces the separate per-neuron dot products used in a loop-based version?
Options (A|B|C|D): Manual addition of the weight values | A Python for loop over the neurons | A linear activation applied to each neuron | One matrix multiplication for the whole layer
Correct Answer: One matrix multiplication for the whole layer

Question Text: What is the main practical benefit of representing a layer's activations and weights as 2D arrays (matrices) in code?
Options (A|B|C|D): It eliminates the need for any bias parameters | It makes the source code easier to read | It changes the mathematical form of the sigmoid | Parallel hardware can then run the math far faster
Correct Answer: Parallel hardware can then run the math far faster

Question Text: Which piece of hardware was originally built to accelerate computer graphics yet turns out to be very effective for deep learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): The GPU (graphics processing unit) | The CPU (central processing unit) | The system memory (RAM) | The hard disk drive (HDD)
Correct Answer: The GPU (graphics processing unit)

Question Text: When forming the product A·B of two matrices, what condition must the dimensions satisfy?
Options (A|B|C|D): A must have more columns than B has columns | The rows of A must equal the columns of B | The columns of A must equal the rows of B | Both matrices must have exactly one column
Correct Answer: The columns of A must equal the rows of B

Question Text: Which NumPy function carries out matrix multiplication of two arrays?
Options (A|B|C|D): np.array_split() | np.multiply() | np.sum() | np.matmul()
Correct Answer: np.matmul()

Question Text: What is the effect of 'A.T' (equivalently 'np.transpose(A)') on a matrix A?
Options (A|B|C|D): It reflects A over its main diagonal, swapping rows and columns | It strips out the bias vector that is being stored inside A | It subtracts the mean so that each column of A averages to zero | It raises every individual element of A to the second power
Correct Answer: It reflects A over its main diagonal, swapping rows and columns

Question Text: Why does the order of the factors matter when multiplying matrices, so that A·B generally differs from B·A?
Options (A|B|C|D): NumPy permits matrix multiplication in only one single fixed order | The columns of the first factor must match the rows of the second | Matrix products are fully commutative, so the order has no effect | The bias term is required to appear inside the first factor matrix
Correct Answer: The columns of the first factor must match the rows of the second

Question Text: In modern Python, which operator serves as shorthand for a call to matmul between two arrays?
When training a Keras model in TensorFlow, what is specified during the 'compile' step?
Options (A|B|C|D): The loss function the network will be trained against | The predictions the model makes on fresh inputs | The arrangement of layers that defines the model | The packaging of the model into a mobile app
Correct Answer: The loss function the network will be trained against

Question Text: In the Keras 'fit' function, what does the 'epochs' argument specify?
Options (A|B|C|D): What fraction of the data is held out for testing | How many full passes the optimizer makes over the data | How many layers are stacked inside the network | How many units are placed in each hidden layer
Correct Answer: How many full passes the optimizer makes over the data

Question Text: Which call actually launches the training loop of a compiled Keras model?
Options (A|B|C|D): model.compile() | model.start() | model.train() | model.fit()
Correct Answer: model.fit()

Question Text: What kind of algorithm does TensorFlow use under the hood to minimize a neural network's cost function?
Options (A|B|C|D): Gradient descent, such as the Adam optimizer | The normal equation solved in closed form | Principal component analysis of the inputs | K-means clustering of the training points
Correct Answer: Gradient descent, such as the Adam optimizer

Question Text: After running 100 iterations of gradient descent on a network, what is the expected outcome for the parameters?
Options (A|B|C|D): They collapse so that every parameter is zero | They drift back to their initial random values | They settle near values that minimize the cost | They grow to roughly double their starting size
Correct Answer: They settle near values that minimize the cost

Question Text: By the standard convention, the loss function measures the error on which of the following?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the hidden-layer outputs | The full training set at once | A single training example | The weights but not the biases
Correct Answer: A single training example

Question Text: How is the cost function J defined in terms of the per-example loss function?
Options (A|B|C|D): The largest loss over all examples | The average loss across all examples | The total sum of the input features | The derivative of the loss values
Correct Answer: The average loss across all examples

Question Text: In logistic regression, what is the purpose of the loss term -y*log(f) - (1-y)*log(1-f), where f is the predicted probability and y is the label?
Options (A|B|C|D): To set the learning rate alpha | To score the prediction on one example | To average the model's weights | To count the active neurons
Correct Answer: To score the prediction on one example

Question Text: Why is the cost function J written as a function of the parameters W and b?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the inputs x and y are what serve as the model parameters | Because J depends only on the training data and not on W and b | Because varying W and b shifts the predictions and thus the loss | Because the values of W and b are permanently held constant
Correct Answer: Because varying W and b shifts the predictions and thus the loss

Question Text: When building a neural network in TensorFlow with Keras, in which step do you specify the loss function?
Options (A|B|C|D): Inside each Dense layer | In the model.compile() call | It is chosen automatically | In the model.fit() call
Correct Answer: In the model.compile() call

Question Text: For classifying handwritten digits as either 0 or 1, which loss function is the standard choice?
Options (A|B|C|D): Mean squared error | Binary cross-entropy | Sparse categorical cross-entropy | Mean absolute (linear) loss
Correct Answer: Binary cross-entropy

Question Text: In the binary cross-entropy formula, what does the symbol 'y' denote?
Options (A|B|C|D): The network's predicted output | The chosen activation function | The ground-truth target label | The count of training examples
Correct Answer: The ground-truth target label

Question Text: In TensorFlow's Keras losses, what is the class name for binary cross-entropy?
Options (A|B|C|D): CrossEntropyBinary | LogisticLoss | BinaryCrossentropy | BCELoss
Correct Answer: BinaryCrossentropy

Question Text: How does the binary cross-entropy loss relate to logistic regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It applies strictly to multiclass softmax-only problems | It is a fundamentally different and unrelated formula | It is exactly the loss used to train logistic regression | It removes any need for gradient descent while training it
Correct Answer: It is exactly the loss used to train logistic regression

Question Text: What is the primary task of the backpropagation algorithm?
Options (A|B|C|D): To gather more labeled training examples for the model | To produce the model's very first set of forward predictions | To compute the cost's derivatives w.r.t. the parameters | To enlarge the network by adding several hidden units
Correct Answer: To compute the cost's derivatives w.r.t. the parameters

Question Text: Why is the algorithm called "backpropagation"?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it deletes hidden layers from the whole network | Because it was historically the first method ever invented | Because it always runs forward from Layer 0 up to Layer 3 | Because it computes derivatives from output back to input
Correct Answer: Because it computes derivatives from output back to input

Question Text: In backpropagation, what role does the computation graph serve?
Options (A|B|C|D): It plots out the trained model's final accuracy curve | It hides the network's parameters from the user in training | It randomly initializes all of the network's weights | It splits the calculation into nodes to derive gradients
Correct Answer: It splits the calculation into nodes to derive gradients

Question Text: Why is backpropagation more efficient than computing each derivative separately by hand for large networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): It avoids using any calculus or the chain rule at all | It skips the entire output layer to save computation | It reuses intermediate results to avoid redundant work | It reruns a billion full forward steps for each neuron
Correct Answer: It reuses intermediate results to avoid redundant work

Question Text: For a small network whose cost is J = (1/2)*d^2, where d is the final output quantity, what is the first step of backpropagation?
Options (A|B|C|D): Computing the derivative of J with respect to d | Resetting all the weights to zero | Predicting the output value a | Sweeping left to right through the graph
Correct Answer: Computing the derivative of J with respect to d

Question Text: What does the abbreviation ReLU stand for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Repeated Logarithmic Unit | Realized Linear Unit | Rectified Linear Unit | Recursive Linear Unit
Correct Answer: Rectified Linear Unit

Question Text: Which activation function is the most common default choice for hidden layers in modern neural networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sigmoid | Polynomial | ReLU | Linear
Correct Answer: ReLU

Question Text: Why do practitioners often prefer ReLU over sigmoid for hidden-layer activations?
Options (A|B|C|D): It tends to let the network train faster | It looks more elegant on a plot | It forces the whole network to stay linear | It outputs only negative values
Correct Answer: It tends to let the network train faster

Question Text: In TensorFlow, how do you set a ReLU activation on a Dense layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): activation="relu" | activation="rectified" | activation="max0" | activation="sigmoid"
Correct Answer: activation="relu"

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: For a binary classification problem, which activation function should the output layer use?
Options (A|B|C|D): ReLU, giving a non-negative score | Linear, giving any real-valued score | Softmax over the two raw class scores | Sigmoid, giving a probability in (0, 1)
Correct Answer: Sigmoid, giving a probability in (0, 1)

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: You are predicting tomorrow's stock price, which can be either positive or negative. Which output-layer activation is most appropriate?
Options (A|B|C|D): ReLU, clamping output to be non-negative | Softmax across price buckets | Linear, allowing any positive or negative value | Sigmoid, squashing output to (0, 1)
Correct Answer: Linear, allowing any positive or negative value

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: In a regression problem where the target y can only be non-negative (e.g., a house price), which output-layer activation is recommended?
Options (A|B|C|D): Linear, since it can output any real value | Tanh, since it restricts output to (-1, 1) | ReLU, since it can output only values >= 0 | Sigmoid, since it restricts output to (0, 1)
Correct Answer: ReLU, since it can output only values >= 0

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: What is the effect of using a linear activation g(z) = z in the output layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): The learning algorithm is guaranteed to diverge | The network is converted into a classifier | The output is forced into the range [0, 1] | The output can take any positive or negative value
Correct Answer: The output can take any positive or negative value

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: Why is Sigmoid the natural choice for the output unit in binary classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): It computes faster than ReLU at the output | Its output is a probability that y equals 1 | It works only with labels of 0 and 9 | It speeds up prediction at inference time
Correct Answer: Its output is a probability that y equals 1

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: What is a significant drawback of using linear activation functions in the hidden layers?
Options (A|B|C|D): The whole network reduces to plain linear regression | It blocks gradient descent from being applied | It makes the network far too complex to train | It restricts the network to 8x8 image inputs
Correct Answer: The whole network reduces to plain linear regression

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: What is the common rule of thumb for choosing activation functions in hidden layers?
Options (A|B|C|D): Use a linear activation for every hidden unit | Use no activation function in hidden layers | Use a sigmoid activation for every hidden unit | Use ReLU as the default for hidden layers
Correct Answer: Use ReLU as the default for hidden layers

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: If every neuron in a large neural network used a linear activation, what would the network be unable to fit?
Options (A|B|C|D): Simple averages of the input features | Anything more complex than linear regression | Straight lines through the input data | Plain numerical (non-image) data
Correct Answer: Anything more complex than linear regression

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: Compared with Sigmoid, why does ReLU tend to help hidden layers learn faster?
Options (A|B|C|D): It only functions when the inputs are stored as dense tensors | It is cheap to compute and stays non-flat for large positive z | It maps every positive input value straight down to exactly zero | It forces all of the hidden-unit weights to remain strictly negative
Correct Answer: It is cheap to compute and stays non-flat for large positive z

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: Stacking hidden layers that all use linear activations defeats what?
Options (A|B|C|D): The requirement to collect training data | The ability to run the Adam optimizer | The ability to accelerate training on GPUs | The whole purpose of using a neural network
Correct Answer: The whole purpose of using a neural network

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: How does a multiclass classification problem differ from binary classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is solved with unsupervised learning | It always requires fewer network layers | It uses no target labels y at all | It predicts one of three or more categories
Correct Answer: It predicts one of three or more categories

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: What is Softmax regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): A scheme for the lossy compression of large image files | A statistical method for estimating a Gaussian's mean value | A reinforcement-learning method for optimal policy control | A generalization of logistic regression to multiple classes
Correct Answer: A generalization of logistic regression to multiple classes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: In a 10-class handwritten-digit recognition task, what does a Softmax output layer produce?
Options (A|B|C|D): A list of raw pixel intensity values | Ten probabilities that sum to 1 | A 2D matrix filled with zeros | A single integer label from 0 to 9
Correct Answer: Ten probabilities that sum to 1

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: In TensorFlow, which loss function is recommended for multiclass classification with integer class labels?
Options (A|B|C|D): BinaryCrossentropy (two-class loss) | MeanSquaredError (regression loss) | SparseCategoricalCrossentropy loss | KLDivergence (distribution loss)
Correct Answer: SparseCategoricalCrossentropy loss

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: How does multi-label classification differ from multi-class classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is restricted to ordinary single-variable linear regression tasks | It relies on exactly one sigmoid output neuron for the whole network | It can attach several labels to one input (e.g., car and pedestrian) | It is really just a disguised form of unsupervised cluster discovery
Correct Answer: It can attach several labels to one input (e.g., car and pedestrian)

Question Text: In terms of the dataset provided, what is the fundamental difference between supervised classification and clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Supervised classification needs large data, while clustering needs small data | Supervised classification gives a target label y per input, while clustering gives only inputs x | Supervised classification uses only inputs x, while clustering uses both x and labels y | There is no real difference, since both just find decision boundaries
Correct Answer: Supervised classification gives a target label y per input, while clustering gives only inputs x

Question Text: Looking at a scatter plot of the data, how can you tell a supervised classification task from an unsupervised clustering task?
Options (A|B|C|D): Supervised points are marked by class (e.g., crosses vs. circles), while clustering points are unlabeled dots | Supervised points are scattered randomly across the plot, while clustering points form tight structured groups | Supervised data always forms smoothly curving nonlinear bands, while clustering data always falls on a line | Supervised data is always plotted in full 3D space, while clustering data is shown flat in only two dimensions
Correct Answer: Supervised points are marked by class (e.g., crosses vs. circles), while clustering points are unlabeled dots

Question Text: Which algorithm learns a decision boundary between known, labeled classes?
Options (A|B|C|D): Principal Component Analysis | Logistic regression | The normal equation | The K-means clustering algorithm
Correct Answer: Logistic regression

Question Text: Why is clustering classified as an unsupervised learning algorithm?
Options (A|B|C|D): It applies a sigmoid function to output class probabilities | It finds structure in data without being given labeled right answers | It requires the user to supply the target variable y | It must be guided step by step by a human analyst
Correct Answer: It finds structure in data without being given labeled right answers

Question Text: In the Google News example, why is grouping related stories treated as clustering rather than supervised classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the story groupings are actually produced by a previously fitted linear regression model | Because topics shift daily and staff cannot keep hand-labeling which stories belong together | Because every incoming news story already arrives carefully pre-labeled by staff journalists | Because the system can process only attached photographs and cannot read any of the article text
Correct Answer: Because topics shift daily and staff cannot keep hand-labeling which stories belong together

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: What is the primary objective of clustering, an unsupervised technique applied to unlabeled data?
Options (A|B|C|D): To minimize the prediction bias of a linear model | To uncover groups or patterns not specified or labeled in advance | To fit weights and biases that match given output labels | To predict a continuous target value from input features
Correct Answer: To uncover groups or patterns not specified or labeled in advance

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: Applied to DNA microarray data, what does clustering let researchers discover?
Options (A|B|C|D): Distinct types of individuals grouped by genetic activity, with no labels supplied | A regression estimate predicting the eventual dollar cost of a medical procedure | The single shortest connecting path running between two given specified DNA segments | The single most frequently expressed gene found across an entire human population
Correct Answer: Distinct types of individuals grouped by genetic activity, with no labels supplied

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: How does market segmentation illustrate clustering applied to a customer database?
Options (A|B|C|D): It manually tags each customer as Type 1 or Type 2 by their age | It automatically finds distinct groups by motivation and behavior | It predicts the exact product a customer buys via linear regression | It compresses the customer count to make plotting easier
Correct Answer: It automatically finds distinct groups by motivation and behavior

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: Which statement best describes what makes a clustering algorithm "unsupervised"?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is supplied the correct output label for each input example | It must figure out on its own what structure exists in the data | It relies on a cross-validation set to reduce model variance | It is restricted to discovering only linear decision boundaries
Correct Answer: It must figure out on its own what structure exists in the data

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: Why might an astronomer apply clustering to data about bodies in space?
Options (A|B|C|D): To compute the distance to Mars using a GPS positioning method | To predict a star's surface temperature with supervised learning | To attach names to stars that are already individually identified | To see whether bodies group into coherent structures like galaxies
Correct Answer: To see whether bodies group into coherent structures like galaxies

Question Text: What two steps does the K-means clustering algorithm repeat on each iteration?
Options (A|B|C|D): Running a full forward propagation pass, then performing complete backpropagation of the output errors | Assigning each point to its nearest centroid, then moving each centroid to the mean of its points | Computing the best-fit regression line's slope, then algebraically solving for its y-intercept | Applying min-max feature scaling to all the inputs, then performing per-feature mean normalization
Correct Answer: Assigning each point to its nearest centroid, then moving each centroid to the mean of its points

Question Text: What must happen before K-means can start its iterative assign-and-update loop?
Options (A|B|C|D): The cost function's global minimum must be computed first | Every training example must first be labeled by hand | The K cluster centroids must be randomly initialized | The data must be mapped into an infinite-dimensional space
Correct Answer: The K cluster centroids must be randomly initialized

Question Text: What happens during the "move centroid" step of K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): The whole dataset is recentered to have zero mean | Each centroid is reset to the mean of the points currently assigned to it | The number of clusters K is incremented by one | Each data point is shifted to coincide with its nearest neighbor
Correct Answer: Each centroid is reset to the mean of the points currently assigned to it

Question Text: Why might a t-shirt manufacturer run K-means on customers' height and weight data?
Options (A|B|C|D): To group customers into sizes such as small, medium, and large | To predict each customer's gender from their measurements | To total up the fabric cost required for every shirt | To flag which incoming t-shirt orders are fraudulent
Correct Answer: To group customers into sizes such as small, medium, and large

Question Text: What does K-means take as input with respect to labels?
Options (A|B|C|D): A set of training examples that carry no labels | A vector y holding the ground-truth label of each example | A continuous target variable such as a price for each example | A binary classification label, either 0 or 1, per example
Correct Answer: A set of training examples that carry no labels

Question Text: In K-means, what does the parameter μ_k denote?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total number of training examples | The position of the k-th cluster centroid | The straight-line distance between two data points | The step size used to update the model
Correct Answer: The position of the k-th cluster centroid

Question Text: What is the recommended way to choose the initial positions of the K cluster centroids?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sort the data and take the first K points in order | Set all K centroids at the origin point (0, 0) | Pick K training examples at random and use their locations | Place the centroids as far from the data as possible
Correct Answer: Pick K training examples at random and use their locations

Question Text: Which cluster centroid does K-means assign a training example x^(i) to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The centroid located closest to that example | The centroid that currently carries the highest weight | A centroid selected at random by the algorithm | The centroid located farthest from that example
Correct Answer: The centroid located closest to that example

Question Text: If the first training example x^(1) is assigned to cluster centroid 1, what value does the assignment variable c^(1) take?
How does a cluster centroid's location change during the move step of K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): It stays fixed in place until the algorithm converges | It jumps to the single point that is farthest from it | It moves to the average location of its assigned points | It drifts steadily toward the origin each iteration
Correct Answer: It moves to the average location of its assigned points

Question Text: In the assignment step of K-means, what does the algorithm do for each training example x^(i)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It relocates the nearest centroid onto x^(i) | It places x^(i) into a randomly chosen cluster | It assigns x^(i) to its closest cluster centroid | It computes the mean of every point in the dataset
Correct Answer: It assigns x^(i) to its closest cluster centroid

Question Text: When assigning a point x^(i) to a cluster, which quantity does K-means minimize?
Options (A|B|C|D): The square of the gradient-descent learning step size | The squared distance from x^(i) to its assigned centroid | The summed total of all feature values belonging to x^(i) | The variance of every feature computed over the whole dataset
Correct Answer: The squared distance from x^(i) to its assigned centroid

Question Text: Which variable records the index of the cluster that a given training example is assigned to?
Options (A|B|C|D): K | μ_k | x^(i) | c^(i)
Correct Answer: c^(i)

Question Text: During the assignment step of K-means, are the cluster centroids μ_1 through μ_K updated?
Options (A|B|C|D): No, they hold still while only the point assignments change | Yes, every centroid is randomly reset before assigning points | No, each centroid is removed and then later replaced entirely | Yes, each one shifts toward the points lying nearest to it
Correct Answer: No, they hold still while only the point assignments change

Question Text: What happens when a point x^(i) is exactly equidistant from two cluster centroids?
Options (A|B|C|D): The algorithm halts at once with an unrecoverable runtime error | It is permanently counted as a member of both clusters all at once | It may go to either one, by convention the lower-indexed centroid | Both of the tied centroids are immediately moved onto that point
Correct Answer: It may go to either one, by convention the lower-indexed centroid

Question Text: In the K-means update step, how is the new position of each cluster centroid computed?
Options (A|B|C|D): The mean of the coordinates of every example in the entire training set | The coordinates of the single training point lying nearest to the old centroid | The mean of the coordinates of the points currently assigned to that centroid | The old centroid position shifted by a fixed step toward the coordinate origin
Correct Answer: The mean of the coordinates of the points currently assigned to that centroid

Question Text: A cluster has exactly two assigned points, (1, 2) and (3, 4). After the update step, where does its centroid move?
During the centroid update step of K-means, which quantity is held fixed while the centroids are recomputed?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of training examples m | The cluster assignment c^(i) of each point | The gradient-descent learning rate alpha | The positions of the cluster centroids
Correct Answer: The cluster assignment c^(i) of each point

Question Text: In the K-means update step, what is the usual handling when a centroid has no points assigned to it?
Options (A|B|C|D): The distortion cost immediately jumps to infinity | The algorithm halts and reports convergence | The centroid is left in place or simply removed | A nearby point is automatically reassigned to it
Correct Answer: The centroid is left in place or simply removed

Question Text: How does the centroid update step affect the K-means distortion (cost function) J?
Options (A|B|C|D): It decreases J or leaves it unchanged | It resets J exactly to zero each time | It always increases J by a fixed amount | It has no measurable effect on J at all
Correct Answer: It decreases J or leaves it unchanged

Question Text: Which condition signals that the K-means algorithm has converged?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every training example ends up inside a single cluster | The gradient-descent learning rate has decayed to zero | The iteration count becomes equal to the number of features | The cost function stops dropping and assignments hold steady
Correct Answer: The cost function stops dropping and assignments hold steady

Question Text: A full K-means iteration occurs in which no point changes its cluster and no centroid moves. What does this indicate?
Options (A|B|C|D): Successful convergence of the algorithm | A clear need for additional clusters | High variance in the fitted model | A logic bug somewhere in the code
Correct Answer: Successful convergence of the algorithm

Question Text: While running K-means, the distortion is still decreasing but only by a tiny amount each iteration. What is the standard response?
Options (A|B|C|D): Restart the whole algorithm from scratch | Treat it as effectively converged and stop | Manually nudge the centroids to new spots | Increase the number of clusters K and rerun
Correct Answer: Treat it as effectively converged and stop

Question Text: Run from different starting points, does K-means always reach the same final clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Yes, because the cost function has a single global minimum | No, the result depends on the random initialization of the centroids | Yes, the procedure is fully deterministic regardless of setup | No, because the dataset itself is resampled each iteration
Correct Answer: No, the result depends on the random initialization of the centroids

Question Text: Why is it useful to track the cost function J across iterations of K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): To confirm the algorithm is actually reducing the distortion | To decide how large the output clusters should be | To compute the mean value of each input feature | To select the gradient-descent learning rate for the run
Correct Answer: To confirm the algorithm is actually reducing the distortion

Question Text: What does the K-means cost function (distortion) J measure for a given clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): The average squared distance from each point to its assigned centroid | The total count of the distinct clusters used in the current solution | The probability that a given training example is a flagged outlier anomaly | The numerical gap between the dataset's feature mean and feature variance
Correct Answer: The average squared distance from each point to its assigned centroid

Question Text: Which expression is the K-means distortion cost J, where mu_c^(i) is the centroid assigned to example x^(i)?
Options (A|B|C|D): -y*log(f) - (1-y)*log(1-f) | (1/m) * sum over i of ||x^(i) - mu_c^(i)|| | w . x + b | (1/m) * sum over i of ||x^(i) - mu_c^(i)||^2
Correct Answer: (1/m) * sum over i of ||x^(i) - mu_c^(i)||^2

Question Text: What is the objective of the K-means algorithm with respect to the distortion cost J?
Options (A|B|C|D): Choose assignments c^(i) and centroids mu_k that minimize J | Hold J pinned at some fixed constant and nonzero value | Make J turn out exactly equal to the overall feature variance | Drive the value of J upward to its largest attainable level
Correct Answer: Choose assignments c^(i) and centroids mu_k that minimize J

Question Text: A K-means solution has a very high distortion cost J. What does this generally indicate?
Options (A|B|C|D): Points are tightly packed around their assigned centroids | The algorithm has settled at the true global minimum | Centroids are placed poorly and points sit far from their centers | The clustering is using far too many separate clusters
Correct Answer: Centroids are placed poorly and points sit far from their centers

Question Text: Why compare the final distortion J across several K-means runs that used different random initializations?
Options (A|B|C|D): To count how many input features the dataset contains | To select the run that produced the lowest-distortion clustering | To estimate how old the training examples happen to be | To find out which run was given the largest learning rate
Correct Answer: To select the run that produced the lowest-distortion clustering

Question Text: Why does the assignment step of K-means guarantee that the cost function J cannot increase?
Options (A|B|C|D): It runs gradient descent with a sufficiently small step size alpha | Each point is sent to its closest centroid, minimizing its distance term | It attaches a class label to each example in the dataset | It moves every centroid to the mean of its assigned points
Correct Answer: Each point is sent to its closest centroid, minimizing its distance term

Question Text: How does the move-centroid step of K-means help reduce the cost function J?
Options (A|B|C|D): It randomly relocates every one of the centroids in order to explore more of the surrounding feature space | It deliberately pushes all of the centroids far apart so as to maximize the total spacing between them | With assignments fixed, it sets each centroid to the mean, minimizing that cluster's squared distances | It rescales every single one of the input features so that each separate one ends up with exactly zero mean
Correct Answer: With assignments fixed, it sets each centroid to the mean, minimizing that cluster's squared distances

Question Text: Considering its two steps, why does K-means eventually stop improving?
Options (A|B|C|D): The cluster count K keeps growing larger until the iterations run out | It gradually exhausts the working memory the machine has available | It reaches the very last training example and then simply halts there | It minimizes a cost bounded below by zero, settling into a minimum
Correct Answer: It minimizes a cost bounded below by zero, settling into a minimum

Question Text: Is K-means guaranteed to reach the global minimum of its cost function?
Options (A|B|C|D): No, it can become trapped in a local minimum | No, because the procedure tends to diverge | Yes, it reaches the global optimum every run | Yes, but only when the data carries labels
Correct Answer: No, it can become trapped in a local minimum

Question Text: By alternating between assigning points from centroids and updating centroids from assignments, what does K-means accomplish?
Options (A|B|C|D): It steadily lowers the total distortion of the dataset | It raises the number of features in the dataset | It turns the procedure into a supervised method | It enters a cycle that repeats endlessly forever
Correct Answer: It steadily lowers the total distortion of the dataset

Question Text: Why can K-means produce different final clusters depending on which random initial centroids are chosen?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because each run trains on a randomly resampled subset of the data | Because it can converge to different local minima from different starts | Because the chosen value of K silently changes between runs | Because feature scaling is recomputed differently on every run
Correct Answer: Because it can converge to different local minima from different starts

Question Text: What can happen if a single random initialization of K-means is "unlucky"?
Options (A|B|C|D): The algorithm loops forever and never actually reaches convergence | It settles on a high-distortion clustering versus a better start | The distortion cost function takes on a negative final value | Every data point is silently dropped from the dataset entirely
Correct Answer: It settles on a high-distortion clustering versus a better start

Question Text: With three true clusters in the data, what poor outcome can a bad set of starting centroids lead to in K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): The algorithm lands on the global minimum on the first pass | Each data point is automatically given a ground-truth label | One centroid claims two clusters while another gets no points | All three centroids collapse onto a single identical location
Correct Answer: One centroid claims two clusters while another gets no points

Question Text: What is the recommended way to choose the initial cluster centroids for K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): Set every centroid equal to the overall mean of the dataset | Choose K random examples from the training set as the centroids | Pick K arbitrary points anywhere in the feature space | Use the coordinates of the first K rows stored in the database
Correct Answer: Choose K random examples from the training set as the centroids

Question Text: What is the main purpose of running K-means several times with different random initializations?
Options (A|B|C|D): To grow the number of examples in the training set | To find the clustering that achieves the lowest distortion cost | To gradually rotate the final cluster centroids over time | To consume more CPU cycles during the training job
Correct Answer: To find the clustering that achieves the lowest distortion cost

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Why is running K-means multiple times with different random initializations a standard practice?
Options (A|B|C|D): To reduce the risk of a poor local minimum and lower distortion | To deliberately burn extra CPU time without any benefit | To force the algorithm to use a much larger learning rate | To raise the total count of training examples being used
Correct Answer: To reduce the risk of a poor local minimum and lower distortion

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: When performing 100 random restarts of K-means, how should the initial centroids be set for each run?
Options (A|B|C|D): Choose the K points that lie farthest from all the data | Pick K random training examples as that run's centroids | Initialize every centroid at the fixed origin (0, 0) | Place all centroids at the single center of the dataset
Correct Answer: Pick K random training examples as that run's centroids

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: After running K-means with 100 different random initializations, which clustering should you keep?
Options (A|B|C|D): The run that produced the largest number of clusters | The clustering from whichever run finished last | The run whose centroids moved the greatest distance | The clustering that achieved the lowest distortion cost
Correct Answer: The clustering that achieved the lowest distortion cost

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: What is a likely consequence of one "unlucky" random initialization when running K-means just once?
Options (A|B|C|D): It converges to a local minimum that groups the data poorly | The training dataset is permanently erased from memory | The reported distortion automatically drops to exactly zero | The algorithm fails to ever converge to a solution
Correct Answer: It converges to a local minimum that groups the data poorly

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: With three groups present in the data, how can a bad initial placement of centroids affect a K-means run?
Options (A|B|C|D): One centroid grabs two groups while another captures none | The distortion cost function turns into a negative number | The centroids endlessly orbit the data in fixed circles | All three centroids converge onto one shared location
Correct Answer: One centroid grabs two groups while another captures none

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Number of Clusters K
Question Text: Is there always one single "correct" number of clusters K for a given dataset?
Options (A|B|C|D): Yes, the math always pins down one exact value of K | Yes, but only when the Elbow Method is applied first | No, it is often ambiguous and depends on the application | No, K must always equal the number of input features
Correct Answer: No, it is often ambiguous and depends on the application

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Number of Clusters K
Question Text: What situation makes choosing the number of clusters K genuinely ambiguous?
Options (A|B|C|D): The numeric values of the features are very large | The processor running the algorithm is unusually slow | The data shows no clear sign of how many groups exist | The learning rate chosen for the run is too small
Correct Answer: The data shows no clear sign of how many groups exist

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing K Based on Downstream Use
Question Text: What does it mean to choose the number of clusters K based on "downstream use"?
Options (A|B|C|D): Choosing K by how the clusters serve a specific application | Always selecting the largest value of K that is feasible | Setting K to match whatever a competitor happens to use | Picking K from the distortion cost value and nothing else
Correct Answer: Choosing K by how the clusters serve a specific application

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing K Based on Downstream Use
Question Text: A t-shirt company clusters customer measurements to define sizes. How should it decide between 3 and 5 size clusters?
Options (A|B|C|D): By flipping a fair coin to choose one of the two candidate options | By applying the standard Elbow Method to the distortion curve alone | By weighing better-fitting garments against the cost of more sizes | By simply setting K equal to the median customer height in the data
Correct Answer: By weighing better-fitting garments against the cost of more sizes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing K Based on Downstream Use
Question Text: When the goal is a downstream application, how should you evaluate different candidate values of K?
Options (A|B|C|D): Ignore distortion entirely and judge by the labels | Run K-means for each K and pick the one best for the goal | Average together the outputs of the K=3 and K=5 runs | Only ever test the smallest candidate value of K
Correct Answer: Run K-means for each K and pick the one best for the goal

Question Text: When plotting distortion against K, what shape signals that the Elbow Method will not give a clear choice of K?
Options (A|B|C|D): A perfectly straight vertical line rising on the far left of the plot | A distortion value that stays completely flat at zero across every K | A single very pronounced and sharp bend occurring right at K equals 3 | A smooth curve where distortion falls gradually without a sharp elbow
Correct Answer: A smooth curve where distortion falls gradually without a sharp elbow

Question Text: On an Elbow Method graph, what quantity is plotted along the horizontal axis?
Options (A|B|C|D): The distortion cost function J | The number of training examples m | The number of clusters K | The learning rate alpha
Correct Answer: The number of clusters K

Question Text: What is a common reason practitioners often avoid relying on the Elbow Method to choose K?
Options (A|B|C|D): It requires the dataset to already be labeled | It is far too computationally expensive to run | It often yields a smooth curve with no clear optimal K | It can only be applied to two-dimensional data
Correct Answer: It often yields a smooth curve with no clear optimal K

Question Text: On an Elbow Method plot, which feature of the curve identifies the K you should choose?
Options (A|B|C|D): The value of K at which the cost J first reaches zero | The K closest to the average value of the cost J | The K corresponding to the highest point on the curve | The K past which the cost J drops much more slowly
Correct Answer: The K past which the cost J drops much more slowly

Question Text: What is often a more dependable alternative to the Elbow Method for selecting K?
Options (A|B|C|D): Running K-means only a single time | Choosing K from the goals of the downstream application | Switching to a supervised learning algorithm instead | Selecting K with a random number generator
Correct Answer: Choosing K from the goals of the downstream application

Question Text: In the density-estimation approach to anomaly detection, what does the model fundamentally learn?
Options (A|B|C|D): A decision boundary separating pre-labeled good and bad examples | A probability model p(x) marking high- vs low-likelihood regions | A count of how many examples fall in each category | The slope of a line fit through the data points
Correct Answer: A probability model p(x) marking high- vs low-likelihood regions

Question Text: Using a trained model p(x), how is a faulty aircraft engine identified from its measurements x_test?
Options (A|B|C|D): By matching it against engines built at a different factory | By tracking the engine's physical location over time | By checking whether p(x_test) is below a small threshold epsilon | By confirming the engine produces measurable heat output
Correct Answer: By checking whether p(x_test) is below a small threshold epsilon

Question Text: What kind of data is primarily used to train the model p(x) for anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Examples of typical, mostly normal behavior | Examples drawn solely from already-shipped units | Randomly generated synthetic numbers | Only the anomalous or defective examples
Correct Answer: Examples of typical, mostly normal behavior

Question Text: What does a low value of p(x) indicate about a new test example?
Options (A|B|C|D): It sits exactly at the distribution's peak | It closely resembles the training examples | It is unusual relative to the normal data seen before | It is most likely a normal, expected example
Correct Answer: It is unusual relative to the normal data seen before

Question Text: Why might a data center apply density estimation to its computers?
Options (A|B|C|D): To increase the network's bandwidth and speed | To group users according to their stated preferences | To flag machines behaving very differently from the norm | To estimate the combined physical weight of the servers
Correct Answer: To flag machines behaving very differently from the norm

Question Text: What is the common visual name for the shape of a Gaussian distribution?
Options (A|B|C|D): The sharp triangular peak | The straight linear line | The bell-shaped curve | The flat square distribution
Correct Answer: The bell-shaped curve

Question Text: As the variance (sigma squared) of a Gaussian increases, how does its curve change?
Options (A|B|C|D): It becomes wider and shorter | It shifts rightward along the axis | It stays exactly the same shape | It grows skinnier and taller
Correct Answer: It becomes wider and shorter

Question Text: By the rules of probability, what is the total area under any Gaussian curve?
Options (A|B|C|D): One hundred | 0.5 | Zero | One
Correct Answer: One

Question Text: How does changing the mean parameter mu affect the plot of a Gaussian?
Options (A|B|C|D): It shifts the distribution's center left or right | It adjusts only the peak height of the curve | It widens or narrows the spread of the curve | It flips the curve upside down vertically
Correct Answer: It shifts the distribution's center left or right

Question Text: Why must a skinnier (smaller-variance) Gaussian be taller than a wider one?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the total area under it must stay equal to one | Because it is merely a fixed plotting convention | Because a taller curve simply looks more bell-like | Because the mean must exceed the variance numerically
Correct Answer: Because the total area under it must stay equal to one

Question Text: What is the purpose of the threshold epsilon in anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): To compute the mean value of the training data | To set the cutoff below which p(x) is flagged anomalous | To set the step size used by gradient descent | To fix the number of layers in a neural network
Correct Answer: To set the cutoff below which p(x) is flagged anomalous

Question Text: Using the threshold epsilon, when is a test example declared an anomaly?
Options (A|B|C|D): When p(x) is exactly zero | When p(x) is less than epsilon | When p(x) equals epsilon exactly | When p(x) is greater than epsilon
Correct Answer: When p(x) is less than epsilon

Question Text: In aircraft manufacturing, when is a newly built engine flagged for inspection?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the threshold epsilon turns out to exceed one | When its probability p(x) falls below epsilon | When it rolls off the assembly line as expected | When its measurements exactly match the training mean
Correct Answer: When its probability p(x) falls below epsilon

Question Text: What is a recognized practical difficulty when applying the threshold epsilon?
Options (A|B|C|D): Choosing an appropriate value of epsilon is quite hard | Epsilon can only be used with one-dimensional inputs | Epsilon is always forced to be larger than one | Epsilon must replace the model's cost function
Correct Answer: Choosing an appropriate value of epsilon is quite hard

Question Text: In system monitoring, what happens when a computer behaves in a way never observed before?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is automatically assigned the normal class label of y equals 0 | It is flagged as anomalous because p(x) likely drops below epsilon | It is silently ignored by the detector and is simply never reported | It instead receives an unusually high p(x) value above the threshold
Correct Answer: It is flagged as anomalous because p(x) likely drops below epsilon

Question Text: What is the core idea behind collaborative filtering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Removing every item that has received low ratings | Recommending items based solely on their listed price | Hand-labeling each individual user's preferences in advance | Using many users' ratings of many items to find patterns
Correct Answer: Using many users' ratings of many items to find patterns

Question Text: In a movie recommender, what does the label y(i, j) represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total running time of movie i | The calendar year movie i was released | The display name of user j | The rating that user j gave to movie i
Correct Answer: The rating that user j gave to movie i

Question Text: Why is the economic value of recommender systems considered very large for many companies?
Options (A|B|C|D): They eliminate any need to maintain a website | They drive a large share of sales via relevant suggestions | They collect trillions of dollars in fees from users | They directly lower the retail price of each item
Correct Answer: They drive a large share of sales via relevant suggestions

Question Text: When a user has not yet rated a particular movie, what is the recommender's goal?
Options (A|B|C|D): To predict how that user would likely rate the movie | To remove that user's record from the dataset | To assign the movie a uniformly random rating | To require the user to rate the movie at once
Correct Answer: To predict how that user would likely rate the movie

Question Text: Which statement is true about binary labels in recommender systems?
Options (A|B|C|D): They capture engagement such as a click, like, or full view | They apply only to five-star rating scales and nothing else | They encode the calendar year in which an item was produced | They can take on infinitely many distinct continuous values
Correct Answer: They capture engagement such as a click, like, or full view

Question Text: What is the role of the input layer in a feedforward neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is only active during backpropagation | It outputs the final predicted class label | It caches the per-epoch training loss values | It receives the raw feature values x of an example
Correct Answer: It receives the raw feature values x of an example

Question Text: What do the hidden layers of a neural network primarily do?
Options (A|B|C|D): They transform earlier activations into more useful representations | They completely remove the need for any output layer in the network | They forward the raw input labels straight to the output unchanged | They store a permanent verbatim copy of the entire full training set
Correct Answer: They transform earlier activations into more useful representations

Question Text: What is the purpose of the output layer in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): To collect every model parameter in one tensor | To automatically normalize the input features | To produce the network's final prediction | To initialize the weights of the earlier layers
Correct Answer: To produce the network's final prediction

Question Text: Why is the input layer sometimes called Layer 0 rather than Layer 1?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is fully hidden from the end user of the model | It plays no role at all during inference | It is the starting point counted before the hidden layers | It contains exactly zero computational units
Correct Answer: It is the starting point counted before the hidden layers

Question Text: Which statement about hidden layers is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Different hidden layers may have different numbers of units | They are optional decorations with no computational role | They sit between the input layer and the output layer | They compute intermediate activations that aid expressiveness
Correct Answer: They are optional decorations with no computational role

Question Text: What is an activation in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): The particular learning rate that gets assigned to one given layer | A stored internal copy of the training example's true target label | A neuron's output value after its activation function is applied | The total count of the individual units contained within a layer
Correct Answer: A neuron's output value after its activation function is applied

Question Text: Why are nonlinear activation functions important in neural networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): They remove the need for any learnable parameters | They let the network model patterns a plain linear map cannot | They force all of the network's outputs to be binary | They keep every layer linear to simplify the math
Correct Answer: They let the network model patterns a plain linear map cannot

Question Text: Which activation function squashes its input to an output strictly between 0 and 1?
Options (A|B|C|D): Linear (identity) | ReLU | Dot product | Sigmoid
Correct Answer: Sigmoid

Question Text: Which activation function is defined by the rule max(0, z)?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sigmoid | ReLU | Soft threshold | Linear (identity)
Correct Answer: ReLU

Question Text: What happens if every neuron in a deep network uses a linear activation function?
Options (A|B|C|D): The network automatically turns itself into a binary classifier | The whole network collapses to an equivalent single linear model | The hidden layers quietly stop multiplying inputs by their weights | The network gains strictly more expressive representational power
Correct Answer: The whole network collapses to an equivalent single linear model

Question Text: What does the term neural network architecture refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The order in which training examples are seen | Only the optimizer chosen to train the network | The color scheme used when plotting the network | The arrangement of layers and units in the network
Correct Answer: The arrangement of layers and units in the network

Question Text: When a network is described as having four layers, which layers are usually counted?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the single input layer of the network | Every individual matrix multiplication performed | The hidden layers plus the output layer, not the input | Only the hidden layers, excluding the output
Correct Answer: The hidden layers plus the output layer, not the input

Question Text: Which is a common architecture pattern in feedforward networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): Hidden units often decrease toward the output layer | All hidden layers are required to be the same size | The output layer always has the most units | Every effective network has exactly one hidden layer
Correct Answer: Hidden units often decrease toward the output layer

Question Text: Why does the choice of network architecture matter?
Options (A|B|C|D): It becomes irrelevant once the weights are initialized | It only affects how the network diagram is drawn | It governs how information is transformed through the network | It removes the need to use activation functions
Correct Answer: It governs how information is transformed through the network

Question Text: Which statement about network architecture is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Architecture is merely cosmetic and unrelated to computation | Architecture choices directly affect the model's modeling power | Architecture specifies the structure of the computations performed | Hidden layers and the output layer may differ in size
Correct Answer: Architecture is merely cosmetic and unrelated to computation

Question Text: What is forward propagation in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): The updating of the weights using computed gradients | The evaluation of only the regularization penalty term | The random shuffling of the training set each epoch | The input-to-output computation of each layer's activations
Correct Answer: The input-to-output computation of each layer's activations

Question Text: Why is forward propagation given that name?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always increases the value of the network's output | It is used solely to predict future time steps | Activations are passed from earlier layers to later ones | It can only run after backpropagation has finished
Correct Answer: Activations are passed from earlier layers to later ones

Question Text: In a three-hidden-layer digit classifier, what computation order does forward propagation follow?
Options (A|B|C|D): y to x to loss | a3 to a2 to a1 to x | x to a1 to a2 to a3 | w to b to x
Correct Answer: x to a1 to a2 to a3

Question Text: What does f(x) denote in the neural-network setting?
Options (A|B|C|D): The activation function applied at a neuron | The training loss on a single example | The count of hidden units in the network | The output the network computes as a function of x
Correct Answer: The output the network computes as a function of x

Question Text: Which statement about forward propagation is a confusion that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is the same as the algorithm that adjusts the weights | It proceeds from the input layer to the output layer | It computes the layer activations one layer at a time | It is the inference pass using the current parameters
Correct Answer: It is the same as the algorithm that adjusts the weights

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: How is a single neuron in a hidden layer of a neural network best described computationally?
Options (A|B|C|D): A unit computing g(w*x + b), much like logistic regression | A standalone cost function that the network needs to minimize | A verbatim, unchanged copy of its own input feature vector | A small unsupervised clustering step applied to the inputs
Correct Answer: A unit computing g(w*x + b), much like logistic regression

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: What is the key intuition behind calling a hidden neuron a "logistic unit"?
Options (A|B|C|D): It carries no learnable parameters of any kind during the whole training | It emits an unbounded, growing number of separate independent outputs | It completely eliminates the need for any additive bias term whatsoever | It applies a weighted sum then an activation, like logistic regression
Correct Answer: It applies a weighted sum then an activation, like logistic regression

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: Why is viewing each hidden neuron as a logistic-regression-like unit a useful perspective?
Options (A|B|C|D): It shows layers are built from simple repeated units | It guarantees every hidden unit is human-interpretable | It demonstrates that backpropagation is optional | It implies hidden layers can be removed entirely
Correct Answer: It shows layers are built from simple repeated units

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Network Layers as Logistic Units
Question Text: When several neurons in one layer each compute their own output, what does the layer produce as a whole?
Options (A|B|C|D): A confusion matrix of the predictions | A vector of activations passed to the next layer | A full cost surface over the parameters | A batch of training labels for the data
Correct Answer: A vector of activations passed to the next layer

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: In a simple introductory neural-network classification example, what is the network trained to recognize?
Options (A|B|C|D): Whether a listed house is expensive or cheap | Whether an input image is the digit 0 or 1 | Whether an email article is spam or not | Whether a cup of coffee tastes good
Correct Answer: Whether an input image is the digit 0 or 1

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: In a handwritten-digit recognition network, what serves as the input x?
Options (A|B|C|D): A short string of text describing the digit | A single scalar temperature measurement | A grid of pixel intensity values from the image | Only the final predicted class probability
Correct Answer: A grid of pixel intensity values from the image

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: Why is handwritten-digit recognition a useful illustrative example for neural networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is a pure regression task with smooth continuous numeric outputs | It needs absolutely no activation functions at any layer involved | It can be trained quite successfully without any labeled examples | It maps raw pixel data to a digit class through layered computation
Correct Answer: It maps raw pixel data to a digit class through layered computation

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: In a binary handwritten-digit classifier, what is the network's final output meant to represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of hidden layers in the network | The mean brightness across the image | The total count of pixels in the image | The confidence or probability of the target class
Correct Answer: The confidence or probability of the target class

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: Which belief about a handwritten-digit classifier is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That its final numeric output can be thresholded to decide a class label | That it memorizes one fixed image rather than a pixel-to-label mapping | That its raw grayscale input image is represented internally as numbers | That its hidden layers transform the input over successive stages
Correct Answer: That it memorizes one fixed image rather than a pixel-to-label mapping

Question Text: What does a Dense layer represent in TensorFlow?
Options (A|B|C|D): The standard fully connected layer type | A loss function used during training | A built-in clustering routine for data | A container that holds the dataset
Correct Answer: The standard fully connected layer type

Question Text: What does specifying units=3 when creating a TensorFlow Dense layer mean?
Options (A|B|C|D): The layer processes only three training examples | The overall model has exactly three output classes | The layer contains three neurons (units) | The input tensor is required to have rank 3
Correct Answer: The layer contains three neurons (units)

Question Text: When a Dense layer is applied to an input, what does it compute?
Options (A|B|C|D): The full gradient of the entire model, computed fully automatically | Output activations from the input and the layer's own parameters | The supervised training labels, read directly from the supplied data | The single global minimum of the model's overall cost function
Correct Answer: Output activations from the input and the layer's own parameters

Question Text: Why is a Dense layer called "dense"?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because each neuron uses every activation from the previous layer | Because it can only ever be applied to raw, unprocessed image data | Because it deliberately omits all of its additive per-neuron biases | Because it actively compresses the dataset to save on memory
Correct Answer: Because each neuron uses every activation from the previous layer

Question Text: Which misunderstanding about Dense layers should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That they can be composed together to form a network | That they are an output-only layer, not a general layer type | That they can sit in both hidden and output positions | That they hold parameters and apply activation functions
Correct Answer: That they are an output-only layer, not a general layer type

Question Text: In TensorFlow, what is a tensor best thought of as for practical purposes?
Options (A|B|C|D): A data structure that stores matrices and n-d arrays efficiently | A specialized neural-network activation function applied in layers | Another internal name for the gradient descent algorithm itself | A scheme for encoding categorical class labels and nothing more
Correct Answer: A data structure that stores matrices and n-d arrays efficiently

Question Text: What typically happens when you pass a NumPy array into a TensorFlow operation?
Options (A|B|C|D): It can no longer be converted back into an array afterward | It is converted into a TensorFlow tensor for the computation | It is silently turned into an ordinary Python text string | It is automatically rescaled to have a zero mean per feature
Correct Answer: It is converted into a TensorFlow tensor for the computation

Question Text: How do you convert a TensorFlow tensor back into a NumPy array?
Options (A|B|C|D): By calling its .numpy() method | By transposing the tensor twice | By applying a sigmoid to it | By passing it through compile()
Correct Answer: By calling its .numpy() method

Question Text: Why does TensorFlow favor matrix-style (tensor) representations of data?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes tensors identical to the labels | It removes any need for hidden layers | It lets TensorFlow process data efficiently at scale | It guarantees a higher final model accuracy
Correct Answer: It lets TensorFlow process data efficiently at scale

Question Text: Which common misunderstanding about tensors and NumPy arrays should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That both of them can represent model inputs and activations | That TensorFlow quietly maintains its own internal data format | That they are unrelated data, not two forms of similar numbers | That conversion between the two formats is usually possible
Correct Answer: That they are unrelated data, not two forms of similar numbers

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: What does TensorFlow's Sequential API let you do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Pick optimal hyperparameters automatically | Skip forward propagation at inference time | Cluster the training set into groups | Chain layers in order to build a neural network
Correct Answer: Chain layers in order to build a neural network

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: What is the main convenience of building a model with Sequential rather than applying each layer by hand?
Options (A|B|C|D): It fundamentally changes the underlying math of the built model | It restricts every network it builds to one single hidden layer | It can only ever be used to build models for regression tasks | It manages the layer chaining for both training and prediction
Correct Answer: It manages the layer chaining for both training and prediction

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: Which method runs inference on new inputs with a trained Sequential model?
Options (A|B|C|D): model.loss(X_new) | model.scale(X_new) | model.predict(X_new) | model.gradient(X_new)
Correct Answer: model.predict(X_new)

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: Which statement about Sequential models is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It chains the Dense layers in the exact order that you list them | Its computation is still ordinary layer-by-layer forward propagation | It exposes convenient methods for both training and prediction | It removes the network structure instead of just specifying it
Correct Answer: It removes the network structure instead of just specifying it

Question Text: Why implement forward propagation by hand even though TensorFlow can do it for you?
Options (A|B|C|D): To build intuition and make debugging easier | To eliminate the need for activation functions | To replace TensorFlow in all production work | To prove that ML libraries are unnecessary
Correct Answer: To build intuition and make debugging easier

Question Text: In a from-scratch forward pass, what does a dense function compute?
Options (A|B|C|D): The L2 regularization constant that scales the layer's weights | The Adam optimizer's adaptive per-parameter learning rate value | The total prediction cost summed over the whole training dataset | This layer's activations from the prior layer and its parameters
Correct Answer: This layer's activations from the prior layer and its parameters

Question Text: In a hand-built dense layer, what does the loop over units do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It computes each neuron's activation one at a time | It ranks the input features by importance | It rescales every weight to equal one | It changes how many training examples there are
Correct Answer: It computes each neuron's activation one at a time

Question Text: Why is understanding a from-scratch implementation useful in practice?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes backpropagation no longer necessary | It helps you reason about what the library does underneath | It guarantees hand-written code outruns TensorFlow | It removes the need for matrix operations
Correct Answer: It helps you reason about what the library does underneath

Question Text: What is the core computation performed by a single dense layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): A dot product plus bias, then an activation function | A cost average followed by a regularization term | A random sample followed by a threshold | A cluster assignment followed by normalization
Correct Answer: A dot product plus bias, then an activation function

Question Text: In a manual dense layer, why is each column of the weight matrix W pulled out separately?
Options (A|B|C|D): Columns are only needed during backpropagation | Each column stores one training example | Each column holds the parameter vector for one unit | Each column is a label from the dataset
Correct Answer: Each column holds the parameter vector for one unit

Question Text: What does a dense function return?
Options (A|B|C|D): The activations of the current layer | The decision boundary equation | The original input passed through unchanged | The complete training history
Correct Answer: The activations of the current layer

Question Text: Why is a dense layer routine described as a "general" implementation?
Options (A|B|C|D): It drops the activation and computes only a raw linear map | It works only on the output layer, never on the hidden ones | It runs correctly on just one specific small toy dataset | It captures the layer's computation pattern for any input
Correct Answer: It captures the layer's computation pattern for any input
