Question Text: Which statement about a manual dense function is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Its computed activation output can be passed into the next layer | It is a fundamentally different algorithm from any Dense layer | The very same routine can be reused across many network layers | It relies on the layer's own weight matrix and its bias terms
Correct Answer: It is a fundamentally different algorithm from any Dense layer

Question Text: Why is vectorization important for implementing neural networks?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes the need for activation functions | It enables efficient matrix operations on modern hardware | It turns classification problems into regression | It guarantees the model will not overfit
Correct Answer: It enables efficient matrix operations on modern hardware

Question Text: What hardware benefit does vectorized neural-network code provide?
Options (A|B|C|D): It avoids all floating-point arithmetic and saves compute | It requires specialized neuromorphic spiking hardware to run | It runs matrix multiplications efficiently on CPUs and GPUs | It accelerates only very small models, never larger ones
Correct Answer: It runs matrix multiplications efficiently on CPUs and GPUs

Question Text: How does vectorization compare with computing each neuron separately in a loop?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes the resulting network shallower | It produces a mathematically different model | It works only for linear models | It does many related computations together far faster
Correct Answer: It does many related computations together far faster

Question Text: Why was vectorization a major enabler of large-scale deep learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes labeled data unnecessary | It removes the need for large training datasets | It forces hidden layers to stay linear | It makes huge networks feasible when matrix ops are fast
Correct Answer: It makes huge networks feasible when matrix ops are fast

Question Text: Which statement about vectorization in neural networks is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Modern hardware is well suited to these operations | It leaves the underlying model unchanged | Matrix-based implementations are central to its speed | It is just prettier code, not a gain in speed or scale
Correct Answer: It is just prettier code, not a gain in speed or scale

Question Text: Which operation is central to implementing efficient forward propagation through a dense neural network layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): String concatenation | Matrix multiplication | One-hot encoding | Quicksort on inputs
Correct Answer: Matrix multiplication

Question Text: Why does expressing a dense layer as a matrix multiplication make it computationally efficient?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes the need for any bias terms | It forces the layer output to be discrete | It computes the weighted sums for all units at once | It converts the input tensors directly into labels
Correct Answer: It computes the weighted sums for all units at once

Question Text: In a vectorized dense layer, what does the quantity z represent before the activation function is applied?
Options (A|B|C|D): The learning-rate schedule for training | The total loss measured over the network | The final ground-truth labels of the data | The matrix of weighted sums plus the biases
Correct Answer: The matrix of weighted sums plus the biases

Question Text: After computing the pre-activation matrix z in a vectorized layer, what is the next step?
Options (A|B|C|D): An activation is applied elementwise to z | The gradients are computed with no loss | The input replaces z and z is discarded | The model is considered fully trained
Correct Answer: An activation is applied elementwise to z

Question Text: Which statement about using matrix multiplication in neural networks is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is just notation, not a real efficiency tool | It computes many neuron outputs together | Frameworks rely on it for layer computations | Modern GPUs and TPUs accelerate it well
Correct Answer: It is just notation, not a real efficiency tool

Question Text: What are the three broad steps for training a neural network in TensorFlow/Keras?
Options (A|B|C|D): Build a tree, prune it, and take a vote | Pick K, place centroids, assign points | Specify the model, compile it, fit to data | Normalize labels, cluster, threshold output
Correct Answer: Specify the model, compile it, fit to data

Question Text: What is the main role of model.compile in the TensorFlow training workflow?
Options (A|B|C|D): It predicts outputs on brand-new data | It selects the plotting style for charts | It erases the model's existing weights | It sets the loss function and optimizer
Correct Answer: It sets the loss function and optimizer

Question Text: What does calling model.fit(X, Y) do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It converts NumPy arrays to Python lists | It trains the model on the data X and Y | It only draws a diagram of the network | It runs one forward pass and then halts
Correct Answer: It trains the model on the data X and Y

Question Text: In the context of model.fit, what does the number of epochs specify?
Options (A|B|C|D): How many full passes are made over the data | The number of labels in the dataset | The shape of an input tensor | The number of output units in the model
Correct Answer: How many full passes are made over the data

Question Text: Which belief about TensorFlow training code is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The choice of loss function matters | compile and fit hide real optimization work | Specifying a model is only one of the steps | A few lines means learning is trivial
Correct Answer: A few lines means learning is trivial

Question Text: How is the loss function defined?
Options (A|B|C|D): The performance on a single training example | The model's layer-by-layer architecture | The complete set of learned model weights | The averaged performance over all examples
Correct Answer: The performance on a single training example

Question Text: How is the cost function defined in terms of the loss?
Options (A|B|C|D): A shortcut used only for drawing contours | The same object as the input feature vector | The average of the loss over the training set | The activation of the final output neuron
Correct Answer: The average of the loss over the training set

Question Text: Why is it useful to distinguish the loss function from the cost function?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees better generalization to new data | It makes the network's hidden layers unnecessary | It separates per-example error from the overall objective | It removes the need for labeled training data
Correct Answer: It separates per-example error from the overall objective

Question Text: How does the loss/cost distinction relate to training a model in TensorFlow?
Options (A|B|C|D): TensorFlow ignores cost and uses only activations | You specify a loss and it minimizes the averaged cost | Loss functions only matter for unsupervised learning | The cost equals a single output logit of the model
Correct Answer: You specify a loss and it minimizes the averaged cost

Question Text: Which type of problem is binary cross-entropy used for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Unsupervised clustering | PCA-based visualization | Multi-output regression | Binary classification
Correct Answer: Binary classification

Question Text: Why is binary cross-entropy given that name?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the label takes one of two classes | Because it can only be evaluated by hand | Because it requires exactly two hidden layers | Because it squares the binary output values
Correct Answer: Because the label takes one of two classes

Question Text: Which quantities appear directly in the binary cross-entropy loss?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the regularization parameter | The true label y and the prediction f(x) | Only the input feature vector x | Only the chosen model architecture
Correct Answer: The true label y and the prediction f(x)

Question Text: Why is binary cross-entropy a natural loss for classifying handwritten digits as 0 versus 1?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it lets the network skip the sigmoid | Because the output value must be negative | Because it is binary and the output is a probability | Because the network needs two hidden layers
Correct Answer: Because it is binary and the output is a probability

Question Text: Which statement about binary cross-entropy is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is the right fit for binary classification outputs | It is closely related to logistic-regression loss | It is a universal loss for any task and output type | Common frameworks provide a named version of it
Correct Answer: It is a universal loss for any task and output type

Question Text: In neural-network training, what is backpropagation used for?
Options (A|B|C|D): To set every weight to the same value before the first epoch | To cluster hidden-layer activations into groups before training | To replace the forward pass when making predictions at inference time | To compute the gradients of the cost with respect to the parameters
Correct Answer: To compute the gradients of the cost with respect to the parameters

Question Text: How does backpropagation relate to forward propagation in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): Forward propagation only renders tensors for visualization purposes | They are identical procedures run in two different directions only | Forward propagation becomes optional once backpropagation is in use | Forward propagation produces outputs; backpropagation supplies gradients
Correct Answer: Forward propagation produces outputs; backpropagation supplies gradients

Question Text: Why is backpropagation important when training a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees that the model reaches zero error on every example | It assigns the correct labels to unlabeled training data | Gradient updates need to know how each parameter affects the cost | It removes any need for activation functions in the layers
Correct Answer: Gradient updates need to know how each parameter affects the cost

Question Text: What role does a framework like TensorFlow play with respect to backpropagation?
Options (A|B|C|D): It requires you to derive every gradient by hand beforehand | It runs backpropagation only on plain NumPy arrays, not tensors | It automatically applies backpropagation to compute the needed gradients | It blocks backpropagation so only the forward pass can run
Correct Answer: It automatically applies backpropagation to compute the needed gradients

Question Text: Which statement about backpropagation is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is frequently automated by modern deep-learning libraries | It is how the trained network produces predictions on new inputs | It computes derivatives of the cost during the training process | It is a central mechanism used to update parameters while learning
Correct Answer: It is how the trained network produces predictions on new inputs

Question Text: What range of outputs can the ReLU activation function produce?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only values between 0 and 1 | Only strictly negative values | Only whole-number integers | Zero or any positive value
Correct Answer: Zero or any positive value

Question Text: What does the abbreviation ReLU stand for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Reduced logistic update | Rectified linear unit | Residual learning unit | Regression lookup unit
Correct Answer: Rectified linear unit

Question Text: Why can ReLU be a natural choice for the output unit in some regression tasks?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always converts its input into a valid probability | It forces the predicted output to be exactly 0 or 1 | It keeps outputs non-negative when the target cannot be negative | It is the only activation that can process image inputs
Correct Answer: It keeps outputs non-negative when the target cannot be negative

Question Text: Which belief about ReLU is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is a common and effective choice for hidden-layer units | It outputs 0 for negative inputs and rises linearly for positive ones | It acts like a sigmoid, squashing every output into the range 0 to 1 | It is a widely used activation function in modern neural networks
Correct Answer: It acts like a sigmoid, squashing every output into the range 0 to 1

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: Which output-layer activation is the natural default for binary classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): No activation at all | ReLU | Sigmoid | Linear
Correct Answer: Sigmoid

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: For a regression target y that can be either positive or negative, which output-layer activation is recommended?
Options (A|B|C|D): ReLU | Linear | Softmax | Sigmoid
Correct Answer: Linear

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: For a regression target y that must be non-negative, which output-layer activation is recommended?
Options (A|B|C|D): Step function | Linear | ReLU | Sigmoid
Correct Answer: ReLU

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: Why does the best choice of output-layer activation depend on the target variable y?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the chosen activation rewrites the dataset's labels | Because the activation's output range should match the values predicted | Because each layer of a network must use a distinct activation | Because the hidden layers fix the output type automatically
Correct Answer: Because the activation's output range should match the values predicted

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing Activation Functions for Output Layers
Question Text: When choosing an output-layer activation, which belief is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sigmoid is the natural output choice for binary classification | One single output activation is always best for any task whatsoever | The output design should reflect the target variable's value range | Linear and ReLU can each fit different kinds of regression targets
Correct Answer: One single output activation is always best for any task whatsoever

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: What is the common rule of thumb for activations in the hidden layers of a network?
Options (A|B|C|D): Use linear activations throughout every hidden layer | Use sigmoid in every hidden layer regardless of the task | Use no activation function in the hidden layers | Use ReLU as the default activation in hidden layers
Correct Answer: Use ReLU as the default activation in hidden layers

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: Why are linear activations a poor choice for the hidden layers of a network?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are simply far too slow to evaluate during model training | They have a zero derivative, so the hidden layer cannot be trained | They collapse the network into plain linear or logistic regression | They make the entire network overfit badly on every dataset given
Correct Answer: They collapse the network into plain linear or logistic regression

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: What is one practical reason ReLU is emphasized as a good default for hidden layers?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is simple to use and effective for building powerful networks | It restricts every neuron's output to the range 0 to 1 | It makes the role of every individual neuron interpretable | It removes the need to tune any hyperparameters at all
Correct Answer: It is simple to use and effective for building powerful networks

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: Defaulting to ReLU in hidden layers supports which broader principle about network design?
Options (A|B|C|D): Hidden layers need nonlinearities so that depth adds modeling power | Hidden layers should mirror the output layer's activation exactly | Every task should constrain hidden layers to one fixed output range | Backpropagation becomes unnecessary once ReLU units are used here
Correct Answer: Hidden layers need nonlinearities so that depth adds modeling power

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why ReLU Is Preferred in Hidden Layers
Question Text: Regarding ReLU in hidden layers, which belief is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Hidden layers generally benefit from a nonlinear activation | Linear activations are usually a poor default for hidden layers | It serves as a practical nonlinear default rather than a probability output | It is chosen because hidden layers are meant to output probabilities
Correct Answer: It is chosen because hidden layers are meant to output probabilities

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: How does the choice of output-layer activation typically differ between binary classification and regression in a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): Classification often uses sigmoid; regression fits the target range | Both tasks should reliably use a sigmoid output activation function | Both tasks should reliably use a plain linear output activation | Regression often uses sigmoid; classification fits the class count
Correct Answer: Classification often uses sigmoid; regression fits the target range

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: Why is a sigmoid activation a natural choice for the output of a binary classifier?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes the overall network a purely linear function of its inputs | It forces the output to be exactly 0 or exactly 1 with no values between | It keeps the output unbounded so any real value is allowed | It squashes the output into the range 0 to 1, readable as a probability
Correct Answer: It squashes the output into the range 0 to 1, readable as a probability

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: For a regression target that can be either positive or negative, why is a linear output activation a sensible choice?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because regression outputs never use any activation function at all | Because a linear output can produce values of either sign without bound | Because a linear output rounds every prediction to an integer | Because a linear output predicts discrete class labels accurately
Correct Answer: Because a linear output can produce values of either sign without bound

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: When would a ReLU activation be a sensible choice for a regression output rather than a linear one?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the task is actually binary classification in disguise | When the model should be trained without any labels | When the target value is constrained to be non-negative | When the loss must be computed inside the output unit
Correct Answer: When the target value is constrained to be non-negative

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Binary Classification vs. Regression Output Design
Question Text: Which belief about choosing an output activation is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Binary classification and regression can call for different outputs | The allowed range of the target value should inform the activation | The architecture alone fixes the activation, regardless of the range | A sigmoid maps the output into a 0-to-1 probability-like value range
Correct Answer: The architecture alone fixes the activation, regardless of the range

Question Text: What is the main difference between clustering and supervised classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): Clustering predicts the known labels while classification groups unaided | Clustering handles only image data while classification handles text | Clustering finds structure in unlabeled data; classification uses labels | They are identical except for which optimizer trains the model used
Correct Answer: Clustering finds structure in unlabeled data; classification uses labels

Question Text: Why is grouping customers into market segments usually treated as a clustering task rather than supervised classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because every customer belongs to infinitely many distinct categories | Because clustering methods only work on business and finance data | Because the segments are typically not provided in advance as labeled outputs | Because supervised classification cannot be trained with gradient descent
Correct Answer: Because the segments are typically not provided in advance as labeled outputs

Question Text: What does supervised classification require during training that clustering does not?
Options (A|B|C|D): An explicit cost function for it to try to minimize | A separate held-out validation set used for tuning | More than just a single input feature per example | Labeled examples giving the correct output category
Correct Answer: Labeled examples giving the correct output category

Question Text: Why is clustering best described as structure discovery?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it always splits any given dataset into exactly two classes | Because it surfaces patterns or groupings latent in unlabeled data | Because it memorizes the category label attached to each example | Because it outputs one continuous numeric prediction per example
Correct Answer: Because it surfaces patterns or groupings latent in unlabeled data

Question Text: Which statement about clustering versus classification is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Supervised classification depends on having labeled examples | Clustering operates on data that has no output labels | Clustering is merely classification that achieves lower accuracy | The two address related tasks but rest on different label assumptions
Correct Answer: Clustering is merely classification that achieves lower accuracy

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: What is the core goal of clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): To strip every source of variation out of the dataset | To uncover useful structure in data that has no output labels | To fit a best line through a set of labeled points | To estimate class probabilities for a binary label
Correct Answer: To uncover useful structure in data that has no output labels

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: Why is clustering categorized as unsupervised learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it is unable to process numerical features | Because each example must be labeled manually by a person | Because it receives no correct output label for any example | Because it is given no input features whatsoever
Correct Answer: Because it receives no correct output label for any example

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: Which task best illustrates clustering as structure discovery?
Options (A|B|C|D): Predicting a house's sale price from its floor area | Diagnosing a tumor as either benign or malignant | Sorting unlabeled news articles into emergent topic groups | Translating a sentence from English into Spanish
Correct Answer: Sorting unlabeled news articles into emergent topic groups

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: Why can clustering still be valuable even when the true groups are unknown in advance?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it only runs when the groups are already plainly obvious | Because it can expose patterns that help organize or understand the data | Because it removes any need to inspect the resulting groups | Because it is guaranteed to return the single correct grouping
Correct Answer: Because it can expose patterns that help organize or understand the data

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Clustering as Structure Discovery in Unlabeled Data
Question Text: Which belief about clustering is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): During training it is told the correct group label for each example | It tends to place similar examples together within the same group | It can serve as a genuinely useful tool for exploratory data analysis | It searches for hidden structure without any labeled output data
Correct Answer: During training it is told the correct group label for each example

Question Text: What is the K-means algorithm trying to accomplish?
Options (A|B|C|D): Estimate a continuous output by fitting a line | Locate the global minimum of a neural network's loss | Predict a target value y from input features x | Partition the data into K groups by similarity
Correct Answer: Partition the data into K groups by similarity

Question Text: What does the K in K-means stand for?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of clusters the algorithm forms | The number of labels already present in the data | The number of training iterations that are required | The number of input features in the data
Correct Answer: The number of clusters the algorithm forms

Question Text: Why is K-means considered an unsupervised algorithm?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it accepts only categorical input data | Because it disregards the distances between points | Because it operates without any labeled outputs | Because it outputs a probability for each class
Correct Answer: Because it operates without any labeled outputs

Question Text: What does K-means produce as output for each example?
Options (A|B|C|D): The value of a gradient or derivative | An assignment to one of the K clusters | A real-valued regression prediction | A binary anomaly score and nothing else
Correct Answer: An assignment to one of the K clusters

Question Text: Which belief about K-means is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It groups unlabeled input data points into K clusters by similarity | It is one very common example of an unsupervised clustering algorithm | The value of K is chosen in advance by the practitioner who runs it here | It learns predefined labeled categories, like supervised classification
Correct Answer: It learns predefined labeled categories, like supervised classification

Question Text: In K-means clustering, what does a centroid represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The running average of the cost function | A human-chosen labeled training example | The data point farthest from all clusters | The representative center point of a cluster
Correct Answer: The representative center point of a cluster

Question Text: How is each centroid typically computed in K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): By sorting the examples and taking the median index | By selecting the single densest data point | By maximizing distance to every data point | By averaging the points assigned to that cluster
Correct Answer: By averaging the points assigned to that cluster

Question Text: Why are centroids central to how K-means works?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are stored so the raw training data can be discarded | They are only consulted once the algorithm has converged | They serve as the reference points for assigning each example to a cluster | They supply the ground-truth labels needed for supervised training
Correct Answer: They serve as the reference points for assigning each example to a cluster

Question Text: What happens to the centroids over the course of K-means training?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are repeatedly recomputed from their assigned examples | They are discarded after the first assignment pass | They gradually turn into the target class labels | They stay fixed at their random initial positions
Correct Answer: They are repeatedly recomputed from their assigned examples

Question Text: Which common misconception about K-means centroids should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): A centroid can shift to a new location on each iteration | A centroid acts as a reference for assigning nearby points | A centroid must coincide with an actual data point in its cluster | A centroid is the mean of the points currently assigned to it
Correct Answer: A centroid must coincide with an actual data point in its cluster

Question Text: What occurs during the assignment step of K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each prediction is compared against the ground-truth label | Every centroid is moved to the mean of the whole dataset | The current number of clusters is automatically doubled | Each example is assigned to its nearest centroid
Correct Answer: Each example is assigned to its nearest centroid

Question Text: When the assignment step calls a centroid the "nearest" one, nearness is measured by what?
Options (A|B|C|D): Order of the examples in the alphabet | Closeness of their data-collection timestamps | Agreement in the predicted output label | Distance from the example to the centroid
Correct Answer: Distance from the example to the centroid

Question Text: Why is the assignment step needed in each K-means iteration?
Options (A|B|C|D): It locks in the correct number of clusters K | It strips out the outliers before clustering begins | It computes the gradients used for backpropagation | It decides which cluster currently fits each example best
Correct Answer: It decides which cluster currently fits each example best

Question Text: What does a single assignment step produce?
Options (A|B|C|D): A cluster index labeling each example | Updated regression weights for the model | A freshly recomputed feature matrix | A set of anomaly-detection thresholds
Correct Answer: A cluster index labeling each example

Question Text: Which misconception about the K-means assignment step should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Examples are assigned at random each pass, ignoring centroid positions | Each example is assigned to whichever centroid is closest to it | Assignment is one of the two steps K-means repeats each round | An example's assignment can change from one iteration to the next
Correct Answer: Examples are assigned at random each pass, ignoring centroid positions

Question Text: What occurs during the centroid update step of K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): The data is relabeled using the ground-truth classes | Each centroid moves to the mean of its assigned points | The algorithm's learning rate is decreased a notch | Each example is reassigned to the nearest centroid
Correct Answer: Each centroid moves to the mean of its assigned points

Question Text: Why does the centroid update depend on the current cluster assignments?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because each centroid is recomputed from the points assigned to it | Because the centroid update is driven by backpropagated gradients | Because K-means relies on externally supplied ground-truth labels | Because the centroids are defined without any reference to the data
Correct Answer: Because each centroid is recomputed from the points assigned to it

Question Text: What is the intuition behind moving a centroid to the mean of its assigned points?
Options (A|B|C|D): It deliberately drives the clustering cost toward its maximum value | It positions the centroid centrally among that cluster's points | It makes all of the points within that cluster become identical | It forces every cluster to end up holding the very same point count
Correct Answer: It positions the centroid centrally among that cluster's points

Question Text: How does the centroid update step interact with the assignment step that follows it?
Options (A|B|C|D): The moved centroids permanently freeze every later cluster assignment | The moved centroids can change which examples are now nearest to each | The moved centroids reset the chosen cluster count K all on their own | The moved centroids remove any further need to check for convergence
Correct Answer: The moved centroids can change which examples are now nearest to each

Question Text: Which misconception about the centroid update step should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Centroids are updated independently of their assigned points | Updating centroids is one of K-means' two repeated steps | Each centroid is recomputed from its current cluster members | A centroid's position can change on every iteration
Correct Answer: Centroids are updated independently of their assigned points

Question Text: When is K-means considered to have converged?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the cluster labels match a hidden answer key | When all clusters end up with the same number of points | After it has completed exactly K full iterations | When the assignments and centroids stop changing much
Correct Answer: When the assignments and centroids stop changing much

Question Text: Why can K-means stop after enough rounds of assigning points and updating centroids?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the centroid coordinates eventually turn negative | Because the data points are consumed and disappear | Because further steps stop meaningfully improving the clustering | Because unsupervised methods optimize no objective at all
Correct Answer: Because further steps stop meaningfully improving the clustering

Question Text: What is a practical sign that K-means has converged?
Options (A|B|C|D): The algorithm switches over to a regression objective | The centroids and assignments stay stable across iterations | The clustering cost begins to rise sharply each round | The number of input features collapses down to one
Correct Answer: The centroids and assignments stay stable across iterations

Question Text: Does reaching convergence guarantee K-means found the globally best clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): No, because reaching convergence means the whole algorithm has failed | No, it can settle at a local solution that depends on initialization | Yes, because the underlying K-means clustering objective is convex | Yes, convergence on this objective always gives the global optimum
Correct Answer: No, it can settle at a local solution that depends on initialization

Question Text: Which misconception about K-means convergence should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Converging means the best clustering over all initializations was found | The starting centroid positions can affect the final clustering | The algorithm can settle into a stable, unchanging solution | Convergence means the repeated steps no longer change much
Correct Answer: Converging means the best clustering over all initializations was found

Question Text: What does the K-means distortion (cost) objective measure?
Options (A|B|C|D): The summed pairwise distance between every pair of cluster centroids | The probability that a given data point is an anomaly or outlier | The total squared distance from points to their assigned centroids | The classification accuracy achieved on labeled training examples
Correct Answer: The total squared distance from points to their assigned centroids

Question Text: Why is a smaller value of the K-means distortion objective generally better?
Options (A|B|C|D): It proves the clustering model was trained with supervised labels | It implies there are now more clusters than there are input features | It means points lie closer on average to their assigned centroids | It indicates the underlying data are linearly separable into classes
Correct Answer: It means points lie closer on average to their assigned centroids

Question Text: How does the distortion objective relate to the K-means algorithm's iterations?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is computed only after training and is unrelated to the steps | Each iteration tends to decrease it until the algorithm converges | It is maximized so that clusters spread as far apart as possible | It is ignored entirely once the value of K has been chosen
Correct Answer: Each iteration tends to decrease it until the algorithm converges

Question Text: Which distances contribute to the K-means distortion objective?
Options (A|B|C|D): Distances from each point to predefined labeled categories | Distances measured only between the cluster centroids themselves | Distances from each point to the coordinate origin only | Distances from each point to the centroid it is assigned to
Correct Answer: Distances from each point to the centroid it is assigned to

Question Text: Which statement about the K-means distortion objective is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It quantifies supervised prediction error rather than within-cluster fit | K-means iterations act to reduce it rather than increase it | Its value depends on both the assignments and the centroid locations | Lower values usually reflect tighter clustering around centroids
Correct Answer: It quantifies supervised prediction error rather than within-cluster fit

Question Text: Why is K-means guaranteed to converge?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because each step is designed so the distortion never increases | Because the centroids are permanently frozen right after initialization | Because it performs gradient descent on a globally convex function | Because it gradually memorizes the correct cluster labels for the points
Correct Answer: Because each step is designed so the distortion never increases

Question Text: How does the assignment step (reassigning each point to its nearest centroid) support convergence?
Options (A|B|C|D): It forces every point into a single shared cluster | It changes how many features each point is described by | It can only decrease or leave unchanged the current distortion | It randomizes the objective so the algorithm can explore freely
Correct Answer: It can only decrease or leave unchanged the current distortion

Question Text: How does the centroid update step support convergence?
Options (A|B|C|D): It recomputes the centroids while ignoring the points assigned to them | It converts the clustering task into a supervised regression problem | Moving each centroid to its points' mean cannot raise the objective | It deliberately raises the objective to escape a local minimum
Correct Answer: Moving each centroid to its points' mean cannot raise the objective

Question Text: Why does K-means converging NOT imply it found the globally optimal clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the distortion objective always reaches exactly zero at the end | Because different initializations can settle into different local optima | Because convergence can only ever occur when the data are fully labeled | Because the K-means algorithm does not actually optimize any objective
Correct Answer: Because different initializations can settle into different local optima

Question Text: Which statement about why K-means converges is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It converges because each repeated step improves or preserves the objective | It converges because it secretly already knows the true clusters | The choice of initialization still affects the final result | Convergence does not guarantee reaching the best global solution
Correct Answer: It converges because it secretly already knows the true clusters

Question Text: Why does K-means require an initialization step before its main loop?
Options (A|B|C|D): It must pick initial centroids before it alternates assignments and updates | It needs ground-truth class labels to be supplied before it can start | It begins by selecting the anomaly detection threshold epsilon to use | It must fit a regression line to the data before the main loop can begin
Correct Answer: It must pick initial centroids before it alternates assignments and updates

Question Text: Why can the choice of random initialization matter a lot in K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): Different starting centroids can lead to different final clusterings | The randomness changes how many features each data point has | Random starting points decide whether or not the data are labeled | Initialization only affects the plots, not the actual clustering results
Correct Answer: Different starting centroids can lead to different final clusterings

Question Text: What is a common and recommended way to initialize the centroids in K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): Reuse final centroids from an unrelated dataset without checking | Place every centroid at the origin (all zeros) on every run | Begin with one centroid located at each individual example | Pick K of the training examples at random as the centroids
Correct Answer: Pick K of the training examples at random as the centroids

Question Text: What risk arises from a poor random initialization of the centroids?
Options (A|B|C|D): The algorithm silently turns into a supervised classifier | Some of the input features are dropped from the data | The distortion cost function becomes mathematically undefined | It may converge to a worse, suboptimal local clustering
Correct Answer: It may converge to a worse, suboptimal local clustering

Question Text: Which statement about K-means initialization is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Initialization happens first, before the iterative assignment and update steps | Choosing the centroids at random is a common initialization approach | The starting centroids can strongly affect the final clustering outcome | Initialization is irrelevant since K-means always returns one fixed result
Correct Answer: Initialization is irrelevant since K-means always returns one fixed result

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Why is it useful to run K-means several times with different random initializations?
Options (A|B|C|D): To average the predicted labels together across all of the runs | To enlarge the dataset by generating extra synthetic examples | To guarantee that the global optimum is reached on every single run | To improve the chance of finding a lower-distortion clustering
Correct Answer: To improve the chance of finding a lower-distortion clustering

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: After running K-means from several random initializations, which solution is typically kept?
Options (A|B|C|D): The run achieving the lowest distortion objective value | The run that ended with the largest distortion value | The run that produced the most equally sized clusters | The very first run obtained, regardless of its quality
Correct Answer: The run achieving the lowest distortion objective value

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Why does keeping the best of several K-means runs help in practice?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because random initializations automatically change the number of clusters | Because a single run can get stuck in a poorer local solution | Because clustering fundamentally requires labeled data to work | Because K-means has no objective function to compare runs with
Correct Answer: Because a single run can get stuck in a poorer local solution

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: What is the main trade-off of using multiple random initializations?
Options (A|B|C|D): It costs more computation for a better chance of a good result | It guarantees the resulting clusters will be interpretable | It selects the value of K automatically at no extra cost | It uses less computation while also reducing robustness
Correct Answer: It costs more computation for a better chance of a good result

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Which statement about using multiple random initializations is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The best run is chosen by comparing the distortion objective values | Different starts can lead to different local clustering solutions | They are unnecessary because one random start is always good enough | Repeating K-means improves robustness against poor starting centroids
Correct Answer: They are unnecessary because one random start is always good enough

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Number of Clusters K
Question Text: In the K-means algorithm, what does the value of K specify?
Options (A|B|C|D): How many clusters the algorithm forms | The step size used during each update | How many input features to retain | The threshold for flagging anomalies
Correct Answer: How many clusters the algorithm forms

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Number of Clusters K
Question Text: Why is selecting a value of K for K-means often not straightforward?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because a larger value of K always yields a strictly better result | Because unlabeled data gives no ground truth on the right group count | Because the value of K must always equal the number of input features | Because K-means itself selects the one correct K with no human input
Correct Answer: Because unlabeled data gives no ground truth on the right group count

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Number of Clusters K
Question Text: Which approach gives a quantitative way to help choose K for K-means?
Options (A|B|C|D): Read K from the output activation function | Set K equal to the number of training examples | Always fix K at 2 regardless of data | Track how the distortion cost changes as K varies
Correct Answer: Track how the distortion cost changes as K varies

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Number of Clusters K
Question Text: Why can practical judgment matter when selecting K, beyond minimizing the cost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the most useful K can depend on how the clusters get used | Because the distortion cost is entirely irrelevant to this choice | Because the best choice of K is reliably always the largest one | Because the value of K only carries meaning in supervised learning
Correct Answer: Because the most useful K can depend on how the clusters get used

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Number of Clusters K
Question Text: Which belief about choosing K is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The choice of K can weigh both the cost behavior and downstream uses | Choosing a value of K is one part of applying the K-means algorithm | The chosen value of K directly affects the resulting cluster output | One obviously correct value of K always exists regardless of context
Correct Answer: One obviously correct value of K always exists regardless of context

Question Text: What is the elbow method trying to accomplish?
Options (A|B|C|D): Pick a learning rate for gradient descent | Estimate the probability that a point is anomalous | Choose K from a bend in the distortion-versus-K curve | Select the activation used in the output layer
Correct Answer: Choose K from a bend in the distortion-versus-K curve

Question Text: Why is the elbow method often limited in practice?
Options (A|B|C|D): Distortion rises as K grows larger | It is hardwired to always return K equal to 1 | It works only on fully labeled datasets | The distortion curve frequently shows no clear bend
Correct Answer: The distortion curve frequently shows no clear bend

Question Text: If the distortion curve shows no clear elbow, what is the reasonable conclusion?
Options (A|B|C|D): The method is inconclusive, so other judgment is needed | The data has no real structure and cannot be clustered | K-means has failed outright and cannot be rerun at all | A supervised model must be substituted for the clustering
Correct Answer: The method is inconclusive, so other judgment is needed

Question Text: Why is relying blindly on the elbow method a poor idea?
Options (A|B|C|D): It assigns class labels to the data in an incorrect way | Distortion bears no relationship to true clustering quality | The chosen value of K never affects the final clustering result | It can give false confidence when the bend is faint or absent
Correct Answer: It can give false confidence when the bend is faint or absent

Question Text: Which belief about the elbow method is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is a heuristic that can fail to be clear | Practical considerations may still matter alongside it | It always pins down the single best value of K | It relies on the trend of the distortion cost
Correct Answer: It always pins down the single best value of K

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing K Based on Downstream Use
Question Text: Why might the intended downstream use of clusters matter when choosing K?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the most useful clustering depends on its later purpose | Because only the largest possible K is ever useful | Because the value of K has no effect on applications | Because downstream tasks make cluster quality irrelevant
Correct Answer: Because the most useful clustering depends on its later purpose

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing K Based on Downstream Use
Question Text: Which scenario best reflects choosing K based on downstream use?
Options (A|B|C|D): Picking the K that yields the most useful market segments | Setting K equal to the total number of input features | Assigning K by the alphabetical order of the cluster names | Choosing K=3 simply because that value is a common default
Correct Answer: Picking the K that yields the most useful market segments

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing K Based on Downstream Use
Question Text: What does choosing K by downstream use add beyond the elbow method?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees the single globally optimal clustering of the data | It converts K-means into a fully supervised classification method | It stresses usefulness for the application, not just curve shape | It proves that the underlying distortion cost objective does not matter
Correct Answer: It stresses usefulness for the application, not just curve shape

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing K Based on Downstream Use
Question Text: Why is choosing K by downstream use a practical rather than purely theoretical concern?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because supervised labels fix the correct answer regardless of K | Because K-means optimizes no underlying distortion cost objective | Because practical choices disregard the underlying data entirely | Because clustering often supports decisions outside the algorithm
Correct Answer: Because clustering often supports decisions outside the algorithm

Question Text: What is the main goal of anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Find the shortest path through a graph | Predict a continuous value from labels | Flag examples that look unusual versus normal | Group unlabeled data into clusters
Correct Answer: Flag examples that look unusual versus normal

Question Text: Why is anomaly detection often practical in a manufacturing setting?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it works only when every defect is known beforehand | Because the method needs no input features | Because normal behavior can be modeled and odd units flagged | Because defective products outnumber good ones
Correct Answer: Because normal behavior can be modeled and odd units flagged

Question Text: What makes anomaly detection different from standard supervised learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): It outputs binary class labels by fitting a logistic regression model to the data | It flags deviations from normal instead of needing many labeled positive examples | It assumes that every future anomaly already appears within the available training data | It requires the data to be clustered into groups before any model training begins
Correct Answer: It flags deviations from normal instead of needing many labeled positive examples

Question Text: In the aircraft-engine example, what are the features meant to capture?
Options (A|B|C|D): Measurements like heat and vibration of the engine | The ranked output of a recommender system | Just the labels good and bad | Only the engine's serial number
Correct Answer: Measurements like heat and vibration of the engine

Question Text: Which belief about anomaly detection is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is useful precisely when anomalies are rare | It needs many labeled anomaly examples to be useful | It can model normal behavior and flag low-probability cases | It is commonly applied in quality-control settings
Correct Answer: It needs many labeled anomaly examples to be useful

Question Text: In anomaly detection, what does density estimation aim to do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Estimate the slope of the fitted linear regression line | Estimate how likely each value of x is under normal data | Count how many hidden layers the trained model contains | Assign every example to its closest cluster centroid here
Correct Answer: Estimate how likely each value of x is under normal data

Question Text: Why is modeling the probability density p(x) of normal examples useful for anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Examples with very low estimated p(x) can be flagged as anomalies | It guarantees perfectly perfect recall across every anomaly seen | It directly supplies the ground-truth supervised labels we need | It removes any need to choose or engineer input features at all
Correct Answer: Examples with very low estimated p(x) can be flagged as anomalies

Question Text: After fitting p(x) on normal data, what role does p(x_test) play for a new example?
Options (A|B|C|D): It replaces the input feature vector entirely during scoring time | It sets how many separate clusters the data ought to form first | It exactly equals the model's average training loss value here | It is compared to a threshold to decide if the example is unusual
Correct Answer: It is compared to a threshold to decide if the example is unusual

Question Text: Why are points lying far from the high-density region treated as more suspicious?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because they tend to have lower probability under the normal model | Because the distance value is the only feature the model ever uses | Because they always form a brand-new separate class on their own | Because supervised learning methods reliably fail on every one
Correct Answer: Because they tend to have lower probability under the normal model

Question Text: Which statement about density-based anomaly detection is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The value p(x) reflects how typical a given data point really is | Examples with a low estimated probability value can be flagged | It models the anomalies directly rather than modeling normal data | A chosen threshold applied to p(x) is one part of the method
Correct Answer: It models the anomalies directly rather than modeling normal data

Question Text: What is a Gaussian (normal) distribution?
Options (A|B|C|D): A bell-shaped probability distribution over a numeric variable | A discrete list of the possible output classes for a label | An objective function that is minimized during data clustering | A type of binary decision tree used to split examples apart
Correct Answer: A bell-shaped probability distribution over a numeric variable

Question Text: Which parameter determines where a Gaussian distribution is centered?
Options (A|B|C|D): The anomaly decision threshold epsilon | The total count of training examples | The mean mu | The variance of the distribution shape
Correct Answer: The mean mu

Question Text: Which parameter controls the spread or width of a Gaussian distribution?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total number of input features n | The mean parameter taken by itself | The optimizer's learning rate alpha | The variance, or standard deviation
Correct Answer: The variance, or standard deviation

Question Text: Why does a Gaussian curve become taller as it gets narrower?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the total area under the curve must stay equal to 1 | Because the mean automatically grows larger as width shrinks | Because every Gaussian curve must share one fixed peak height | Because the anomaly threshold epsilon was just decreased a bit
Correct Answer: Because the total area under the curve must stay equal to 1

Question Text: Which statement about the Gaussian distribution is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It can serve as the underlying model in anomaly detection | Changing the mean widens the curve rather than shifting it | The overall shape of the Gaussian curve is bell-shaped here | The mean and the variance each play a different distinct role
Correct Answer: Changing the mean widens the curve rather than shifting it

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Mean and Variance in Gaussian Modeling
Question Text: When fitting a Gaussian to data, what does the mean mu represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The center value around which the data tends to lie | The number of anomalies found within the dataset here | The threshold value that is used to classify the points | The total area that is enclosed under the whole curve
Correct Answer: The center value around which the data tends to lie

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Mean and Variance in Gaussian Modeling
Question Text: When fitting a Gaussian to data, what does the variance represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The size of the training set considered alone | The exact probability that a point is an anomaly | How spread out the values are around the mean | The number of separate features contained in x
Correct Answer: How spread out the values are around the mean

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Mean and Variance in Gaussian Modeling
Question Text: How does increasing the variance change the shape of a Gaussian curve?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes the curve wider and generally lower overall | It always makes the anomalies far easier to detect | It turns the continuous distribution into a discrete one | It shifts the center of the curve to the left side
Correct Answer: It makes the curve wider and generally lower overall

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Mean and Variance in Gaussian Modeling
Question Text: Why do the mean and variance matter for Gaussian-based anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): They fix the value of K that gets used inside the K-means routine | They define the probability model judging whether values are typical | They remove the need to select any input features for the model | They are used only for plotting and never by the algorithm itself
Correct Answer: They define the probability model judging whether values are typical

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Mean and Variance in Gaussian Modeling
Question Text: Which statement about the mean and variance is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The variance parameter controls the width of the curve | The mean parameter controls the location of the curve | Both quantities together help to define a Gaussian shape | They are interchangeable rather than center and spread
Correct Answer: They are interchangeable rather than center and spread

Question Text: How are the parameters of a Gaussian typically estimated from training data?
Options (A|B|C|D): By reading the parameter values off of a plotted graph by hand | By computing each feature's mean and variance from the data | By using only the anomalous examples found inside the dataset | By first running a full K-means clustering pass on the data
Correct Answer: By computing each feature's mean and variance from the data

Question Text: What kind of data is most useful for fitting the normal-behavior model in anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Examples drawn only from the held-out test set | A tiny handful of anomalies, and nothing else | Randomly generated class labels for the points | A large set of mostly normal examples
Correct Answer: A large set of mostly normal examples

Question Text: Why might a few accidental anomalies in the training set not ruin the fitted Gaussian model?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the estimated parameters do not depend on the data | Because the anomalies are always removed fully automatically | Because the method ignores the training set data entirely | Because the training set is dominated by normal examples
Correct Answer: Because the training set is dominated by normal examples

Question Text: In the simple anomaly model that treats features independently, what is estimated separately for each feature?
Options (A|B|C|D): A separate vocabulary of possible class labels | A mean and variance specific to that feature | A distinct learning rate value for that feature | A different number of clusters for each feature
Correct Answer: A mean and variance specific to that feature

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multivariate Anomaly Detection via Per-Feature Gaussians
Question Text: In the simple anomaly-detection model p(x) = p(x1)p(x2)...p(xn), how are multiple input features handled?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each feature gets its own Gaussian, and these are multiplied into one model | Each feature must be paired with its own class label before fitting | All features are averaged into a single value before fitting one Gaussian | Features, not training examples, are grouped into clusters
Correct Answer: Each feature gets its own Gaussian, and these are multiplied into one model

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multivariate Anomaly Detection via Per-Feature Gaussians
Question Text: Multiplying the per-feature probabilities as p(x1)p(x2)...p(xn) relies on which assumption about the features?
Options (A|B|C|D): That all features are forced to share one common mean | That anomalous cases occur more often than normal ones | That every feature takes only binary values 0 or 1 | That the features are modeled as statistically independent
Correct Answer: That the features are modeled as statistically independent

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multivariate Anomaly Detection via Per-Feature Gaussians
Question Text: Why can the product of per-feature probabilities p(x1)p(x2)...p(xn) become very small for an anomaly?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the threshold epsilon is forced to be a negative number | Because anomalous features are dropped before the product is taken | Because just one unusual feature value can pull the whole product low | Because this product model always evaluates exactly to zero
Correct Answer: Because just one unusual feature value can pull the whole product low

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multivariate Anomaly Detection via Per-Feature Gaussians
Question Text: What is an important benefit of modeling each feature with its own one-dimensional Gaussian and multiplying them?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes the anomaly threshold epsilon entirely unnecessary | It recovers the exact true data distribution every time | It scales to many features while keeping each fit simple | It removes any need to estimate means or variances at all
Correct Answer: It scales to many features while keeping each fit simple

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multivariate Anomaly Detection via Per-Feature Gaussians
Question Text: Which statement about fitting one Gaussian per feature and multiplying them is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That all features share one identical Gaussian rather than each having its own fit | That the overall probability is the product of the per-feature probabilities | That this independent-product form is a practical simplification | That different features can have different means and variances
Correct Answer: That all features share one identical Gaussian rather than each having its own fit

Question Text: In the anomaly-detection algorithm, what is epsilon?
Options (A|B|C|D): A threshold deciding whether p(x) is low enough to flag an anomaly | The total count of input features present in each training example | The mean parameter of the Gaussian distribution fitted to the data | The step size used by the gradient descent parameter update rule
Correct Answer: A threshold deciding whether p(x) is low enough to flag an anomaly

Question Text: Using threshold epsilon, when is a new example x flagged as anomalous?
Options (A|B|C|D): When its probability p(x) is greater than epsilon | When its probability p(x) is less than epsilon | When the example x happens to have exactly two features | When the fitted Gaussian mean for x equals zero
Correct Answer: When its probability p(x) is less than epsilon

Question Text: Why is a threshold epsilon still needed even after p(x) has been modeled?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because epsilon takes the place of the fitted Gaussian parameters | Because the value of p(x) cannot be computed without epsilon | Because a rule is needed to turn probabilities into anomaly decisions | Because epsilon is what makes the feature distributions Gaussian
Correct Answer: Because a rule is needed to turn probabilities into anomaly decisions

Question Text: What is the likely effect of setting the threshold epsilon too high?
Options (A|B|C|D): The detector silently turns itself into a fully supervised classifier | The Gaussian probability model fitted to the data is no longer used at all | No example can ever cross the raised threshold to be flagged | Many normal examples get flagged as anomalies along with true ones
Correct Answer: Many normal examples get flagged as anomalies along with true ones

Question Text: Which statement about the threshold epsilon is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That it is chosen to balance how aggressively anomalies are flagged | That changing it changes which examples get flagged | That a lower p(x) reflects a more unusual example | That it is set automatically and needs no evaluation or tuning
Correct Answer: That it is set automatically and needs no evaluation or tuning

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building and Evaluating an Anomaly Detection System
Question Text: Why does evaluating an anomaly-detection system matter while you are still developing it?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guides smarter choices of features and the threshold epsilon | It guarantees the finished model will generalize perfectly | It is a step that applies only to supervised learning systems | It lets you proceed without collecting any training data
Correct Answer: It guides smarter choices of features and the threshold epsilon

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building and Evaluating an Anomaly Detection System
Question Text: What is one key challenge in evaluating an anomaly-detection system?
Options (A|B|C|D): No labels of any kind are ever obtainable for evaluation | True anomalies are rare, so evaluation data is highly imbalanced | The algorithm simply cannot be tested before it is deployed | How well it does turns out not to depend on epsilon at all
Correct Answer: True anomalies are rare, so evaluation data is highly imbalanced

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building and Evaluating an Anomaly Detection System
Question Text: How do a few labeled anomaly examples help even though the method is largely unsupervised?
Options (A|B|C|D): They let you judge whether the system catches rare abnormal cases | They fully replace the normal examples in the training set | They are useful only for choosing the number of clusters | They make the per-feature Gaussian assumption unnecessary
Correct Answer: They let you judge whether the system catches rare abnormal cases

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building and Evaluating an Anomaly Detection System
Question Text: What kind of development decisions can a labeled evaluation set support?
Options (A|B|C|D): Dropping the cost function from the procedure entirely | Choosing how many hidden layers a network should have | Recasting the anomaly task as a regression problem | Choosing better features and a better value of epsilon
Correct Answer: Choosing better features and a better value of epsilon

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building and Evaluating an Anomaly Detection System
Question Text: Which belief about evaluating an anomaly detector is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That development genuinely benefits from this feedback | That even a small labeled set is valuable for tuning and assessment | That being unsupervised means labeled examples are never needed | That the rarity of anomalies makes evaluation tricky
Correct Answer: That being unsupervised means labeled examples are never needed

Question Text: How is a cross-validation set typically used in anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): To tune epsilon and features using labeled normals and anomalies | To select the number of clusters K used to partition the data | To estimate only the mean of the Gaussian model fitted to the data | To take the place of the training set when fitting the model
Correct Answer: To tune epsilon and features using labeled normals and anomalies

Question Text: Why is the training set usually mostly normal examples, with anomalies placed in the cross-validation and test sets?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because normal examples provide no useful signal for training | Because the model fits normal behavior while anomalies gauge detection | Because anomalous examples simply cannot be stored in a dataset | Because a cross-validation set never requires any labels
Correct Answer: Because the model fits normal behavior while anomalies gauge detection

Question Text: When there are very few anomalies in total, what practical problem can arise with the data splits?
Options (A|B|C|D): You may lack enough anomalies for a separate test set | The fitted Gaussian model suddenly becomes exact | Cross-validation turns into the same thing as clustering | You are forced to switch over to linear regression
Correct Answer: You may lack enough anomalies for a separate test set

Question Text: If scarce anomaly data forces you to use the cross-validation set as your only evaluation set, what trade-off appears?
Options (A|B|C|D): You can still tune, but lose a fair final test estimate | You no longer need a threshold epsilon at all | You turn anomaly detection into supervised classification | You make overfitting impossible from then on
Correct Answer: You can still tune, but lose a fair final test estimate

Question Text: Which belief about train/CV/test splits in anomaly detection is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That normal examples dominate the available data | That very rare anomalies may force special handling of the splits | That labeled anomalies are especially valuable for evaluation | That balanced-classification splitting always applies with no adaptation
Correct Answer: That balanced-classification splitting always applies with no adaptation

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Precision, Recall, and F1 for Rare Anomalies
Question Text: Why are precision and recall preferred over plain accuracy when evaluating an anomaly detector on a highly imbalanced dataset?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because they let you evaluate the detector without choosing a decision threshold | Because a classifier that labels everything normal can still score very high accuracy | Because these metrics are designed exclusively for regression-style numeric outputs | Because the rarity of anomalies makes nearly all evaluation metrics numerically equal
Correct Answer: Because a classifier that labels everything normal can still score very high accuracy

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Precision, Recall, and F1 for Rare Anomalies
Question Text: Precision answers which question about a model's anomaly predictions?
Options (A|B|C|D): How many input features were used to make each prediction | Of the examples flagged as anomalies, how many truly were anomalies | Of all the true anomalies present, how many did the model catch | What is the average variance of the fitted Gaussian model
Correct Answer: Of the examples flagged as anomalies, how many truly were anomalies

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Precision, Recall, and F1 for Rare Anomalies
Question Text: Recall answers which question about a model's anomaly predictions?
Options (A|B|C|D): Of all the true anomalies present, how many did the model catch | What is the average predicted probability of normal examples | Of the examples labeled normal, how many were correctly labeled | How many cluster centroids the model ended up using
Correct Answer: Of all the true anomalies present, how many did the model catch

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Precision, Recall, and F1 for Rare Anomalies
Question Text: Why is the F1 score a useful single metric when both precision and recall matter?
Options (A|B|C|D): It collapses precision and recall into one balanced number | It reports the mean of the fitted Gaussian distribution directly | It is exactly equal to accuracy on imbalanced datasets | It selects the anomaly threshold epsilon for you automatically
Correct Answer: It collapses precision and recall into one balanced number

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Precision, Recall, and F1 for Rare Anomalies
Question Text: Which belief about evaluating a detector on rare anomalies is a mistake to hold?
Options (A|B|C|D): Precision and recall together give a fuller picture than accuracy | Severe class imbalance can make raw accuracy misleading | A very high overall accuracy guarantees the detector is performing well | The F1 score offers a balanced trade-off between the two metrics
Correct Answer: A very high overall accuracy guarantees the detector is performing well

Question Text: In which situation is anomaly detection typically the better choice over supervised learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): When every output must come from a fixed, known set of classes | When labeled positive examples cover every future failure mode | When positive examples are very few and future anomalies may look novel | When the two classes are present in roughly equal proportions
Correct Answer: When positive examples are very few and future anomalies may look novel

Question Text: Why can anomaly detection outperform supervised learning on fraud or security problems?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because anomaly detection works without using any features | Because new attack patterns can appear that no labeled data captured | Because supervised methods are unable to handle binary labels | Because fraudulent activity always follows a Gaussian distribution
Correct Answer: Because new attack patterns can appear that no labeled data captured

Question Text: Why is supervised learning often the better fit for tasks like spam detection or weather prediction?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because future positive cases tend to resemble patterns already labeled | Because these tasks come with no labeled examples to learn from | Because anomaly detection is incapable of working with text data | Because supervised learning deliberately ignores past examples
Correct Answer: Because future positive cases tend to resemble patterns already labeled

Question Text: What is the key conceptual difference between anomaly detection and supervised learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Anomaly detection inherently requires the use of far fewer input features than any supervised learning method ever does | Supervised learning is really just unsupervised learning supplied with a much larger amount of labeled training data | The two approaches differ only in the way their final prediction results are charted and plotted for a human review | Anomaly detection models normality and flags deviations; supervised learning trains on labeled examples of each class
Correct Answer: Anomaly detection models normality and flags deviations; supervised learning trains on labeled examples of each class

Question Text: Which belief about choosing between anomaly detection and supervised learning is a mistake to hold?
Options (A|B|C|D): Having only a few positive examples by itself always means supervised learning is the right call | Supervised learning assumes that future positive cases will closely resemble the past positive cases | Both the quantity of labeled data and the novelty of future positive cases genuinely matter here | Certain detection tasks are simply a much better structural fit for anomaly detection than for labels
Correct Answer: Having only a few positive examples by itself always means supervised learning is the right call

Question Text: Which situation is the best fit for anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): A task where positive examples are frequent and highly repetitive | A task where abnormal behavior is rare and may appear in new forms | A task that involves predicting continuous house prices from features | A task with a small fixed set of well-labeled outcomes seen repeatedly
Correct Answer: A task where abnormal behavior is rare and may appear in new forms

Question Text: Why are many security applications good candidates for anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because anomaly detection is guaranteed to produce zero false positives | Because attackers may invent brand-new behaviors absent from past data | Because the data in security tasks contains no usable features | Because every security problem is fundamentally a clustering problem
Correct Answer: Because attackers may invent brand-new behaviors absent from past data

Question Text: Why is monitoring machines in a data center a natural fit for anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because data-center machines always form a few obvious clusters | Because only supervised learning can read CPU-load measurements | Because the label of every machine is known perfectly in advance | Because a hacked or faulty machine can behave in unfamiliar ways
Correct Answer: Because a hacked or faulty machine can behave in unfamiliar ways

Question Text: Why does diagnosing a known, well-characterized disease lean toward supervised learning rather than anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because a known, well-characterized disease reliably produces no observable symptoms whatsoever | Because the positive class is a previously seen condition, not an open-ended novel failure mode | Because supervised learning can be fully trained without using any labeled examples of the disease | Because anomaly detection is structurally unable to make any use of the available medical features
Correct Answer: Because the positive class is a previously seen condition, not an open-ended novel failure mode

Question Text: Which belief about when to use anomaly detection is a mistake to hold?
Options (A|B|C|D): The surrounding application context should inform the decision | Some rare-event tasks are still better served by supervised learning | Rarity alone decides it, and whether future positives are novel is irrelevant | Anomaly detection shines when abnormal cases may be new or evolving
Correct Answer: Rarity alone decides it, and whether future positives are novel is irrelevant

Question Text: Why can careful feature engineering greatly improve an anomaly detector?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because an anomaly detector can only ever make use of one single input feature | Because better features can make anomalies stand out more clearly from normal data | Because the features stop mattering entirely once the threshold epsilon has been fixed | Because the Gaussian modeling assumption strictly forbids adding any brand-new features
Correct Answer: Because better features can make anomalies stand out more clearly from normal data

Question Text: What is a good sign that a newly engineered feature is helping an anomaly detector?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total number of labeled examples in the dataset increases | It forces every example into a single shared Gaussian peak | It strips essentially all of the variance out of the data | Normal examples keep a high p(x) while known anomalies get a low p(x)
Correct Answer: Normal examples keep a high p(x) while known anomalies get a low p(x)

Question Text: Why can a ratio of two raw quantities be a useful engineered feature for anomaly detection?
Options (A|B|C|D): Some abnormal cases only stand out once relationships between raw quantities are captured | Taking ratios is fully guaranteed to make any input data perfectly Gaussian in its shape | Ratio features can only ever turn out to be useful inside unsupervised clustering algorithms | Using ratio features removes any further need to ever evaluate the trained anomaly detector
Correct Answer: Some abnormal cases only stand out once relationships between raw quantities are captured

Question Text: In a fraud-detection example, why might adding a user's typing speed as a feature help?
