Options (A|B|C|D): Because it adds a signal that can separate suspicious users from normal ones | Because the typing-speed value itself becomes the anomaly threshold | Because a typing-speed feature proves the user is fraudulent | Because it makes all of the original features unnecessary
Correct Answer: Because it adds a signal that can separate suspicious users from normal ones

Question Text: Which belief about feature engineering for anomaly detection is a mistake to hold?
Options (A|B|C|D): Relationships between different measurements can carry useful signal | Designing extra informative features can materially boost detection | If the first features fail, nothing more can be done to improve the model | Evaluation results can guide which features you choose to add
Correct Answer: If the first features fail, nothing more can be done to improve the model

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Transforming Features to Be More Gaussian
Question Text: A Gaussian anomaly detection model assumes each feature is roughly normally distributed. Why might you transform a feature before feeding it into such a model?
Options (A|B|C|D): To convert the continuous feature into a discrete output class label | To reduce the total number of training examples present in the set | To reshape a skewed feature so it better fits the Gaussian assumption | To decide how many distinct clusters the underlying data actually contains
Correct Answer: To reshape a skewed feature so it better fits the Gaussian assumption

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Transforming Features to Be More Gaussian
Question Text: Which transformation is a common way to make a right-skewed feature x look more Gaussian?
Options (A|B|C|D): Multiplying every value of x by zero | Sorting the examples by x | Taking the logarithm, log(x) | Replacing x with its target label
Correct Answer: Taking the logarithm, log(x)

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Transforming Features to Be More Gaussian
Question Text: When applying a log transform as log(x + c) for a small constant c, why add the constant first?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because some values of x may be zero, and log(0) is undefined | Because adding a constant increases the sample size | Because it guarantees a perfectly Gaussian result | Because the constant removes the need to tune epsilon
Correct Answer: Because some values of x may be zero, and log(0) is undefined

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Transforming Features to Be More Gaussian
Question Text: Besides taking a logarithm, what is another transformation used to reduce skew in a feature?
Options (A|B|C|D): Running K-means on that single feature | Raising the feature to a fractional power, such as x^0.4 | Always replacing the feature with its mean value | Recoding the feature as a categorical class label
Correct Answer: Raising the feature to a fractional power, such as x^0.4

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Transforming Features to Be More Gaussian
Question Text: Which statement about transforming features is a misconception you should avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Histograms are a handy way to inspect a feature's distribution | Better-shaped features can improve anomaly detection | Raw features must always be used exactly as collected | A suitable transformation can make a feature more symmetric
Correct Answer: Raw features must always be used exactly as collected

Question Text: What is the core goal of collaborative filtering?
Options (A|B|C|D): To predict house prices from square footage and location | To partition the users into exactly K fixed, disjoint groups | To flag anomalies hidden within raw transaction log data | To recommend items by learning patterns in user-item ratings
Correct Answer: To recommend items by learning patterns in user-item ratings

Question Text: Why is the approach called collaborative filtering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because recommendations emerge from the collective behavior of many users | Because each candidate item is carefully reviewed and approved by a moderator | Because the users are manually grouped into teams that pick out items together | Because two entirely separate models must actively collaborate to run it
Correct Answer: Because recommendations emerge from the collective behavior of many users

Question Text: What kind of data is central to a collaborative filtering system?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the price and text description of each item | A table of user ratings or preferences for items | A list of preset anomaly-detection thresholds | Only demographic labels attached to each user
Correct Answer: A table of user ratings or preferences for items

Question Text: Why can collaborative filtering recommend a movie that a particular user has not yet rated?
Options (A|B|C|D): It reads and memorizes the full text of each movie's script | It can only ever recommend items that the user has already rated | It disregards all ratings and recommends movies entirely at random | It infers the missing preference from similar users and items
Correct Answer: It infers the missing preference from similar users and items

Question Text: Which statement about collaborative filtering is a misconception you should avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is well suited to recommendation tasks | It learns its patterns from observed ratings across many users | It can estimate ratings that are currently missing | It needs a hand-written rule for every user-item pair
Correct Answer: It needs a hand-written rule for every user-item pair

Question Text: What is a recommender system with per-item features?
Options (A|B|C|D): A system that draws on known item attributes, such as genre, to make recommendations | A step-by-step procedure for selecting the best value of K within the K-means method | A recommender that deliberately ignores every available item attribute when predicting | A clustering algorithm that groups only the users together by their ratings
Correct Answer: A system that draws on known item attributes, such as genre, to make recommendations

Question Text: In a movie recommender, which of these is an example of a per-item feature?
Options (A|B|C|D): The anomaly-detection threshold epsilon | The total number of users in the dataset | How much romance or action the movie contains | Whether the user feels tired today
Correct Answer: How much romance or action the movie contains

Question Text: Why can per-item features help a recommender system?
Options (A|B|C|D): They give structured information about what each item is like | They are useful only for detecting anomalies | They let the system work without any users at all | They make every recommendation identical for all users
Correct Answer: They give structured information about what each item is like

Question Text: Conceptually, how does the model use per-item features to predict a rating?
Options (A|B|C|D): It orders items by title alphabetically and recommends from the top | It groups item feature vectors into K centroids and assigns each a label | It matches a user's learned preferences against each item's features | It predicts each item's feature vector from its listed price alone
Correct Answer: It matches a user's learned preferences against each item's features

Question Text: Which statement about per-item-feature recommenders is a misconception you should avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Different users can hold genuinely different preferences over the same features | Recommendations come only from popularity, not from matching features to tastes | This approach differs from a system built purely on fixed hand-crafted rules | Item attributes can be combined with each user's tastes to personalize results
Correct Answer: Recommendations come only from popularity, not from matching features to tastes

Question Text: In a recommender system, what do a user's parameters represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single fixed class label permanently assigned to that user | Numbers describing the user's preferences over item features | The total count of distinct movies the user has watched so far | The learning rate the optimizer applies while training the model
Correct Answer: Numbers describing the user's preferences over item features

Question Text: In a recommender system, what do an item's features represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only how many ratings the item has received | Numbers describing the attributes of that item | The current value of the cost function | The identity of the user currently logged in
Correct Answer: Numbers describing the attributes of that item

Question Text: Conceptually, how is a predicted rating formed from a user's parameters and an item's features?
Options (A|B|C|D): By treating the anomaly score as the rating label | By assigning the item to its nearest centroid | By taking the largest rating found anywhere in the data | By comparing the user's preferences with the item's features
Correct Answer: By comparing the user's preferences with the item's features

Question Text: Why is representing recommendation through user-preference parameters and item-feature values useful?
Options (A|B|C|D): It works only when ratings are strictly binary, such as like or dislike | It frames recommendation as measuring how well a user's tastes fit an item | It forces every single user to end up preferring the very same set of items | It lets the system make accurate predictions without any observed data at all
Correct Answer: It frames recommendation as measuring how well a user's tastes fit an item

Question Text: In collaborative filtering that does not use hand-crafted item features, what is a key appeal of the approach?
Options (A|B|C|D): It produces recommendations without any optimization step | It learns useful item characteristics directly from ratings data | It works only when the dataset contains no users yet | It needs a manually written attribute vector for every item
Correct Answer: It learns useful item characteristics directly from ratings data

Question Text: Why is learning item features from ratings useful compared with relying only on hand-crafted item features?
Options (A|B|C|D): Learned features cannot generalize beyond the training items | Hand-crafted feature vectors are essentially always optimal | Manual features can miss preference dimensions that data reveals | Learned features eliminate the need for any rating data
Correct Answer: Manual features can miss preference dimensions that data reveals

Question Text: When a collaborative filtering model uses no hand-crafted item features, what is it actually learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): The exact written review text users wrote for each given item | Latent item features and user preferences that explain ratings | Only the single global average rating taken across all the items | One single clustering label assigned to each individual user
Correct Answer: Latent item features and user preferences that explain ratings

Question Text: Why can the features learned by collaborative filtering be more powerful than fixed human-defined descriptors?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are guaranteed to match named genre categories exactly | They let the system recommend without users rating anything | They are tuned to help predict ratings, not bound by human labels | They make every learned dimension fully human-interpretable
Correct Answer: They are tuned to help predict ratings, not bound by human labels

Question Text: Which statement about collaborative filtering that learns features from ratings is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It cannot function unless every item has manual features | Observed ratings can serve as a learning signal for features | Learning features from data is a strength of the method | The algorithm can infer item representations from rating patterns
Correct Answer: It cannot function unless every item has manual features

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Learning Item Features from User Ratings
Question Text: In collaborative filtering, how are item features obtained when they are not provided in advance?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are fixed to one identical value that is shared across all items | They are copied directly from the raw text of each item's own title | They are inferred so that, with user parameters, they explain ratings | They are drawn at random just once and then left permanently unchanged
Correct Answer: They are inferred so that, with user parameters, they explain ratings

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Learning Item Features from User Ratings
Question Text: Why can user ratings reveal hidden item features only indirectly?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because every user is assumed to rate all of the items identically | Because ratings reflect how users respond to shared item properties | Because every rating embeds the item's full plot synopsis verbatim | Because each rating is simply an average of the item's own timestamps
Correct Answer: Because ratings reflect how users respond to shared item properties

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Learning Item Features from User Ratings
Question Text: Which kind of item feature might collaborative filtering learn even though no one labeled it in advance?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of gradient-descent steps taken while training the model | The precise legal copyright registration status of the given movie | A latent trait such as how romantic or action-heavy a movie feels | The strength of the rating user's stored login account password
Correct Answer: A latent trait such as how romantic or action-heavy a movie feels

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Learning Item Features from User Ratings
Question Text: What makes the item features learned by collaborative filtering "latent"?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are produced exclusively by the K-means algorithm | They are by definition impossible to ever interpret | They reside only inside the held-out test set | They are hidden variables inferred from data, not given labels
Correct Answer: They are hidden variables inferred from data, not given labels

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Joint Optimization of User Parameters and Item Features
Question Text: In collaborative filtering, which quantities are optimized jointly?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the displayed titles assigned to items | Only the threshold used for anomaly detection | The cluster count and the centroid locations | User parameters and item features at the same time
Correct Answer: User parameters and item features at the same time

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Joint Optimization of User Parameters and Item Features
Question Text: Why is it important to optimize user parameters and item features jointly?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because users and items are best learned with no shared interaction at all | Because good user preferences depend on good item features and vice versa | Because it lets the model skip defining any cost function whatsoever | Because doing so guarantees exactly correct ratings for every single pair
Correct Answer: Because good user preferences depend on good item features and vice versa

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Joint Optimization of User Parameters and Item Features
Question Text: When user parameters and item features are learned together, what objective is being minimized?
Options (A|B|C|D): The prediction error on the observed ratings | The Gaussian variance estimated for each feature | The total number of items that remain unrated | The hinge loss used to train support vector machines
Correct Answer: The prediction error on the observed ratings

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Joint Optimization of User Parameters and Item Features
Question Text: Conceptually, how should you think about the user parameters and item features that are learned together?
Options (A|B|C|D): As two separate models that never influence one another at all | As fixed constants chosen up front before any rating is observed | As discrete labels handed out by a preliminary clustering step | As cooperating representations that must fit to explain ratings
Correct Answer: As cooperating representations that must fit to explain ratings

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Joint Optimization of User Parameters and Item Features
Question Text: Which statement about jointly optimizing user parameters and item features is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Joint learning is central to how collaborative filtering works | User parameters can be learned correctly while ignoring item features | Both sides are fit together to explain the observed ratings | The optimization couples the user and item quantities together
Correct Answer: User parameters can be learned correctly while ignoring item features

Question Text: How does the task change when a recommender system uses binary labels instead of numeric ratings?
Options (A|B|C|D): It assigns the identical engagement prediction to every single item | It must cluster all of the data before any training can begin | It stops modeling individual users and predicts one global label | It predicts whether a user will engage rather than a 1-to-5 score
Correct Answer: It predicts whether a user will engage rather than a 1-to-5 score

Question Text: Which of the following is an example of a binary label in a recommender system?
Options (A|B|C|D): A movie that received a rating of 4.5 stars | The average variance of the item's features | Whether the user clicked on or purchased the item | The exact running time of a movie in minutes
Correct Answer: Whether the user clicked on or purchased the item

Question Text: Why might binary labels be more natural than star ratings in some recommender applications?
Options (A|B|C|D): Binary labels remove all uncertainty from predictions | Many systems observe click or no-click, not numeric scores | Binary labels are only ever helpful for clustering tasks | Computing star ratings is mathematically impossible
Correct Answer: Many systems observe click or no-click, not numeric scores

Question Text: With binary engagement labels, how does the recommendation goal change?
Options (A|B|C|D): It estimates the likelihood of a positive interaction | It learns nothing beyond each item's popularity | It predicts a continuous value such as a house price | It abandons personalizing results for each user
Correct Answer: It estimates the likelihood of a positive interaction

Question Text: Which statement about binary-label recommender systems is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The learning objective shifts with the type of label used | Some applications naturally use yes-or-no interaction labels | Recommenders must always predict multi-level star ratings | Recommendations can still be personalized per user
Correct Answer: Recommenders must always predict multi-level star ratings

Question Text: Why is logistic-style collaborative filtering well suited to binary labels such as click or no-click?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees a perfectly correct ranking across all the candidate items | It maps each user-item score to a value in (0, 1) read as a probability | It can only ever be trained as a plain real-valued regression model | It discards the learned item feature vectors entirely before scoring
Correct Answer: It maps each user-item score to a value in (0, 1) read as a probability

Question Text: In logistic-style collaborative filtering, what is applied to the raw user-item score to produce the prediction?
Options (A|B|C|D): A square-root normalization step | A k-means clustering assignment | A contour-plot transformation | A logistic sigmoid function
Correct Answer: A logistic sigmoid function

Question Text: When collaborative filtering is adapted to binary labels with a sigmoid, what does the model's output represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The index of the user's assigned centroid | The total number of users in the rating matrix | The estimated chance of positive user engagement | A real-valued price assigned to the movie
Correct Answer: The estimated chance of positive user engagement

Question Text: Why is logistic-style collaborative filtering similar in spirit to logistic regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It passes a score through a sigmoid to model binary outcomes | It works only when the data are linearly separable | It ignores the observed labels during training | It predicts an unlimited number of distinct rating levels
Correct Answer: It passes a score through a sigmoid to model binary outcomes

Question Text: Which statement about logistic-style collaborative filtering is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): A sigmoid is used to produce the binary-style output | It adapts collaborative filtering to handle binary labels | Binary recommendation first requires predicting 1-to-5 star values | It can directly estimate a probability-like chance of interaction
Correct Answer: Binary recommendation first requires predicting 1-to-5 star values

Question Text: What does mean normalization do in a recommender system?
Options (A|B|C|D): It subtracts an item's average rating so the model learns deviations | It rescales all of the input features to unit variance and nothing else | It divides each observed rating by the total number of users seen | It permanently replaces every one of the missing ratings with zero
Correct Answer: It subtracts an item's average rating so the model learns deviations

Question Text: Why does mean normalization help for a new item or user that has little rating data?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes any need to ever collect rating data from new users | It converts the recommendation task into pure anomaly detection | It gives a sensible baseline instead of forcing predictions to zero | It guarantees flawless cold-start recommendations every single time
Correct Answer: It gives a sensible baseline instead of forcing predictions to zero

Question Text: After ratings are mean-normalized, what does the model actually learn to predict?
Options (A|B|C|D): The exact missing ratings with zero uncertainty | The number of clusters present in the user matrix | How each user deviates from an item's average rating | The single most popular movie across all users
Correct Answer: How each user deviates from an item's average rating

Question Text: After training on mean-normalized data, how is a final predicted rating recovered?
Options (A|B|C|D): By multiplying the prediction by a small epsilon | By keeping the item mean and discarding the prediction | By adding the item's mean back to the predicted deviation | By snapping the result to the nearest centroid
Correct Answer: By adding the item's mean back to the predicted deviation

Question Text: Which statement about mean normalization is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It permanently erases the original rating scale rather than just shifting it | It is especially useful in settings where the rating data happen to be sparse | The item mean can always be added back onto a normalized prediction later on | It changes which underlying quantity the model is actually learning to predict
Correct Answer: It permanently erases the original rating scale rather than just shifting it

Question Text: What is the cold-start problem for new users in a recommender system?
Options (A|B|C|D): The Gaussian variance used by the model is too small | A new user lacks enough ratings for the system to personalize | The recommender's servers have gone offline | Every item feature vector has become all zeros
Correct Answer: A new user lacks enough ratings for the system to personalize

Question Text: Why is the cold-start problem difficult in collaborative filtering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the catalog of items is always too small | Because there is little data to infer the user's preferences | Because collaborative filtering cannot make use of ratings | Because every new user is known to like the average item
Correct Answer: Because there is little data to infer the user's preferences

Question Text: Which general strategy can begin to address the cold-start problem for a new user?
Options (A|B|C|D): Asking the new user to rate a few items up front | Pinning the user to one random cluster permanently | Switching the system over to anomaly detection | Excluding the new user from training from now on
Correct Answer: Asking the new user to rate a few items up front

Question Text: When asking a new user to rate a few items to ease cold start, why choose those items carefully?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because informative items reveal a user's preferences faster than random ones | Because mean normalization stops mattering the very moment a new user exists | Because every single item happens to convey exactly the same information overall | Because brand-new users are simply not permitted to rate the most popular items
Correct Answer: Because informative items reveal a user's preferences faster than random ones

Question Text: Which statement about the cold-start problem is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Some initial ratings are usually needed to estimate preferences | Sparse early data makes the new-user case challenging | Collaborative filtering can personalize perfectly with no signals | Thoughtful recommender design can partly mitigate it
Correct Answer: Collaborative filtering can personalize perfectly with no signals

Question Text: What is TensorFlow AutoDiff mainly used for when training a collaborative filtering model?
Options (A|B|C|D): Selecting how many hidden layers the network ought to use overall | Replacing the observed rating data with the learned item features | Clustering the movies into groups before the training run begins | Automatically computing derivatives of the model's cost function
Correct Answer: Automatically computing derivatives of the model's cost function

Question Text: Why is AutoDiff especially helpful for collaborative filtering specifically?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the collaborative filtering model turns out to have no free parameters to fit | Because relying on it altogether removes any need to collect observed rating data | Because its cost function does not match standard Dense layers yet must be optimized | Because it somehow guarantees the resulting recommendations are exactly correct
Correct Answer: Because its cost function does not match standard Dense layers yet must be optimized

Question Text: Which TensorFlow tool provides the automatic differentiation used here?
Options (A|B|C|D): tf.GradientTape | tf.keras.metrics | tf.data.Dataset | tf.Sequential
Correct Answer: tf.GradientTape

Question Text: When using AutoDiff for collaborative filtering, what is the main thing you must supply?
Options (A|B|C|D): A manually built decision tree | Only the final list of recommended movies | Code that computes the cost function J | The exact gradient formulas worked out by hand
Correct Answer: Code that computes the cost function J

Question Text: Which statement about using TensorFlow for collaborative filtering is a misunderstanding to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): TensorFlow only aids standard neural nets and cannot optimize this | Collaborative filtering still involves optimizing parameters | TensorFlow can cut down the calculus you do by hand | AutoDiff can be applied to custom cost functions
Correct Answer: TensorFlow only aids standard neural nets and cannot optimize this

Question Text: What is a maximal margin classifier?
Options (A|B|C|D): A clustering method that assigns each data point to the nearest of K learned centroids | A linear classifier whose boundary maximizes distance to the nearest point of each class | A regressor that simply predicts the mean target value computed over the whole training set | A linear classifier that just minimizes total misclassifications with no regard to margin
Correct Answer: A linear classifier whose boundary maximizes distance to the nearest point of each class

Question Text: Why is maximizing the margin between classes considered desirable?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes the need for any labeled training examples during the fitting step | A wider margin tends to give a more robust boundary on cleanly separable data | It guarantees perfect classification accuracy on any future unseen data points | It always yields a curved, nonlinear decision boundary between the two classes
Correct Answer: A wider margin tends to give a more robust boundary on cleanly separable data

Question Text: Under what conditions is a maximal margin classifier most naturally applicable?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the outputs are continuous real-valued quantities to predict | When the two classes are linearly separable with no errors allowed | When the data must first be grouped into clusters before labeling them | When the training data carry no class labels and must be explored
Correct Answer: When the two classes are linearly separable with no errors allowed

Question Text: What does a maximal margin classifier aim to separate, and how?
Options (A|B|C|D): The support vectors from the points that are not support vectors | The two labeled classes, using the widest possible gap between them | The training examples from the held-out test examples | The input features from the output labels of each example
Correct Answer: The two labeled classes, using the widest possible gap between them

Question Text: Which statement about maximal margin classifiers is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): They depend on labeled training examples to fit the boundary | They place a linear boundary that fully separates the two classes | The width of the margin around the boundary is what they optimize | They are built to fit continuous targets rather than class labels
Correct Answer: They are built to fit continuous targets rather than class labels

Question Text: In an SVM-style classifier, what is the margin?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total count of support vectors used by the model | The length of the feature vector describing each example | The distance from the decision boundary to the closest training points | The average prediction error measured across the training set
Correct Answer: The distance from the decision boundary to the closest training points

Question Text: Why does a larger margin around the decision boundary often help?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees the training error will always be exactly zero | It removes any need to apply a nonlinear kernel function | It pushes the data points into a higher-dimensional space | It makes the classifier less sensitive to small shifts in the data
Correct Answer: It makes the classifier less sensitive to small shifts in the data

Question Text: Which training examples most directly determine the size of the margin?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the examples held out in the test set | Only the points that end up misclassified | The points lying closest to the decision boundary | The points lying farthest from the decision boundary
Correct Answer: The points lying closest to the decision boundary

Question Text: Why is the margin central to the reasoning behind a maximal margin classifier?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the boundary is chosen precisely to maximize it when separating | Because the margin directly stands in for the class labels in training | Because the margin equals the number of distinct classes that are present | Because the margin is numerically identical to the model's accuracy
Correct Answer: Because the boundary is chosen precisely to maximize it when separating

Question Text: Which statement about the margin is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Larger margins can contribute to a more robust boundary | The margin is determined by the points nearest the boundary | The margin is just another name for overall classification accuracy | The margin is a geometric property of the boundary and nearby points
Correct Answer: The margin is just another name for overall classification accuracy

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sensitivity of Maximal Margin Classifiers to Outliers
Question Text: Why can a maximal margin classifier be sensitive to outliers?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it ignores the distances between data points entirely | Because one unusual point can strongly shift the widest boundary | Because outliers are always treated as the centroids of clusters | Because it is trained without using any of the class labels at all
Correct Answer: Because one unusual point can strongly shift the widest boundary

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sensitivity of Maximal Margin Classifiers to Outliers
Question Text: What problem can a maximal margin classifier's sensitivity to outliers cause?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of input features shrinks too far | The data loses all of its class label information | The model stops being a linear classifier | The boundary it learns may generalize poorly to new data
Correct Answer: The boundary it learns may generalize poorly to new data

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sensitivity of Maximal Margin Classifiers to Outliers
Question Text: Why does sensitivity to outliers make the maximal margin classifier a practical limitation?
Options (A|B|C|D): Outliers occur only in regression problems, never in classification | Cross-validation is fundamentally unable to detect the issue | Real datasets are essentially always perfectly separable | Real datasets often hold noisy points that should not dominate the boundary
Correct Answer: Real datasets often hold noisy points that should not dominate the boundary

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sensitivity of Maximal Margin Classifiers to Outliers
Question Text: How does an outlier's strong influence on the boundary motivate the use of soft margins?
Options (A|B|C|D): It shows that maximizing the margin serves no real useful purpose | Permitting a few violations can curb overreaction to odd points | It converts the classifier into a pure anomaly detector model | It removes any need for a kernel function inside the model
Correct Answer: Permitting a few violations can curb overreaction to odd points

Question Text: What is a soft margin?
Options (A|B|C|D): A margin that lets some points fall inside it or be misclassified | A strict rule that every point must be separated perfectly | A margin concept that applies only to regression trees | A distance measured only between cluster centroids
Correct Answer: A margin that lets some points fall inside it or be misclassified

Question Text: Why are soft margins introduced?
Options (A|B|C|D): To avoid having to perform any cross-validation when tuning | To force every single training point to be classified correctly | To make the classifier less sensitive to outliers and overlap | To maximize how many support vectors the trained model uses
Correct Answer: To make the classifier less sensitive to outliers and overlap

Question Text: What is the key trade-off when using a soft margin?
Options (A|B|C|D): It lowers both bias and variance simultaneously on every dataset | It automatically turns the decision boundary into a nonlinear one | It tolerates some training errors in exchange for generalization | It removes every region of class overlap present in the dataset
Correct Answer: It tolerates some training errors in exchange for generalization

Question Text: How is the best amount of softness for a soft margin typically chosen?
Options (A|B|C|D): By always permitting exactly one misclassification | By setting the margin width down to zero | By selecting the largest possible kernel degree | By tuning it with cross-validation
Correct Answer: By tuning it with cross-validation

Question Text: Which statement about soft margins is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Controlled margin violations can improve a model's generalization | Allowing some misclassifications always makes the classifier worse | Soft margins help handle overlapping classes and stray outliers | Cross-validation can be used to tune how soft the margin should be
Correct Answer: Allowing some misclassifications always makes the classifier worse

Question Text: An SVM's soft margin allows some training points to violate the margin or be misclassified. How does this relate to the bias-variance tradeoff?
Options (A|B|C|D): It lowers both bias and variance at the same time with no cost | It raises bias slightly but lowers variance, often improving test accuracy | It affects only training error and is unrelated to generalization | It removes variance entirely since the margin is now fixed
Correct Answer: It raises bias slightly but lowers variance, often improving test accuracy

Question Text: Why might a hard-margin SVM that insists on perfectly separating the training data exhibit high variance?
Options (A|B|C|D): It relies on far too few input features to fit the decision boundary | It systematically underfits and ignores fine structure within the data | It treats the labels as noise and discards most of the training data | It becomes overly sensitive to the exact training points and outliers
Correct Answer: It becomes overly sensitive to the exact training points and outliers

Question Text: Why can a softer (more permissive) SVM margin sometimes generalize better than a strict one?
Options (A|B|C|D): It tolerates some training error to reduce sensitivity to the sample | It restricts the model to using only one single input dimension | It eliminates the need for any support vectors in the final model | It deliberately maximizes the number of training errors that it makes
Correct Answer: It tolerates some training error to reduce sensitivity to the sample

Question Text: What role does cross-validation play when tuning the softness of an SVM's margin?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees that the model will achieve zero bias | It selects the margin softness that best balances bias and variance | It replaces the training set with held-out validation data | It fixes the number of output classes before training begins
Correct Answer: It selects the margin softness that best balances bias and variance

Question Text: Which belief about bias and variance in SVMs is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Some added bias can reduce harmful variance on new data | Generalization can matter more than a perfect training fit | Soft margins are one way to manage the bias-variance tradeoff | The strictest possible separation is always best for future data
Correct Answer: The strictest possible separation is always best for future data

Question Text: What is a support vector classifier?
Options (A|B|C|D): An algorithm that outputs continuous regression values | A centroid-based method for clustering unlabeled data | A soft-margin linear classifier for classification tasks | A method for rescaling features before training
Correct Answer: A soft-margin linear classifier for classification tasks

Question Text: Why is a support vector classifier given that name?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it can only be implemented with vectorized code | Because it is fully determined by points on or inside the margin | Because its outputs are interpreted as support probabilities | Because it converts every training point into a support vector
Correct Answer: Because it is fully determined by points on or inside the margin

Question Text: How does a support vector classifier differ from a maximal margin classifier?
Options (A|B|C|D): It predicts continuous outputs instead of discrete class labels | It permits some margin violations instead of perfect separation | It always maps the data through a nonlinear kernel beforehand | It discards every point that lies far away from the boundary
Correct Answer: It permits some margin violations instead of perfect separation

Question Text: What kind of data is a support vector classifier better equipped to handle than a maximal margin classifier?
Options (A|B|C|D): Data with outliers and overlapping, non-separable classes | Continuous regression targets with no class labels | Datasets where only a few labels are missing | Features that exist only in infinite dimensions
Correct Answer: Data with outliers and overlapping, non-separable classes

Question Text: Which statement about support vector classifiers is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): They can perform well by allowing controlled margin violations | The idea of a margin is still central to how they work | They are classification models rather than regression models | They are only useful when the data is perfectly separable
Correct Answer: They are only useful when the data is perfectly separable

Question Text: In a support vector classifier, what are the support vectors?
Options (A|B|C|D): The training points on or inside the margin that shape the boundary | The hidden units that make up a neural network layer | Every training example that the model classifies correctly | The input features that carry the highest variance
Correct Answer: The training points on or inside the margin that shape the boundary

Question Text: Why are these particular training points called support vectors?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because they are the key points that fix the decision boundary | Because they are stored as vectors inside a tensor math library | Because they have the largest magnitude among all the examples | Because they are used only for regression-style fitting tasks
Correct Answer: Because they are the key points that fix the decision boundary

Question Text: Why do support vectors influence the classifier more than points far from the boundary?
Options (A|B|C|D): Their count determines how many input dimensions are used | Distant points shift the boundary more than nearby points do | The boundary is constrained most directly by nearby margin points | They happen to be the only training examples that carry labels
Correct Answer: The boundary is constrained most directly by nearby margin points

Question Text: In a soft-margin support vector classifier, can a misclassified training point be a support vector?
Options (A|B|C|D): Yes, because points inside the margin still help set the boundary | No, because support vectors must always be classified correctly | Only when the model is solving a regression problem | Only if that point happens to be the mean of its class
Correct Answer: Yes, because points inside the margin still help set the boundary

Question Text: Which belief about support vectors is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every training example influences the classifier equally | Soft-margin violations can also produce support vectors | Points near the boundary matter disproportionately | Support vectors are defined in relation to the margin
Correct Answer: Every training example influences the classifier equally

Question Text: What geometric shape is the decision boundary of a linear support vector classifier in two dimensions?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single point | A binary tree | A line | A flat plane
Correct Answer: A line

Question Text: What geometric shape is the decision boundary of a linear support vector classifier in three dimensions?
Options (A|B|C|D): A round cluster | A straight line | A plane | A single point
Correct Answer: A plane

Question Text: What general term describes a linear decision boundary that separates data in an arbitrary number of dimensions?
Options (A|B|C|D): Centroid | Hyperplane | Kernel surface | Residual map
Correct Answer: Hyperplane

Question Text: Why is the concept of a hyperplane useful when describing linear classifiers?
Options (A|B|C|D): It extends the idea of a linear separator beyond drawable dimensions | It is another name for the model's training loss function | It signals that the classifier's boundary is actually nonlinear | It is a construct that applies only to decision trees
Correct Answer: It extends the idea of a linear separator beyond drawable dimensions

Question Text: Which belief about hyperplanes is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): They only apply in four or more dimensions, not to lines or planes | Lines and planes are simply lower-dimensional hyperplanes | Linear classifiers can be described in terms of hyperplanes | The same geometric idea generalizes across all dimensions
Correct Answer: They only apply in four or more dimensions, not to lines or planes

Question Text: In what situation does a linear classifier perform poorly?
Options (A|B|C|D): When a single straight boundary can't separate the classes | When the input has more than one numeric feature column | When the gap (margin) between the two classes is too wide | When the class labels are binary instead of continuous values
Correct Answer: When a single straight boundary can't separate the classes

Question Text: Which scenario best motivates moving beyond a straight-line separator?
Options (A|B|C|D): A regression task mapping one numeric input to one output | A class sitting in the middle range of a feature, not one side | A dataset that arrives with no class labels attached to it | A dataset whose two classes sit on opposite sides of a single line
Correct Answer: A class sitting in the middle range of a feature, not one side

Question Text: Why does allowing a soft margin (permitting some misclassifications) fail to fix every nonlinear problem?
Options (A|B|C|D): A soft margin automatically bends the boundary into a curve | A soft margin erases all overlap between the two classes | A soft margin only applies to multiclass classification problems | Tolerating some errors keeps the boundary's basic shape
Correct Answer: Tolerating some errors keeps the boundary's basic shape

Question Text: The inability of a straight boundary to separate certain classes motivates which idea?
Options (A|B|C|D): Stripping the class labels off the data before any training | Switching to using decision trees as the one and only alternative | Mapping data to a higher dimension where a line can separate it | Always collapsing the data down to a single chosen feature
Correct Answer: Mapping data to a higher dimension where a line can separate it

Question Text: Which belief about linear classifiers is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The feature representation can affect whether classes are separable | Mapping to higher dimensions can sometimes make classes separable | Some data structures genuinely require a nonlinear approach to separate | Any classification task can be solved just by softening the boundary
Correct Answer: Any classification task can be solved just by softening the boundary

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Core Idea of Support Vector Machines
Question Text: What is the central idea behind a support vector machine?
Options (A|B|C|D): Map data to a higher dimension, then fit a linear separator | Fit a polynomial curve directly to the numeric output labels | Replace each class with its centroid, then classify by distance | Run a decision tree first and classify based on its output
Correct Answer: Map data to a higher dimension, then fit a linear separator

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Core Idea of Support Vector Machines
Question Text: Why can moving the data to a higher-dimensional space help with classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees a wider margin back in the original space | It always reduces the amount of training data that is required | Points inseparable by a line originally may separate there | It automatically detects and removes outlier data points
Correct Answer: Points inseparable by a line originally may separate there

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Core Idea of Support Vector Machines
Question Text: After the data is mapped to a higher-dimensional space, what stays linear?
Options (A|B|C|D): The chosen kernel parameter value | The original feature space, which stays unchanged | The class labels assigned to the points | The classifier built in the transformed space
Correct Answer: The classifier built in the transformed space

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Core Idea of Support Vector Machines
Question Text: How does the SVM approach address cases where a straight-line classifier failed?
Options (A|B|C|D): It shows that linear classifiers were never actually useful at all | It abandons the idea of maximizing the margin entirely | It changes the representation so nonlinear patterns can separate | It recasts the whole classification task as a regression problem
Correct Answer: It changes the representation so nonlinear patterns can separate

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Core Idea of Support Vector Machines
Question Text: Which belief about how SVMs work is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Higher-dimensional representations are central to the intuition | They draw arbitrary curves in the original space with no remapping | Changing the representation can help with nonlinear data | A linear classifier is still what gets found in the transformed space
Correct Answer: They draw arbitrary curves in the original space with no remapping

Question Text: In the context of SVMs, what does mapping data to a higher dimension mean?
Options (A|B|C|D): Deriving new transformed coordinates from the original features | Making several duplicate copies of each example's own class label | Cutting down the number of feature columns that are used | Sorting the training examples into ascending feature order
Correct Answer: Deriving new transformed coordinates from the original features

Question Text: In the drug-dosage example, what extra coordinate is added to make the one-dimensional data two-dimensional?
Options (A|B|C|D): The reciprocal (inverse) of the dosage | The dosage value squared | The class label assigned to each point | The natural logarithm of the dosage
Correct Answer: The dosage value squared

Question Text: Why is adding a transformed coordinate (such as the squared feature) useful?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes any need to keep support vectors at all | It converts the classification task into a clustering task | It can expose a linear separation unavailable before | It guarantees the data will become normally distributed
Correct Answer: It can expose a linear separation unavailable before

Question Text: What is the transformed (higher-dimensional) space meant to achieve?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single representative centroid computed for each class | A space where a linear separator splits the classes | A guaranteed lower compute cost than every other method | A way to skip having to choose any kernel parameters at all
Correct Answer: A space where a linear separator splits the classes

Question Text: Which belief about higher-dimensional mapping is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is only for visualization, not for enabling linear separation | New transformed coordinates are derived from the original inputs | Added features can make separating the classes easier | SVM intuition frequently begins from this mapping idea
Correct Answer: It is only for visualization, not for enabling linear separation

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Finding a Support Vector Classifier in Higher Dimensions
Question Text: Once the data has been mapped to a higher-dimensional space, what does the algorithm search for?
Options (A|B|C|D): A regression tree fit directly to the numeric class labels | A linear separator that splits the transformed data | A separate Gaussian distribution for each class | A single centroid point representing each class
Correct Answer: A linear separator that splits the transformed data

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Finding a Support Vector Classifier in Higher Dimensions
Question Text: Why is finding a linear separator in the transformed space central to SVMs?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it picks the right number of dimensions on its own | Because remapping handles nonlinearity yet the classifier stays linear | Because SVMs avoid relying on any linear boundary whatsoever | Because the transformed data no longer needs to retain any class labels
Correct Answer: Because remapping handles nonlinearity yet the classifier stays linear

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Finding a Support Vector Classifier in Higher Dimensions
Question Text: How is a new, unseen example classified after this kind of transformation?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is assigned to the nearest class centroid in the original space | It is classified only if it exactly matches an existing training point | It is always given the label of the overall training-set mean | It is mapped to the same space, then judged by which side it falls
Correct Answer: It is mapped to the same space, then judged by which side it falls

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Finding a Support Vector Classifier in Higher Dimensions
Question Text: What type of separator does the SVM find in the higher-dimensional space?
Options (A|B|C|D): A linear support vector classifier | A standalone single decision tree | A nearest-neighbor similarity graph | A Gaussian mixture density model
Correct Answer: A linear support vector classifier

Question Text: In a support vector machine, what is a kernel function?
Options (A|B|C|D): A scaler that centers every single input feature to have a zero mean | A rule that counts how many support vectors a trained model uses | A loss term that penalizes each misclassified training example | A function giving point similarity as if mapped to higher dimensions
Correct Answer: A function giving point similarity as if mapped to higher dimensions

Question Text: Why are kernel functions useful in support vector machines?
Options (A|B|C|D): They guarantee that the resulting classifier is always globally optimal | They are useful only for plotting decision boundaries in two dimensions | They make labeled training examples completely unnecessary for fitting | They use high-dimensional relationships without transforming each point
Correct Answer: They use high-dimensional relationships without transforming each point

Question Text: What does a kernel value conceptually encode about two examples?
Options (A|B|C|D): How many of the input features should be kept for the final model | The width of the SVM's margin separating the two classes apart | Their similarity or relationship in the transformed feature space | The exact discrete class label assigned to each of the two examples
Correct Answer: Their similarity or relationship in the transformed feature space

Question Text: Why are kernel functions closely tied to nonlinear classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): They convert a support vector machine into a neural network | They make the original input data perfectly linearly separable | They make it practical to exploit higher-dimensional feature relationships | They restrict the classifier to operate on one-dimensional inputs
Correct Answer: They make it practical to exploit higher-dimensional feature relationships

Question Text: Which statement about SVM kernels is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): A kernel lets the SVM compare points in a higher-dimensional space | A kernel is just preprocessing unrelated to the classifier geometry | A kernel is central to making a nonlinear SVM practical to train | A kernel can avoid explicitly transforming each single input data point
Correct Answer: A kernel is just preprocessing unrelated to the classifier geometry

Question Text: What kind of structure does a polynomial kernel help a support vector machine capture?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only strictly linear relationships present in the original space | Binary class labels and nothing about feature interactions | Relationships from polynomial feature expansions, such as squared terms | Class membership decided by nearest-neighbor voting
Correct Answer: Relationships from polynomial feature expansions, such as squared terms

Question Text: Squaring a single feature (for example, replacing a dosage value with dosage squared) illustrates a polynomial kernel because:
Options (A|B|C|D): Squaring a feature defines a decision-tree branching rule | Squaring a feature draws values from a Gaussian distribution | Squaring a feature is a simple case of a polynomial feature transformation | Squaring a feature measures the variance reduction at a tree split
Correct Answer: Squaring a feature is a simple case of a polynomial feature transformation

Question Text: What is the conceptual role of the polynomial kernel?
Options (A|B|C|D): To remove the need for cross-validation during model training | To select the SVM's support vectors uniformly at random | To let the SVM act as if polynomial features were present | To force the soft-margin penalty all the way down to zero
Correct Answer: To let the SVM act as if polynomial features were present

Question Text: When is a polynomial kernel especially helpful?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the task is regression rather than classification | When curved boundaries or feature interactions help separate classes | When the data has no labels and only clustering is possible | When the classes are already separable by a straight line
Correct Answer: When curved boundaries or feature interactions help separate classes

Question Text: Which statement about polynomial kernels is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are one common type of kernel used by support vector machines | They can implicitly add nonlinear relationships among features | They can help a support vector machine perform nonlinear classification | They merely memorize squared inputs instead of polynomial relations
Correct Answer: They merely memorize squared inputs instead of polynomial relations

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Kernel Degree and Feature Space Complexity
Question Text: What does the degree of a polynomial kernel control?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of support vectors the trained model ends up using | The amount of random Gaussian noise added to each of the inputs | How complex the implied polynomial feature relationships can be | The count of distinct classes that are present in the dataset
Correct Answer: How complex the implied polynomial feature relationships can be

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Kernel Degree and Feature Space Complexity
Question Text: What generally happens as the degree of a polynomial kernel increases?
Options (A|B|C|D): The implied feature space can represent more complex relationships | The classifier collapses to a purely linear decision boundary | The margin between the classes disappears entirely | The training data no longer requires any class labels
Correct Answer: The implied feature space can represent more complex relationships

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Kernel Degree and Feature Space Complexity
Question Text: Why can a very high polynomial-kernel degree be risky?
Options (A|B|C|D): It reliably lowers variance with no accompanying downside | It can make the model too flexible and hurt generalization | It turns the support vector machine into an unsupervised method | It blocks the use of cross-validation for tuning
Correct Answer: It can make the model too flexible and hurt generalization

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Kernel Degree and Feature Space Complexity
Question Text: Why is kernel degree treated as a hyperparameter to choose rather than a fixed value?
Options (A|B|C|D): The degree must always equal the number of input features | Degree only ever matters when fitting regression trees | The data labels set the degree automatically during training | Different tasks may need different levels of nonlinear complexity
Correct Answer: Different tasks may need different levels of nonlinear complexity

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Kernel Degree and Feature Space Complexity
Question Text: Which statement about kernel degree is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): A larger degree is always better because it is more expressive | Choosing a good degree usually requires some tuning | Degree influences how rich the implied feature space is | More complexity can either help or hurt generalization
Correct Answer: A larger degree is always better because it is more expressive

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Using Cross-Validation to Tune Kernel Parameters
Question Text: Why is cross-validation used with kernelized support vector machines?
Options (A|B|C|D): To pick margin softness and kernel parameters by generalization | To infer the missing class labels for the training set examples | To replace the model's chosen support vectors with brand-new ones | To carry out the kernel-trick computation itself automatically
Correct Answer: To pick margin softness and kernel parameters by generalization

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Using Cross-Validation to Tune Kernel Parameters
Question Text: Which parameters of an SVM typically need tuning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Soft-margin strength, polynomial degree, and RBF gamma | The Gaussian means used for anomaly detection | The number of hidden layers in the network | The values of the input class labels themselves
Correct Answer: Soft-margin strength, polynomial degree, and RBF gamma

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Using Cross-Validation to Tune Kernel Parameters
Question Text: Why tune these SVM parameters instead of leaving them at arbitrary defaults?
Options (A|B|C|D): Support vector machines actually possess no adjustable parameters to set | They strongly shape the classifier's flexibility and generalization power | Cross-validation always forces the fitted SVM decision rule to be linear | Parameter tuning here is relevant only to unsupervised clustering methods
Correct Answer: They strongly shape the classifier's flexibility and generalization power

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Using Cross-Validation to Tune Kernel Parameters
Question Text: What practical outcome does cross-validation support when tuning an SVM?
Options (A|B|C|D): Picking settings that perform best on held-out data, not just training data | Guaranteeing the trained model reaches exactly zero error on the held-out set | Removing any need at all to use a kernel function during the model fitting | Assigning the correct class labels to all of the examples fully automatically
Correct Answer: Picking settings that perform best on held-out data, not just training data

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Using Cross-Validation to Tune Kernel Parameters
Question Text: Which statement about SVM hyperparameters is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Generalization to new data is the goal of tuning | Kernels and margins involve trade-offs that can be tuned | There is one best setting that holds regardless of the dataset | Held-out performance should guide which settings are chosen
Correct Answer: There is one best setting that holds regardless of the dataset

Question Text: The RBF kernel used in support vector machines is most commonly known by which other name?
Options (A|B|C|D): The sigmoid kernel | The Gaussian kernel | The polynomial kernel | The linear kernel
Correct Answer: The Gaussian kernel

Question Text: What makes the RBF kernel useful for classification tasks?
Options (A|B|C|D): It models nonlinear relationships using distance-based similarity | It needs infinite-dimensional coordinates listed explicitly | It removes the need for any support vectors at all | It can only be applied to regression problems
Correct Answer: It models nonlinear relationships using distance-based similarity

Question Text: In terms of the implicit feature space it represents, how is the RBF kernel best characterized?
Options (A|B|C|D): It corresponds exactly to the axis-aligned splits of a decision tree | It corresponds to appending one single new feature dimension only | It corresponds to a fixed low-dimensional linear projection of inputs | It corresponds to an effectively infinite-dimensional feature space
Correct Answer: It corresponds to an effectively infinite-dimensional feature space

Question Text: Why can an RBF-kernel SVM separate overlapping nonlinear class patterns better than a single straight-line decision boundary?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes every class perfectly separable in the original space | It weighs nearby observations more strongly when classifying a point | It always predicts whichever class is in the majority | It discards distance information between the data points
Correct Answer: It weighs nearby observations more strongly when classifying a point

Question Text: Which statement about the RBF kernel is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It relates to infinite-dimensional feature spaces | It is a widely used kernel choice in SVMs | It is simply another name for the linear kernel | It captures nonlinear similarity based on distance
Correct Answer: It is simply another name for the linear kernel

Topic (Sub-Lesson): RBF Kernel as Weighted Nearest Neighbors
Question Text: Why does classifying a new point with an RBF kernel behave somewhat like a weighted nearest-neighbors method?
Options (A|B|C|D): Nearby training examples exert more influence than distant ones | Only the single closest example is ever consulted | No distance information enters the calculation at all | All training points contribute exactly equal influence
Correct Answer: Nearby training examples exert more influence than distant ones

Topic (Sub-Lesson): RBF Kernel as Weighted Nearest Neighbors
Question Text: In the analogy that an RBF-kernel SVM acts like weighted nearest neighbors, what exactly is being weighted?
Options (A|B|C|D): The influence of each example, higher when it is closer | The class labels, which are used directly as the weights | The examples equally, so each one gets identical weight | Only support vectors, given weight one and others zero
Correct Answer: The influence of each example, higher when it is closer

Topic (Sub-Lesson): RBF Kernel as Weighted Nearest Neighbors
Question Text: Why is calling an RBF-kernel SVM a weighted nearest-neighbors method only an intuition rather than an exact description?
Options (A|B|C|D): The RBF kernel can only ever be applied to strictly one-dimensional input data sets | The RBF kernel completely discards all neighboring points when forming any prediction | It is still an SVM using kernelized inner products, not a literal neighbor algorithm | Weighted nearest neighbors and kernel SVMs are provably identical learning algorithms
Correct Answer: It is still an SVM using kernelized inner products, not a literal neighbor algorithm

Topic (Sub-Lesson): RBF Kernel as Weighted Nearest Neighbors
Question Text: How does the weighted nearest-neighbors analogy help explain how an RBF-kernel SVM classifies points?
Options (A|B|C|D): It shows why local neighborhoods strongly shape nonlinear decisions | It proves the classifier is linear in the original feature space | It removes any need to tune the gamma hyperparameter | It explains how pruning reduces the depth of a tree
Correct Answer: It shows why local neighborhoods strongly shape nonlinear decisions

Topic (Sub-Lesson): RBF Kernel as Weighted Nearest Neighbors
Question Text: Which belief about comparing an RBF-kernel SVM to weighted nearest neighbors is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That closer points exert stronger influence on the result | That locality of the data points matters for the result | That the underlying method actually remains an SVM | That an RBF-kernel SVM is literally standard nearest neighbors
Correct Answer: That an RBF-kernel SVM is literally standard nearest neighbors

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gamma and Local Influence in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: What does the gamma hyperparameter control in the RBF kernel?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of distinct classes being predicted | The mean of a Gaussian used in anomaly detection | The maximum depth allowed for a decision tree | How quickly similarity falls off with distance
Correct Answer: How quickly similarity falls off with distance

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gamma and Local Influence in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: What is the effect of increasing the value of gamma in the RBF kernel?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of support vectors becomes fixed in advance | The decision boundary automatically becomes linear | Influence becomes more local and falls off faster with distance | Influence becomes completely uniform across all points
Correct Answer: Influence becomes more local and falls off faster with distance

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gamma and Local Influence in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: What is the effect of decreasing the value of gamma in the RBF kernel?
Options (A|B|C|D): The classifier ends up ignoring all but one single training point | The margin of the resulting classifier is forced down toward zero | Each point's influence grows broader, reaching more distant points | The kernel effectively converts into a fixed-degree polynomial one
Correct Answer: Each point's influence grows broader, reaching more distant points

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gamma and Local Influence in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: Why is gamma considered an important hyperparameter to tune in an RBF-kernel SVM?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes any need to perform cross-validation when fitting the model | It is fixed directly by the class labels attached to the training data | It strongly affects how flexible and local the decision boundary becomes | It is relevant only for regression problems and never for classification
Correct Answer: It strongly affects how flexible and local the decision boundary becomes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Gamma and Local Influence in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: Which belief about the gamma hyperparameter is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That gamma controls sensitivity to distance between points | That gamma generally needs to be tuned for good results | That too much locality from high gamma can hurt generalization | That a higher gamma is always better since it focuses on nearby points
Correct Answer: That a higher gamma is always better since it focuses on nearby points

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Distance-Based Similarity in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: Which quantity is central to how the RBF kernel measures similarity between two examples?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of distinct classes | The distance between the two points | The variance of the class labels | The depth of the decision tree
Correct Answer: The distance between the two points

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Distance-Based Similarity in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: Under the RBF kernel, how does the distance between two points affect their measured similarity?
Options (A|B|C|D): Closer points are treated as more similar than farther ones | Farther points are always treated as the more similar ones | Similarity is set only by whether labels match | Distance is ignored once the points are transformed
Correct Answer: Closer points are treated as more similar than farther ones

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Distance-Based Similarity in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: Why does squared distance, ‖x − x'‖², appear in the formula for the RBF kernel?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is mathematically equivalent to the entropy of the data's class labels | It counts how many support vectors the trained model ends up retaining | It is merely a loss function that is reserved only for regression problems | It is how the kernel turns point-to-point distance into a similarity score
Correct Answer: It is how the kernel turns point-to-point distance into a similarity score

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Distance-Based Similarity in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: Why is distance-based similarity helpful for nonlinear classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): It forces every learned class boundary to remain a single perfectly straight line | It functions correctly only when the given input data is strictly low-dimensional | It lets the model operate without ever using any of the data's actual class labels | Local neighborhoods can reveal structure that a single global straight line misses
Correct Answer: Local neighborhoods can reveal structure that a single global straight line misses

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Distance-Based Similarity in the RBF Kernel
Question Text: Which belief about similarity in the RBF kernel is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That nearer points count more than far-away points | That spatial closeness is central to how the kernel behaves | That this similarity helps capture nonlinear patterns | That similarity has nothing to do with geometry between examples
Correct Answer: That similarity has nothing to do with geometry between examples

Question Text: What does the kernel trick allow a support vector machine to do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Hand-select the optimal full set of support vectors before any fitting even begins | Compute inner products in a high-dimensional space without mapping the data there | Standardize and rescale all of the raw input features prior to training the model | Convert a binary classifier into a continuous-valued regression model automatically
Correct Answer: Compute inner products in a high-dimensional space without mapping the data there

Question Text: Why is the kernel trick valuable in practice?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees a fully interpretable, human-readable final decision boundary | It removes any need to choose or tune the model's hyperparameters at all | It applies only to ordinary least-squares linear regression style problems | It cuts computation while still enabling rich high-dimensional comparisons
Correct Answer: It cuts computation while still enabling rich high-dimensional comparisons

Question Text: What does the kernel trick let an SVM avoid doing explicitly?
Options (A|B|C|D): Running any form of cross-validation to pick parameters from the given data | Measuring the similarity or distance between individual pairs of data points | Making any use of the available class labels during the training procedure | Constructing all the transformed coordinates in the higher-dimensional space
Correct Answer: Constructing all the transformed coordinates in the higher-dimensional space

Question Text: Why is the kernel trick especially important when using a kernel such as the RBF kernel?
Options (A|B|C|D): The RBF kernel relies on no notion of distance between any points whatsoever | Using the RBF kernel removes the need to retain any support vectors at all | Its implied feature space can be extremely large or even infinite-dimensional | The RBF kernel always induces a strictly linear, flat, global decision boundary
Correct Answer: Its implied feature space can be extremely large or even infinite-dimensional

Question Text: Which belief about the kernel trick is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That a kernel must physically expand each example into explicit coordinates | That a kernel makes high-dimensional comparisons computationally feasible | That a kernel evaluates a similarity function between given pairs of points | That a kernel implies some underlying transformed higher-dimensional space
Correct Answer: That a kernel must physically expand each example into explicit coordinates

Question Text: What does it mean for an SVM to capture high-dimensional relationships without explicit transformation?
Options (A|B|C|D): It deliberately conceals all the class labels from the underlying learning algorithm | It uses the effect of a high-dimensional feature map without listing those features | It permanently discards all of its support vectors from the final trained SVM model | It performs no transformation of the input data in any conceptual sense whatsoever
Correct Answer: It uses the effect of a high-dimensional feature map without listing those features

Question Text: Why is capturing high-dimensional relationships without explicit transformation a big deal computationally?
Options (A|B|C|D): It gives SVMs rich nonlinear structure without paying the full cost of expansion | It is relevant only to decision and regression trees, and never to any kind of SVM | It removes any need to tune, fit, or estimate the model's parameters at all here | It makes every classifier run in constant time no matter how large the data gets
Correct Answer: It gives SVMs rich nonlinear structure without paying the full cost of expansion

Question Text: How does the idea of high-dimensional relationships without explicit transformation connect to kernels?
Options (A|B|C|D): Kernels remove the need to compare any pairs of points by their mutual similarity | Kernels are entirely unrelated to any form of higher-dimensional reasoning at all | Kernels serve purely as convenient tools for plotting and visualizing the raw data | Kernel functions are the mechanism computing those transformed-space relationships
Correct Answer: Kernel functions are the mechanism computing those transformed-space relationships

Question Text: What kind of feature spaces can the idea of implicit high-dimensional mapping support?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only those spaces formed by appending one single brand-new feature dimension | Very high-dimensional and even effectively infinite-dimensional feature spaces | Only flat two-dimensional spaces that are described by exactly two input features | Only discrete spaces that carry no underlying geometric or metric structure at all
Correct Answer: Very high-dimensional and even effectively infinite-dimensional feature spaces

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Kernels Make SVMs Computationally Feasible
Question Text: Why do kernels make SVMs computationally feasible?
Options (A|B|C|D): They completely remove the optimization step from the SVM training process altogether | They force every single training point in the entire data set to become a support vector | They reduce the fully fitted SVM down to a simple K-means style clustering routine | They skip costly explicit transformations yet still allow high-dimensional comparisons
Correct Answer: They skip costly explicit transformations yet still allow high-dimensional comparisons

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Kernels Make SVMs Computationally Feasible
Question Text: What computational burden do kernels help an SVM avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Directly constructing and operating on the huge transformed feature spaces | Applying any soft margin in order to tolerate a few misclassified points | Computing any measure of similarity or distance between pairs of examples | Learning at all from data that happens to carry genuine, informative labels
Correct Answer: Directly constructing and operating on the huge transformed feature spaces

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Kernels Make SVMs Computationally Feasible
Question Text: Why is avoiding explicit transformation especially important for nonlinear kernels?
Options (A|B|C|D): Nonlinear kernels never rely on any underlying notion of point-to-point similarity | Nonlinear kernels can somehow be trained without any input data being present | Their transformed feature spaces can be very large or even infinite-dimensional | Nonlinear kernels are reliably always cheaper to compute than linear ones are
Correct Answer: Their transformed feature spaces can be very large or even infinite-dimensional

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Kernels Make SVMs Computationally Feasible
Question Text: What practical outcome follows from kernels making SVMs computationally feasible?
Options (A|B|C|D): Choosing hyperparameters becomes wholly unnecessary | Powerful nonlinear SVMs become usable on real-world problems | SVMs turn into an unsupervised clustering method | The support vectors stop influencing the boundary
Correct Answer: Powerful nonlinear SVMs become usable on real-world problems

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Why Kernels Make SVMs Computationally Feasible
Question Text: Which belief about kernelized SVMs is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That kernels make rich transformed-space reasoning tractable to compute | That computational feasibility is a genuine part of their broad appeal | That their nonlinear power comes free without computational insight | That an explicit feature expansion could otherwise be far too expensive
Correct Answer: That their nonlinear power comes free without computational insight

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Polynomial Kernels Implicitly Create New Features
Question Text: How does a polynomial kernel create new features implicitly?
