Options (A|B|C|D): It strips out every interaction term that exists between input features | It copies the original feature vector straight through, fully unchanged | It simply appends one shared constant bias term to each input vector | It acts as if polynomial combinations of features were added, unlisted
Correct Answer: It acts as if polynomial combinations of features were added, unlisted

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Polynomial Kernels Implicitly Create New Features
Question Text: What kind of new features does a polynomial kernel effectively imply?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the single binary class label that is assigned to each example | Only the plain arithmetic mean computed across all the input features | Only the Euclidean distance from each data point to a fixed centroid | Squared terms and pairwise interaction products of input features
Correct Answer: Squared terms and pairwise interaction products of input features

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Polynomial Kernels Implicitly Create New Features
Question Text: Why are the extra features implied by a polynomial kernel useful?
Options (A|B|C|D): They guarantee that the model reaches exactly zero training error | They function correctly only when given one-dimensional input data | They render the use of any support vectors entirely unnecessary here | They can render previously inseparable patterns linearly separable
Correct Answer: They can render previously inseparable patterns linearly separable

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Polynomial Kernels Implicitly Create New Features
Question Text: Why are the polynomial features said to be created implicitly rather than explicitly?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are produced by backpropagation inside a neural network layer | The kernel computes their effect without building the expanded vector | They exist solely within the class labels of the training data points | They are deliberately hidden from both the end user and the model
Correct Answer: The kernel computes their effect without building the expanded vector

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How RBF Kernels Correspond to Infinite-Dimensional Feature Spaces
Question Text: Which striking property characterizes the RBF kernel?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always operates within exactly three fixed feature dimensions only | It captures relationships as if points lived in infinite dimensions | It is mathematically identical to a plain degree-two polynomial kernel | It can only assign a single class label per run of the full algorithm
Correct Answer: It captures relationships as if points lived in infinite dimensions

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How RBF Kernels Correspond to Infinite-Dimensional Feature Spaces
Question Text: Why is the RBF kernel's infinite-dimensional feature space not computationally hopeless?
Options (A|B|C|D): The SVM secretly collapses the whole problem down to a single fixed dimension | The kernel trick yields needed relationships without infinite coordinates | The infinite dimensions are simply ignored entirely during the model training | The RBF kernel requires no numerical optimization at all in order to be fitted
Correct Answer: The kernel trick yields needed relationships without infinite coordinates

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How RBF Kernels Correspond to Infinite-Dimensional Feature Spaces
Question Text: What does the infinite-dimensional view of the RBF kernel help explain?
Options (A|B|C|D): Why the kernel classifier effectively reduces to a simple decision tree | Why the gamma hyperparameter is always pinned to a value of exactly zero | Why the RBF kernel can model very flexible nonlinear decision boundaries | Why the training labels become entirely unnecessary for fitting the model
Correct Answer: Why the RBF kernel can model very flexible nonlinear decision boundaries

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How RBF Kernels Correspond to Infinite-Dimensional Feature Spaces
Question Text: What is the relationship between the RBF kernel and explicit features?
Options (A|B|C|D): It requires you to manually construct each of infinitely many features | It somehow manages to operate without using any feature representation | It captures the effect of infinitely many features without writing them | It draws on only one single raw input feature at any given moment in time
Correct Answer: It captures the effect of infinitely many features without writing them

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Support Vector Machines for Nonlinear Classification
Question Text: Why are support vector machines effective for nonlinear classification problems?
Options (A|B|C|D): They pair margin-based separation with kernels capturing nonlinearity | They require the classes to be linearly separable in raw input space | They replace the need for any input features entirely during fitting | They are unsupervised and so need no labeled training data whatsoever
Correct Answer: They pair margin-based separation with kernels capturing nonlinearity

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Support Vector Machines for Nonlinear Classification
Question Text: What specific mechanism lets a support vector machine form nonlinear class boundaries?
Options (A|B|C|D): Restricting the model to hard margins only | Using kernels such as polynomial or RBF kernels | Predicting the mean feature vector of each class | Discarding the support vectors after training
Correct Answer: Using kernels such as polynomial or RBF kernels

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Support Vector Machines for Nonlinear Classification
Question Text: How should you interpret the boundary learned by a kernel SVM?
Options (A|B|C|D): As a plain regression tree that is fitted with added margin constraints | As linear in an implicit transformed space but nonlinear in input space | As a Gaussian density model chiefly used for unsupervised anomaly detection | As a simple nearest-centroid rule applied within the original input space
Correct Answer: As linear in an implicit transformed space but nonlinear in input space

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Support Vector Machines for Nonlinear Classification
Question Text: Why is a kernel SVM often preferable to forcing a straight boundary in the original feature space?
Options (A|B|C|D): It can separate patterns that no single straight line fits well | It guarantees a fully transparent and interpretable final model | It always ends up relying on strictly fewer support vectors | It removes any need to perform cross-validation when tuning
Correct Answer: It can separate patterns that no single straight line fits well

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Support Vector Machines for Nonlinear Classification
Question Text: Which statement about nonlinear SVMs is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Kernels are genuinely central to how these models capture nonlinear data | They are particularly well suited to data exhibiting nonlinear patterns | Linear separation within the transformed space is the guiding intuition | They discard the margin-based linear classifier instead of extending it
Correct Answer: They discard the margin-based linear classifier instead of extending it

Question Text: What best describes a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): A margin-based classifier that fundamentally relies on a fixed kernel function | A clustering algorithm that assigns each given data point to some centroid | A model making a sequence of feature-based decisions to reach a prediction | A model that simply averages together all of the available input features
Correct Answer: A model making a sequence of feature-based decisions to reach a prediction

Question Text: What are the terminal prediction nodes at the bottom of a decision tree called?
Options (A|B|C|D): Support vectors | Hyperplanes | Leaf nodes | Hidden units
Correct Answer: Leaf nodes

Question Text: What is the single topmost node of a decision tree called?
Options (A|B|C|D): The support node | The loss node | The root node | The margin node
Correct Answer: The root node

Question Text: What makes decision trees conceptually appealing?
Options (A|B|C|D): They need absolutely no training data whatsoever in order to make predictions | They can be followed directly as a clear series of if-then style decisions | They are mathematically guaranteed to be the single most accurate model around | They are narrowly applicable only to raw image inputs and nothing else at all
Correct Answer: They can be followed directly as a clear series of if-then style decisions

Question Text: Which statement about decision trees is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are usable only for regression, not for classification | They handle classification by branching on feature values | They have both a root node and leaf nodes | They make predictions by traversing a path from root to leaf
Correct Answer: They are usable only for regression, not for classification

Question Text: What is the main difference between a classification tree and a regression tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): A classification tree predicts categories while a regression tree predicts numbers | A classification tree uses input features whereas a regression tree does not use any | A classification tree contains leaf nodes whereas a regression tree has none at all | A classification tree is fully supervised whereas a regression tree is unsupervised
Correct Answer: A classification tree predicts categories while a regression tree predicts numbers

Question Text: What does a classification tree typically output at a leaf?
Options (A|B|C|D): A Gaussian probability density value | A discrete class such as cat or not cat | A continuous average of the target values | A kernel-based similarity score
Correct Answer: A discrete class such as cat or not cat

Question Text: What does a regression tree typically output at a leaf?
Options (A|B|C|D): A support vector that defines the splitting boundary | A numeric prediction such as an average target value | A single discrete class label drawn from a fixed set | A learned coefficient used for rescaling each feature
Correct Answer: A numeric prediction such as an average target value

Question Text: Why are both classification trees and regression trees called decision trees despite differing outputs?
Options (A|B|C|D): Both split the data recursively using feature-based decision rules | Both are strictly required to use Gini impurity to choose their splits | Both fundamentally depend on an underlying kernel function to operate | Both can only ever predict simple binary outputs at their leaf nodes
Correct Answer: Both split the data recursively using feature-based decision rules

Question Text: Which statement about classification and regression trees is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Classification trees are the variant that predicts categorical labels | They differ in every mechanism rather than sharing branching structure | Both of them ultimately make their splits based on raw feature values | Regression trees are the variant that predicts continuous numeric values
Correct Answer: They differ in every mechanism rather than sharing branching structure

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Root Nodes, Internal Nodes, and Leaf Nodes
Question Text: What is the single topmost node in a decision tree called?
Options (A|B|C|D): The support node | The margin node | The entropy node | The root node
Correct Answer: The root node

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Root Nodes, Internal Nodes, and Leaf Nodes
Question Text: What is the function of an internal node in a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It tests a feature and routes the example down one chosen branch | It stores only the final prediction value for that whole branch | It holds the loss value that is computed and used during training | It averages together all of the input features at that tree depth
Correct Answer: It tests a feature and routes the example down one chosen branch

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Root Nodes, Internal Nodes, and Leaf Nodes
Question Text: What is the role of a leaf node in a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It produces the final prediction | It rescales the incoming feature values | It computes information gain for other nodes | It poses another feature-split question
Correct Answer: It produces the final prediction

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Root Nodes, Internal Nodes, and Leaf Nodes
Question Text: Why are leaf nodes considered terminal nodes?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because they never make use of any features | Because no further splits occur below them | Because they always contain perfectly pure labels | Because they are always removed during pruning
Correct Answer: Because no further splits occur below them

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Root Nodes, Internal Nodes, and Leaf Nodes
Question Text: Which statement about decision tree node terminology is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Internal nodes split examples using feature tests | All nodes serve one identical role, not distinct ones | The root node always sits at the very top of the tree | Leaf nodes generate the tree's final predictions
Correct Answer: All nodes serve one identical role, not distinct ones

Question Text: How does a trained classification tree produce a prediction for a single new example?
Options (A|B|C|D): It runs gradient descent on the example at test time | It averages the labels of all examples in the training set | It assigns the class of the nearest training centroid | It follows split decisions from the root down to a leaf
Correct Answer: It follows split decisions from the root down to a leaf

Question Text: At an internal node of a classification tree, what decides whether an example is sent to the left or right child?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model's overall accuracy on the training set | The order in which the examples were loaded | The outcome of that node's test on a feature value | The total number of distinct classes present
Correct Answer: The outcome of that node's test on a feature value

Question Text: Why are classification trees often described as interpretable models?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are structurally incapable of overfitting data | They are guaranteed to reach the highest accuracy | Each prediction traces a readable path of decisions | They make every prediction using a single feature
Correct Answer: Each prediction traces a readable path of decisions

Question Text: What does a classification tree do once an example arrives at a leaf node?
Options (A|B|C|D): It returns the class label stored at that leaf | It grows an extra branch beneath that leaf | It restarts the traversal from the root node | It re-normalizes the feature values once more
Correct Answer: It returns the class label stored at that leaf

Question Text: Which statement about how a classification tree decides is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): A prediction is computed as one global formula over the inputs | Reaching a leaf yields the class label stored at that leaf | A prediction is a sequence of local yes-or-no test outcomes | Each internal node applies a test to a single feature value
Correct Answer: A prediction is computed as one global formula over the inputs

Question Text: What does Gini impurity measure for a node in a classification tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): How mixed the class labels are among that node's examples | How many branches descend from that node in the tree | The total count of examples present in the whole dataset | The average value of a feature across that node's examples
Correct Answer: How mixed the class labels are among that node's examples

Question Text: Under what condition is a node's Gini impurity at its lowest value?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the split at the node uses a numeric threshold | When the node holds as many examples as possible | When all of the node's examples share one single class | When the tree containing the node has many levels
Correct Answer: When all of the node's examples share one single class

Question Text: Why does a node with a mix of class labels count as more impure?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is structurally unable to be split any further | It necessarily holds fewer examples than a pure node | Its class membership is a less certain blend of labels | Its feature values for those examples are missing
Correct Answer: Its class membership is a less certain blend of labels

Question Text: How is Gini impurity used while a classification tree is being built?
Options (A|B|C|D): To set the learning rate used for updating the model | To score how well a candidate split yields purer children | To directly output the predicted class at each leaf node | To overwrite the training labels during the fitting step
Correct Answer: To score how well a candidate split yields purer children

Question Text: Which belief about Gini impurity is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Candidate splits are scored using the impurity of children | It directly reports a node's prediction accuracy, not label mix | A node mixing several classes carries higher impurity | A node of a single class has impurity at its minimum value
Correct Answer: It directly reports a node's prediction accuracy, not label mix

Question Text: Why is the impurity after a split computed as a weighted average over the child nodes rather than a plain average?
Options (A|B|C|D): Weighted averaging is only ever meaningful for regression trees | Weighting the children removes any need for the class labels | Every child must contribute equally regardless of how many it holds | The children may hold different counts and should count proportionally
Correct Answer: The children may hold different counts and should count proportionally

Question Text: Under weighted Gini impurity, what makes a candidate split attractive?
Options (A|B|C|D): It produces the largest possible number of child branches | It increases the overall depth of the resulting tree most | Its children are fairly pure once weighted by their sizes | It always splits on the feature having the most categories
Correct Answer: Its children are fairly pure once weighted by their sizes

Question Text: Why is it not enough to judge a split by the purity of just one of its child nodes?
Options (A|B|C|D): The impurity of the parent node never changes after a split | One of the two children produced by a split is always empty | A split's quality depends on both children and their sizes | Only the right child of a split affects the prediction made
Correct Answer: A split's quality depends on both children and their sizes

Question Text: A split yields one tiny but perfectly pure child and one large impure child. What does size weighting help prevent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Overrating the split just for its tiny pure branch | The use of continuous features when splitting | The later pruning of branches from the tree | The creation of any leaf nodes within the tree
Correct Answer: Overrating the split just for its tiny pure branch

Question Text: Which statement about evaluating a split is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): A split can be scored well while ignoring its child node sizes | Split quality is judged by comparing it against alternatives | Weighted impurity reflects both child purity and child sizes | Both child nodes of a split bear on that split's quality
Correct Answer: A split can be scored well while ignoring its child node sizes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Classification Tree
Question Text: How does a classification tree pick the best split to use at a given node?
Options (A|B|C|D): It maximizes the count of leaf nodes created right away | It always uses the feature listed first in the dataset | It takes the split that reduces impurity most among candidates | It picks the feature holding the largest raw numeric value
Correct Answer: It takes the split that reduces impurity most among candidates

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Classification Tree
Question Text: When candidate splits at a node are compared, what quantity is actually being weighed?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the finished tree's accuracy on the training set | The number of missing values present in the dataset | How pure the resulting children are under each split | Only the impurity of the parent node before splitting
Correct Answer: How pure the resulting children are under each split

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Classification Tree
Question Text: Why is choosing a split considered a local decision during tree building?
Options (A|B|C|D): Local choices are made only when building regression trees | Each node weighs only the data subset that reached it | The whole dataset is disregarded after the root split | The tree is built all at once from the leaves upward
Correct Answer: Each node weighs only the data subset that reached it

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Classification Tree
Question Text: What broader benefit can a strong split near the top of a tree provide?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees the finished tree will never overfit the data | It removes any need to prune the tree after it is built | It makes every leaf in the tree perfectly pure on its own | It separates the data into subsets that ease later splits
Correct Answer: It separates the data into subsets that ease later splits

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Classification Tree
Question Text: Which belief about choosing the best split is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The data subset reaching the current node drives the choice | The best split depends on the data, so it varies by node | Several candidate features are compared before one is chosen | The best split is fixed by feature name or order, not impurity
Correct Answer: The best split is fixed by feature name or order, not impurity

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handling Numeric Features in Decision Trees
Question Text: How can a decision tree split on a continuous-valued (numeric) feature?
Options (A|B|C|D): Require a separate kernel function for each feature | Treat numeric features as unusable for any splitting | Choose a threshold and route examples left or right | Convert each numeric value into a class label first
Correct Answer: Choose a threshold and route examples left or right

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handling Numeric Features in Decision Trees
Question Text: What form does a split on a numeric feature x typically take at a node?
Options (A|B|C|D): A test that drops the feature from the node | A request for the exact mean of that column | A simultaneous comparison against every training point | A yes/no test of the form "is x ≤ t?" for some threshold t
Correct Answer: A yes/no test of the form "is x ≤ t?" for some threshold t

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handling Numeric Features in Decision Trees
Question Text: Why does splitting on a numeric feature require selecting a specific threshold?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each numeric feature admits exactly one valid split point | Numeric features are otherwise unusable inside any tree | Many distinct cutoffs can divide the values into branches | Thresholds are relevant only when building regression trees
Correct Answer: Many distinct cutoffs can divide the values into branches

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handling Numeric Features in Decision Trees
Question Text: In what way is a numeric (threshold) split like a categorical split?
Options (A|B|C|D): Both stop producing predictions at the leaves | Both avoid using any impurity measure | Both route examples down branches using a feature test | Both always create more than two branches
Correct Answer: Both route examples down branches using a feature test

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handling Numeric Features in Decision Trees
Question Text: Which statement about numeric features in decision trees is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Trees can only split on categorical features | Numeric features must be discretized before any tree can use them | Trees handle continuous features directly via threshold tests | A numeric split can never be binary
Correct Answer: Trees handle continuous features directly via threshold tests

Question Text: How is the threshold for a continuous feature chosen at a decision-tree node?
Options (A|B|C|D): Test candidate cutoffs and keep the best impurity reduction | Always set it to the feature's median value across the node | Pick a random value once and never reconsider that choice | Always fix it at zero regardless of the data distribution
Correct Answer: Test candidate cutoffs and keep the best impurity reduction

Question Text: What are the usual candidate thresholds for a continuous feature?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only the integer values within the feature's range | Only the smallest observed value of the feature | Only the largest observed value of the feature | Values lying between consecutive sorted feature values
Correct Answer: Values lying between consecutive sorted feature values

Question Text: Why are midpoints between consecutive sorted values used as candidate thresholds?
Options (A|B|C|D): They let the node skip the impurity computation | They force the threshold to equal an observed data point | They mark the only points where branch membership can change | They reliably maximize the tree's eventual depth
Correct Answer: They mark the only points where branch membership can change

Question Text: When comparing candidate thresholds for one feature, which threshold is selected?
Options (A|B|C|D): The one written using the fewest decimal places of all | The one lying closest to the dataset's overall global mean | The one giving the best split quality on this node's data | The one having the smallest numeric value among candidates
Correct Answer: The one giving the best split quality on this node's data

Question Text: Which statement about choosing a threshold for a continuous feature is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Candidate cutoffs must be scored by split quality, not assumed equal | Split quality is irrelevant to which threshold is chosen | Any reasonable cutoff performs equally well without scoring | The threshold should be fixed before seeing the node's data
Correct Answer: Candidate cutoffs must be scored by split quality, not assumed equal

Question Text: Why is decision-tree growth described as recursive?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every training example is copied into all of the branches | The whole tree is built from the leaves up to the root | The same split-selection step is reapplied to each subset | Recursion here refers to pruning the already-finished tree
Correct Answer: The same split-selection step is reapplied to each subset

Question Text: After the root split is made, how is each resulting child subset handled?
Options (A|B|C|D): By replacing its labels with probabilities only | As a smaller tree-building problem of the same kind | By halting the algorithm right away | By averaging its features into a single new feature
Correct Answer: As a smaller tree-building problem of the same kind

Question Text: Why is recursion a natural fit for building decision trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): Recursion guarantees perfect training classification | Each split yields subproblems on smaller subsets of the data | Only numeric features can be processed recursively | Every tree is constrained to exactly two levels
Correct Answer: Each split yields subproblems on smaller subsets of the data

Question Text: When does recursive tree growth stop at a given node?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only once the root node has become completely pure | After exactly three levels have formed in each one case | As soon as each available feature has been used once | When a stopping criterion says not to split that node
Correct Answer: When a stopping criterion says not to split that node

Question Text: Which statement about recursive tree growth is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every split, not just the root, is chosen from its own subset's data | Subtrees ignore their local data and reuse the root's split | Only the root split is data-driven; later splits are fixed in advance | Stopping criteria play no role in where growth ends
Correct Answer: Every split, not just the root, is chosen from its own subset's data

Question Text: In a classification tree, what is typically stored as the prediction at a leaf?
Options (A|B|C|D): The averaged feature vector of the examples at that leaf | The full decision path from that leaf back up to the root | Only the computed impurity score belonging to that leaf | The majority class of training examples reaching the leaf
Correct Answer: The majority class of training examples reaching the leaf

Question Text: Why is the majority class used as a leaf's prediction?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes the need for labels during training | It applies only when the leaf is perfectly pure | It matches the prediction to the region's most common label | It guarantees zero error on every training example
Correct Answer: It matches the prediction to the region's most common label

Question Text: What prediction does a terminal leaf give when it still contains mixed classes?
Options (A|B|C|D): It outputs the majority class among that leaf's examples | It becomes unable to output any class prediction at all | It must first be converted into a regression-style leaf | It is deleted from the tree automatically during training
Correct Answer: It outputs the majority class among that leaf's examples

Question Text: Why can two different leaves of the same tree predict the same class?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each class is allowed to appear within only a single leaf | Majority voting is performed only once at the root node | The tree accidentally duplicates labels across its leaves | Separate feature-space regions can share a majority label
Correct Answer: Separate feature-space regions can share a majority label

Question Text: Which statement about leaf outputs in a classification tree is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only pure leaves are permitted to be terminal | A leaf's impurity equals its predicted class label | An impure leaf can still predict via its majority class | A leaf must be perfectly pure before it can predict a class
Correct Answer: An impure leaf can still predict via its majority class

Question Text: Why are decision trees especially prone to overfitting if left unconstrained?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are restricted to using only a single chosen input feature | They are mathematically unable to produce pure leaf nodes | They keep splitting until the leaves capture training noise | They systematically underfit whenever the dataset is small
Correct Answer: They keep splitting until the leaves capture training noise

Question Text: What does it mean to say a decision tree has overfit the data?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always predicts whichever class is most frequent | It uses numeric rather than categorical input features | It has too few branches to capture the training set | It performs well on training data but poorly on new data
Correct Answer: It performs well on training data but poorly on new data

Question Text: Why can growing a decision tree to a very large depth be risky?
Options (A|B|C|D): Deep trees force every split node to be strictly binary | Deep trees can no longer assign predictions at their leaves | Deep trees may memorize training patterns that fail to generalize | Deep trees stop using impurity measures to choose splits
Correct Answer: Deep trees may memorize training patterns that fail to generalize

Question Text: Which property of the training data most directly drives a tree toward overfitting?
Options (A|B|C|D): The dataset containing exactly two target classes | Random noise and accidental patterns in the examples | The use of categorical features instead of numeric | The presence of both root nodes and leaf nodes in it
Correct Answer: Random noise and accidental patterns in the examples

Question Text: Which belief about making a decision tree larger is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That a sufficiently deep tree is capable of overfitting | That generalization can worsen even as training fit improves | That a deeper tree is always better since it lowers training error | That controlling tree complexity is worth doing in practice
Correct Answer: That a deeper tree is always better since it lowers training error

Question Text: Why might you deliberately limit how far a decision tree grows during training?
Options (A|B|C|D): To turn a classification tree into a regression tree | To force every leaf node to remain impure | To stop the model from using any input features | To reduce overfitting by preventing an overly complex tree
Correct Answer: To reduce overfitting by preventing an overly complex tree

Question Text: Which of the following is a valid way to limit a decision tree's growth?
Options (A|B|C|D): Require a minimum number of examples in a node before splitting | Keep splitting until each leaf contains a single example | Strip all thresholds away from the numeric features | Swap the impurity measure out for a kernel function
Correct Answer: Require a minimum number of examples in a node before splitting

Question Text: Why can limiting a tree's growth improve how well it generalizes?
Options (A|B|C|D): It automatically increases the number of available features | It makes the tree fit the training set perfectly | It guarantees that every resulting split is pure | It keeps the tree from chasing tiny details that may be noise
Correct Answer: It keeps the tree from chasing tiny details that may be noise

Question Text: What trade-off typically comes with limiting a decision tree's growth?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model gets converted into an unsupervised learning method | The tree invariably grows even more complex than it ever was before | The tree completely loses its ability to make any predictions | It fits the training data less perfectly but generalizes better
Correct Answer: It fits the training data less perfectly but generalizes better

Question Text: Which belief about limiting tree growth is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That growth control has a direct effect on tree complexity | That a smaller tree can sometimes generalize better than a larger one | That overfitting is a genuine concern for decision trees | That stopping splits early must always hurt model performance
Correct Answer: That stopping splits early must always hurt model performance

Question Text: What does pruning a decision tree refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): Rescaling the input features at each internal node | Replacing leaf predictions with cluster centroids | Removing some branches from a grown tree to simplify it | Adding extra branches to the tree after training ends
Correct Answer: Removing some branches from a grown tree to simplify it

Question Text: What is the main reason for pruning a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): To convert a classification task into a clustering task | To drive the training error as low as it can possibly go | To reduce overfitting and improve generalization | To strip the target labels out of the dataset
Correct Answer: To reduce overfitting and improve generalization

Question Text: Which branches are the best candidates for pruning?
Options (A|B|C|D): Those adding complexity without enough held-out benefit | Every branch that happens to end in a pure leaf | Only the single branch emerging from the root node | Only branches that split on numeric thresholds
Correct Answer: Those adding complexity without enough held-out benefit

Question Text: How does pruning affect the complexity of a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It reduces complexity by cutting parts of the tree back | It has no bearing on the tree's complexity at all | It raises the total number of leaves in the tree | It reliably makes the tree deeper than before
Correct Answer: It reduces complexity by cutting parts of the tree back

Question Text: Which belief about a pruned branch is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That pruning serves as a genuinely useful tool for combating overfitting | That pruning reliably yields a much simpler overall model than the original | That it must have been useless in training, not harmful for generalization | That training fit and out-of-sample generalization can diverge widely
Correct Answer: That it must have been useless in training, not harmful for generalization

Question Text: Why are decision trees often said to perform automatic feature selection?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are limited to using just one feature across the whole tree | They delete every unused column from the data before training | They demand that manual feature selection be completed first | They split on the most useful features and can ignore the rest
Correct Answer: They split on the most useful features and can ignore the rest

Question Text: What typically happens to an uninformative feature when a decision tree is trained?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is averaged into the choice at every split | It may simply never get chosen for any split | It becomes the predicted value at a leaf | It is automatically placed at the root node
Correct Answer: It may simply never get chosen for any split

Question Text: Why is a tree's ability to ignore unhelpful features convenient in practice?
Options (A|B|C|D): It entirely removes any need to preprocess the input data beforehand | It means that careful feature engineering can simply never matter at all | It makes the resulting tree absolutely guaranteed to be interpretable | It can focus on informative features without needing every one to help
Correct Answer: It can focus on informative features without needing every one to help

Question Text: When a tree chooses which feature to split on at a node, what is that choice based on?
Options (A|B|C|D): The alphabetical order of the feature's column name | How much the feature improves split quality at the current node | The physical units in which the feature is measured | How often the feature is mentioned in the data's documentation
Correct Answer: How much the feature improves split quality at the current node

Question Text: Which belief about automatic feature selection in trees is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That the tree ranks features globally once and never reevaluates | That some available features may end up never being used at all | That different features may be considered at different tree nodes | That a feature's usefulness depends on the data reaching a node
Correct Answer: That the tree ranks features globally once and never reevaluates

Question Text: When a categorical feature value is missing for a training example in a decision tree, what is one simple imputation strategy?
Options (A|B|C|D): Fill it in with the most common category | Always flag the row as anomalous | Discard the entire training dataset | Replace it with the leaf's class prediction
Correct Answer: Fill it in with the most common category

Question Text: Beyond filling a missing categorical value with the most frequent category, what is a more informed imputation idea?
Options (A|B|C|D): Randomly draw a category each split | Drop every row that has no missing values | Force its value to zero everywhere | Use a correlated feature to infer its likely value
Correct Answer: Use a correlated feature to infer its likely value

Question Text: How might a missing numeric feature value be handled when fitting a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Convert the affected example into a single support vector | Automatically prune the entire branch that the example reaches | Impute it with the mean, median, or a correlated predictor | Create a brand-new dedicated class label specifically for it
Correct Answer: Impute it with the mean, median, or a correlated predictor

Question Text: Why do strategies for handling missing values matter when fitting decision trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): Missing values only ever affect the very first root split | Missing entries effectively reduce the task to plain clustering | Trees simply cannot otherwise be trained on the given data | A split still needs usable values or a practical fallback rule
Correct Answer: A split still needs usable values or a practical fallback rule

Question Text: Which belief about missing data in decision trees is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Numeric and categorical gaps may be handled in different ways | Correlated features can guide imputation of a gap | Rows with any missing value must always be discarded outright | Several imputation strategies are available to choose from
Correct Answer: Rows with any missing value must always be discarded outright

Question Text: What kind of output is a regression tree designed to predict?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single discrete category | An assigned cluster index | A classifier's margin width | A continuous numeric target value
Correct Answer: A continuous numeric target value

Question Text: In what way does a regression tree still resemble a classification tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It still always requires explicit class labels in order to train | It splits on features and routes examples through the tree | It has no leaf nodes located anywhere within its structure | It is fundamentally unable to accept any categorical features
Correct Answer: It splits on features and routes examples through the tree

Question Text: Compared with a classification tree, what changes at the leaves of a regression tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Leaves output numeric values, not class labels | Leaves are removed from the structure | Leaves emit kernel scores, not outputs | Leaves must each hold one example
Correct Answer: Leaves output numeric values, not class labels

Question Text: Why might a regression tree be preferable to fitting a single straight line?
Options (A|B|C|D): It requires no training data at all in order to be fit | It fits piecewise patterns, giving regions distinct predictions | It reframes the regression problem as a classification task instead | It always attains the lowest possible bias of any model whatsoever
Correct Answer: It fits piecewise patterns, giving regions distinct predictions

Question Text: Which belief about regression trees is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): The branching idea still applies to numeric targets | Trees can be adapted to produce numeric outputs | Leaf predictions differ in the regression setting | Decision trees can only classify, never predict numbers
Correct Answer: Decision trees can only classify, never predict numbers

Question Text: In a regression tree, what is stored at each leaf node?
Options (A|B|C|D): A class label such as cat or not-cat | A kernel similarity score | A count of support vectors | A single numeric prediction value
Correct Answer: A single numeric prediction value

Question Text: Why is it valid for a regression-tree leaf to output just one number?
Options (A|B|C|D): Regression trees ignore the training targets when predicting | By that depth the tree has stopped consulting any features | A numeric output must stay constant across all of the data | Examples reaching that leaf are summarized by one prediction
Correct Answer: Examples reaching that leaf are summarized by one prediction

Question Text: How does a regression tree produce a prediction for a new example?
Options (A|B|C|D): It returns the global mean target regardless of the path taken | It averages the new example against all stored training points | It outputs the parent node's variance as the predicted value | It follows the splits to a leaf and returns that leaf's value
Correct Answer: It follows the splits to a leaf and returns that leaf's value

Question Text: Why does storing a numeric value at each leaf make a regression tree a piecewise model?
Options (A|B|C|D): Leaves exist only while the tree is training and then vanish afterward | The fitted model behaves as one straight line across all of the inputs | Different leaves give different constants to different input regions | Every leaf in the tree is required to predict the exact same number
Correct Answer: Different leaves give different constants to different input regions

Question Text: Which belief about regression-tree leaves is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Path-dependent prediction still occurs in regression | Leaves store only classes and cannot hold numbers | Leaves can hold numeric values, not just labels | The tree's branching structure still matters
Correct Answer: Leaves store only classes and cannot hold numbers

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regression Tree Predictions as Leaf Averages
Question Text: How is the prediction at a regression-tree leaf most commonly chosen?
Options (A|B|C|D): By using the median class label in it | By fitting a regression line within the leaf | By averaging the targets of the training examples in it | By taking only the maximum target value present
Correct Answer: By averaging the targets of the training examples in it

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regression Tree Predictions as Leaf Averages
Question Text: Why is the mean target a sensible value to predict at a leaf?
Options (A|B|C|D): It represents that region's targets with one central number | It guarantees a residual of exactly zero for each example | It is valid only when the output variable is strictly binary | It discards the training targets within that region entirely
Correct Answer: It represents that region's targets with one central number

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regression Tree Predictions as Leaf Averages
Question Text: If a leaf ends up containing only one training example, what is its prediction?
Options (A|B|C|D): The global mean of all targets | Simply that single example's target value | Undefined, so it makes no prediction | Invalid, so the leaf must be pruned
Correct Answer: Simply that single example's target value

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regression Tree Predictions as Leaf Averages
Question Text: Why do different leaves of a regression tree often predict different values?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because every leaf is forced to hold an equal number of examples | Because the leaf averages are fixed in advance before any training | Because each leaf's output value is assigned uniformly at random | Because their regions hold examples with different average targets
Correct Answer: Because their regions hold examples with different average targets

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regression Tree Predictions as Leaf Averages
Question Text: Which belief about regression-tree predictions is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Averaging the targets in a region is a common rule for leaves | Each leaf fits a fitted formula rather than often just an average | Routing through the splits decides which leaf's value is used | Different leaves of the same tree can predict different constants
Correct Answer: Each leaf fits a fitted formula rather than often just an average

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sum of Squared Residuals for Split Selection
Question Text: When deciding which split to make at a node of a regression tree, which quantity is the standard criterion to minimize?
Options (A|B|C|D): The sum of squared residuals within the child nodes | The Gini impurity of the labels at the node | The margin to the nearest training point | The Shannon entropy of the label distribution
Correct Answer: The sum of squared residuals within the child nodes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sum of Squared Residuals for Split Selection
Question Text: What makes squared residuals a sensible quantity to minimize when splitting a regression tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): They reward splits whose leaves give accurate numeric predictions | They apply only to neural networks, not to trees | They convert the regression task into a classification task | They let the tree split without using the target values
Correct Answer: They reward splits whose leaves give accurate numeric predictions

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sum of Squared Residuals for Split Selection
Question Text: A candidate split lowers the total sum of squared residuals compared with the parent node. What does this tell you?
Options (A|B|C|D): The resulting tree now uses more input features | The child regions' leaf values fit their targets better | The split makes the tree easier to interpret | The model has switched to unsupervised learning
Correct Answer: The child regions' leaf values fit their targets better

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sum of Squared Residuals for Split Selection
Question Text: When measuring fit for a regression-tree split, why are residuals squared rather than left as raw differences?
Options (A|B|C|D): Squaring forces each leaf to contain a single class | Squaring penalizes large errors more and is easy to optimize | Squaring makes every error positive and exactly equal | Squaring lets the split ignore the target values
Correct Answer: Squaring penalizes large errors more and is easy to optimize

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Sum of Squared Residuals for Split Selection
Question Text: Which belief about the split criterion for a regression tree is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That the quality of a split depends on the target values | That Gini impurity is the natural objective for numeric targets | That residual-based error is a natural objective for regression | That a lower sum of squared residuals means a better split
Correct Answer: That Gini impurity is the natural objective for numeric targets

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Regression Tree
Question Text: At a node of a regression tree, how is the best split among the candidates selected?
Options (A|B|C|D): By picking the split that most reduces error, e.g. squared residuals | By picking whatever split makes the tree deepest right away | By picking the feature that has the most distinct categories | By always reusing the same feature that was used at the root
Correct Answer: By picking the split that most reduces error, e.g. squared residuals

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Regression Tree
Question Text: In classification trees a good split produces purer child nodes. What is the analogous goal for a regression-tree split?
Options (A|B|C|D): Child nodes that each hold as many examples as possible | Child nodes whose target values a single leaf value fits well | Child nodes that together use as many features as possible | Child nodes holding exactly equal numbers of examples
Correct Answer: Child nodes whose target values a single leaf value fits well

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Regression Tree
Question Text: Why do the numeric target values y enter directly into split selection for a regression tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because a split is scored by how well it predicts those targets | Because only the root node is allowed to look at y | Because y is added to the inputs and used as a feature | Because regression trees split without looking at the inputs
Correct Answer: Because a split is scored by how well it predicts those targets

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Regression Tree
Question Text: After the best split at a node has been chosen, how does building the regression tree proceed?
Options (A|B|C|D): The numeric targets are first binned into discrete categories | The same split-selection step is repeated on each child subset | Building stops, since a regression tree allows only one split | The feature values are discarded before any further splitting
Correct Answer: The same split-selection step is repeated on each child subset

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Choosing the Best Split in a Regression Tree
Question Text: Which belief about how a regression tree picks its splits is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That fit to the numeric target is what scores each split | That the best split is found without using the target values | That several candidate splits are compared at each node | That how well leaves fit the targets drives the choice
Correct Answer: That the best split is found without using the target values

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Regression Trees with Multiple Predictors
Question Text: Given several input predictors, how does a regression tree make use of them?
Options (A|B|C|D): It must commit to one predictor for the whole tree | It works only when there are exactly two predictors | It averages all predictors into one before any split | It may split on different features at different nodes
Correct Answer: It may split on different features at different nodes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Regression Trees with Multiple Predictors
Question Text: Why is it useful that a regression tree can split on different predictors at different nodes?
Options (A|B|C|D): Different predictors can be informative in different regions | It guarantees the resulting tree will be perfectly balanced | It lets the tree be built without using the target values | It makes every predictor contribute equally at every node
Correct Answer: Different predictors can be informative in different regions

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Regression Trees with Multiple Predictors
Question Text: When several predictors are available, how does a node decide which feature to split on?
Options (A|B|C|D): It cycles through the predictors in alphabetical order | It reuses the same feature that the parent node split on | It always splits on the feature with the largest mean value | It compares splits across features and takes the best error drop
Correct Answer: It compares splits across features and takes the best error drop

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Regression Trees with Multiple Predictors
Question Text: After one feature is chosen to split a node, what is true of the rest of the tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Deeper nodes may still split on other features if they help | The numeric target must be rescaled before continuing | All of the remaining predictors are discarded outright | The entire tree is now locked to that one feature
Correct Answer: Deeper nodes may still split on other features if they help

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Regression Trees with Multiple Predictors
Question Text: Which belief about how a regression tree uses several predictors is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That a tree can vary which feature it uses by region | That a tree merges predictors via one global linear formula | That each split is chosen locally and from the data | That different branches may split on different features
Correct Answer: That a tree merges predictors via one global linear formula

Question Text: Why does a very deep regression tree tend to have high variance?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always just predicts the global mean of the target | It can fit training-data quirks that do not generalize | It throws away the training data and never uses it | It is unable to handle numeric input features at all
Correct Answer: It can fit training-data quirks that do not generalize

Question Text: Why does a very shallow regression tree tend to have high bias?
Options (A|B|C|D): It may be too simple to capture real structure in y | It relies on far too many input predictors at once | It contains too many leaves to summarize cleanly | It always overfits whatever training set it is given
Correct Answer: It may be too simple to capture real structure in y

Question Text: How does the depth of a regression tree relate to the bias-variance tradeoff?
Options (A|B|C|D): Greater depth can lower bias but raise variance | Greater depth always lowers both bias and variance | Depth only changes how long training takes to run | Depth matters for classification trees but not regression
Correct Answer: Greater depth can lower bias but raise variance

Question Text: Why is a validation set useful when choosing the complexity of a regression tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees the tree will reach zero error on test data | It removes any need to prune the tree afterward | It makes the depth of the tree completely irrelevant | It helps pick a size that balances fit and generalization
Correct Answer: It helps pick a size that balances fit and generalization

Question Text: Which belief about the complexity of a regression tree is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That very shallow trees are prone to underfitting the data | That very deep trees are prone to overfitting the data | That good generalization comes from balancing bias and variance | That the deepest tree is best since it minimizes train error
Correct Answer: That the deepest tree is best since it minimizes train error

Question Text: Why set a minimum number of samples required in each leaf or node of a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): To convert the tree into a linear regression model | To block splits creating tiny regions that overfit noise | To raise the count of features used at each split | To force every leaf to become perfectly pure
Correct Answer: To block splits creating tiny regions that overfit noise

Question Text: How does raising the minimum-samples-per-leaf requirement affect the resulting decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It rescales and normalizes the target values | It makes every split strictly binary and pure | It forces the tree to stay simpler with fewer splits | It automatically grows the tree to a greater depth
Correct Answer: It forces the tree to stay simpler with fewer splits

Question Text: Why can leaves containing only a handful of training examples be problematic?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are strictly required before any pruning can occur | They reliably improve generalization to new data | They are unable to output numeric predictions at all | Their predictions hinge too heavily on a few noisy points
Correct Answer: Their predictions hinge too heavily on a few noisy points

Question Text: How does increasing the minimum-samples-per-leaf setting tend to affect a tree's variance?
Options (A|B|C|D): Larger minimum leaf sizes always increase the model's variance very sharply | It has no measurable effect on the variance of the fitted model at all | Larger minimum leaf sizes usually reduce variance by smoothing predictions | It changes only the training speed and leaves the variance untouched
Correct Answer: Larger minimum leaf sizes usually reduce variance by smoothing predictions

Question Text: Which belief about the minimum-samples-per-leaf setting is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That permitting leaves with one or two examples is always harmless | That leaves with very few examples can overfit noise | That minimum-size constraints can act to regularize a tree | That this setting is a tunable control over model complexity
Correct Answer: That permitting leaves with one or two examples is always harmless

Question Text: What is cost complexity pruning of a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Growing the tree deeper until training error reaches zero | Pruning that balances tree fit against a penalty on tree size | A kernel-based method for fitting nonlinear trees | A technique that rescales features before each split
Correct Answer: Pruning that balances tree fit against a penalty on tree size

Question Text: Why include a complexity penalty when pruning a decision tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): To remove the need for any target values at all | To maximize the total number of branches grown | To discourage overly large trees that tend to overfit | To make every leaf predict one identical value
Correct Answer: To discourage overly large trees that tend to overfit

Question Text: Conceptually, what quantity does cost complexity pruning seek to minimize?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of leaves and nothing else | The depth of the tree and nothing else | Training error alone, with no penalty applied | Prediction error plus a penalty for tree complexity
Correct Answer: Prediction error plus a penalty for tree complexity

Question Text: How does cost complexity pruning differ from simply minimizing training error?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always retains the single deepest tree available | It applies only to classification trees, never regression | It disregards how well the tree fits the data entirely | It values simpler trees when added complexity is not worth it
Correct Answer: It values simpler trees when added complexity is not worth it

Question Text: Which belief about cost complexity pruning is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That the best tree is the one with lowest training error, ignoring its size | That a tree's complexity can be penalized explicitly during the pruning step | That pruning trades off quality of the fit against the model's simplicity | That penalizing model complexity can improve how well it generalizes to data
Correct Answer: That the best tree is the one with lowest training error, ignoring its size

Question Text: What is the purpose of adding a complexity penalty to a decision tree's objective?
Options (A|B|C|D): To guarantee that all leaves end up perfectly pure | To increase the number of splits the tree makes | To make large trees pay an extra cost in the objective | To substitute for and replace the target variable
Correct Answer: To make large trees pay an extra cost in the objective

Question Text: Under a tree complexity penalty, which trees are penalized most heavily?
Options (A|B|C|D): Trees that were trained on larger datasets | More complex trees with more leaves or structure | Smaller trees that have only a few leaves | Trees that rely on numeric predictor variables
Correct Answer: More complex trees with more leaves or structure

Question Text: Why is a tree complexity penalty useful when fitting a model?
Options (A|B|C|D): It curbs overfitting by discouraging needless complexity | It affects only runtime and not generalization at all | It guarantees the most accurate possible training fit | It removes the need to use a validation set
Correct Answer: It curbs overfitting by discouraging needless complexity

Question Text: What trade-off does a tree complexity penalty create during fitting?
Options (A|B|C|D): The tree is forced to stop using any predictors | The model always becomes simpler with no loss of fit | Extra complexity must be justified by enough gain in fit | The penalty constrains only the very first root split
Correct Answer: Extra complexity must be justified by enough gain in fit

Question Text: Which belief about a tree complexity penalty is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That a simpler tree can sometimes generalize better to unseen data | That model complexity can and often should be penalized when fitting | That overfitting is closely linked to having excess tree structure | That adding more branches is always free from a modeling standpoint
Correct Answer: That adding more branches is always free from a modeling standpoint

Question Text: In cost complexity pruning, what does the hyperparameter alpha control?
Options (A|B|C|D): The variance of a Gaussian distribution | The learning rate used by gradient descent | The number of target classes in the data | How strongly tree complexity is penalized
Correct Answer: How strongly tree complexity is penalized

Question Text: What is the effect of increasing alpha, the cost complexity pruning hyperparameter?
Options (A|B|C|D): The target values are rescaled before the tree is ever fit to them | The algorithm prefers simpler, smaller trees much more strongly | Every split made within the tree is driven to be perfectly pure | The tree is necessarily forced to grow deeper and add more splits
Correct Answer: The algorithm prefers simpler, smaller trees much more strongly

Question Text: What happens when alpha, the cost complexity pruning hyperparameter, is very small?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model is converted into an unsupervised clustering method instead | The penalty on complexity is weak, so larger trees are more acceptable | The tree becomes completely unable to make predictions for any input | All of the leaves immediately collapse together into a single root node
Correct Answer: The penalty on complexity is weak, so larger trees are more acceptable

Question Text: Why is alpha treated as a tunable hyperparameter rather than a fixed constant?
Options (A|B|C|D): Different datasets need different complexity-vs-fit trade-offs | It is read directly from the labels with no choice involved | It is effectively always set to zero in practice | It is relevant only for classification trees, not regression
Correct Answer: Different datasets need different complexity-vs-fit trade-offs

Question Text: Which belief about the pruning hyperparameter alpha is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That alpha generally needs to be tuned separately for each dataset | That alpha governs the trade-off between fit quality and model simplicity | That one fixed alpha value is universally optimal for every tree problem | That alpha directly influences the preferred final size of the tree
Correct Answer: That one fixed alpha value is universally optimal for every tree problem

Question Text: In cost-complexity pruning of a decision tree, why is cross-validation used to choose the complexity parameter alpha rather than fixing it by hand?
Options (A|B|C|D): To compute the predicted target values stored at the leaf nodes | To guarantee the lowest possible error on the training set | To find the pruning strength that generalizes best to unseen data | To force the tree to use every available predictor at least once
Correct Answer: To find the pruning strength that generalizes best to unseen data

Question Text: When sweeping over a grid of candidate alpha values during cost-complexity pruning, what quantity is actually compared across those values?
Options (A|B|C|D): The total number of leaf nodes in the pruned tree that each alpha yields | The alphabetical ordering of the predictor names chosen at the tree splits | The held-out validation performance of the pruned tree each alpha yields | The depth of the single root split that gets selected by each candidate tree
Correct Answer: The held-out validation performance of the pruned tree each alpha yields

Question Text: Why is it a mistake to select the pruning parameter alpha using training error alone?
Options (A|B|C|D): Training error keeps favoring larger trees and need not track generalization | Training error stays exactly the same no matter which alpha is used | Cross-validation applies only to neural networks, never to trees | Alpha takes effect only at prediction time, not during fitting
Correct Answer: Training error keeps favoring larger trees and need not track generalization

Question Text: After validating across candidate alpha values, which choice of alpha is generally preferred?
Options (A|B|C|D): Whichever alpha produces the single largest number of leaves | The smallest alpha available, so almost nothing is pruned | One that balances fit against simplicity well on held-out data | The largest alpha available, collapsing the tree to its root
Correct Answer: One that balances fit against simplicity well on held-out data

Question Text: Which belief about tuning a pruning hyperparameter like alpha should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That alpha ought to be tuned with generalization as the goal | That training performance by itself is enough to select alpha reliably | That the size of the tree is itself a modeling decision to make | That validation results can expose when a tree is overfitting
Correct Answer: That training performance by itself is enough to select alpha reliably

Question Text: Once cross-validation has identified a good value of the cost-complexity parameter alpha, what is the usual next step?
Options (A|B|C|D): Discard alpha and simply keep the largest unpruned tree | Average all the candidate pruned trees with equal weight | Switch the model from a tree to a neural network instead | Select or refit the final pruned tree using that chosen alpha
Correct Answer: Select or refit the final pruned tree using that chosen alpha

Question Text: After tuning the complexity parameter, why is it preferable to deploy a single pruned tree rather than every candidate that was evaluated?
Options (A|B|C|D): Two decision trees can never be compared against each other | One final model chosen at the tuned complexity setting is what is wanted | All of the candidate pruned trees turn out to have identical accuracy | Pruning is only an analysis step and yields nothing deployable
Correct Answer: One final model chosen at the tuned complexity setting is what is wanted

Question Text: Conceptually, what does the final pruned tree selected at the tuned alpha represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The deepest tree the training data can possibly support | The tree with the lowest training error whatever its size | A chosen balance between predictive fit and model simplicity | The tree that manages to use the largest number of predictors
Correct Answer: A chosen balance between predictive fit and model simplicity

Question Text: Why can a pruned tree outperform the full unpruned tree on new data?
Options (A|B|C|D): Cutting away unnecessary branches can reduce overfitting | Smaller trees are able to memorize more training examples | The act of pruning relabels the targets at the leaves | Pruning automatically raises accuracy on the training set
Correct Answer: Cutting away unnecessary branches can reduce overfitting

Question Text: Which belief about choosing the final tree should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That keeping the full unpruned tree is always the best choice | That the selection should rest on validation evidence | That pruning the tree can improve how it generalizes | That the final model reflects a tuned complexity choice
Correct Answer: That keeping the full unpruned tree is always the best choice

Question Text: What best describes gradient boosting for a regression task?
Options (A|B|C|D): A support vector machine using nonlinear kernel functions | A single large regression tree fit once over all of the data | A clustering method that locates K centroids among the data points | An ensemble that builds regression trees in sequence to refine predictions
Correct Answer: An ensemble that builds regression trees in sequence to refine predictions

Question Text: Why is the method called "boosting"?
Options (A|B|C|D): It scales up the value of every input feature before any single tree is fit | Each new tree is added to reduce the current ensemble's errors step by step | It relies purely on mapping the data into much higher-dimensional kernel spaces | It boosts the dataset by duplicating many of its rows to make the sample larger
Correct Answer: Each new tree is added to reduce the current ensemble's errors step by step

Question Text: What kind of base learners does gradient boosting for regression typically use?
Options (A|B|C|D): Centroids produced by K-means clustering | Small (shallow) regression trees | Logistic regression classifiers exclusively | Large, deeply layered neural networks
Correct Answer: Small (shallow) regression trees

Question Text: How does the ensemble's prediction change as gradient boosting proceeds?
Options (A|B|C|D): It alternates between tree outputs and SVM outputs | It is updated by adding in the contribution of each new tree | It is averaged directly with the observed target labels | It is locked in after the very first tree is trained
Correct Answer: It is updated by adding in the contribution of each new tree

Question Text: Which misunderstanding about gradient boosting should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That it is an ensemble built from multiple base learners | That it is one very deep tree rather than many trees added in sequence | That its trees are introduced in successive stages of training | That its predictions are refined iteratively as trees are added
Correct Answer: That it is one very deep tree rather than many trees added in sequence

Question Text: What is one major difference between gradient boosting and AdaBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are precisely the same underlying algorithm and differ in name alone, producing identical fitted models every time | Gradient boosting fits new learners to residual or gradient signals, while AdaBoost reweights examples by past mistakes | Gradient boosting never uses decision trees as its learners, whereas AdaBoost is required to always use them as learners | Gradient boosting works only for clustering tasks, while AdaBoost works only on regression problems with numeric targets
Correct Answer: Gradient boosting fits new learners to residual or gradient signals, while AdaBoost reweights examples by past mistakes

Question Text: How does AdaBoost place more emphasis on hard-to-classify examples?
Options (A|B|C|D): By lowering the overall learning rate down to zero | By raising their weights after they are misclassified | By deleting them from the dataset before the next round | By restricting every base learner to be a deep tree
Correct Answer: By raising their weights after they are misclassified

Question Text: How does gradient boosting improve the ensemble at each step?
Options (A|B|C|D): By assigning exactly one fixed output class label to each newly added tree | By fitting the next learner to the current ensemble's residual or gradient | By pruning the previously added trees back out of the existing ensemble again | By selecting the separating hyperplane that happens to have the largest margin
Correct Answer: By fitting the next learner to the current ensemble's residual or gradient

Question Text: Why is the distinction between gradient boosting and AdaBoost conceptually important?
Options (A|B|C|D): One of the two methods is fully supervised while the other one is unsupervised | Both are boosting methods, yet they correct their errors by different mechanisms | The difference between them matters only when plotting their training curves | Given identical training data they will always return the exact same final model
Correct Answer: Both are boosting methods, yet they correct their errors by different mechanisms

Question Text: Which misunderstanding about boosting methods should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That every boosting algorithm updates the ensemble by the very same mechanism | That fitting the residual errors is the central idea behind gradient boosting | That AdaBoost and gradient boosting are closely related yet genuinely distinct | That reweighting the misclassified points is the central idea behind AdaBoost
Correct Answer: That every boosting algorithm updates the ensemble by the very same mechanism

Question Text: In gradient boosting for regression, what is the initial prediction (before any trees are added) typically set to?
Options (A|B|C|D): A fresh random guess for each training example | The single largest observed target value | A constant equal to the mean of the target values | A deep decision tree fit on all features
Correct Answer: A constant equal to the mean of the target values

Question Text: Why does gradient boosting begin with a single constant prediction rather than a complex model?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes fitting the first tree unnecessary entirely | It guarantees the final ensemble will be a linear function | It gives a baseline that later trees can correct toward | It removes any need to compute residuals later
Correct Answer: It gives a baseline that later trees can correct toward

Question Text: When the loss is squared error, which constant minimizes that loss and is therefore the natural starting value?
Options (A|B|C|D): A value of zero for every example | The mean of y in the training data | The median of the input feature values | The largest label in the training set
Correct Answer: The mean of y in the training data

Question Text: What role does the initial constant prediction play in the overall boosting algorithm?
Options (A|B|C|D): It eliminates the need to specify a loss function | It is the starting ensemble whose errors later trees reduce | It fixes the number of leaves used in every tree | It serves as the final model whenever trees stay shallow
Correct Answer: It is the starting ensemble whose errors later trees reduce

Question Text: Which statement about the first step of gradient boosting is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The ensemble must start from a complex tree rather than a simple constant | The starting value of the whole ensemble can simply be a single constant | Later trees in the sequence simply refine an initial baseline prediction value | Boosting builds up its overall model in an iterative, stagewise fashion overall
Correct Answer: The ensemble must start from a complex tree rather than a simple constant

Question Text: In gradient boosting for regression with squared-error loss, what are the pseudo-residuals at a given step?
Options (A|B|C|D): The Gini impurity values measured at each leaf | Only the final prediction stored at each leaf | The current errors, y minus the current prediction | The support vectors selected by the ensemble
Correct Answer: The current errors, y minus the current prediction

Question Text: Why are pseudo-residuals central to the gradient boosting procedure?
Options (A|B|C|D): The next tree fits them, so the ensemble corrects its errors | They permanently overwrite the original target values used here | They exist only to select the pruning parameter alpha used | They quantify the class impurity present at each node split
Correct Answer: The next tree fits them, so the ensemble corrects its errors

Question Text: Why are these targets called pseudo-residuals rather than plain residuals in the general boosting framework?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because they apply to classification problems only | Because they are random labels unrelated to the loss | Because for general losses they are gradients, not raw errors | Because they are always exactly zero once training converges
Correct Answer: Because for general losses they are gradients, not raw errors

Question Text: For an example with squared-error loss, what does a large positive pseudo-residual indicate about the current model?
Options (A|B|C|D): That example's feature values are missing | That example should be dropped from training | The model is currently overpredicting that example | The model is currently underpredicting that example
Correct Answer: The model is currently underpredicting that example

Question Text: Which statement about pseudo-residuals is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are tightly connected to the gradients of the chosen loss | They indicate exactly what the next learner should correct | They are arbitrary targets unrelated to the ensemble's errors | They are computed directly from the model's current predictions
Correct Answer: They are arbitrary targets unrelated to the ensemble's errors

Question Text: At each step of gradient boosting regression, what is the newly added tree trained to predict?
Options (A|B|C|D): The original labels, ignoring prior trees | The current residuals, or pseudo-residuals | The margins of the support vectors | The epsilon threshold for anomalies
Correct Answer: The current residuals, or pseudo-residuals

Question Text: Why does fitting each new tree to the current residuals help the ensemble improve?
Options (A|B|C|D): It does away with needing an initial prediction | It teaches the tree to correct the ensemble's current errors | It turns the method into an unsupervised one | It guarantees zero training error after a single tree
Correct Answer: It teaches the tree to correct the ensemble's current errors

Question Text: If the residuals across some input region are mostly positive, what will the newly fit tree tend to do there?
Options (A|B|C|D): Force that region to be pruned away | Add a positive correction in that region | Recast that region as a classification leaf | Shrink the ensemble's prediction in that region
Correct Answer: Add a positive correction in that region
