Options (A|B|C|D): Limiting tree depth is one way of controlling overall model complexity | Deeper trees are always better since they fit the training data closely | Boosting can still work quite well even when using relatively small trees | Restricting the maximum tree depth helps guard against severe overfitting
Correct Answer: Deeper trees are always better since they fit the training data closely

Question Text: In XGBoost tree pruning, what role does the gamma parameter play?
Options (A|B|C|D): It counts the number of training examples in each class | It scales each tree's learning rate before adding it | It sets a minimum gain a split must exceed to be retained | It converts raw leaf scores into class probabilities
Correct Answer: It sets a minimum gain a split must exceed to be retained

Question Text: Conceptually, how does XGBoost use gamma when deciding whether to keep a split?
Options (A|B|C|D): It uses gamma to choose the model's initial prediction | It compares gain minus gamma against zero to decide pruning | It adds gamma to each training label before fitting | It sets gamma equal to the total number of trees built
Correct Answer: It compares gain minus gamma against zero to decide pruning

Question Text: If the gain of a split minus gamma comes out negative, what does XGBoost do to that branch?
Options (A|B|C|D): It prunes (removes) the branch | It raises the learning rate for that branch | It promotes the branch to become the root | It randomly reweights the branch's data
Correct Answer: It prunes (removes) the branch

Question Text: What is the effect of increasing the gamma value in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): The boosting loss function is replaced entirely by a different objective | Trees automatically become deeper, adding more split levels than before | Pruning grows more aggressive, since splits need larger gain to survive | Leaf output magnitudes automatically grow larger across every tree built
Correct Answer: Pruning grows more aggressive, since splits need larger gain to survive

Question Text: What is the lambda parameter in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): A regularization term inside the similarity and leaf-output formulas | The step size, also called the learning rate, used by the boosting | The total count of boosted trees the trained model will end up building | The probability cutoff used to assign a final classification label
Correct Answer: A regularization term inside the similarity and leaf-output formulas

Question Text: Why is the lambda parameter useful in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): It reduces overfitting by shrinking scores and leaf outputs | It removes the pruning step from training entirely | It forces every tree to grow to maximum depth | It is used solely for visualizing the trees afterward
Correct Answer: It reduces overfitting by shrinking scores and leaf outputs

Question Text: Where does lambda appear within the XGBoost regression formulas?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only in the cross-validation scoring procedure used during tuning | Only inside the final binary classification decision threshold value | In denominators that moderate the similarity and leaf-output values | Only within the model's single initial baseline prediction value
Correct Answer: In denominators that moderate the similarity and leaf-output values

Question Text: What is the practical effect of increasing lambda in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Residuals stop being used in any way when the trees are built | The effective learning rate is steadily driven down toward zero | Individual observations and leaves exert less extreme influence | Tree building switches over to a fully unsupervised method
Correct Answer: Individual observations and leaves exert less extreme influence

Question Text: Which statement about lambda in XGBoost is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Lambda shrinks the computed similarity scores | Lambda is a regularization-strength parameter | Lambda helps the model resist overfitting | Lambda is just another name for the learning rate
Correct Answer: Lambda is just another name for the learning rate

Question Text: Numerically, how does lambda change an XGBoost similarity score?
Options (A|B|C|D): It increases the numerator, so the score becomes larger | It drops the parent node out of the gain calculation | It enlarges the denominator, so the score becomes smaller | It flips the signs of the residuals in the numerator
Correct Answer: It enlarges the denominator, so the score becomes smaller

Question Text: Why does adding lambda to the denominator shrink the similarity score?
Options (A|B|C|D): Adding lambda normalizes the labels before summing them | The same summed residuals are divided by a larger quantity | Adding lambda makes all of the residuals vanish to zero | Adding lambda forces the resulting gain to be negative
Correct Answer: The same summed residuals are divided by a larger quantity

Question Text: Why can shrinking similarity scores make pruning more likely to occur?
Options (A|B|C|D): It forces a larger fraction of the leaves to become pure | It blocks residuals from being recomputed each round | Smaller gain values more easily fall below the gamma threshold | It causes the trees to grow deeper before pruning runs
Correct Answer: Smaller gain values more easily fall below the gamma threshold

Question Text: What broader modeling benefit does shrinking similarity scores support?
Options (A|B|C|D): It has an effect only for classification problems | It removes any need to also limit tree depth | It guarantees a strictly better fit to the training set | It regularizes the tree and can reduce overfitting
Correct Answer: It regularizes the tree and can reduce overfitting

Question Text: Which belief about lambda's effect on similarity scores is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Lambda can lower the value of computed similarity scores | Lambda is one component of XGBoost's regularization | Lambda's shrinkage influences gain and therefore pruning | Lambda touches only leaf outputs, not split-evaluation terms
Correct Answer: Lambda touches only leaf outputs, not split-evaluation terms

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: How does lambda affect an XGBoost leaf output value?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes all leaf predictions from the tree | It sets each leaf output equal to the learning rate | It reverses the sign of every leaf's output value | It enlarges the formula's denominator, shrinking the output
Correct Answer: It enlarges the formula's denominator, shrinking the output

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: Why does shrinking leaf output values act as a form of regularization?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes the need to compute gain for any split | It forces every leaf in the tree to predict exactly zero | Smaller corrections reduce sensitivity to individual observations | It guarantees the model reaches higher training accuracy
Correct Answer: Smaller corrections reduce sensitivity to individual observations

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: What happens to a leaf output value when lambda increases from 0 to a positive number?
Options (A|B|C|D): Its magnitude typically decreases | Its value always exactly doubles | It stops depending on the residuals | It turns into a discrete class label
Correct Answer: Its magnitude typically decreases

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: Why is shrinking leaf outputs with lambda especially important for leaves holding few observations?
Options (A|B|C|D): Few-observation leaves are pruned automatically in every case | Leaves with few observations are always harmless to predictions | Without shrinkage, a tiny leaf could make an overly extreme correction | Lambda adjusts only leaves that hold many observations
Correct Answer: Without shrinkage, a tiny leaf could make an overly extreme correction

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: Which belief about lambda and leaf outputs is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Shrinking leaf outputs is a regularization effect | Lambda can reduce the size of a leaf's correction | Leaf output values stay unchanged when lambda changes | The leaf-output formula genuinely depends on lambda
Correct Answer: Leaf output values stay unchanged when lambda changes

Question Text: In an XGBoost regression tree, what does the numeric value stored in a leaf represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The single predicted class label for the region | The count of training rows reaching the leaf | A correction to the running prediction | The information gain of the split above it
Correct Answer: A correction to the running prediction

Question Text: How is the output value of an XGBoost regression leaf computed?
Options (A|B|C|D): The most frequent class among the rows | The Gini impurity measured at that leaf | Sum of residuals over (count + lambda) | The mean of the feature values in the leaf
Correct Answer: Sum of residuals over (count + lambda)

Question Text: When the regularization term lambda equals zero, the XGBoost regression leaf output reduces to which quantity?
Options (A|B|C|D): The configured learning-rate value | The overall mean of the target variable | The predicted class probability value | The mean residual in the leaf
Correct Answer: The mean residual in the leaf

Question Text: Once a tree's structure (its splits) has been fixed, why must leaf output values still be assigned?
Options (A|B|C|D): They fix how many input features the model uses | They relabel the branches for visualization | They set how much the tree shifts each region | They overwrite the stored residuals with targets
Correct Answer: They set how much the tree shifts each region

Question Text: Which statement about XGBoost leaf outputs is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are mere branch names, not numeric values | They are derived from residual information | They can be shrunk by the lambda term | They are numeric corrections, not branch labels
Correct Answer: They are mere branch names, not numeric values

Question Text: Which symbol is conventionally used for the learning rate in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Gamma | Lambda | Cover | Eta
Correct Answer: Eta

Question Text: What role does the learning rate play in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): It computes the similarity score per leaf | It sets only the model's initial prediction | It directly picks the optimal split point | It scales each new tree's output when added
Correct Answer: It scales each new tree's output when added

Question Text: What is the main trade-off of choosing a smaller learning rate in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model gives up its additive structure | More trees are needed for strong accuracy | The leaf output values stop mattering at all | The initial prediction is no longer required
Correct Answer: More trees are needed for strong accuracy

Question Text: Which statement about eta (the XGBoost learning rate) is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is a regularization penalty like lambda | It is a step size for tree contributions | It controls how fast the ensemble grows | It scales the size of each tree's update
Correct Answer: It is a regularization penalty like lambda

Question Text: After a new tree is built, how is each example's updated prediction obtained?
Options (A|B|C|D): Replace the prediction with the tree's raw residual | Average all prior trees and drop the newest one | Convert residuals straight into final labels | Add the leaf output times the learning rate to it
Correct Answer: Add the leaf output times the learning rate to it

Question Text: Why are the model's predictions updated after each tree rather than only at the end?
Options (A|B|C|D): The next round's residuals depend on them | Trees are independent, so it is optional | Plotting the ensemble requires it | Gamma forces it even when residuals do not
Correct Answer: The next round's residuals depend on them

Question Text: If a newly added tree supplies a useful correction for an example, what is the effect?
Options (A|B|C|D): The model resets to its initial guess | Its prediction moves nearer the target | That leaf's output value becomes zero | The example is dropped from the training set
Correct Answer: Its prediction moves nearer the target

Question Text: How does the learning rate enter the prediction-update step in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): It assigns the example to a specific leaf | It controls how deep the tree may grow | It scales the tree's correction term | It fixes the sign of each residual value
Correct Answer: It scales the tree's correction term

Question Text: Which statement about how XGBoost updates predictions is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): The ensemble's predictions are additive overall | Trees are merged only at the very end | The running prediction is refreshed each round | Later residuals depend on the current prediction
Correct Answer: Trees are merged only at the very end

Question Text: What is the core sequential idea behind how XGBoost builds its trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): Trees are added to the model in random order | Residuals are computed only once at the start | Every tree predicts the full target on its own | Each new tree targets the ensemble's residuals
Correct Answer: Each new tree targets the ensemble's residuals

Question Text: Why is XGBoost's training described as sequential correction?
Options (A|B|C|D): It keeps correcting its remaining errors | It trains all of the trees at the same time | It sorts features alphabetically before each split | It reassigns the class labels on every round
Correct Answer: It keeps correcting its remaining errors

Question Text: How does fitting each tree to the current residuals benefit the ensemble?
Options (A|B|C|D): It refines the fit gradually, not in one tree | It guarantees the model will never overfit | It removes any need for regularization terms | It makes the very first tree irrelevant
Correct Answer: It refines the fit gradually, not in one tree

Question Text: After predictions improve following a tree, what happens before the next tree is fit?
Options (A|B|C|D): Fresh residuals are computed for it | The learning rate is reset all the way to 1 | Training halts no matter the current error | The same tree is reused without any changes
Correct Answer: Fresh residuals are computed for it

Question Text: Which statement about XGBoost's sequential nature is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each tree is trained on the updated residuals | Boosting proceeds in a stagewise manner | Each tree is unrelated to earlier trees | Residuals are recomputed between rounds
Correct Answer: Each tree is unrelated to earlier trees

Question Text: In XGBoost, the metric called cover for a node is best described as which quantity?
Options (A|B|C|D): The denominator of the similarity score before lambda is added | The numerator of the similarity score, the squared sum of residuals | The output value assigned to that leaf after pruning | The reduction in loss achieved by splitting at that node
Correct Answer: The denominator of the similarity score before lambda is added

Question Text: For an XGBoost regression tree built with squared error loss, the cover of a leaf simplifies to which value?
Options (A|B|C|D): The mean of the residuals that are assigned to that leaf | The count of residuals that fall in that leaf | The number of input features available in the dataset | The sum of the squared residuals collected in that leaf
Correct Answer: The count of residuals that fall in that leaf

Question Text: Why does cover matter while XGBoost is growing a tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It replaces gain as the criterion used to choose every split | It enforces a minimum-support requirement on each leaf | It is consulted only when forming the model's final prediction | It sets the pruning threshold gamma applied to each branch
Correct Answer: It enforces a minimum-support requirement on each leaf

Question Text: How does the cover of a leaf differ between XGBoost classification and regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It depends on predicted probabilities for classification but a count for regression | It is computed by exactly the same formula in both task settings | It equals the predicted class label in the classification setting only | It is well defined for regression trees yet undefined for classification trees
Correct Answer: It depends on predicted probabilities for classification but a count for regression

Question Text: Which statement about cover in XGBoost is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Cover has an influence on how leaves are permitted to grow | Cover can take a different form depending on the task type | Cover is just another name for gain or for the leaf output | Cover originates from the denominator of the similarity score
Correct Answer: Cover is just another name for gain or for the leaf output

Question Text: What does the min_child_weight setting control in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): The smallest cover a child node may have to be kept | The exact maximum depth that is allowed for each tree | The number of distinct classes present in the target | The learning-rate value that is applied at each child node
Correct Answer: The smallest cover a child node may have to be kept

Question Text: Why is min_child_weight a useful hyperparameter to tune?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees that every resulting leaf will be perfectly pure | It removes any need to also tune the pruning value gamma | It blocks leaves that rest on too little effective data | It takes effect only once the model has finished its training
Correct Answer: It blocks leaves that rest on too little effective data

Question Text: How does min_child_weight use a node's cover when deciding whether a split is allowed?
Options (A|B|C|D): It ignores cover entirely and counts the raw rows in the child | It requires the cover of a child to clear a set threshold | It forces the cover of a node to equal the regularization term lambda | It overrides the cover value and uses residual signs in its place
Correct Answer: It requires the cover of a child to clear a set threshold

Question Text: Which modeling problem can a well-chosen min_child_weight help reduce?
Options (A|B|C|D): Underfitting from packing too many rows per leaf | Class imbalance present in the training labels | Overfitting caused by tiny, unstable leaves | Poor scaling among the numeric input features
Correct Answer: Overfitting caused by tiny, unstable leaves

Question Text: Which belief about min_child_weight is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It governs which of the candidate splits are permitted | It acts as a regularization-style control over tree growth | It depends on the cover quantity computed for a node | It refers to physical feature weights, not child support
Correct Answer: It refers to physical feature weights, not child support

Question Text: What task is XGBoost for classification designed to perform?
Options (A|B|C|D): Finding the nearest neighbors for each incoming query point | Estimating a single continuous numeric target value only | Grouping unlabeled examples into clusters that it discovers | Predicting class probabilities or labels with boosted trees
Correct Answer: Predicting class probabilities or labels with boosted trees

Question Text: Which setting is the most common entry point when first learning XGBoost classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sorting examples into two categories, like yes versus no | Estimating the variance of a Gaussian distribution from data | Predicting a continuous drug dosage amount for each patient | Selecting the number of clusters K that best fits the data
Correct Answer: Sorting examples into two categories, like yes versus no

Question Text: How does XGBoost classification relate to XGBoost regression?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is the very same method but skips all regularization terms | It belongs to an entirely separate family of algorithms | It drops decision trees in favor of a single linear model | It keeps the boosting idea but adapts the loss to probabilities
Correct Answer: It keeps the boosting idea but adapts the loss to probabilities

Question Text: Which scikit-learn-style class does the XGBoost library provide for classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): SupportVectorBoost | XGBClassifier | LinearClassifierTree | KMeansClassifier
Correct Answer: XGBClassifier

Question Text: Which statement about XGBoost classification is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Probability-based loss formulas play a role in the method | It builds boosted decision trees in order to classify data | XGBoost provides a dedicated variant meant for classification | XGBoost handles only regression and cannot classify
Correct Answer: XGBoost handles only regression and cannot classify

Question Text: By default, what initial prediction does XGBoost use before any trees are added in a classification task?
Options (A|B|C|D): The majority class label taken straight from the data | A predicted probability of 0.5 for every example | A residual fixed at zero for every example in the data | A predicted probability of 1.0 for every example
Correct Answer: A predicted probability of 0.5 for every example

Question Text: Why does XGBoost classification need an initial probability prediction at all?
Options (A|B|C|D): It serves merely as a convenience when plotting the data | It permanently locks in each example's final class label | It is the baseline from which residuals and updates are computed | It removes the need for any logistic transformation later on
Correct Answer: It is the baseline from which residuals and updates are computed

Question Text: Intuitively, what does an initial prediction of 0.5 represent for each example?
Options (A|B|C|D): An even 50 percent chance of the positive class to start | Complete certainty that the example is in the positive class | Complete certainty that the example is in the negative class | The average value of the input features for that example
Correct Answer: An even 50 percent chance of the positive class to start

Question Text: If a dataset is heavily skewed toward one class, what could conceptually replace the default 0.5 as the starting prediction?
Options (A|B|C|D): The gain threshold gamma that is used during pruning | The total number of input features present in the dataset | The maximum tree depth permitted during model training | A baseline probability set to the observed class prevalence
Correct Answer: A baseline probability set to the observed class prevalence

Question Text: Which belief about the initial classification prediction is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It begins with a hard class label, not a baseline probability | A value of 0.5 is a common default starting point for it | Trees added afterward gradually refine this baseline value | It enables the very first round of residual calculation
Correct Answer: It begins with a hard class label, not a baseline probability

Question Text: In XGBoost classification, what does a residual for a training example represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The decision threshold that gets applied to the predicted class | The gain value that was computed at the tree's root node split | The observed binary label minus the current predicted probability | The total number of leaves present within the current boosted tree
Correct Answer: The observed binary label minus the current predicted probability

Question Text: Why are classification residuals (observed label minus predicted probability) useful when fitting the next tree in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): They eliminate the need for using any loss function during training | They become relevant only after the final model has been built | They directly set each new tree's maximum allowed depth limit | They show how current probability predictions should be corrected
Correct Answer: They show how current probability predictions should be corrected

Question Text: Conceptually, how do XGBoost classification residuals differ from plain regression residuals?
Options (A|B|C|D): They stay fixed and do not change at all across the boosting rounds | They never depend in any way on the model's current predictions | They are tied to predicted probabilities, not continuous targets | They are always larger in magnitude than the regression residuals
Correct Answer: They are tied to predicted probabilities, not continuous targets

Question Text: After XGBoost updates its predicted probabilities in a boosting round, what happens to the classification residuals?
Options (A|B|C|D): They stay fixed for all later trees | They are converted into support vectors | They are replaced by the Gini impurity measure | They are recomputed from the newly updated predictions
Correct Answer: They are recomputed from the newly updated predictions

Question Text: Which statement about classification residuals in XGBoost is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Errors in predicted probabilities drive the next tree | Boosting ignores prediction errors and fits only hard labels at every stage | Residuals are recomputed as the predictions change | Each round's updates depend on the current predicted probabilities
Correct Answer: Boosting ignores prediction errors and fits only hard labels at every stage

Question Text: How does the leaf similarity score for XGBoost classification differ from the regression version?
Options (A|B|C|D): It drops the lambda regularization term out of the formula entirely | Its denominator adds a p(1 - p) term per example for probability p | It depends only on the total number of distinct classes in the data | It is computed in exactly the same way as the Gini impurity does
Correct Answer: Its denominator adds a p(1 - p) term per example for probability p

Question Text: What stays the same between the regression and classification similarity scores in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): The numerator is still the squared sum of the residuals | The lambda regularization term disappears completely | Gain is no longer derived from similarity scores | Leaf outputs are no longer numeric values
Correct Answer: The numerator is still the squared sum of the residuals

Question Text: Why does the classification similarity-score denominator depend on the predicted probabilities?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the loss curvature depends on the current probability estimates | Because the model must count classes instead of examples | Because the predicted probabilities are identical in every round | Because leaf cover has no relationship to the predictions
Correct Answer: Because the loss curvature depends on the current probability estimates

Question Text: Because the classification similarity score uses a probability-based denominator, what is one consequence as boosting proceeds?
Options (A|B|C|D): The full tree structure is locked in right after the very first round | The scores can shift in nuanced ways as predictions improve over rounds | The lambda regularization term stops having any further effect on scores | Gamma takes over the role of the model's learning rate going forward
Correct Answer: The scores can shift in nuanced ways as predictions improve over rounds

Question Text: Which statement about classification similarity scores in XGBoost is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): They include probability-dependent terms | The formula is adapted specifically for classification | The lambda regularization term is still present | They use exactly the same denominator as the regression scores
Correct Answer: They use exactly the same denominator as the regression scores

Question Text: What is the role of gain when growing an XGBoost classification tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It counts only the positively labeled examples within a given node | It computes each leaf's final predicted class probability directly | It measures how much a split improves the classification objective | It selects the overall learning rate applied throughout the model
Correct Answer: It measures how much a split improves the classification objective

Question Text: Which quantities are combined to compute the gain of a candidate classification split?
Options (A|B|C|D): Just the decision threshold combined with the lambda penalty term | The two child leaf similarity scores, minus the parent node's score | Only the depth of the root node taken together with the gamma value | Only the final predicted class labels assigned at the child leaves
Correct Answer: The two child leaf similarity scores, minus the parent node's score

Question Text: Why can the gain for the same split differ between regression and classification trees in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Gain is in fact computed identically across every kind of learning task | The similarity-score formulas differ, as classification adds probabilities | Classification simply discards all of the residual-like information it had | Regression trees in XGBoost do not actually make any use at all of gain
Correct Answer: The similarity-score formulas differ, as classification adds probabilities

Question Text: When comparing candidate splits in an XGBoost classification tree, what does a larger gain indicate?
Options (A|B|C|D): A smaller learning rate being applied across the boosted model | A guarantee that the resulting final tree will end up perfectly pure | A larger number of distinct target classes present in the dataset | A more useful split under the current classification objective
Correct Answer: A more useful split under the current classification objective

Question Text: Which statement about gain in XGBoost classification is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): Classification splits each get their own similarity scores computed for them | Gain is still the quantity used to rank the candidate splits considered | Split quality is judged just like in a plain majority-vote decision tree | XGBoost relies on specialized gain formulas tailored for classification
Correct Answer: Split quality is judged just like in a plain majority-vote decision tree

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cover as a Constraint in Classification Trees
Question Text: What does cover represent for a leaf in an XGBoost classification tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): The final predicted output probability produced by the leaf | The entropy of the assigned class labels falling in the leaf | The count of distinct target classes represented in the leaf | A probability-based measure of the leaf's effective support
Correct Answer: A probability-based measure of the leaf's effective support

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cover as a Constraint in Classification Trees
Question Text: Why can cover act as a constraint on whether a classification leaf is allowed to form?
Options (A|B|C|D): Cover takes effect only after the tree has already been fully pruned back | Leaves with too little effective support get blocked to curb overfitting | Cover automatically chooses the positive class as the leaf's prediction | Cover sets the overall learning rate used throughout the entire model
Correct Answer: Leaves with too little effective support get blocked to curb overfitting

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cover as a Constraint in Classification Trees
Question Text: How does cover for an XGBoost classification leaf differ from a simple count of examples?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always equals exactly the number of observations in that leaf | It depends on predicted probabilities, not just the raw example count | It bears no relationship at all to the leaf's computed similarity score | It is something defined only for regression trees, never for these
Correct Answer: It depends on predicted probabilities, not just the raw example count

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cover as a Constraint in Classification Trees
Question Text: Which modeling problem does the cover constraint help address in XGBoost classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): The complete absence of any initial baseline prediction value | Unstable leaves built from too little effective information | An outright inability to compute the predicted probabilities | Having far too many input features present in the dataset
Correct Answer: Unstable leaves built from too little effective information

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Cover as a Constraint in Classification Trees
Question Text: Which statement about cover in XGBoost classification trees is a misconception that should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is just a raw example count with no link to current probabilities | It depends directly on the current predicted probabilities of examples | It contributes to regularizing the overall boosted model output | It can act to constrain how the tree is allowed to grow further
Correct Answer: It is just a raw example count with no link to current probabilities

Question Text: In an XGBoost classifier, what value does each leaf of a tree output?
Options (A|B|C|D): A final hard class label (0 or 1) for the region | The calibrated class probability for that region | A score adjustment added to the running log-odds total | The number of training examples covered by the leaf
Correct Answer: A score adjustment added to the running log-odds total

Question Text: Why does the leaf-output formula for classification differ from the one used for regression in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): The lambda regularization term is dropped for classification | Its denominator reflects the logistic-loss probability terms | Classification leaves output labels, not numbers at all | The two leaf formulas are in fact identical in both cases
Correct Answer: Its denominator reflects the logistic-loss probability terms

Question Text: What is the effect of a positive leaf output in an XGBoost classifier on predictions for examples reaching that leaf?
Options (A|B|C|D): It drops those examples from the training set | It triggers immediate pruning of that branch | It drives every predicted probability down toward zero | It raises the predicted score for the positive class
Correct Answer: It raises the predicted score for the positive class

Question Text: Why are the leaf outputs of an XGBoost classifier not themselves probabilities?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the leaf outputs are never numeric values in the first place | Because they are additive scores later mapped to probabilities | Because each leaf output must equal exactly either zero or one | Because probabilities are used only during the model's training phase
Correct Answer: Because they are additive scores later mapped to probabilities

Question Text: Which belief about XGBoost classification leaf outputs is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That probabilities come only after a transformation | That the logistic loss shapes the leaf formula | That a leaf stores the final class probability directly | That leaf outputs are additive score corrections
Correct Answer: That a leaf stores the final class probability directly

Question Text: Which function converts an internal log-odds score into a probability in XGBoost classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): The ReLU function | The information-gain function | The logistic (sigmoid) function | The squaring function
Correct Answer: The logistic (sigmoid) function

Question Text: When the sigmoid is applied to a log-odds value of 0, what probability does it produce?
Options (A|B|C|D): 1 | 0 | 0.5 | Undefined

Question Text: Why does XGBoost work in log-odds space internally rather than directly in probabilities during classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because trees are able to predict only class labels | Because log-odds always stay between 0 and 1 | Because log-odds form an additive scale updated stage by stage | Because probabilities cannot be computed while training
Correct Answer: Because log-odds form an additive scale updated stage by stage

Question Text: After the sigmoid is applied, what happens to the result of a positive increase in the log-odds score?
Options (A|B|C|D): The predicted positive-class probability rises | The predicted probability is forced to decrease | The split's information gain becomes zero | The example's class label becomes missing
Correct Answer: The predicted positive-class probability rises

Question Text: Which belief about converting log-odds to probability in XGBoost is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That log-odds are an additive internal representation | That the internal score is already a probability | That a log-odds value of 0 maps to 0.5 | That a conversion step is required at all
Correct Answer: That the internal score is already a probability

Question Text: What role does the logistic function play in XGBoost classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): It maps the ensemble's raw score to a positive-class probability | It regularizes the magnitude of every computed tree leaf output value | It computes the cover statistic that gets used at each tree node | It selects the single best feature split inside each grown tree node
Correct Answer: It maps the ensemble's raw score to a positive-class probability

Question Text: Once a new tree has updated the running scores, how does XGBoost apply the logistic function?
Options (A|B|C|D): It uses it to convert information gain into gamma | It applies it to compute the initial prediction only | It applies it to produce updated predicted probabilities | It uses it to replace all future tree building
Correct Answer: It applies it to produce updated predicted probabilities

Question Text: How does increasing the input score affect the output of the logistic function?
Options (A|B|C|D): It triggers automatic pruning of the tree | It yields a higher positive-class probability | It necessarily yields a lower probability | It causes new classes to be created
Correct Answer: It yields a higher positive-class probability

Question Text: Which belief about the logistic function in XGBoost classification is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That it is the same thing as the loss function | That it maps a raw score to a probability | That the loss is a separate, distinct concept | That it is central to producing binary outputs
Correct Answer: That it is the same thing as the loss function

Question Text: Why does the gamma parameter act as a tree-complexity control in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Gamma is just another name for the learning rate | Gamma converts internal scores into probabilities | A split is kept only if its gain exceeds gamma | Gamma sets a hard cap on the number of features
Correct Answer: A split is kept only if its gain exceeds gamma

Question Text: How does raising the gamma parameter help reduce overfitting in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): It forces every leaf to become perfectly pure | It averages all of the trees together into one | It removes the need for any validation set | It discourages weak splits that add little benefit
Correct Answer: It discourages weak splits that add little benefit

Question Text: Which kind of candidate branch is most likely to be removed as the gamma parameter increases?
Options (A|B|C|D): Branches holding positive examples only | Branches whose split has low gain | Only the single root branch | Branches whose split has high gain
Correct Answer: Branches whose split has low gain

Question Text: Which belief about gamma as a complexity control in XGBoost is a misconception to avoid?
Options (A|B|C|D): That it affects only runtime, not model structure | That a higher gamma can produce simpler trees | That it influences whether branches are kept | That it regularizes overall tree complexity
Correct Answer: That it affects only runtime, not model structure

Question Text: In XGBoost, why can the regularization parameter lambda be viewed as controlling how sensitive the model is to individual observations?
Options (A|B|C|D): It shrinks similarity scores and leaf outputs so single observations carry less influence | It converts hard, discrete class labels into smoothed soft probability targets for fitting | It assigns a fixed weight of exactly zero to each individual outlier point that it detects | It deletes statistically flagged observations from the dataset before training begins
Correct Answer: It shrinks similarity scores and leaf outputs so single observations carry less influence

Question Text: As lambda increases in XGBoost, what happens to the influence of a leaf dominated by a few large residuals?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is forced to become the tree root | The number of input features changes | Its output correction is reduced | Its output correction always grows larger
Correct Answer: Its output correction is reduced

Question Text: Why is reducing the model's sensitivity to individual observations (via lambda) useful in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is genuinely relevant only for unsupervised, unlabeled clustering-style tasks | It can help prevent overfitting to small or noisy parts of the training data | It removes any need at all to build decision trees within the boosting model | It firmly guarantees that the model will deliberately underfit the training data
Correct Answer: It can help prevent overfitting to small or noisy parts of the training data

Question Text: How is lambda's dampening of observation influence reflected in the XGBoost formulas?
Options (A|B|C|D): Lambda appears in the denominators that dampen the score and output magnitudes | Lambda is added straight onto the supplied target labels before any model fitting | Lambda flips the algebraic sign of each residual that gets computed at a tree node | Lambda automatically sets the value of the gamma complexity parameter for you
Correct Answer: Lambda appears in the denominators that dampen the score and output magnitudes

Question Text: Which misunderstanding about lambda as a sensitivity control should be avoided in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): That it shrinks leaf outputs by appearing within its own denominator terms | That it can genuinely reduce the predictive influence of single observations | That it serves as one of XGBoost's central regularization parameters here | That it touches only tree pruning, not how strongly leaves shift predictions
Correct Answer: That it touches only tree pruning, not how strongly leaves shift predictions

Question Text: What is the main similarity between XGBoost used for regression and XGBoost used for classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): Both depend on a single set of identical formulas all the way through | Both add boosted trees in sequence to refine the current predictions | Both produce calibrated probabilities as their sole form of model output | Both are unsupervised methods that learn without any labeled targets
Correct Answer: Both add boosted trees in sequence to refine the current predictions

Question Text: What is a key difference between regression and classification XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Regression relies on boosted trees while classification simply uses none | Regression alone begins with no initial baseline prediction to boost from | They use different objective functions and different output interpretations | Classification alone is structurally unable to apply any regularization here
Correct Answer: They use different objective functions and different output interpretations

Question Text: In regression XGBoost, what do the outputs of each added tree usually correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Errors in the numeric predictions | The number of features in the data | The margins of support vectors | Only the predicted class probabilities
Correct Answer: Errors in the numeric predictions

Question Text: In classification XGBoost, what do the outputs of each added tree usually correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): The assignments of individual data points to their clusters | Errors that were made in choosing the depth of the tree | The raw, unprocessed values of the model's input features | Score or probability errors under a classification loss
Correct Answer: Score or probability errors under a classification loss

Question Text: Which misunderstanding should be avoided when comparing regression and classification XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): That both remain additive boosted-tree methods built on a shared procedure | That they are wholly unrelated algorithms, not task versions of one framework | That their core formulas differ depending on the chosen objective function | That the outputs they each produce are ultimately interpreted in different ways
Correct Answer: That they are wholly unrelated algorithms, not task versions of one framework

Question Text: Why is XGBoost described as an additive tree-based model?
Options (A|B|C|D): It relies on only a single decision tree when forming all of its predictions | It keeps adding brand-new input features instead of ever adding any new trees | The final prediction sums an initial value plus many later tree contributions | It averages all of its trees together equally with no real ordering at all
Correct Answer: The final prediction sums an initial value plus many later tree contributions

Question Text: In XGBoost's additive model, what does each newly added tree contribute?
Options (A|B|C|D): A freshly relabeled set of the training targets | A representative centroid for one data cluster | A correction to the current running prediction | A fixed threshold value used when pruning trees
Correct Answer: A correction to the current running prediction

Question Text: How does the learning rate interact with XGBoost's additive prediction?
Options (A|B|C|D): It selects the model's initial baseline prediction | It converts a regression model into a classifier | It removes the need for the lambda parameter | It scales each tree's contribution before it is summed in
Correct Answer: It scales each tree's contribution before it is summed in

Question Text: Why can an additive ensemble of trees be a powerful predictor?
Options (A|B|C|D): Additivity forces the model to stay linear in the inputs | Only the single largest tree truly matters | Many small corrections combine into a flexible predictor | It blocks any use of regularization terms
Correct Answer: Many small corrections combine into a flexible predictor

Question Text: Which misunderstanding about additive prediction in XGBoost should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That only the final tree determines the model's output | That the ensemble is built up in a stagewise additive way | That all earlier trees remain part of the running sum | That the prediction is updated cumulatively over trees
Correct Answer: That only the final tree determines the model's output

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regularization and Pruning in XGBoost Trees
Question Text: What are two major regularization ideas built into XGBoost trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): Feature scaling combined with PCA | Bootstrap bagging combined with dropout | Pruning with gamma and shrinkage with lambda | Splitting only by entropy and by Gini impurity
Correct Answer: Pruning with gamma and shrinkage with lambda

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regularization and Pruning in XGBoost Trees
Question Text: Why does XGBoost include regularization while building its trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): To fully eliminate any need to compute residuals during fitting | To convert the boosting procedure into an unsupervised method | To help prevent overfitting while keeping strong predictive power | To guarantee perfectly flawless accuracy on the full training set
Correct Answer: To help prevent overfitting while keeping strong predictive power

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regularization and Pruning in XGBoost Trees
Question Text: How do the gamma and lambda parameters play different roles in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Gamma applies only to regression tasks while lambda applies to classification | Gamma decides whether branches are kept; lambda shrinks scores and outputs | Gamma shrinks the resulting leaf outputs while lambda keeps useful branches | Gamma and lambda are simply the same single hyperparameter under two names
Correct Answer: Gamma decides whether branches are kept; lambda shrinks scores and outputs

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regularization and Pruning in XGBoost Trees
Question Text: Why can pruning and shrinkage controls improve an XGBoost model's generalization?
Options (A|B|C|D): They force every individual tree to fit its training data exactly | They strip away the additive boosted structure of the model | They discourage overly complex or aggressive tree corrections | They make any later use of cross-validation fully unnecessary
Correct Answer: They discourage overly complex or aggressive tree corrections

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Regularization and Pruning in XGBoost Trees
Question Text: Which misunderstanding about XGBoost regularization should be avoided?
Options (A|B|C|D): That improving the model's generalization is one of its central design goals | That gamma and lambda both genuinely act as real regularization terms here | That its power comes only from adding trees, not from controlling complexity | That both pruning and shrinkage meaningfully influence the final model outcome
Correct Answer: That its power comes only from adding trees, not from controlling complexity

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: What is the standard way to apply a ReLU activation to a Dense layer in a Sequential model?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: During training with fit(), what does a single 'epoch' correspond to?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: What is the main purpose of the model.compile() step in the Keras training workflow?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: In a Sequential model, how is a per-layer activation function usually specified?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: If model.compile() uses the BinaryCrossentropy loss, what should the model's final layer be designed to output?
Options (A|B|C|D): An unbounded real value on (-inf, +inf) | The learned weights of the hidden layers | A probability between 0 and 1 | The number of epochs left in training

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: In the call model.fit(X, y, epochs=10), what does the value 10 specify?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: Which Keras layer creates a fully connected layer in which every input connects to every output neuron?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: In the call model.fit(X, y, epochs=100), what does the value 100 determine?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: How is an activation such as 'relu' or 'sigmoid' usually assigned to a layer in a Sequential model?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Running K-means from several random centroid initializations and keeping the best result is most worthwhile for which range of the number of clusters K?

Correct Answer: K roughly between 2 and 10

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: After running K-means from 50 to 1000 different random initializations, which criterion selects the final clustering to keep?
Options (A|B|C|D): The run with the smallest distortion J(c, mu) | The run with the most spread-out centroids | The run that took the most iterations | The run with the fewest points per cluster
Correct Answer: The run with the smallest distortion J(c, mu)

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: About how many random initializations (complete runs of K-means) are typically recommended to obtain a high-quality clustering?
Options (A|B|C|D): Somewhere from 50 to 1000 | Exactly 10,000 runs | Just 1 to 5 runs | No fewer than 1 million
Correct Answer: Somewhere from 50 to 1000

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: How are the individual layers usually supplied to the tf.keras.Sequential constructor?
Options (A|B|C|D): As a dictionary keyed by layer name | As comma-separated positional args | As a list of layer objects | Wrapped inside a nested tuple
Correct Answer: As a list of layer objects

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: When creating a tf.keras.layers.Dense layer, which parameter sets the number of neurons in that layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): units | size | shape | neurons
Correct Answer: units

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: When K-means uses a very large number of clusters (e.g., K = 400), why are multiple random initializations usually less critical than for small K?
Options (A|B|C|D): The distortion cost function genuinely applies only when the value of K is small | Such a large K conveniently places every single centroid right at the global optimum | Such a large value of K stops the algorithm from reaching any local minimum | Each separate run is costly and the very first initialization is usually good enough
Correct Answer: Each separate run is costly and the very first initialization is usually good enough

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: When using multiple random initializations of K-means, what does a single 'run' consist of?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single cluster-assignment step performed just one time only | An elbow-method search sweeping K values to pick out the best one | A single centroid-update step carried out just one single time | K-means from one random start, iterated through to convergence
Correct Answer: K-means from one random start, iterated through to convergence

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: In model.compile(), which argument specifies the function the model is trained to minimize?

Correct Answer: loss

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential

Correct Answer: How many full passes are made over the training data

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: How is a nonlinear activation such as 'relu' or 'sigmoid' normally attached to a Dense layer in a Sequential model?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: For which range of the number of clusters K is it most common and useful to run K-means from multiple random initializations?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only in the special case K equals 1 | Only when K exceeds the example count | K roughly between 2 and 10 | K roughly between 100 and 1000
Correct Answer: K roughly between 2 and 10

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: What is the main trade-off when raising the number of random K-means initializations from 10 to 1000?
Options (A|B|C|D): Total run time grows roughly linearly with the runs | The number of clusters K must rise to match | The final distortion cost is guaranteed to rise | Finding a global minimum becomes less likely
Correct Answer: Total run time grows roughly linearly with the runs

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Across several random initializations of K-means, what does the distortion cost function J let you determine?
Options (A|B|C|D): The number of features in the dataset | Which random run gave the best clustering | The best learning rate for gradient descent | The maximum iterations the run may take
Correct Answer: Which random run gave the best clustering

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Why are multiple random initializations often seen as less necessary when K is very large (e.g., K = 500)?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single random start usually already gives a good result | K-means will keep merging nearby clusters together on its own | The distortion cost J cannot be computed for very large K | Clusterings with large K simply have no local minima at all
Correct Answer: A single random start usually already gives a good result

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: When you perform 50 random initializations of K-means, which quantity are you trying to make as small as possible across the runs?
Options (A|B|C|D): The average spacing between the initial chosen centroids | The total count m of data points in the training set | Sum of squared distances from points to their centroids | The number of iterations the algorithm needs to converge
Correct Answer: Sum of squared distances from points to their centroids

Question Text: Inside a per-neuron loop, which expression correctly computes the scalar z for neuron j (weights w, input a_in, bias b) before the activation is applied?
Options (A|B|C|D): z = np.sum(w + a_in) * b[j] | z = w * a_in * b[j] | z = np.dot(W, b) + a_in | z = np.dot(w, a_in) + b[j]

Question Text: If the input vector a_in has 10 features and the dense layer has 5 units, what is the shape of the weight matrix W when each column holds one unit's weights?

Question Text: How does the bias vector b relate to the neurons in a dense-layer implementation?

Correct Answer: They greatly speed up and streamline the computation

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Building Neural Networks with TensorFlow Sequential
Question Text: What type of object does a Sequential model return when model.predict() is called on a batch of input features?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Running K-means with many random initializations and keeping the best result is generally most worthwhile for which range of K (number of clusters)?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: Which property of the K-means cost function (distortion) is the reason multiple random initializations are recommended?

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Multiple Random Initializations for Better Clustering
Question Text: What is the main trade-off when raising the number of K-means random initializations from 10 to 500?

Question Text: What is a key advantage of TensorFlow tensors over NumPy arrays for deep learning workloads?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are inherently easier for humans to read | They remove the need to define any loss function | They run with hardware acceleration on GPUs and TPUs | They alone are able to store floating-point numbers
Correct Answer: They run with hardware acceleration on GPUs and TPUs

Question Text: Which call is commonly used to turn a Python list or NumPy array into a TensorFlow tensor?
Options (A|B|C|D): tf.make_array() | tf.create() | tf.to_list() | tf.constant()
Correct Answer: tf.constant()

Question Text: Regarding mutability, how does a standard TensorFlow constant tensor differ from a NumPy array?
Options (A|B|C|D): Both can be modified only while connected to the cloud | The tensor is mutable, while the NumPy array cannot be changed | Both are strictly immutable once they have been created | The tensor is immutable, while the NumPy array allows in-place edits
Correct Answer: The tensor is immutable, while the NumPy array allows in-place edits

Question Text: Why is NumPy frequently used alongside TensorFlow in a machine learning pipeline?
Options (A|B|C|D): TensorFlow handles only text data and never numeric data | TensorFlow cannot perform any mathematical operations itself | It is convenient for preprocessing and loading data before conversion | It is required to power on and initialize the GPU
Correct Answer: It is convenient for preprocessing and loading data before conversion

Question Text: Which TensorFlow capability lets tensors record the operations applied to them so gradients can be computed for training?
Options (A|B|C|D): Fully manual, user-driven memory management | A binary-only on-disk storage format | Reliance on ordinary Python lists for storage | Automatic differentiation via computational graphs
Correct Answer: Automatic differentiation via computational graphs

Question Text: In density-estimation anomaly detection, an example is flagged when its estimated probability falls below a value epsilon. What does epsilon represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The probability threshold separating normal from anomalous points | The total number of input features measured in the training set | The learning rate used by the optimization algorithm during fitting | The mean of the fitted normal distribution over the features
Correct Answer: The probability threshold separating normal from anomalous points

Question Text: Which scenario is best suited to anomaly detection via density estimation rather than to standard supervised learning?
Options (A|B|C|D): You have millions of clearly labeled anomalous examples to train on | You must predict a specific category label for every data point | You have very few positive (anomalous) examples but many normal ones | You only need to compute the average value of the data points
Correct Answer: You have very few positive (anomalous) examples but many normal ones

Question Text: A density-estimation model computes p(x_test) for a new point and compares it to threshold epsilon. If p(x_test) >= epsilon, how is the point classified?
Options (A|B|C|D): It indicates the model needs more training data | It signals that the features must be rescaled | It is treated as a normal (non-anomalous) example | It is immediately flagged as an anomaly
Correct Answer: It is treated as a normal (non-anomalous) example

Question Text: A website builds a model p(x) to detect fraudulent user activity by flagging behavior with low probability. Which set of features would most plausibly drive p(x)?
Options (A|B|C|D): Typing speed, login frequency, and access location | The user's preferred website color scheme | The alphabetical ranking of the username | The brand printed on the user's keyboard
Correct Answer: Typing speed, login frequency, and access location

Question Text: In a density-estimation system that flags points with p(x) below threshold epsilon, what is the most likely effect of setting epsilon extremely high?
Options (A|B|C|D): The system stops flagging any point as an anomaly | Almost every data point gets flagged as an anomaly | The probability p(x) rises automatically for all points | The stored training data is erased from the model
Correct Answer: Almost every data point gets flagged as an anomaly

Question Text: In a probabilistic anomaly detection model that fits p(x) and uses threshold epsilon, when is an input x classified as an anomaly?
Options (A|B|C|D): When p(x) is greater than the threshold epsilon | When p(x) is exactly equal to one | When p(x) equals the mean of the distribution | When p(x) is less than the threshold epsilon
Correct Answer: When p(x) is less than the threshold epsilon

Question Text: While building a Gaussian-based anomaly detector, you find that one feature's histogram is strongly non-Gaussian (heavily skewed). What is the recommended response?
Options (A|B|C|D): Leave it unchanged, since Gaussian models prefer skewed inputs | Apply a transform such as log(x) or sqrt(x) to make it more Gaussian | Scale the feature up by a large constant factor | Drop the feature from the model right away
Correct Answer: Apply a transform such as log(x) or sqrt(x) to make it more Gaussian

Question Text: Why is overall (total) accuracy usually a poor metric for evaluating an anomaly detection system?
Options (A|B|C|D): Anomalies are so rare that always predicting 'normal' still scores high accuracy | Anomaly detection involves no real mathematical computation that could be scored | Accuracy is a metric that is mathematically defined only for regression problems | Accuracy can be computed and reported only with a full 3D visualization plot
Correct Answer: Anomalies are so rare that always predicting 'normal' still scores high accuracy

Question Text: You have 10,000 normal examples and 20 anomalous examples. Which data split is most appropriate for building and evaluating an anomaly detector?
Options (A|B|C|D): Put all 20 of the anomalous examples directly into the training set for fitting | Train on mostly normal data; split the rare anomalies across the CV and test sets | Discard the anomalies entirely, since they are not really needed anywhere | Train on 10 of the anomalies and then evaluate on the other 10 anomalies
Correct Answer: Train on mostly normal data; split the rare anomalies across the CV and test sets

Question Text: Compared with supervised learning, in which situation is anomaly detection specifically more advantageous?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the dataset is perfectly balanced between two classes | When future anomalies may look nothing like those seen so far | When you have a very large number of positive examples to learn from | When the goal is to predict a continuous price value
Correct Answer: When future anomalies may look nothing like those seen so far

Question Text: In the common biological analogy for an artificial neuron, which part of a biological neuron corresponds to the inputs the artificial neuron receives?
Options (A|B|C|D): The myelin sheath | The nucleus | The axon | The dendrites
Correct Answer: The dendrites

Question Text: In an artificial neuron, which component plays the role that biological 'synaptic strength' plays between two connected neurons?
Options (A|B|C|D): The weights | The activation function | The bias term | The input vector
Correct Answer: The weights

Question Text: What is a primary difference in how biological neurons and artificial neurons communicate information?
Options (A|B|C|D): Artificial neurons faithfully reproduce every detail of biological neurons | Biological neurons fire discrete electrical spikes, whereas artificial neurons pass continuous numeric values | Biological neurons rely on matrix multiplication, whereas artificial neurons rely on chemical signals | Biological neurons have no connections to any other neurons
Correct Answer: Biological neurons fire discrete electrical spikes, whereas artificial neurons pass continuous numeric values

Question Text: Why do modern researchers treat the brain-to-network analogy as loose inspiration rather than a strict design guide?
Options (A|B|C|D): Biological brains are now known not to rely on electricity at all to operate | Artificial neurons in practice are physically built from real organic material | Biology is far too simple to ever supply any useful engineering ideas at all | Key training methods like backpropagation lack a clear biological counterpart
Correct Answer: Key training methods like backpropagation lack a clear biological counterpart

Question Text: Considering how information flows through a neuron, the biological axon is most comparable to which part of an artificial neuron model?
Options (A|B|C|D): The summation step | The learning rate | The output signal | The input features
Correct Answer: The output signal

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: Traditional machine learning often relies on manually engineered features. Viewing deep learning as 'learned feature engineering,' how does it differ?
Options (A|B|C|D): It folds feature extraction directly into the model's optimization process | It completely removes the need for any data preprocessing step whatsoever | It demands even more manual feature selection than linear models ever do | It swaps the entire learning process out for fixed, hard-coded human rules
Correct Answer: It folds feature extraction directly into the model's optimization process

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: Which statement best describes the hierarchical nature of feature learning across the layers of a multi-layer network?
Options (A|B|C|D): Successive layers turn raw inputs into increasingly abstract representations | Hidden layers simply store exact copies of the input data | Every layer learns the same level of feature detail at once | Early layers capture abstractions while later layers recover raw pixels
Correct Answer: Successive layers turn raw inputs into increasingly abstract representations

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: In end-to-end learning, the network is trained jointly from raw input to final output. What is a primary advantage of this for feature engineering?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees every learned feature is easy for humans to interpret | The features themselves are tuned to minimize the task's loss function | It stops the model from relying on gradients during training | It makes the choice of network architecture entirely irrelevant
Correct Answer: The features themselves are tuned to minimize the task's loss function

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: How do hidden layers typically help a network solve problems that are not linearly separable in the original input space?
Options (A|B|C|D): By mapping inputs into a new feature space where the classes separate | By ignoring the inputs and attending only to the labels | By ensuring no transformation is applied to the input signal | By shrinking the network down to a single neuron
Correct Answer: By mapping inputs into a new feature space where the classes separate

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Neural Networks as Learned Feature Engineering
Question Text: Which of the following is a common challenge when treating neural networks as automated feature engineers?
Options (A|B|C|D): Neural networks are unable to process multi-dimensional inputs | Manual feature engineering is always more accurate than learned features | The networks simply refuse to learn from large datasets | The learned features act as black boxes that are hard to interpret
Correct Answer: The learned features act as black boxes that are hard to interpret

Question Text: Which activation function is defined by the expression f(x) = max(0, x), outputting x when x is positive and 0 otherwise?
Options (A|B|C|D): Rectified linear unit (ReLU) | Logistic sigmoid | Leaky rectified linear unit | Hyperbolic tangent (tanh)
Correct Answer: Rectified linear unit (ReLU)

Question Text: Why are non-linear activation functions placed between the hidden layers of a neural network?
Options (A|B|C|D): They let stacked layers model non-linear relationships | They speed up matrix multiplication on a GPU | They keep the weight values from ever reaching zero | They lower the memory needed to store the weights
Correct Answer: They let stacked layers model non-linear relationships

Question Text: If every layer of a deep neural network uses only a linear (identity) activation, what is the effective expressive power of the whole model?
Options (A|B|C|D): It behaves as a single linear transformation | It can approximate any non-linear function | It doubles its parameter count automatically | It turns into an unsupervised clustering model
Correct Answer: It behaves as a single linear transformation

Question Text: Which output-layer activation is most appropriate for multi-class classification where each input belongs to exactly one of several mutually exclusive classes?
Options (A|B|C|D): ReLU | Softmax | Logistic sigmoid | Linear (identity)
Correct Answer: Softmax

Question Text: Using g for the activation function, W and b for a layer's weights and bias, what is the standard formula for the activation vector a^[l] of layer l in terms of a^[l-1]?
By convention, when reporting the number of layers in a neural network, which layer is not counted in the total?
Options (A|B|C|D): The final activation layer | The output layer | The input layer | The first hidden layer
Correct Answer: The input layer

Question Text: In neural network design, what does the term 'depth' of a network refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The count of hidden plus output layers | The largest value the activation can take | The bit precision used to store weights | The number of neurons in the input layer
Correct Answer: The count of hidden plus output layers

Question Text: In a fully connected (dense) layer, how are its neurons connected to the previous layer?
Options (A|B|C|D): Neurons link only to their neighbors in the same layer | Each neuron links to just one previous-layer neuron | Each neuron links to every previous-layer neuron | Neurons link directly to the output layer only
Correct Answer: Each neuron links to every previous-layer neuron

Question Text: What does the 'width' of a hidden layer describe?
Options (A|B|C|D): The gap between the input and output layers | The number of neurons in that layer | The spread of values in the input vector x | The number of training iterations needed
Correct Answer: The number of neurons in that layer

Question Text: If a network's layers are indexed from 0 to L, what does the quantity a^[L] typically represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The raw input features | The weights of the first layer | The bias of the hidden layers | The output layer's activation
Correct Answer: The output layer's activation

Question Text: Inside a single neuron during forward propagation, what are the two computations performed, in order?
Options (A|B|C|D): Add the weights, then subtract the bias term | Compute weights·inputs + bias, then apply g(z) | Pick one weight at random, then scale the input | Differentiate the input, then apply a sigmoid
Correct Answer: Compute weights·inputs + bias, then apply g(z)

Question Text: Why is forward propagation usually implemented with vectorized matrix operations instead of explicit Python for-loops over neurons?
Options (A|B|C|D): It removes the need to ever run backpropagation | Vectorized math runs far faster on CPUs and GPUs | For-loops cannot evaluate ReLU activations at all | It cuts the number of parameters the model needs
Correct Answer: Vectorized math runs far faster on CPUs and GPUs

Question Text: In a network with layers indexed 1, 2, 3, why must activation a[1] be computed before a[2]?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because backprop runs between each pair | Because bias b[2] is derived from a[1] | Because a[1] is always initialized to zero | Because a[2] takes a[1] as its input
Correct Answer: Because a[2] takes a[1] as its input

Question Text: During forward propagation, how is the activation of the input layer a[0] typically defined?
Options (A|B|C|D): As a vector filled with ones | As the weight matrix W[1] | As the feature vector x | As the final prediction y-hat
Correct Answer: As the feature vector x

Question Text: What is the primary purpose of applying an activation function such as sigmoid or ReLU during forward propagation?
Options (A|B|C|D): To compute the loss between output and target | To update the weights as the model trains | To inject non-linearity into the model's output | To rescale the input features into the 0-to-1 range
Correct Answer: To inject non-linearity into the model's output

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: When feeding an 8x8 pixel image into a fully connected network, the 2D grid is reshaped into a single 1D vector of 64 values. What is this step called?
Options (A|B|C|D): Scaling | Pooling | Flattening | Normalization
Correct Answer: Flattening

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: Which activation function is commonly used in the hidden layers of a modern digit-recognition network so it can learn complex non-linear patterns?
Options (A|B|C|D): Constant output | Hard step function | Identity (linear) | ReLU
Correct Answer: ReLU

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: For a network classifying digits 0-9 whose output is a softmax over 10 classes with integer labels, which loss function is most suitable?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sparse categorical cross-entropy | Huber regression loss | Binary cross-entropy loss | Mean squared error loss
Correct Answer: Sparse categorical cross-entropy

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: In a deep network for handwritten digit recognition, what do the earliest hidden layers typically learn to detect?
Options (A|B|C|D): The average brightness of the image | Simple features like edges and strokes | The complete digit shape in one step | The final per-class probabilities
Correct Answer: Simple features like edges and strokes

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Handwritten Digit Recognition with Neural Networks
Question Text: If the input layer has 64 features and the first hidden layer has 25 neurons in a dense network, how many weight parameters connect the two layers, excluding biases?
When defining the first Dense layer in a tf.keras Sequential model, which constructor argument tells the layer the dimensions of each input sample?
Options (A|B|C|D): input_units | input_shape | batch_size | kernel_size
Correct Answer: input_shape

Question Text: If the 'activation' argument is omitted when constructing a tf.keras Dense layer, which behavior is applied to the layer's output?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sigmoid is applied | Softmax is applied | No nonlinearity (linear) | ReLU is applied
Correct Answer: No nonlinearity (linear)

Question Text: Which tf.keras class lets you build a model by stacking Dense layers in a simple linear order, one feeding into the next?
Options (A|B|C|D): tf.keras.layers.Stack | tf.keras.Sequential | tf.keras.ModelGroup | tf.Module
Correct Answer: tf.keras.Sequential

Question Text: A Dense layer computes activation(inputs · kernel + bias). What does the 'kernel' variable hold?
Options (A|B|C|D): The weights matrix | The activation map | The bias vector | The layer's inputs
Correct Answer: The weights matrix

Question Text: Which argument of the Dense layer constructor controls the starting values assigned to the weights before training?
Options (A|B|C|D): activation | trainable | kernel_initializer | bias_regularizer
Correct Answer: kernel_initializer

Question Text: Which statement best captures what lim x→a f(x) means?
Options (A|B|C|D): The value that f must equal exactly at the point x = a | The slope of the graph of f at the single point x = a | The value f approaches near a, even if f(a) differs | The total area under the curve of f from 0 out to a
Correct Answer: The value f approaches near a, even if f(a) differs

Question Text: A function approaches 3 as x → 2 from the left and approaches 5 as x → 2 from the right. What can you conclude about lim x→2 f(x)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It exists and is equal to the left-hand value 3 | It exists only in the case where f(2) equals 4 | It exists and equals the average of the two, 4 | It does not exist (one-sided limits differ)
Correct Answer: It does not exist (one-sided limits differ)

Question Text: When direct substitution into a limit produces the indeterminate form 0/0, what does that result tell you?
