Options (A|B|C|D): First-In, First-Out | First-In, Last-Out | Lowest-Index, First-Out | Last-In, First-Out
Correct Answer: Last-In, First-Out

Question Text: Which stack operation removes and usually returns the top item?
Options (A|B|C|D): peek | pop | enqueue | search
Correct Answer: pop

Question Text: Which real-world feature is modeled most naturally by a stack?
Options (A|B|C|D): Print jobs served in order | Taxi requests by arrival time | Routing on a weighted graph | Undo history in an editor
Correct Answer: Undo history in an editor

Question Text: In a linked-list stack, why are both push and pop O(1)?
Options (A|B|C|D): A stack holds at most one value | They touch only the top/head node | Python caches the prior results | The stack is kept sorted each time
Correct Answer: They touch only the top/head node

Question Text: Which built-in Python class provides stack (LIFO) behavior directly?
Options (A|B|C|D): queue.SimpleQueue | the built-in tuple | the built-in dict | queue.LifoQueue
Correct Answer: queue.LifoQueue

Question Text: Which term refers to the single top node of a tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Edge | Child | Leaf | Root
Correct Answer: Root

Question Text: What property makes a tree a binary tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every node has at most two children | All nodes store only numeric values | The root node alone has two children | Every node has exactly two children
Correct Answer: Every node has at most two children

Question Text: Which description of the parent/child relationship in a tree is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): The root is the deepest node | A parent sits below its child | A child connects to many roots | It links nodes in a hierarchy
Correct Answer: It links nodes in a hierarchy

Question Text: Which application is a real use of a tree structure?
Options (A|B|C|D): Constant-time array indexing | A file system directory layout | Fast hash-table key lookups | First-in first-out scheduling
Correct Answer: A file system directory layout

Question Text: In a tree, what does a node's "level" refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The key value stored at the node | The count of its direct children | Its depth layer in the hierarchy | Total edges across the whole tree
Correct Answer: Its depth layer in the hierarchy

Question Text: In graph theory, what is a graph?
Options (A|B|C|D): An ordered list of distinct tuples | A queue of weighted priority items | A set of nodes connected by edges | A binary tree containing no cycles
Correct Answer: A set of nodes connected by edges

Question Text: Which statement correctly describes a directed graph?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every edge is required to carry the same weight | An edge A to B does not imply an edge B to A | Each node is limited to at most two children | Every node must be connected to all others
Correct Answer: An edge A to B does not imply an edge B to A

Question Text: What makes a graph a weighted graph?
Options (A|B|C|D): It automatically runs a breadth-first search over all its nodes | Its edges each carry a numeric value, such as distance or cost | It must always contain at least one closed cycle of its edges | It is restricted to storing only large individual data items
Correct Answer: Its edges each carry a numeric value, such as distance or cost

Question Text: Which Python data structure is commonly used to represent a basic graph as an adjacency list?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single integer value that stores one chosen vertex's own index | A tuple that holds nothing more than disjoint sets of the vertices | A queue object whose only job is to order all the graph's nodes | A dictionary that maps each vertex to a list of adjacent vertices
Correct Answer: A dictionary that maps each vertex to a list of adjacent vertices

Question Text: Which statement correctly contrasts trees with general graphs?
Options (A|B|C|D): Trees are allowed to contain cycles quite freely, whereas general graphs never may | Graphs can never carry any edge weights, yet trees are always permitted to carry one | Trees have no cycles and stay fully connected, while graphs may split or loop freely | Graphs always come bundled with one single root node, but ordinary trees never do
Correct Answer: Trees have no cycles and stay fully connected, while graphs may split or loop freely

Question Text: Which two components must a correct recursive function include?
Options (A|B|C|D): A separate loop and a dictionary | A class definition and an object | A base case and a recursive case | An explicit queue and a stack
Correct Answer: A base case and a recursive case

Question Text: What is the main purpose of memoization?
Options (A|B|C|D): To replace each and every recursive call with an equivalent plain loop | To store results of repeated subproblems and skip later recomputation | To deliberately increase a program's overall memory usage on purpose | To keep all of the cache's stored values fully sorted at every moment
Correct Answer: To store results of repeated subproblems and skip later recomputation

Question Text: What is the likely result of running recursive code that has no correct base case?
Options (A|B|C|D): It quietly collapses down to a constant O(1) running time | It returns None right after it makes only a single quick call | It silently switches itself over to ordinary plain iteration | It raises a RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
Correct Answer: It raises a RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded

Question Text: In a memoized recursive Fibonacci function, what happens before a new value is computed?
Options (A|B|C|D): The function converts the argument n into a string | The function clears the cache of all entries | The function checks whether that result is already cached | The function sorts the cache by key order
Correct Answer: The function checks whether that result is already cached

Question Text: Why is dynamic programming considered an optimization over plain recursion for problems like Fibonacci?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because Python disallows recursive functions that lack a cache | Because dynamic programming removes the need for any base case | Because plain recursion is unable to solve such problems at all | Because repeated subproblems would otherwise be recomputed many times
Correct Answer: Because repeated subproblems would otherwise be recomputed many times

Question Text: Which statement best describes linear search?
Options (A|B|C|D): It must always be implemented using recursion rather than a simple loop | It repeatedly halves the remaining sorted search space at each step | It checks each element until the target is found or the whole list ends | It can only ever be applied to the keys stored in a Python dictionary
Correct Answer: It checks each element until the target is found or the whole list ends

Question Text: Which condition must hold before binary search can be applied to a list?
Options (A|B|C|D): The list must already be sorted | The list must be stored as a linked list | The list must contain only unique values | The target value must sit at index 0
Correct Answer: The list must already be sorted

Question Text: In an iterative binary search using pointers named first and last, how is the middle index computed?
Options (A|B|C|D): middle = len(list) / 2 | middle = search_value // 2 | middle = (first + last) // 2 | middle = first + last
Correct Answer: middle = (first + last) // 2

Question Text: During binary search, if search_value is less than ordered_list[middle], what is the next step?
Options (A|B|C|D): Restart the search from index 0 | Set last = middle - 1 | Return False right away | Set first = middle + 1
Correct Answer: Set last = middle - 1

Question Text: Which pairing of worst-case time complexities is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Both linear and binary search are O(n^2) | Linear search is O(n), binary search is O(log n) | Linear search is O(log n), binary search is O(n) | Both linear and binary search are O(1)
Correct Answer: Linear search is O(n), binary search is O(log n)

Question Text: Which ordering rule defines a binary search tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): Left subtree values are less than the node and right subtree values are greater | Left subtree values are greater than the node and right subtree values are less | Every node is required to have exactly two children | The root node always holds the smallest value
Correct Answer: Left subtree values are less than the node and right subtree values are greater

Question Text: How does a search in a binary search tree decide whether to move left or right at each step?
Options (A|B|C|D): By comparing the target value to the current node's value | By following first-in, first-out queue order | By choosing the side that currently has fewer nodes | By sorting the entire tree before each step
Correct Answer: By comparing the target value to the current node's value

Question Text: When deleting a node that has two children from a binary search tree, what is the standard approach?
Options (A|B|C|D): Delete that node's entire subtree | Convert the whole tree to a list first | Swap the node with the tree's root | Replace the node with its in-order successor
Correct Answer: Replace the node with its in-order successor

Question Text: When deleting a node with two children from a binary search tree, which node serves as its in-order successor?
Options (A|B|C|D): The largest node in its left subtree | The smallest node in its right subtree | The parent of the current node | The root node of the whole tree
Correct Answer: The smallest node in its right subtree

Question Text: Why is in-order traversal especially significant for a binary search tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It visits only the tree's leaf nodes | It reverses the structure of the tree | It guarantees constant O(1) search time | It visits the values in ascending sorted order
Correct Answer: It visits the values in ascending sorted order

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Depth First (DFS) vs. Breadth First (BFS) Search
Question Text: What is the core traversal pattern of breadth-first search (BFS)?
Options (A|B|C|D): Processes the graph's edges strictly in order of increasing weight | Visits nodes level by level, ordered by their distance from start | Goes as deep as it possibly can go, and only then it backtracks | Always descends into the single leftmost child node first of all
Correct Answer: Visits nodes level by level, ordered by their distance from start

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Depth First (DFS) vs. Breadth First (BFS) Search
Question Text: Which data structure naturally drives a breadth-first search (BFS)?
Options (A|B|C|D): A stack (last-in, first-out) | A queue (first-in, first-out) | A min-heap keyed on edge weight | A plain set with no ordering
Correct Answer: A queue (first-in, first-out)

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Depth First (DFS) vs. Breadth First (BFS) Search
Question Text: Why must DFS and BFS on a graph keep track of visited nodes?
Options (A|B|C|D): To keep the graph's adjacency list fully sorted at all times | To guarantee that the resulting path found is the shortest one | To stop cycles from causing repeat visits or endless looping | To convert the original graph into a minimum spanning tree
Correct Answer: To stop cycles from causing repeat visits or endless looping

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Depth First (DFS) vs. Breadth First (BFS) Search
Question Text: In which situation is breadth-first search (BFS) usually the better choice over DFS?
Options (A|B|C|D): When only recursive backtracking is allowed | When the target is likely near the start node | When edge weights must always be minimized | When the graph contains no edges at all
Correct Answer: When the target is likely near the start node

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Depth First (DFS) vs. Breadth First (BFS) Search
Question Text: Which statement correctly describes depth-first search (DFS)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It needs no visited set even when graphs contain cycles | It works only on trees and never on any general graph at all | It explores deep via recursion or a stack, then backtracks | It visits nodes level by level using a first-in queue
Correct Answer: It explores deep via recursion or a stack, then backtracks

Question Text: What is the central idea of selection sort?
Options (A|B|C|D): Compare only adjacent items, swapping until sorted | Insert each value into a BST, then read it in order | Merge two halves that are already sorted | Find the smallest remaining item and swap it into place
Correct Answer: Find the smallest remaining item and swap it into place

Question Text: How does insertion sort build up sorted order?
Options (A|B|C|D): It partitions the list around a chosen pivot value | It pushes the largest value to the end on each pass | It replaces the current node with its in-order successor | It grows a sorted left side, placing each new item in its spot
Correct Answer: It grows a sorted left side, placing each new item in its spot

Question Text: What is the average- and worst-case time complexity of bubble, selection, and insertion sort?
Options (A|B|C|D): O(n log n) | O(log n) | O(1) | O(n^2)

Question Text: In an optimized bubble sort, an is_sorted flag is set to True at the start of each pass. Why?
Options (A|B|C|D): To assume order holds unless a swap proves otherwise | To force one extra, unnecessary pass each time | To count how many values still remain unsorted | To switch the routine into selection-sort behavior
Correct Answer: To assume order holds unless a swap proves otherwise

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Divide & Conquer: Merge Sort and Quicksort
Question Text: What divide-and-conquer strategy does merge sort use?
Options (A|B|C|D): Split into halves, sort each recursively, then merge them | Run binary search on the list before sorting it | Repeatedly swap adjacent values until none are out of order | Choose a pivot and scan the list with a single pointer
Correct Answer: Split into halves, sort each recursively, then merge them

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Divide & Conquer: Merge Sort and Quicksort
Question Text: Which slicing correctly splits my_list into two halves for merge sort, given mid = len(my_list) // 2?
Options (A|B|C|D): left = my_list[:mid], right = my_list[mid:] | left = my_list[1:mid], right = my_list[mid+1:] | left = my_list[mid:], right = my_list[:mid] | left = my_list[:], right = []
Correct Answer: left = my_list[:mid], right = my_list[mid:]

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Divide & Conquer: Merge Sort and Quicksort
Question Text: What is a key tradeoff of merge sort?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is fast on large lists but needs O(n) extra space | It is slow on large lists but needs no extra memory | It requires every value in the list to be unique | It works only on linked lists, not arrays
Correct Answer: It is fast on large lists but needs O(n) extra space

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Divide & Conquer: Merge Sort and Quicksort
Question Text: In a quicksort partition that selects the pivot from a fixed end of the subarray, which element is used as the pivot?
Options (A|B|C|D): The middle element | The first element | The smallest element | The largest element
Correct Answer: The first element

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Divide & Conquer: Merge Sort and Quicksort
Question Text: After partitioning in quicksort, why recurse on the ranges up to partition_index - 1 and from partition_index + 1 rather than including partition_index?
Options (A|B|C|D): To force the resulting sort order to become reliably stable here | To exclude the pivot's settled slot and avoid infinite recursion | To keep the combined length of the two sublists exactly unchanged | To guarantee a worst-case running time bounded above by O(log n)
Correct Answer: To exclude the pivot's settled slot and avoid infinite recursion

Question Text: Which statement correctly distinguishes an iterable from an iterator?
Options (A|B|C|D): An iterable yields values one by one, while an iterator is just a stored collection | An iterable must always be a list, while an iterator must always be a generator | An iterable and an iterator are interchangeable, identical objects in Python | An iterable is something you can loop over; an iterator yields values one at a time
Correct Answer: An iterable is something you can loop over; an iterator yields values one at a time

Question Text: Which of the following is an example of an iterable?
Options (A|B|C|D): A list | A single recursive call | Only an instance of queue.SimpleQueue | Only the value returned by next()
Correct Answer: A list

Question Text: What key behavior is characteristic of an iterator?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is consumed and finished once it is exhausted | It works only when iterating over dictionaries | It can always rewind itself back to the start | It always holds every one of its values in memory
Correct Answer: It is consumed and finished once it is exhausted

Question Text: Why can Python loop over lists, strings, ranges, dicts, and files alike?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because Python turns each one into a DataFrame | Because each of them can produce an iterator | Because each of them supports index access | Because each of them is a mutable object
Correct Answer: Because each of them can produce an iterator

Question Text: Which analogy best captures the relationship between an iterable and an iterator?
Options (A|B|C|D): Iterable is a bucket, while the iterator is simply the label printed on it | Iterable is a music player, while the iterator is the playlist loaded into it | Iterable is a returned result, while the iterator is the calling function's name | Iterable is a playlist; iterator is the player moving through it track by track
Correct Answer: Iterable is a playlist; iterator is the player moving through it track by track

Question Text: When you apply the splat operator * to an iterator (for example, inside a function call), what happens to that iterator?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is rewound to its first item | Its remaining values get sorted | It is converted into a dictionary | It is fully unpacked and consumed
Correct Answer: It is fully unpacked and consumed

Question Text: After running nums = [1, 2, 3]; it = iter(nums); print(*it), what is true about it immediately afterward?
Options (A|B|C|D): it points back to the first item | it still yields all three values | it has been turned into a list | it is now exhausted
Correct Answer: it is now exhausted

Question Text: If you call print(*it) on an iterator and then call print(*it) a second time, why does the second call display nothing?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because the call to iter(nums) just returns the value None | Because print() is simply unable to display iterator objects | Because the * unpacking operator works only on string values | Because the first unpacking already exhausted the iterator
Correct Answer: Because the first unpacking already exhausted the iterator

Question Text: An iterator has been exhausted by unpacking it with *, but you need to iterate over the same underlying values again. What should you do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Create a fresh iterator from the data | Call next() with a negative index | Convert the old iterator into a set | Call .append() on the old iterator
Correct Answer: Create a fresh iterator from the data

Question Text: What general principle about iterators does splat (*) unpacking illustrate?
Options (A|B|C|D): Lists themselves can never be iterated more than once | Iterators are single-pass, so unpacking can exhaust them | Calling enumerate() permanently alters the original data | The zip() function always sorts the output it produces
Correct Answer: Iterators are single-pass, so unpacking can exhaust them

Question Text: When a for loop begins iterating over an iterable, what is the first thing it does internally?
Options (A|B|C|D): Checks whether the iterable is already sorted | Calls next() on the original object directly | Converts the iterable into a list of items | Calls iter(iterable) to obtain an iterator
Correct Answer: Calls iter(iterable) to obtain an iterator

Question Text: After obtaining an iterator, how does a for loop keep producing successive values on each pass?
Options (A|B|C|D): By rebuilding the iterable from scratch each pass | By calling next(iterator) repeatedly until StopIteration | By recursively calling itself for every element | By indexing the object from position 0 upward
Correct Answer: By calling next(iterator) repeatedly until StopIteration

Question Text: Why does a for loop stop on its own when the data runs out, instead of raising an error to the user?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because StopIteration signals that iteration is complete | Because Python drops the iterator after 10 steps | Because next() returns False once the data ends | Because the iterable object turns into None
Correct Answer: Because StopIteration signals that iteration is complete

Question Text: You run a for loop over an iterator that was already consumed by an earlier loop. What behavior should you expect from this second loop?
Options (A|B|C|D): The second loop repeats the earlier values | The iterator quietly resets to the start | The second loop may run zero times | The for loop converts into a while loop
Correct Answer: The second loop may run zero times

Question Text: Which statement best captures the underlying mental model of how a for loop works?
Options (A|B|C|D): It can only operate on lists and on tuples | It relies on recursion rather than on iteration | It stores every value in memory before it starts | It is essentially automatic next() calls until done
Correct Answer: It is essentially automatic next() calls until done

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Manual Control with iter() and next()
Question Text: What does the built-in call iter(obj) return when obj is an iterable?
Options (A|B|C|D): A new list built from the object | An iterator created from the iterable | The final value contained in the object | A sorted copy of the original object
Correct Answer: An iterator created from the iterable

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Manual Control with iter() and next()
Question Text: What does the built-in call next(it) do when it is an iterator?
Options (A|B|C|D): Restarts the iterator from the beginning | Returns the next element from the iterator | Converts the iterator into a tuple | Returns the total length of the iterator
Correct Answer: Returns the next element from the iterator

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Manual Control with iter() and next()
Question Text: What happens when you call next(it) after the iterator has already been exhausted?
Options (A|B|C|D): It raises a StopIteration exception | It returns None automatically | It starts over from the first item | It raises a TypeError exception
Correct Answer: It raises a StopIteration exception

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Manual Control with iter() and next()
Question Text: Given nums = [10, 20, 30]; it = iter(nums), then next(it) is called once and next(it) is called again, what does this second call return?
Options (A|B|C|D): 30 | StopIteration | 20 | 10

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Manual Control with iter() and next()
Question Text: What is the main reason for practicing manual iter() and next() control rather than always using a for loop?
Options (A|B|C|D): It guarantees that a StopIteration exception can never be raised at all | It is the single way to turn an existing list into a generator object | It exposes the iterator protocol, aiding understanding and debugging | It permanently removes any future need to write a for loop again
Correct Answer: It exposes the iterator protocol, aiding understanding and debugging

Question Text: What does the call zip(a, b) produce when a and b are two iterables?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single list holding the pairwise sums | A dictionary mapping each index to its value | An iterator of tuples pairing items by position | A generator that sorts both inputs first
Correct Answer: An iterator of tuples pairing items by position

Question Text: If avengers = ['Hulk', 'Thor'] and names = ['Bruce', 'Tony'], what is the first tuple produced by zip(avengers, names)?
Options (A|B|C|D): ('Bruce', 'Hulk') | ('Hulk', 'Bruce') | ('Thor', 'Tony') | (0, 'Hulk')
Correct Answer: ('Hulk', 'Bruce')

Question Text: After building z = zip(a, b) and running print(list(z)), why must you rebuild z before you can loop over the pairs again?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because a zip object holds only one tuple total | Because print deletes the variable named z | Because zip must sort its inputs before looping | Because list(z) consumes the zip iterator
Correct Answer: Because list(z) consumes the zip iterator

Question Text: Conceptually, what does zip() let you accomplish?
Options (A|B|C|D): Attach running index numbers to one iterable | Convert several iterables into a single set | Search through an already sorted list quickly | Stitch multiple iterables together element-by-element
Correct Answer: Stitch multiple iterables together element-by-element

Question Text: Which pitfall should you watch for when working with a zip object?
Options (A|B|C|D): It functions correctly only when every argument given is a dictionary | Materializing it to a list for debugging can exhaust it before reuse | It quietly modifies the original input lists that were passed to it | It silently begins its position indexing at 1 rather than at 0 always
Correct Answer: Materializing it to a list for debugging can exhaust it before reuse

Question Text: When you loop with enumerate(iterable), what extra information does it provide on each iteration compared to looping over the iterable directly?
Options (A|B|C|D): A second independent iterator | A dictionary key for each value | A running index paired with each value | A sorted copy of the iterable
Correct Answer: A running index paired with each value

Question Text: By default, what value does the index from enumerate() start at?
Options (A|B|C|D): 0 | -1 | Depends on the iterable type | 1

Question Text: How do you make enumerate() begin counting its index from 1 instead of 0?
Options (A|B|C|D): enumerate(iterable, first=1) | enumerate(1, iterable) | enumerate(iterable, index=1) | enumerate(iterable, start=1)
Correct Answer: enumerate(iterable, start=1)

Question Text: In the loop "for i, name in enumerate(avengers, start=1):", what does the variable i represent?
Options (A|B|C|D): The count of remaining elements | The length of the avengers list | The index counter, beginning at 1 | The item value at that position
Correct Answer: The index counter, beginning at 1

Question Text: Which of the following is a common misconception about how enumerate() behaves?
Options (A|B|C|D): It mutates the original iterable | It sorts the values before numbering | It fully consumes a dict instantly | It works only on list objects
Correct Answer: It mutates the original iterable

Question Text: When you iterate over a dictionary directly in a for loop, what does it yield on each pass by default?
Options (A|B|C|D): Sorted (key, value) tuples | Key-value pairs | The values | The keys
Correct Answer: The keys

Question Text: Why is calling .items() helpful when looping over a dictionary?
Options (A|B|C|D): It sorts the dictionary's values automatically | It makes the underlying dictionary immutable | It removes any duplicate keys from the dictionary | It yields each entry as a (key, value) tuple
Correct Answer: It yields each entry as a (key, value) tuple

Question Text: Which loop header correctly unpacks each key and value from a dictionary named my_dict?
Options (A|B|C|D): for k in my_dict.values(): | for my_dict in k, v: | for k = v in my_dict: | for k, v in my_dict.items():
Correct Answer: for k, v in my_dict.items():

Question Text: What is one advantage of using .items() over looping the keys and then indexing d[k] inside the loop?
Options (A|B|C|D): It automatically skips any missing keys | It gives pairs directly, avoiding extra lookups | It makes each iteration O(1) regardless of size | It returns only the dictionary values
Correct Answer: It gives pairs directly, avoiding extra lookups

Question Text: Given hero_powers = {'Iron Man': 'Suit', 'Thor': 'Hammer'}, in "for hero, power in hero_powers.items():" what do hero and power refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The key and its matching value | The position index and the value | Two keys from the dictionary | The value first, then its key
Correct Answer: The key and its matching value

Question Text: What is the primary purpose of a list comprehension?
Options (A|B|C|D): To sort a list without comparisons | To build a new list from an iterable concisely | To create a lazy generator automatically | To mutate an existing list in place
Correct Answer: To build a new list from an iterable concisely

Question Text: Which list comprehension is equivalent to a loop that appends num + 1 for each num in nums?
Options (A|B|C|D): [for num in nums: num + 1] | [num + 1 for num in nums] | [num for num in nums + 1] | (num + 1 for num in nums)
Correct Answer: [num + 1 for num in nums]

Question Text: What is a key tradeoff of using deeply nested list comprehensions?
Options (A|B|C|D): They can be harder to read even when shorter | They only ever work on dictionaries | They fully avoid iterator-consumption issues | They always use less memory than generators
Correct Answer: They can be harder to read even when shorter

Question Text: Which comprehension form filters which items are included rather than transforming each output value?
Options (A|B|C|D): [a if condition else b for x in iterable] | print(*iterable) | {key: value for x in iterable} | [expr for x in iterable if condition]
Correct Answer: [expr for x in iterable if condition]

Question Text: How does a list comprehension differ conceptually from a generator expression that produces the same elements?
Options (A|B|C|D): A list comprehension eagerly builds all results at once | A list comprehension requires the pandas library | A list comprehension can only be iterated once | A list comprehension uses older, deprecated syntax
Correct Answer: A list comprehension eagerly builds all results at once

Question Text: How does a dictionary comprehension differ syntactically from a list comprehension?
Options (A|B|C|D): It accepts only dictionaries as its input | It uses () around the output expression | It always produces keys in sorted order | It uses {} with a key: value output expression
Correct Answer: It uses {} with a key: value output expression

Question Text: Which expression is a correctly written dictionary comprehension?
Options (A|B|C|D): {x: -x for x in range(1, 6)} | {x for x in range(1, 6): -x} | [x: -x for x in range(1, 6)] | (x: -x for x in range(1, 6))
Correct Answer: {x: -x for x in range(1, 6)}

Question Text: In the dictionary comprehension {x: -x for x in range(1, 6)}, what does the part "x: -x" specify?
Options (A|B|C|D): An index paired with a list element | A tuple to be unpacked later | A condition used to filter items | A key and its computed value
Correct Answer: A key and its computed value

Question Text: If you write a key: value comprehension but enclose it in [] instead of {}, what actually happens?
Options (A|B|C|D): It forces all keys to become strings | It builds a list rather than a dictionary | It turns into a generator expression | Nothing; Python treats [] and {} the same
Correct Answer: It builds a list rather than a dictionary

Question Text: What do dictionary comprehensions and list comprehensions have in common?
Options (A|B|C|D): Both return lazy iterators, not collections | Both are usable only with nested loops | Both internally rely on recursion | Both are compact ways to loop and build results
Correct Answer: Both are compact ways to loop and build results

Question Text: In Python, what does the expression x % 2 compute?
Options (A|B|C|D): The floor-division result x // 2 | The integer quotient of x and 2 | The remainder of dividing x by 2 | Whether x is an even number
Correct Answer: The remainder of dividing x by 2

Question Text: Which boolean test correctly identifies whether an integer x is even?
In the comprehension [x**2 for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0], what role does the modulo check play?
Options (A|B|C|D): It turns the list into a generator | It formats each output number | It keeps only the even x values | It sorts the squared results
Correct Answer: It keeps only the even x values

Question Text: When using modulo to filter integers, which common mistake should you watch out for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Thinking modulo can return float results only | Confusing x % 2 == 0 (even) with x % 2 == 1 (odd) | Believing modulo only operates on string inputs | Assuming modulo can sort values on its own
Correct Answer: Confusing x % 2 == 0 (even) with x % 2 == 1 (odd)

Question Text: Why is the modulo operator a useful building block when iterating over sequences?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes the lists that it produces completely immutable | It expresses filters like even/odd or every nth item | It fully replaces both the zip() and enumerate() tools | It removes any need for writing explicit loops
Correct Answer: It expresses filters like even/odd or every nth item

Question Text: What is the main difference between a generator expression and a list comprehension?
Options (A|B|C|D): A list comprehension is always the more memory-safe choice | A generator expression stores all values immediately in brackets | A generator expression uses parentheses and yields values lazily | A generator expression works only on dictionaries
Correct Answer: A generator expression uses parentheses and yields values lazily

Question Text: Why are generator expressions considered memory-safe for large data?
Options (A|B|C|D): They sort the values before storing them | They reuse a single list object forever | They automatically spill the data to disk | They do not materialize all values at once
Correct Answer: They do not materialize all values at once

Question Text: Given gen = (x**2 for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0), what does the first call to next(gen) return?
What happens when you pass a generator to list(gen)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It resets the generator back to its start | It converts the generator into a dictionary | It consumes the remaining values into a list | It makes the generator reusable any number of times
Correct Answer: It consumes the remaining values into a list

Question Text: Which key tradeoff applies to generator expressions?
Options (A|B|C|D): They run faster than every other approach | They can only be used together with pandas | They save memory but are one-pass and get consumed | They support direct indexing just like lists
Correct Answer: They save memory but are one-pass and get consumed

Question Text: What distinguishes a generator function from an ordinary function?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always builds and then returns a fully populated list | It must contain at least one while loop somewhere in its body | It must include the *args parameter within its signature | It uses yield instead of return to produce values lazily
Correct Answer: It uses yield instead of return to produce values lazily

Question Text: What does a yield statement do when it executes?
Options (A|B|C|D): It converts the enclosing function into a class | It ends the function for good and clears its state | It sorts every remaining value before returning | It emits a value and pauses the function's state
Correct Answer: It emits a value and pauses the function's state

Question Text: In def count_up_to(n): ... yield i ... i += 1, what happens on the next request for a value?
Options (A|B|C|D): The function raises a TypeError automatically | The function resumes right after the last yield | The function returns all remaining values at once | The function restarts again from the beginning
Correct Answer: The function resumes right after the last yield

Question Text: Why does iteration eventually stop for a generator function?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because a generator can produce only one value | Because calling next() switches it into list mode | Because the yield expression eventually becomes False | Because the function runs out of code, raising StopIteration
Correct Answer: Because the function runs out of code, raising StopIteration

Question Text: Which task is a practical use case for a generator function?
Options (A|B|C|D): Streaming large files one record at a time | Building only weighted graphs | Performing constant-time hash lookups | Reversing a class inheritance chain
Correct Answer: Streaming large files one record at a time

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Chunked pandas Loads for Large Datasets
Question Text: What does pd.read_csv(..., chunksize=1000) return?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single DataFrame capped at 1000 rows total | An iterable yielding one DataFrame chunk at a time | A generator that yields the file paths | A dictionary holding only the column names
Correct Answer: An iterable yielding one DataFrame chunk at a time

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Chunked pandas Loads for Large Datasets
Question Text: What is the intended workflow when processing a file with chunked loading?
Options (A|B|C|D): Convert each chunk into a linked list as you go | Combine every chunk with zip() before any processing | Read a chunk, update a small result, discard it, continue | Load all chunks, keep them all, then sort the whole set
Correct Answer: Read a chunk, update a small result, discard it, continue

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Chunked pandas Loads for Large Datasets
Question Text: Why is keeping a running total often more memory-efficient than appending each chunk's result to a list?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because lists cannot hold numbers produced by pandas | Because it avoids storing many intermediate results | Because chunksize only accepts integer arguments | Because pandas requires the total to be named total
Correct Answer: Because it avoids storing many intermediate results

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Chunked pandas Loads for Large Datasets
Question Text: What tradeoff governs the choice of chunk size in chunked reads?
Options (A|B|C|D): Chunk size only changes the resulting column names | Bigger chunks reduce memory and boost speed in equal measure | Bigger chunks can be faster but use more memory; smaller add overhead | Smaller chunks simply cannot be iterated over
Correct Answer: Bigger chunks can be faster but use more memory; smaller add overhead

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Chunked pandas Loads for Large Datasets
Question Text: If memory keeps growing with file size even though you are reading in chunks, what is the likely cause?
Options (A|B|C|D): The underlying CSV file has not been sorted first | read_csv is returning strings rather than numbers | The chunksize option is supported only on Linux | You are accidentally materializing or retaining too much
Correct Answer: You are accidentally materializing or retaining too much

Question Text: When you open a text file and treat it as an iterable, why is this approach memory-efficient for large files?
Options (A|B|C|D): It caches every line in RAM up front for fast access | It requires integer indexing to reach any given line | It yields one line at a time without loading the whole file | It returns each row as a dictionary keyed by column
Correct Answer: It yields one line at a time without loading the whole file

Question Text: Which loop is the idiomatic way to read an open file object line by line in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): for key, value in file.items(): | for i in range(file): | while file == True: | for line in file:
Correct Answer: for line in file:

Question Text: Reading a file one line at a time, rather than all at once, is an example of which general processing style?
Options (A|B|C|D): Hash collision handling | Subclass inheritance | Streaming-style processing | Operator overloading
Correct Answer: Streaming-style processing

Question Text: What do file objects, range objects, and generators have in common that lets them be looped over efficiently?
Options (A|B|C|D): They implement the iteration protocol and avoid holding all items at once | They each depend on the external pandas library in order to function at all | They can each be looped over only one single time per running program | They are all stored internally as ordinary dictionary objects in memory
Correct Answer: They implement the iteration protocol and avoid holding all items at once

Question Text: Which habit defeats the memory benefit of iterating over a file line by line?
Options (A|B|C|D): Reading the entire file into memory when streaming would suffice | Calling enumerate() while looping over each of the file's lines | Closing the file with a context manager once you finish reading it | Using a plain for loop instead of calling next() by hand yourself
Correct Answer: Reading the entire file into memory when streaming would suffice

Question Text: What is the primary purpose of Python's built-in os module?
Options (A|B|C|D): It formats and pretty-prints exception tracebacks only | It supplies predefined character sets for text validation | It builds DataFrames from dictionaries and lists | It interacts with the operating system, files, and environment
Correct Answer: It interacts with the operating system, files, and environment

Question Text: What does a call to os.getcwd() return?
Options (A|B|C|D): A list of all defined environment variables | The name of the running script file only | A boolean for whether the folder exists | The path of the current working directory
Correct Answer: The path of the current working directory

Question Text: Why is os.environ accessed without parentheses, unlike os.getcwd()?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it is an attribute (an object), not a function to call | Because it returns a plain tuple of values by default | Because it is a class method bound to the os type | Because adding parentheses would sort the variables
Correct Answer: Because it is an attribute (an object), not a function to call

Question Text: Which of the following is provided by Python's string module?
Options (A|B|C|D): string.ascii_lowercase | string.head | string.getcwd | string.read_csv
Correct Answer: string.ascii_lowercase

Question Text: In Python, which statement best describes a built-in function such as len() or sum()?
Options (A|B|C|D): A global tool not bound to any specific object type | A class attribute shared across all its instances | A data value that is stored on an object | A callable tied to one single object instance only
Correct Answer: A global tool not bound to any specific object type

Question Text: In Python, which statement best describes a method such as list.sort() or str.lower()?
Options (A|B|C|D): A keyword-only argument passed into a call | A function that applies to every type equally | A callable bound to a type, invoked with dot syntax | A value that is returned by the type(x) call
Correct Answer: A callable bound to a type, invoked with dot syntax

Question Text: Which of these calls invokes a global function rather than a method on an object?
Options (A|B|C|D): df.head() | sum(prep_times) | text.lower() | prep_times.sort()
Correct Answer: sum(prep_times)

Question Text: Why does calling df.head() raise an AttributeError when df is a plain Python list?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because lists are iterators rather than real objects | Because a list cannot be displayed or printed at all | Because list methods always require keyword arguments | Because head() is defined on DataFrames, not on lists
Correct Answer: Because head() is defined on DataFrames, not on lists

Question Text: Which mental model best captures the difference between functions and methods in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): Functions are public while methods are always private | Functions are global tools; methods are attached to an object | Functions work only on numbers; methods only on strings | Methods always run faster than functions in every case
Correct Answer: Functions are global tools; methods are attached to an object

Question Text: Why is it generally preferable to use Python's built-in functions instead of writing equivalent loops by hand?
Options (A|B|C|D): They eliminate any need for custom functions or modules | They are concise, well-tested, readable, and reduce bugs | They operate only on lists that hold integers | They always return a dictionary as their result
Correct Answer: They are concise, well-tested, readable, and reduce bugs

Question Text: Which built-in function returns the number of items in a list or characters in a string?
Options (A|B|C|D): sorted() | sum() | len() | round()
Correct Answer: len()

Question Text: What does sorted(iterable) do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It returns just the smallest item in the iterable | It can sort iterables of numbers but not of strings | It sorts the iterable in place and returns None | It returns a new list with the items in order
Correct Answer: It returns a new list with the items in order

Question Text: In the expression avg_time = sum(values) / len(values), what role does len(values) play?
Options (A|B|C|D): It rounds the final average automatically | It converts the computed result to a string | It returns the largest value in the list | It supplies the count of values as the divisor
Correct Answer: It supplies the count of values as the divisor

Question Text: What does round(number) do when the ndigits argument is omitted?
Options (A|B|C|D): It rounds the number to 0 decimal places | It returns the rounded value as a string | It raises a TypeError for the missing argument | It rounds the number to 6 decimal places
Correct Answer: It rounds the number to 0 decimal places

Question Text: In Python, what is the pandas library?
Options (A|B|C|D): A standard module imported automatically into every script | A built-in function that reads text strings into memory | A debugging utility that only formats error tracebacks | A third-party package for tabular data manipulation and analysis
Correct Answer: A third-party package for tabular data manipulation and analysis

Question Text: Which statement follows the conventional way to import pandas?
Options (A|B|C|D): import pandas as pandas_lib | import pd as pandas | import pandas as pd | from pandas import dataframe_only
Correct Answer: import pandas as pd

Question Text: Given a dictionary named data_dict, which expression builds a pandas DataFrame from it?
Options (A|B|C|D): pandas.info(data_dict) | pd.read_csv(data_dict) | pd.DataFrame(data_dict) | df.head(data_dict)
Correct Answer: pd.DataFrame(data_dict)

Question Text: Called with no argument, what does a DataFrame's df.head() return?
Options (A|B|C|D): Just the list of column names | Just the data type of each column | The first five rows of the DataFrame | The last five rows of the DataFrame
Correct Answer: The first five rows of the DataFrame

Question Text: What does calling df.info() on a DataFrame report?
Options (A|B|C|D): The contents written out to a new CSV file | A renamed copy of every column header | Terminal commands needed to install pandas | Row count, column dtypes, and memory usage
Correct Answer: Row count, column dtypes, and memory usage

Question Text: In software design, what does the acronym DRY stand for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Do Return Yourself | Data Requires Yield | Debug, Reuse, Yield | Don’t Repeat Yourself
Correct Answer: Don’t Repeat Yourself

Question Text: Following the DRY principle, when is it appropriate to write a custom function?
Options (A|B|C|D): When you keep rewriting the same logic or repeating a task | Only when the logic must call itself recursively | Whenever you reference the same list more than once | Only when no Python built-in can do the job at all
Correct Answer: When you keep rewriting the same logic or repeating a task

Question Text: Why does following the DRY principle improve a codebase?
Options (A|B|C|D): It cuts repeated code and makes logic easy to reuse | It automatically converts class methods into functions | It removes any need to document the code at all | It guarantees that every function runs in O(1) time
Correct Answer: It cuts repeated code and makes logic easy to reuse

Question Text: Which practice best demonstrates the DRY principle?
Options (A|B|C|D): Copying the same loop into three separate files for extra added clarity | Replacing all of a program's modules with their equivalent classes | Removing the return statement from every single function in the module | Wrapping sum(values)/len(values) in one reusable average() function
Correct Answer: Wrapping sum(values)/len(values) in one reusable average() function

Question Text: How should the DRY principle be balanced against using simple built-in tools first?
Options (A|B|C|D): Apply DRY only inside object-oriented, class-based code | Start with the simplest reliable tool, then factor out repetition | Treat DRY as a rule purely about adding more comments | Always write your own function before trying any built-in
Correct Answer: Start with the simplest reliable tool, then factor out repetition

Question Text: What is true of positional arguments in a Python function call?
Options (A|B|C|D): Their names matter more than the order they appear in | Their order determines which parameter each fills | They are allowed only when calling a class method | They must always be written with parameter names
Correct Answer: Their order determines which parameter each fills

Question Text: Which call to round() passes its arguments as keyword arguments?
Options (A|B|C|D): round(3.14159, 2) | round(number=3.14159, ndigits=2) | round(3.14159 as number, 2) | round(=3.14159, =2)
Correct Answer: round(number=3.14159, ndigits=2)

Question Text: What does giving a parameter a default value let a caller do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Define the function without writing any return | Call the function successfully only one single time | Omit that argument when the default behavior is fine | Reorder all of the function's parameters automatically
Correct Answer: Omit that argument when the default behavior is fine

Question Text: Given def average(values, rounded=False):, what happens when you call average(preparation_times) with just one argument?
Options (A|B|C|D): values is assigned the value False | rounded takes its default value of False | An error is raised for the missing rounded | rounded is set to True automatically
Correct Answer: rounded takes its default value of False

Question Text: Which built-in lets you inspect a function's parameter names and default values?
Options (A|B|C|D): iter(func) | print(func) | sorted(func) | help(func)
Correct Answer: help(func)

Question Text: What is a docstring in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): A code comment on its own line that starts with # | A string inside a function that documents what it does | A class attribute that can store only numeric values | An error traceback printed when the function fails
Correct Answer: A string inside a function that documents what it does

Question Text: Where in a function should its docstring be placed?
Options (A|B|C|D): At the very bottom of the source file, after all the other code | On the line directly preceding the function's own def statement | On the first line of the body, right after the def ...: line | As a string argument passed into an inner print() call
Correct Answer: On the first line of the body, right after the def ...: line

Question Text: Which built-in prints a function's full help, including its signature and docstring?
Options (A|B|C|D): sorted(func) | type(func) | help(func) | func.items()
Correct Answer: help(func)

Question Text: What does the attribute func.__doc__ give you?
Options (A|B|C|D): The value the function returns | Only the function's docstring text | A tuple of the function's local variables | The function's first parameter name
Correct Answer: Only the function's docstring text

Question Text: What is the standard structure of a multi-line docstring?
Options (A|B|C|D): A single one-word title with no further text allowed | A summary line, a blank line, then Args and Returns sections | A print statement followed by several code comments | A list of imports placed above a sample traceback
Correct Answer: A summary line, a blank line, then Args and Returns sections

Question Text: What does the built-in map(function, iterable) do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Applies the function to every element of the iterable | Converts the given iterable into a list right away | Builds a dictionary by pairing keys with values | Sorts the iterable using the function as the sort key
Correct Answer: Applies the function to every element of the iterable

Question Text: What type of object does a call to map() return?
Options (A|B|C|D): A lazy iterator known as a map object | A dictionary keyed by element index | A fully built list of results | A tuple containing every result
Correct Answer: A lazy iterator known as a map object

Question Text: After calling result = map(func, data), why is it common to write list(result) before printing it?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because list() must precede every print call | Because map() is lazy and list() forces the values | Because map() accepts only list arguments | Because map() returns its output as a string
Correct Answer: Because map() is lazy and list() forces the values

Question Text: In map(lambda n: n.capitalize(), names), what happens to each item of names?
Options (A|B|C|D): Each name has its first letter capitalized | Each name is converted to lowercase | Each name is dropped when it is empty | Each name is replaced by its position
Correct Answer: Each name has its first letter capitalized

Question Text: In which situation is map() the natural tool to reach for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only when a problem genuinely requires recursion | When applying one transformation to each element | Only for dictionaries that use integer keys | When the original iterable must be changed in place
Correct Answer: When applying one transformation to each element

Question Text: Inside a function definition, what does the *args parameter collect?
Options (A|B|C|D): The function's class attributes into a set | Just the first argument into a one-item list | Extra positional arguments into a tuple | Extra keyword arguments into a dictionary
Correct Answer: Extra positional arguments into a tuple

Question Text: Inside a function definition, what does the **kwargs parameter collect?
Options (A|B|C|D): Extra positional arguments into a tuple | Only string arguments into a fresh list | Any iterator's values into a single set | Extra keyword arguments into a dictionary
Correct Answer: Extra keyword arguments into a dictionary

Question Text: In a function defined as average_kwargs(**kwargs) that averages its named inputs, why call kwargs.values()?
Options (A|B|C|D): To pull the numbers out of the collected dictionary | To turn the keywords back into positional args | To sort the keyword names into alphabetical order | To strip out any duplicated keyword names
Correct Answer: To pull the numbers out of the collected dictionary

Question Text: When you have lists a and b and call average(*a, *b) on a function defined as average(*nums), what does the * at the call site do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It causes the function to return a tuple of lists | It proves **kwargs may be used only in classes | It requires every positional argument to be named | It unpacks each list into separate positional args
Correct Answer: It unpacks each list into separate positional args

Question Text: Which call passes its inputs as keyword arguments, matching a function defined as average_kwargs(**kwargs)?
Options (A|B|C|D): average_kwargs(a=10, b=12, c=18) | average_kwargs(10, 12, 18) as positionals | average_kwargs(values=[10, 12, 18]) only | average_kwargs(*[10, 12, 18]) as positionals
Correct Answer: average_kwargs(a=10, b=12, c=18)

Question Text: What is a lambda in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): A named class method specific to pandas | An anonymous function: lambda args: expression | A special looping construct for iterators | A built-in tool for formatting tracebacks
Correct Answer: An anonymous function: lambda args: expression

Question Text: Which statement correctly describes how a lambda produces its result?
Options (A|B|C|D): Its single expression becomes the output, with no return statement | It may be referenced only once anywhere in a program | It is unable to accept any parameters at all | It needs an explicit return statement to give back a value
Correct Answer: Its single expression becomes the output, with no return statement

Question Text: Which lambda raises x to the power y?
Options (A|B|C|D): lambda power: **power | lambda x, y: x ** y | lambda x: x + x | lambda x, y: x // y and y
Correct Answer: lambda x, y: x ** y

Question Text: In which case is a lambda the better choice than a full def?
Options (A|B|C|D): For complex logic with many branches | Whenever the function must be documented | Only when written inside a class body | For a simple, one-off transformation
Correct Answer: For a simple, one-off transformation

Question Text: Why is a named def usually preferable to a lambda once the logic gets more involved?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because a named def always executes noticeably faster | Because complex, reused logic reads more clearly when named | Because a lambda is completely unable to accept any arguments | Because a lambda expression cannot be stored in a variable
Correct Answer: Because complex, reused logic reads more clearly when named

Question Text: What is the main purpose of a try/except block?
Options (A|B|C|D): To deliberately halt the program at that point | To catch an error so the program keeps running | To turn raised exceptions into code comments | To take the place of all input validation
Correct Answer: To catch an error so the program keeps running

Question Text: In a function that averages numbers and wraps the calculation in try/except, what happens when a TypeError is raised?
Options (A|B|C|D): The program exits at once with no message | A helpful message prints from the except block | The function quietly returns 0 instead | The function's docstring is rewritten on the fly
Correct Answer: A helpful message prints from the except block

Question Text: Which description matches the standard try/except pattern?
Options (A|B|C|D): Try on its own prevents every possible error | Try and except work only alongside recursion | Try the risky code, except runs a fallback on error | Write the except block first, then the try block
Correct Answer: Try the risky code, except runs a fallback on error

Question Text: Why is try/except valuable in a larger script?
Options (A|B|C|D): It turns invalid input into valid input by itself | It replaces the raise statement in every case | It lets the script continue when one part fails | It guarantees that all values are correct
Correct Answer: It lets the script continue when one part fails

Question Text: A method divides a number by the list element at a given index and guards the operation with try/except. Which two errors most naturally need handling here?
Options (A|B|C|D): ValueError and KeyError | ZeroDivisionError and IndexError | TypeError and NameError | StopIteration and SyntaxError
Correct Answer: ZeroDivisionError and IndexError

Question Text: In Python, what information does a traceback provide when an exception is raised?
Options (A|B|C|D): The error type, the failing line, and the call stack | A plain-English comment describing how to fix the bug | A log of every package installed in the environment | A ranking of all exceptions sorted by their severity
Correct Answer: The error type, the failing line, and the call stack

Question Text: Which of the following expressions raises a TypeError in Python?
Which of the following expressions raises a ValueError in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): len('Hello') | '2' + 2 | float('Hello') | sum([1, 2, 3])
Correct Answer: float('Hello')

Question Text: Unlike float('Hello'), why does the call float('2') succeed instead of raising an error?
Options (A|B|C|D): It raises a ValueError because strings are never allowed | It returns a tuple rather than a single float value | It raises a TypeError instead of returning a number | It works because '2' is a valid numeric string
Correct Answer: It works because '2' is a valid numeric string

Question Text: When a traceback includes frames from third-party package code, where should you focus first?
Options (A|B|C|D): On the bottom line, which usually names the actual error type | Strip out every internal package file reference before reading it | On the top frame, since the imported package is always the cause | Skip the bottom line and assume the third-party package is broken
Correct Answer: On the bottom line, which usually names the actual error type

Question Text: What is the primary purpose of the raise statement in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): To catch an error so that execution can quietly continue | To attach runtime comments that document the surrounding code | To deliberately trigger an error with a clear message | To convert an object's instance methods into standalone functions
Correct Answer: To deliberately trigger an error with a clear message

Question Text: In which situation is it most appropriate to use raise to stop execution?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only for syntax errors that are caught before the program runs | When continuing could be dangerous or give wrong results | Whenever you would simply prefer to ignore an error entirely | Only when the offending code happens to sit in a list comprehension
Correct Answer: When continuing could be dangerous or give wrong results

Question Text: A function average_strict(values) computes a mean only for valid collections. Which condition should make it raise a TypeError?
Options (A|B|C|D): When values holds more than ten total items | When values contains floating-point numbers | When values has already been sorted in order | When values is not a list, set, or tuple
Correct Answer: When values is not a list, set, or tuple

Question Text: How does using raise differ in intent from wrapping code in try/except?
Options (A|B|C|D): They serve essentially identical purposes and are freely interchangeable | raise is intended only for handling errors that occur inside package code | raise recovers and continues, while try/except halts execution immediately | try/except recovers and continues, while raise halts with a clear reason
Correct Answer: try/except recovers and continues, while raise halts with a clear reason

Question Text: Why is supplying a custom message when you raise an error beneficial?
Options (A|B|C|D): It fails fast with a clear reason, preventing downstream mistakes | It causes the surrounding code to execute noticeably faster overall | It automatically repairs the invalid input so processing can proceed | It removes any need to perform input type checks elsewhere in the code
Correct Answer: It fails fast with a clear reason, preventing downstream mistakes

Question Text: Which description best characterizes procedural programming?
Options (A|B|C|D): A system of interacting entities that share common interfaces | A coding style that deliberately avoids defining any functions | A directed graph made only of weighted, interconnected nodes | A linear recipe of steps, such as load, clean, analyze, plot
Correct Answer: A linear recipe of steps, such as load, clean, analyze, plot

Question Text: Which description best characterizes object-oriented programming?
Options (A|B|C|D): A specialized strategy whose sole purpose is sorting items in a list | A long, one-time sequence of step-by-step instructions run in order | A system of interacting objects with predictable behaviors | A debugging technique that relies only on scattered print() calls
Correct Answer: A system of interacting objects with predictable behaviors

Question Text: Why does object-oriented design become more useful as a program's complexity grows?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because for-loops gradually stop functioning correctly in larger programs | Because the object-oriented style removes any need to define functions at all | Because a class will always consume less memory than an equivalent script | Because purely step-by-step thinking grows brittle with many parts
Correct Answer: Because purely step-by-step thinking grows brittle with many parts

Question Text: Which everyday analogy best contrasts procedural thinking with object-oriented thinking?
Options (A|B|C|D): Baking a single loaf of bread versus sorting a list of numbers | Planning your day versus planning a whole city | A song playlist versus a full-featured music player app | A zipper mechanism versus a line of people in a queue
Correct Answer: Planning your day versus planning a whole city

Question Text: Which statement best captures the contrast between the procedural and object-oriented styles?
Options (A|B|C|D): The two styles differ only by whether or not you happen to use loops | Procedural code models agents and rules, while object-oriented models recipes | Object-oriented code suits only pandas, while procedural suits only strings | Procedural is step-by-step; object-oriented bundles behavior with data
Correct Answer: Procedural is step-by-step; object-oriented bundles behavior with data

Question Text: In Python, what is a class?
Options (A|B|C|D): A blueprint defining what objects of a type can have and do | A concrete object that already stores real attribute values | A built-in exception type used to signal errors at runtime | A private attribute that belongs to one particular instance
Correct Answer: A blueprint defining what objects of a type can have and do

Question Text: In Python, what is an instance?
Options (A|B|C|D): An attribute whose value is shared across every defined class | A descriptive comment written inside the body of a class | A concrete realization of a class holding actual values | A method name that gets reused when one class inherits another
Correct Answer: A concrete realization of a class holding actual values

Question Text: Given the code class Customer: pass, which statement correctly describes Customer and c1 = Customer()?
Options (A|B|C|D): Customer is an instance, while c1 = Customer() is the actual class | The pass keyword is what creates the c1 instance automatically for you | Customer is the class, and c1 = Customer() makes an instance | Customer() is simply an existing method being called on the object c1
Correct Answer: Customer is the class, and c1 = Customer() makes an instance

Question Text: Why are classes commonly described as blueprints?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because they are always abstract and therefore cannot be used directly | Because they automatically print out their own contents when defined | Because they specify which attributes and methods instances will have | Because they are strictly forbidden from containing any methods at all
Correct Answer: Because they specify which attributes and methods instances will have

Question Text: What does the minimal definition class Customer: pass mainly demonstrate?
Options (A|B|C|D): That every class you write must immediately inherit from another class | That the pass keyword quietly creates a set of default attributes for you | That objects of a type can be used without ever creating an instance | That a class can exist as a blueprint before any attributes or methods
Correct Answer: That a class can exist as a blueprint before any attributes or methods

Question Text: In a Python instance method, what does the first parameter self refer to?
Options (A|B|C|D): The instance on which the method is called | A required module-level global variable | The class object that defines the method | The value the method returns to its caller
Correct Answer: The instance on which the method is called

Question Text: What is the primary purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?
Options (A|B|C|D): To list every method defined on the class | To initialize an instance's attributes at creation time | To compare two objects of the class for equality | To produce the printable string for the object
Correct Answer: To initialize an instance's attributes at creation time

Question Text: Why is it considered good practice to define a class's core attributes inside __init__?
Options (A|B|C|D): It lets Python sort the class's own defined methods automatically | It allows the self parameter to be omitted from later methods | It keeps them in one place so every instance has expected fields | It causes inherited class-level attributes to silently disappear
Correct Answer: It keeps them in one place so every instance has expected fields

Question Text: Given emp = Employee('Korel', 50000), when is the class's __init__ method executed?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only when the salary attribute is reassigned | Only later if you call emp.__init__() by hand | Automatically while the new object is being created | Only when a subclass of Employee is defined
Correct Answer: Automatically while the new object is being created

Question Text: If cust is a Customer instance with a method identify, what is the call cust.identify('Alex') equivalent to?
Options (A|B|C|D): Customer.identify(cust, 'Alex'), passing cust as self | Creating a new class attribute named Alex | A call that runs only when identify returns True | identify(self, Customer) using a global function
Correct Answer: Customer.identify(cust, 'Alex'), passing cust as self

Question Text: What is the conventional name of the first parameter automatically received by a method decorated with @classmethod?
Options (A|B|C|D): obj | cls | init | self
Correct Answer: cls

Question Text: What is a common reason to use a @classmethod in a Python class?
Options (A|B|C|D): To create instance attributes that are strictly kept private | To replace every existing instance method defined on the class | To force inheritance between two otherwise unrelated classes | To define alternative constructors like from_str or from_file
Correct Answer: To define alternative constructors like from_str or from_file

Question Text: If BetterDate.from_str is a class method, what does the call BetterDate.from_str('2020-04-30') do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Returns a plain tuple rather than an object | Builds a new instance by parsing the formatted string | Sorts the characters of the date string alphabetically | Modifies an already existing BetterDate in place
Correct Answer: Builds a new instance by parsing the formatted string

Question Text: Inside a class method, why does the body return cls(year, month, day)?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because cls names the class and creates a new instance | Because class methods are required to return strings | Because __init__ cannot accept any arguments directly | Because cls holds the original input date string
Correct Answer: Because cls names the class and creates a new instance

Question Text: Which statement best distinguishes a @classmethod from a regular instance method?
Options (A|B|C|D): A class method has no way to reach constructor logic | A class method is forbidden from returning objects | A class method is bound to the class, not one instance | A class method automatically replaces __init__
Correct Answer: A class method is bound to the class, not one instance

Question Text: What does the @property decorator allow you to do in a Python class?
Options (A|B|C|D): Expose attribute-like access with validation or read-only behavior | Convert a regular function into a reusable generator object instantly | Create brand-new class attributes on the fly fully automatically | Make a chosen method completely inaccessible from outside the class
Correct Answer: Expose attribute-like access with validation or read-only behavior

Question Text: When a class stores its balance in an attribute named _balance but exposes a balance property, why is the underscore name used internally?
Options (A|B|C|D): To turn the stored balance value into a bound method on the class | To signal internal storage while balance is reached via the property | To allow direct public editing of the stored value without any checks | To guarantee that arithmetic performed on the value runs much faster
Correct Answer: To signal internal storage while balance is reached via the property

Question Text: A Customer class defines a balance property whose setter rejects negatives. What happens when code assigns cust.balance a negative value?
Options (A|B|C|D): The value is silently stored as a string | The setter accepts it and rounds it up to 0 | Nothing happens since balance is read-only | The setter raises a ValueError
Correct Answer: The setter raises a ValueError

Question Text: What advantage does wrapping a value in a property have over exposing the raw _balance attribute directly?
Options (A|B|C|D): Users assign normally while the class still enforces its rules | It shares the single balance value across every instance | It converts the balance into a shared class-level attribute | It removes any need to write a constructor method for the class
Correct Answer: Users assign normally while the class still enforces its rules

Question Text: If balance is defined as a property on the Customer class, how do you read it from an instance cust?
Options (A|B|C|D): cust['balance'] | cust.balance() | cust.balance | Customer.balance(cust)
Correct Answer: cust.balance

Question Text: What best describes an instance attribute in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): A constant provided by a built-in module | A single value shared by all objects of the class | A method that has been decorated with @classmethod | A value that can differ per object, such as self.name
Correct Answer: A value that can differ per object, such as self.name

Question Text: What best describes a class attribute in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): Another name that always points to self | A shared constant or setting defined in the class body | A property that exposes a traceback object | A value stored separately on each individual instance
Correct Answer: A shared constant or setting defined in the class body

Question Text: What is the recommended way to refer to a class attribute from inside a method?
Options (A|B|C|D): ClassName.ATTRIBUTE or type(self).ATTRIBUTE | Declaring global ATTRIBUTE every time | Indexing it as instance['ATTRIBUTE'] | Always writing self.MIN_SALARY instead
Correct Answer: ClassName.ATTRIBUTE or type(self).ATTRIBUTE

Question Text: What is the shadowing trap that can occur with class attributes?
Options (A|B|C|D): Changing one instance silently rewrites the value for every other instance | Attribute shadowing can only arise inside recursive function calls | Assigning p1.MAX_SPEED creates an instance attribute hiding the class one | Class attributes are read-only and can never be modified after definition
Correct Answer: Assigning p1.MAX_SPEED creates an instance attribute hiding the class one

Question Text: To change a shared class value so it updates for all instances, what should you do?
Options (A|B|C|D): Assign it on just one chosen instance, e.g. p1.MAX_SPEED = 7 | Redefine the value inside the class __repr__ method | Wrap the value in a property before reassigning it | Assign it on the class itself, e.g. Player.MAX_SPEED = 7
Correct Answer: Assign it on the class itself, e.g. Player.MAX_SPEED = 7

Question Text: In object-oriented design, what relationship does class inheritance model?
Options (A|B|C|D): A sorting order imposed on a list of related class instances | An 'is-a' relationship where a subclass reuses and extends a parent | A 'uses-a' link that calls another class's functions at runtime | A 'has-a' relationship for storing one object inside another
Correct Answer: An 'is-a' relationship where a subclass reuses and extends a parent

Question Text: Which Python class header correctly declares Manager as a subclass of Employee?
Options (A|B|C|D): class Employee(Manager): | class Manager = Employee: | class Manager extends Employee: | class Manager(Employee):
Correct Answer: class Manager(Employee):

Question Text: What is a subclass able to do with the behavior it inherits from its parent?
Options (A|B|C|D): Add new attributes or methods and override existing ones | Skip the parent constructor under all circumstances | Remove every parent method from the subclass automatically | Inherit it exactly, with no changes or additions allowed
Correct Answer: Add new attributes or methods and override existing ones

Question Text: Inside Manager's constructor (where Manager subclasses Employee), why call Employee.__init__(self, name, salary)?
Options (A|B|C|D): To convert Manager's __init__ into a class method | To register 'project' as a shared class attribute | To reuse the parent's initialization of inherited fields | To compare a Manager object against an Employee object
Correct Answer: To reuse the parent's initialization of inherited fields

Question Text: When is inheritance the appropriate tool to choose?
Options (A|B|C|D): When the subclass is genuinely a kind of the parent | Only for subclasses of pandas DataFrame objects | Only when the two classes define identical methods | Whenever two classes happen to share one method name
Correct Answer: When the subclass is genuinely a kind of the parent

Question Text: What does polymorphism mean in object-oriented programming?
Options (A|B|C|D): Generating special dunder methods for a class automatically | Forcing every class to inherit from one common parent | Code that works on any object type sharing the same interface | Restricting an entire program to a single object instance
Correct Answer: Code that works on any object type sharing the same interface

Question Text: Which scenario is a correct example of polymorphism through a shared interface?
Options (A|B|C|D): Any object with a withdraw(amount) method works in the same code | Polymorphism applies solely to numeric arithmetic operators | Every class must define a __repr__ method before any other | Only lists and tuples can be iterated by one shared loop
Correct Answer: Any object with a withdraw(amount) method works in the same code

Question Text: Why is polymorphism useful when writing reusable code?
Options (A|B|C|D): It forces all classes to store an identical set of attributes | It blocks any subclass from overriding inherited methods | It lets code rely on shared behavior, not concrete class names | It guarantees that each method call runs in O(1) time
Correct Answer: It lets code rely on shared behavior, not concrete class names

Question Text: When the same method name is called on different objects, what happens at runtime?
Options (A|B|C|D): The call becomes a plain function detached from any object | The shared interface is discarded after the first such call | Python always selects the parent class's implementation | Python runs the right method for the object receiving the call
Correct Answer: Python runs the right method for the object receiving the call

Question Text: Which design guideline best captures the spirit of polymorphism?
Options (A|B|C|D): Avoid reusing the same method name across separate classes | Rely on explicit concrete type checks throughout your code | Program to interfaces such as shared methods, not concrete types | Reserve polymorphism strictly for Python's built-in classes
Correct Answer: Program to interfaces such as shared methods, not concrete types

Question Text: What does the Liskov Substitution Principle state?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every subclass must define more methods than its parent does | A subclass must work anywhere its base class is expected | A parent class should inherit from each of its subclasses | Only private attributes are permitted to be substituted
Correct Answer: A subclass must work anywhere its base class is expected

Question Text: What syntactic compatibility does LSP require of a subclass that overrides a method?
