Options (A|B|C|D): Signatures stay compatible, e.g. no new required arguments | The subclass must drop every optional parameter it inherits | Only class attributes need to match; method shapes do not | Each overriding method must take exactly one parameter
Correct Answer: Signatures stay compatible, e.g. no new required arguments

Question Text: Beyond matching signatures, what semantic property does LSP protect?
Options (A|B|C|D): The subclass must use recursion if its base class does | The subclass must return None from every method it defines | The subclass must store fewer attributes than its parent | The subclass must honor the base class's expected behavior
Correct Answer: The subclass must honor the base class's expected behavior

Question Text: Why can adding a new required parameter when overriding a method be dangerous?
Options (A|B|C|D): It silently turns the instance method into a class method | It makes the subclass's method execute noticeably faster | It changes only the docstring, leaving behavior untouched | It can break substitutability for callers using the base interface
Correct Answer: It can break substitutability for callers using the base interface

Question Text: Which is a sound rule of thumb regarding LSP and inheritance?
Options (A|B|C|D): LSP requires that every subclass be immutable | LSP is relevant only to Python's built-in classes | Code that compiles is guaranteed to satisfy LSP | If a design violates LSP, don't use inheritance there
Correct Answer: If a design violates LSP, don't use inheritance there

Question Text: What does overriding a method in a subclass mean?
Options (A|B|C|D): Copying the parent's code into a comment for documentation | Permanently deleting the parent's version of the method | Converting the inherited method into a read-only property | Replacing inherited behavior while keeping the interface consistent
Correct Answer: Replacing inherited behavior while keeping the interface consistent

Question Text: A Manager subclass gives larger raises than a base Employee. Which method is being overridden?
Options (A|B|C|D): The __repr__ method | The give_raise method | The built-in type() function | The MIN_SALARY class attribute
Correct Answer: The give_raise method

Question Text: A Manager's give_raise scales the amount, then calls Employee.give_raise(self, new_amount). Why call the parent method?
Options (A|B|C|D): To reuse the parent's logic after adjusting the amount | To turn the Employee class into an instance object | To avoid having to reference self anywhere | To bypass inheritance for this particular call
Correct Answer: To reuse the parent's logic after adjusting the amount

Question Text: A Point.reflect method checks axis with if/elif branches and an else for invalid input. What does using elif accomplish?
Options (A|B|C|D): It keeps the invalid-axis branch from firing after a valid match | It forces the override to be written using recursion | It requires the reflect method to return a string value | It makes axis comparisons use 'is' rather than '=='
Correct Answer: It keeps the invalid-axis branch from firing after a valid match

Question Text: When overriding a method with a wide API (like a pandas DataFrame method), how should you pass its many parameters along?
Options (A|B|C|D): Avoid overriding the method in the first place | Use *args and **kwargs to forward parameters cleanly | Replace the method with a plain class attribute instead | Rewrite every parameter by hand on each override
Correct Answer: Use *args and **kwargs to forward parameters cleanly

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Access Control: Public vs. Internal APIs
Question Text: In a Python class, what is the effect of naming an attribute with two leading underscores, such as __attr?
Options (A|B|C|D): It marks the attribute public and read-only to every caller outside | It enforces strict privacy that no outside code is ever able to bypass | It triggers name mangling to reduce accidental subclass overrides | It registers the attribute as a recognized built-in special method
Correct Answer: It triggers name mangling to reduce accidental subclass overrides

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Access Control: Public vs. Internal APIs
Question Text: Which naming pattern should you avoid when choosing names for your own class attributes?
Options (A|B|C|D): CamelCase names borrowed from other object-oriented languages | Dunder __name__ names that Python reserves for special methods | Single-underscore _internal names used for private helpers | Lowercase snake_case names that separate words with underscores
Correct Answer: Dunder __name__ names that Python reserves for special methods

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Access Control: Public vs. Internal APIs
Question Text: Which statement best captures Python's philosophy regarding access control on attributes?
Options (A|B|C|D): Naming conventions signal what is public versus internal | Members stay private unless exposed through @property | Only class-level attributes are allowed to be public | Every attribute is inaccessible until explicitly unlocked
Correct Answer: Naming conventions signal what is public versus internal

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Access Control: Public vs. Internal APIs
Question Text: Why might a class store a value as _balance rather than balance?
Options (A|B|C|D): To flag it as internal and expose access through a public interface | To convert the attribute into a bound method callable on the class | To stop the attribute from ever appearing in the object's printed output | To make the stored value automatically immutable after assignment
Correct Answer: To flag it as internal and expose access through a public interface

Question Text: What does defining __eq__(self, other) on a class control?
Options (A|B|C|D): How instances are hashed so they can serve as dict keys | How a brand-new instance of the class gets initialized | How the class's instances are ordered when a list is sorted | What the == operator means for that class's instances
Correct Answer: What the == operator means for that class's instances

Question Text: If a custom class does not implement __eq__, how does == behave by default for its instances?
Options (A|B|C|D): It returns False for every comparison, since no rule is defined | It compares the two objects' full attribute dictionaries field by field | It compares the string each object produces through its repr() | It compares object identity, so only the same object is equal
Correct Answer: It compares object identity, so only the same object is equal

Question Text: In a BankAccount class, why might __eq__ check type(self) == type(other) before comparing values?
Options (A|B|C|D): To convert both operands into strings before they are compared | To permit a match with any object that exposes a numeric attribute | To keep the comparison meaningful and avoid cross-class matches | To make the == operator execute noticeably faster than it usually would
Correct Answer: To keep the comparison meaningful and avoid cross-class matches

Question Text: What is the intended distinction between __str__ and __repr__?
Options (A|B|C|D): __str__ formats only lists, while __repr__ formats only dictionaries | __str__ is mandatory to define, whereas defining __repr__ is forbidden | __str__ is user-friendly; __repr__ is for developers and reproducible | Both methods are fully interchangeable and serve the exact same role
Correct Answer: __str__ is user-friendly; __repr__ is for developers and reproducible

Question Text: If you will implement only one of __str__ or __repr__ on a class, which is the better choice?
Options (A|B|C|D): __repr__, since it doubles as the fallback for __str__ | Neither one; just rely on print() with no methods added | __eq__, since it conveniently covers display needs as well | __str__, because print() will never consult a defined __repr__
Correct Answer: __repr__, since it doubles as the fallback for __str__

Question Text: Why are tuples a good fit for representing fixed records such as a coordinate or a paired name?
Options (A|B|C|D): They automatically remove any duplicate values the moment they are created | They are fully mutable and can be reordered in place whenever needed | They are immutable and ordered, good when data must not change | They always consume strictly less memory than any other container type
Correct Answer: They are immutable and ordered, good when data must not change

Question Text: Which statement about tuple syntax in Python is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): The comma builds the tuple; parentheses are mainly for readability | A tuple is required to hold exactly two elements, never more or fewer | Square brackets are the standard, recommended way to build a tuple | Parentheses on their own are what actually create a tuple in Python
Correct Answer: The comma builds the tuple; parentheses are mainly for readability

Question Text: What is a common gotcha when creating tuples in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): A stray trailing comma can create a tuple unexpectedly | A tuple must be unpacked on the same line it is created | Tuples are only able to hold string elements | Calling .append() on a tuple quietly returns None
Correct Answer: A stray trailing comma can create a tuple unexpectedly

Question Text: What is the main benefit of using tuple unpacking instead of indexing?
Options (A|B|C|D): It binds elements to named variables, improving clarity over indexing | It makes the tuple temporarily mutable for the duration of one line | It sorts the tuple's values into order before any of them are used | It converts the tuple into a dictionary keyed by position automatically
Correct Answer: It binds elements to named variables, improving clarity over indexing

Question Text: Given a two-element tuple named pair, what does the statement girl_name, boy_name = pair do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It joins two separate strings together into a single value | It tests whether the two named variables are equal to each other | It unpacks the tuple's items into two named variables | It converts an existing list into a set of unique items
Correct Answer: It unpacks the tuple's items into two named variables

Question Text: What is the difference between list.append(x) and list.extend(iterable)?
Options (A|B|C|D): append adds one item; extend adds each item from an iterable | append removes items, while extend is the method that adds them | append adds many items, while extend adds just one item | Both behave identically regardless of the argument given
Correct Answer: append adds one item; extend adds each item from an iterable

Question Text: What does list.index(value) do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It sorts the list's elements into ascending order in place | It returns the element that is stored at a given index position | It removes the first matching value from the list without any error | It returns the value's position, or raises ValueError if absent
Correct Answer: It returns the value's position, or raises ValueError if absent

Question Text: What does list.pop(index) return after it runs?
Options (A|B|C|D): The item that was removed | The list's new length after removal | Nothing, since it only deletes | A boolean flag indicating success
Correct Answer: The item that was removed

Question Text: Which statement about the built-in sorted(list_value) is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): It returns just the single first element taken in sorted order | It returns a new sorted list and leaves the original unchanged | It rearranges the elements of the original list directly in place | It is able to sort only lists that are made up entirely of numbers
Correct Answer: It returns a new sorted list and leaves the original unchanged

Question Text: If baby_names.index('Rowen') is called but 'Rowen' is not in the list, what is raised?
Options (A|B|C|D): ValueError | TypeError | KeyError | IndexError
Correct Answer: ValueError

Question Text: In Python, what is an f-string?
Options (A|B|C|D): A built-in function that joins an iterable into one combined string | A triple-quoted string literal reserved only for module docstrings | A string literal prefixed with f that embeds expressions in braces | A string method that converts every letter in the text to uppercase
Correct Answer: A string literal prefixed with f that embeds expressions in braces

Question Text: Given variables cookie_name and cookie_price, which expression correctly uses an f-string to build the sentence?
Options (A|B|C|D): 'Each {cookie_name} cookie costs {cookie_price}' | join('Each ', cookie_name, cookie_price) | f'Each cookie_name cookie costs cookie_price' | f'Each {cookie_name} cookie costs {cookie_price}'
Correct Answer: f'Each {cookie_name} cookie costs {cookie_price}'

Question Text: What practical advantage do f-strings provide when producing output text?
Options (A|B|C|D): They are a readable way to combine variables into a string | They accept only numeric expressions inside braces | They sort the inserted values automatically | They replace the need for dictionaries
Correct Answer: They are a readable way to combine variables into a string

Question Text: What makes f-strings "dynamic" rather than just plain text?
Options (A|B|C|D): They convert every embedded value to lowercase | They store data more compactly than tuples | Expressions inside the braces are evaluated at runtime | They can be written only inside for loops
Correct Answer: Expressions inside the braces are evaluated at runtime

Question Text: When looping with enumerate(names) and printing print(f'Rank {rank+1}: {name}'), why is rank+1 used instead of rank?
Options (A|B|C|D): f-strings require every embedded number to start its counting from 1 | enumerate starts its index at 1, so a value of 1 must be subtracted elsewhere | enumerate starts its index at 0, so adding 1 gives human-friendly numbering | Tuple unpacking in a for loop always begins its counting from 1, not 0
Correct Answer: enumerate starts its index at 0, so adding 1 gives human-friendly numbering

Question Text: What does the expression ', '.join(iterable_of_strings) produce?
Options (A|B|C|D): It searches for a substring while ignoring case | It splits one string into a list of strings | It joins the strings into one string using the separator | It removes duplicate strings from a list
Correct Answer: It joins the strings into one string using the separator

Question Text: Why is it recommended to apply .lower() to text before searching it?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it gives one consistent case so comparisons are case-insensitive | Because join() will only accept input strings that are fully lowercase | Because calling .lower() is what makes the original string immutable | Because uppercase letters simply cannot ever be matched by any search at all
Correct Answer: Because it gives one consistent case so comparisons are case-insensitive

Question Text: Which group of string search tools is case-sensitive, treating 'A' and 'a' as different?
Options (A|B|C|D): the dict.get(), pop(), and update() mapping methods | the join(), split(), and replace() string methods | startswith(), endswith(), and the 'sub' in s test | the lower(), upper(), and type() built-in functions
Correct Answer: startswith(), endswith(), and the 'sub' in s test

Question Text: In [name for name in girl_names if 'angel' in name.lower()], what is the purpose of name.lower()?
Options (A|B|C|D): It sorts the names alphabetically first | It makes the substring check case-insensitive | It turns the list into a generator | It strips spaces from each name
Correct Answer: It makes the substring check case-insensitive

Question Text: Why is join() considered an efficient way to construct a string from many pieces?
Options (A|B|C|D): It automatically changes the letter case of each part as it joins them | It works only on tuples of strings and never on a plain Python list | It strips punctuation from every item before merging them together | It builds one string from all parts at once instead of piece by piece
Correct Answer: It builds one string from all parts at once instead of piece by piece

Question Text: What does calling d.update(other_dict) do to dictionary d?
Options (A|B|C|D): Converts the dictionary into a list of tuples | Adds or overwrites key-value pairs from the other source | Returns only the values stored in the dictionary | Deletes any keys of d not found in other_dict
Correct Answer: Adds or overwrites key-value pairs from the other source

Question Text: Besides passing another dictionary, which argument form does .update() also accept?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only keyword arguments and nothing else | A list of pairs, as in d.update([('key', value)]) | Positional values, as in d.update(1, 2, 3) | A set of keys, as in d.update({'a', 'b'})
Correct Answer: A list of pairs, as in d.update([('key', value)])

Question Text: What does d.pop(key) do when the key is present?
Options (A|B|C|D): Removes the key and returns its value | Sorts the dictionary by its keys | Returns the key itself without removing it | Returns all of the remaining keys
Correct Answer: Removes the key and returns its value

Question Text: Why is d.pop(key, default) often safer to call than d.pop(key)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It converts the dictionary into a defaultdict | It guarantees the default is inserted into d | It prevents a KeyError when the key is missing | It removes several keys in a single call
Correct Answer: It prevents a KeyError when the key is missing

Question Text: How does del d[key] differ from d.pop(key, default)?
Options (A|B|C|D): Both behave in an identical way and each one raises a KeyError on a missing key | del returns the value that it removed, while pop just discards it silently | del raises KeyError on a missing key, while pop with a default returns safely | pop can only remove attributes that were defined directly on a given class
Correct Answer: del raises KeyError on a missing key, while pop with a default returns safely

Question Text: What happens when you write d[key] and that key is not in the dictionary?
Options (A|B|C|D): It deletes the matching dictionary entry | It raises a KeyError | It returns None automatically | It creates the key with an empty value
Correct Answer: It raises a KeyError

Question Text: What does d.get(key) return when the key is missing and no default is given?
Options (A|B|C|D): 0 | False | None | An empty tuple
Correct Answer: None

Question Text: What does d.get(key, default) do when the key is not present?
Options (A|B|C|D): Returns the provided default value instead | Sorts the dictionary before looking up the key | Inserts the key permanently into the dictionary | Raises a default error type
Correct Answer: Returns the provided default value instead

Question Text: When is it appropriate to use d[key] rather than d.get(key)?
Options (A|B|C|D): When you are unsure the key exists | Only when the dictionary is nested | Only when iterating over d.items() | When you are certain the key exists
Correct Answer: When you are certain the key exists

Question Text: Which mental model best captures when to use [] versus .get() for lookups?
Options (A|B|C|D): They behave in the exact same way and differ only in their execution speed | Use [] when you are certain the key exists, and .get() when you are not | Always use .get() everywhere because the square-bracket lookup is now outdated | Use [] when the key is a string and use .get() when the key is a number
Correct Answer: Use [] when you are certain the key exists, and .get() when you are not

Question Text: In Python's collections module, what is a namedtuple?
Options (A|B|C|D): A specialized record type that works only inside pandas | A helper method that unpacks a tuple into separate variables | A tuple subclass with fields reachable by attribute name | A mutable dictionary that accepts only a fixed set of keys
Correct Answer: A tuple subclass with fields reachable by attribute name

Question Text: Why can a namedtuple be clearer to read than a plain tuple?
Options (A|B|C|D): It always consumes far less memory than any equivalent class | It automatically validates the data type of each field value | It is mutable, so its values can be reassigned just like a list | Fields are read by attribute name rather than numeric index
Correct Answer: Fields are read by attribute name rather than numeric index

Question Text: Compared with a plain dictionary, what reliability benefit does a namedtuple offer for record data?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every field is always present, so entry.species needs no .get() | It can store duplicate keys, which a dictionary cannot do at all | It requires no constructor arguments when an instance is created | It keeps its fields automatically sorted by their field name
Correct Answer: Every field is always present, so entry.species needs no .get()

Question Text: Given SpeciesDetails = namedtuple('SpeciesDetails', ['species', 'sex', 'body_mass']), what does calling SpeciesDetails(species, sex, body_mass) produce?
Options (A|B|C|D): A lightweight record instance with named attributes | A plain dictionary keyed by the three given field names | A frozen dataclass instance created automatically for you | A lazy generator object that yields tuples one at a time
Correct Answer: A lightweight record instance with named attributes

Question Text: What makes a namedtuple well suited for representing simple 'record' objects?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is the one and only way to attach named attributes in Python | It stays lightweight yet reads more clearly than a raw tuple | It behaves in every single way identically to a defaultdict(list) | It removes any need whatsoever to define classes or dataclasses
Correct Answer: It stays lightweight yet reads more clearly than a raw tuple

Question Text: What is the Counter class from the collections module primarily used for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Performing integer floor division | Tracking which attributes are private | Building immutable record objects | Counting how often each item occurs
Correct Answer: Counting how often each item occurs

Question Text: Which description best fits a Counter object?
Options (A|B|C|D): A class decorator that is applied to data model definitions | A function that returns a sorted copy of the given list | A dict-like object that tallies item frequencies | A specialized set variant that retains duplicate elements
Correct Answer: A dict-like object that tallies item frequencies

Question Text: What does the .most_common(n) method of a Counter return?
Options (A|B|C|D): The n most frequent items as (item, count) tuples | The n keys that come first in alphabetical order | A dictionary that contains only the keys with numeric values | A generator that yields each unique value exactly once
Correct Answer: The n most frequent items as (item, count) tuples

Question Text: In data work, why is Counter a handy tool to reach for?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is mandatory whenever a comprehension is being written | It converts ordinary strings into tuples of characters | It gives quick frequency tallies useful for validation | It silently fills in any dictionary keys that are missing
Correct Answer: It gives quick frequency tallies useful for validation

Question Text: When tallying values from a dataset with Counter, what subtle issue should you watch for?
Options (A|B|C|D): Counter is completely unable to count plain string values | Key casing matters, since 'Sex' and 'sex' count separately | Counter always orders its results strictly by insertion order | Counter is able to tally keys only when they are all lowercase
Correct Answer: Key casing matters, since 'Sex' and 'sex' count separately

Question Text: What recurring chore does defaultdict eliminate?
Options (A|B|C|D): The repeated 'if key is missing, initialize it' setup logic | The requirement that dictionary keys be kept in tuple order | The need to import anything at all from the collections module | The step of manually sorting a dictionary's keys before use
Correct Answer: The repeated 'if key is missing, initialize it' setup logic

Question Text: With defaultdict(list), what happens the first time you access a key that does not exist yet?
Options (A|B|C|D): A tuple holding all previously seen values appears | A ValueError is raised for the missing key | A randomly chosen default string is inserted | A fresh empty list is created for it automatically
Correct Answer: A fresh empty list is created for it automatically

Question Text: How is defaultdict(int) best characterized?
Options (A|B|C|D): A dict that stores only integers and rejects every string | A counting tool where a missing key starts at integer 0 | A direct drop-in substitute for the built-in set() type | A class-based model designed for structured record data
Correct Answer: A counting tool where a missing key starts at integer 0

Question Text: Why does defaultdict make grouping items into buckets easier?
Options (A|B|C|D): It runs faster because it sorts the keys before grouping them | It blocks duplicate values in much the same way a set does | It auto-creates each bucket so you can append right away | It needs fewer import statements than an ordinary dict does
Correct Answer: It auto-creates each bucket so you can append right away

Question Text: Given weights_by_species = defaultdict(list), what makes weights_by_species[species].append(body_mass) work without any setup check?
Options (A|B|C|D): Calling append happens to work directly on plain integer values | The species value is automatically turned into a class attribute | A missing species key is initialized with an empty list first | The dictionary has been frozen, so appending values is permitted
Correct Answer: A missing species key is initialized with an empty list first

Question Text: What is a dataclass in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): A built-in helper method provided by the pandas library | A tuple variant that is limited to holding named fields | A dictionary subclass that keeps all of its keys sorted | A class-based record type for modeling data cleanly
Correct Answer: A class-based record type for modeling data cleanly

Question Text: Which set of features do dataclasses provide out of the box?
Options (A|B|C|D): Mandatory inheritance from the base namedtuple type | Automatic traversal of nested graph data structures | Built-in persistence straight to a backing database | Typed fields, default values, and a readable repr
Correct Answer: Typed fields, default values, and a readable repr

Question Text: What do the asdict(c) and astuple(c) helper functions do with a dataclass instance c?
Options (A|B|C|D): They convert the instance into a dict or a tuple | They make the instance immutable after it is built | They expose only the instance's property methods | They reorder the instance's fields in place
Correct Answer: They convert the instance into a dict or a tuple

Question Text: Why might you add an @property method inside a dataclass?
Options (A|B|C|D): To make the generated constructor inaccessible to callers | To impose list-style integer indexing rules on the class | To derive a computed value from the stored fields | To force the dataclass to inherit from the tuple type
Correct Answer: To derive a computed value from the stored fields

Question Text: What does declaring a class with @dataclass(frozen=True) accomplish?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes instances immutable once constructed | It hides the field values while keeping them editable | It restricts every field to holding only floats | It quietly converts the class into a namedtuple
Correct Answer: It makes instances immutable once constructed

Topic (Sub-Lesson): print() as a Debugging & Testing Tool
Question Text: Why is print() useful as a debugging and testing tool?
Options (A|B|C|D): It automatically catches and suppresses raised exceptions | It permanently stores the displayed value in a variable | It reveals what the code is doing so you can verify assumptions | It is mandatory at the start of every loop body
Correct Answer: It reveals what the code is doing so you can verify assumptions

Topic (Sub-Lesson): print() as a Debugging & Testing Tool
Question Text: Which workflow uses print() effectively to check a value?
Options (A|B|C|D): Replace every code comment with a print statement | Use print only after the code is fully finished | Print a name first, then define the variable later | Store a value, print it, confirm it matches expectations
Correct Answer: Store a value, print it, confirm it matches expectations

Topic (Sub-Lesson): print() as a Debugging & Testing Tool
Question Text: What is a common syntax requirement when calling print() in Python 3?
Options (A|B|C|D): print is unable to display string values | print must be called using parentheses | print always returns the value it displayed | print runs only in scripts, never in functions
Correct Answer: print must be called using parentheses

Topic (Sub-Lesson): print() as a Debugging & Testing Tool
Question Text: Which example best fits a simple 'run and verify' check?
Options (A|B|C|D): next(print) to step through the function body | print('Hello, world!') to confirm the script runs | print() placed only inside except blocks | help(print) to install the Python interpreter
Correct Answer: print('Hello, world!') to confirm the script runs

Topic (Sub-Lesson): print() as a Debugging & Testing Tool
Question Text: Why is print() often the fastest way to answer 'what is my code doing?'
Options (A|B|C|D): It converts variables into strings permanently | It catches KeyError automatically before you ask | It measures the algorithm's time complexity for you | It gives immediate visible feedback with minimal setup
Correct Answer: It gives immediate visible feedback with minimal setup

Question Text: What does a line starting with # do in Python?
Options (A|B|C|D): It executes only when debugging mode is enabled | It marks the line as the target of a recursive call | It is ignored at runtime and serves as a note for humans | It creates a docstring attached to the function
Correct Answer: It is ignored at runtime and serves as a note for humans

Question Text: What is the main reason to add comments to code?
Options (A|B|C|D): To store variable values outside of program memory | To explain the intent or reasoning behind the code | To slow loops down so the output is easier to read | To rename all of the functions a program defines
Correct Answer: To explain the intent or reasoning behind the code

Question Text: Which statement correctly describes Python comments?
Options (A|B|C|D): They document code for humans and do not affect execution | They must always be written using triple quotes | They change program output but not its runtime | They are permitted only directly above a function
Correct Answer: They document code for humans and do not affect execution

Question Text: In print('Hello, world!')  # Print a message to confirm the script runs, what is the text after # doing?
Options (A|B|C|D): Adding a second, separate function call onto the same code line | Beginning a new multi-line, triple-quoted string literal block | Modifying the text string that the print call will display | Documenting the purpose of this line for a future human reader
Correct Answer: Documenting the purpose of this line for a future human reader

Question Text: Which statement about comment syntax is correct?
Options (A|B|C|D): Comments must be ended with a semicolon | Comments are validated at runtime by type() | Comments start with # and never run | Comments can be used in place of indentation
Correct Answer: Comments start with # and never run

Question Text: What best describes a Python script?
Options (A|B|C|D): A reusable package that is downloaded and installed from PyPI | A loose set of objects that has no defined execution order | A block of comment lines with several functions nested inside | A file of Python code that runs top-to-bottom to do a task
Correct Answer: A file of Python code that runs top-to-bottom to do a task

Question Text: Which analogy captures how a Python script works?
Options (A|B|C|D): A script is like a recipe for the computer to follow | A script is like a frozen, immutable dataclass | A script is like a single key in a dictionary | A script is like a graph with weighted edges
Correct Answer: A script is like a recipe for the computer to follow

Question Text: Which mindset is recommended when writing a script?
Options (A|B|C|D): Write the whole script perfectly before running it | Install every package before writing any code | Start small, run often, test with output, then iterate | Avoid using print() at all during development
Correct Answer: Start small, run often, test with output, then iterate

Question Text: Why does real-world development resemble iterative scripting?
Options (A|B|C|D): Every Python script is required to use recursion | Comments in the code automatically fix any bugs | A script can no longer be edited once it has run | Developers usually build, verify, adjust, and repeat
Correct Answer: Developers usually build, verify, adjust, and repeat

Question Text: At a high level, what are the two core jobs of programming?
Options (A|B|C|D): Writing comments and reading error tracebacks | Sorting lists and printing results to the screen | Installing packages and defining new classes | Managing data and controlling decisions and repetition
Correct Answer: Managing data and controlling decisions and repetition

Question Text: What is a variable, viewed as a mental model?
Options (A|B|C|D): A comment used only to describe a nearby piece of data | A built-in method that is automatically attached to every string | A labeled container that stores a value under a reusable name | A fixed constant whose stored value can never be changed
Correct Answer: A labeled container that stores a value under a reusable name

Question Text: Why are variables useful?
Options (A|B|C|D): They force all stored data to be converted into strings | They automatically pick the best Big O for an algorithm | They remove any need to define and call functions | They let you assign a value once and reuse or update it later
Correct Answer: They let you assign a value once and reuse or update it later

Correct Answer: A jump discontinuity, since the one-sided limits map to distinct values

Question Text: For a rational function f(x) = P(x)/Q(x), which condition makes x = a a removable discontinuity?
Options (A|B|C|D): The factor (x-a) is in Q to a higher power than it is in P | The factor (x-a) is only in P, giving a root rather than a break | The factor (x-a) appears in both P and Q and cancels completely | The factor (x-a) is only in Q, making the function grow unbounded
Correct Answer: The factor (x-a) appears in both P and Q and cancels completely

Question Text: In binary classification, why is a probability of 0.5 a common starting constant for an XGBoost model?
Options (A|B|C|D): It corresponds to a neutral log-odds value of zero | It minimizes the initial sum of squared errors | It balances the initial weights across the leaves | It maximizes the gain of the root node split
Correct Answer: It corresponds to a neutral log-odds value of zero

Question Text: How does the constant initial prediction directly shape the building of the first gradient-boosted tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It scales the learning rate applied in later rounds | It defines the gain threshold used by the pruner | It sets the starting gradients and hessians per sample | It fixes the maximum tree depth allowed for splits
Correct Answer: It sets the starting gradients and hessians per sample

Question Text: When XGBoost is used for a regression task, what property should the constant initial prediction ideally satisfy?
Options (A|B|C|D): It should equal the median value of the target set | It should maximize the coverage of the first leaf | It should minimize the chosen loss over all targets | It should reflect the numeric range of the outputs
Correct Answer: It should minimize the chosen loss over all targets

Question Text: What is the primary purpose of computing residuals against the constant initial prediction before fitting trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): To remove the need for any feature engineering | To rescale the columns of the feature matrix | To give each tree a directional error to fit | To shrink the variance of the final ensemble
Correct Answer: To give each tree a directional error to fit

Question Text: Within the XGBoost objective, what does the constant initial prediction stage represent before any tree is added?
Options (A|B|C|D): A zero-order approximation of the loss | A second-order expansion of each split | A cross-validation of the model bias | A first-order derivative of the leaves
Correct Answer: A zero-order approximation of the loss

Question Text: In XGBoost, the "residual" fit by each new tree generalizes from a plain target-minus-prediction error (under squared error) to a more general quantity under a non-linear loss such as logistic loss. What is this general quantity?
Options (A|B|C|D): The log-odds of each misclassified case | The negative gradient of the chosen loss | The unchanged original target values | The second derivative of the prediction
Correct Answer: The negative gradient of the chosen loss

Question Text: After a new tree is built and a learning rate (eta) is applied, how is each example's residual updated for the next boosting round?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is frozen until the final tree is added | It grows so the model fits more aggressively | It shrinks by the tree's output times eta | It is multiplied by eta before any tree fits
Correct Answer: It shrinks by the tree's output times eta

Question Text: Why does XGBoost work with the gradients and Hessians of the loss rather than with plain target-minus-prediction differences?
Options (A|B|C|D): It stops eta from slowing down convergence | It removes any need to compute target means | It guarantees outliers are fully ignored | A Taylor expansion supports arbitrary losses
Correct Answer: A Taylor expansion supports arbitrary losses

Question Text: In the iterative boosting process, how is the current ensemble prediction obtained before computing the next set of residuals?
Options (A|B|C|D): By taking only the most recent tree's output | By averaging the outputs of all prior trees | By summing the outputs of all prior trees | By picking the single best prior tree's output
Correct Answer: By summing the outputs of all prior trees

Question Text: What is the likely consequence if a training configuration drives the residuals to near zero within the first few boosting iterations?
Options (A|B|C|D): It fits noise and fails to generalize | It raises the cost of the earliest splits | It shrinks the feature pool each tree sees | It yields trees too shallow to fit signal
Correct Answer: It fits noise and fails to generalize

Question Text: Let f be continuous and map the closed interval [0, 1] into [0, 1]. Applying the Intermediate Value Theorem to g(x) = f(x) - x, which statement is guaranteed?
Options (A|B|C|D): Some point satisfies input equal to output | Some point has a derivative equal to zero | Some point's output equals the minimum | Some point's output equals the maximum
Correct Answer: Some point satisfies input equal to output

Question Text: Functions f and g are continuous on [a, b] with f(a) < g(a) and f(b) > g(b). Applying the IVT to f - g, what must hold on (a, b)?
Options (A|B|C|D): They never meet but share a limit | The two graphs cross at least once | They share an equal derivative somewhere | They are equal only at the midpoint
Correct Answer: The two graphs cross at least once

Question Text: For a continuous f on [0, 5] with f(0) = -2 and f(5) = 10, the IVT guarantees at least one root. What is a key limitation of what it tells you?
Options (A|B|C|D): It cannot fix the sign outside the interval | It gives no count of how many roots exist | It cannot confirm differentiability anywhere | It cannot bound the maximum on the interval
Correct Answer: It gives no count of how many roots exist

Question Text: Let f be continuous on [1, 5] with f(1) = 10 and f(5) = 10. Which single condition guarantees, via the IVT, that f(c) = 5 for some c in (1, 5)?
Options (A|B|C|D): f drops below 5 at some interior point | f stays strictly monotonic on the interval | f has a negative average rate of change | f is differentiable at every interior point
Correct Answer: f drops below 5 at some interior point

Question Text: A continuous function f has no roots anywhere on [2, 6]. By the IVT, what must be true of the endpoint values f(2) and f(6)?
Options (A|B|C|D): They must carry the same sign | They must be integers if f is linear | They must be the global extrema of f | They must exceed the interval length
Correct Answer: They must carry the same sign

Question Text: The XGBoost similarity score for a leaf is (sum of residuals)^2 / (count + lambda). How does it treat a leaf whose residuals are large but of mixed sign versus large residuals of the same sign?
Options (A|B|C|D): Mixed signs leave it fixed since count rules | Mixed signs raise it since variance is high | Mixed signs lower it since the sum is squared | Mixed signs zero it since magnitudes cancel
Correct Answer: Mixed signs lower it since the sum is squared

Question Text: A split's gain is computed as Left + Right - Root using leaf similarity scores. How does raising the regularization parameter lambda constrain tree growth?
Options (A|B|C|D): It scales scores up to avoid low gain | It lowers similarity scores, shrinking gain | It leaves scores fixed but raises gamma | It raises similarity scores, growing gain
Correct Answer: It lowers similarity scores, shrinking gain

Question Text: Two candidate leaves have an identical sum of residuals, but Node A holds 3 residuals and Node B holds 10. Using similarity = (sum)^2 / (count + lambda), how do their scores compare?
Options (A|B|C|D): Node B scores lower, having more residual mass | Node A scores higher, having fewer residuals | Node A scores lower, having less residual mass | Node B scores higher, having more residuals
Correct Answer: Node A scores higher, having fewer residuals

Question Text: Using similarity = (sum of residuals)^2 / (count + lambda), what happens to the score and to tree construction as lambda grows without bound?
Options (A|B|C|D): It tends to zero, so no new splits form | It tends to one, so every split looks optimal | It tends to one, so residuals are disregarded | It tends to zero, so the tree maximizes depth
Correct Answer: It tends to zero, so no new splits form

Question Text: For a leaf, the Output Value is (sum of residuals) / (count + lambda) and the Similarity Score is (sum of residuals)^2 / (count + lambda). What is the key difference in their numerators?
Options (A|B|C|D): One takes absolute residuals, the other raw ones | One squares the residual sum, the other does not | One adds lambda, the other drops regularization | One divides by count, the other by count squared
Correct Answer: One squares the residual sum, the other does not

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Derivative as slope and rate of change
Question Text: In the difference quotient definition of the derivative at x, what geometric transition occurs as the step h approaches zero?
Options (A|B|C|D): The tangent through (x, f(x)) and (x+h, f(x+h)) approaches the secant line at x. | The normal through (x, f(x)) and (x+h, f(x+h)) approaches the tangent line at x. | The secant through (x, f(x)) and (x+h, f(x+h)) approaches the normal line at x. | The secant through (x, f(x)) and (x+h, f(x+h)) approaches the tangent line at x.
Correct Answer: The secant through (x, f(x)) and (x+h, f(x+h)) approaches the tangent line at x.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Derivative as slope and rate of change
Question Text: For a strictly concave-up (convex) function f on [a, b], how does the derivative f'(a) at the left endpoint compare to the average rate of change of f across [a, b]?
Options (A|B|C|D): f'(a) is strictly less than the average rate of change over [a, b]. | f'(a) is inversely related to the average rate of change over [a, b]. | f'(a) is strictly greater than the average rate of change over [a, b]. | f'(a) is exactly equal to the average rate of change over [a, b].
Correct Answer: f'(a) is strictly less than the average rate of change over [a, b].

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Derivative as slope and rate of change
Question Text: If V(r) is the volume of a sphere of radius r, what does the derivative V'(r) represent physically?
Options (A|B|C|D): The rate of change of volume per unit radius, equal to the surface area. | The rate of change of radius per unit volume, equal to the circumference. | The rate of change of radius per unit volume, equal to the surface area. | The rate of change of volume per unit radius, equal to the circumference.
Correct Answer: The rate of change of volume per unit radius, equal to the surface area.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Derivative as slope and rate of change
Question Text: At a sharp corner or cusp of an otherwise continuous function f, why is the derivative undefined there?
Options (A|B|C|D): The tangent line there is exactly vertical, giving an indeterminate slope. | The difference quotient's limit grows to infinity as the step shrinks to zero. | The function's values jump discontinuously relative to the nearby interval. | The difference quotient's one-sided limits from the left and right disagree.
Correct Answer: The difference quotient's one-sided limits from the left and right disagree.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): Derivative as slope and rate of change
Question Text: Suppose f'(x) > 0 and f''(x) < 0 for every x. Which statement best describes the behavior of f?
Options (A|B|C|D): Its values fall, while its tangent's steepness steadily declines. | Its values rise, while its tangent's steepness steadily declines. | Its values fall, while its tangent's steepness steadily grows. | Its values rise, while its tangent's steepness steadily grows.
Correct Answer: Its values rise, while its tangent's steepness steadily declines.

Question Text: In the XGBoost gain formula, where each similarity score equals (sum of gradients)^2 / (sum of Hessians + lambda), how does increasing the L2 regularization parameter lambda affect whether a split is accepted?
Options (A|B|C|D): It shrinks the similarity-score denominator, raising total gain. | It shrinks the similarity-score numerator, raising total gain. | It enlarges the similarity-score denominator, lowering total gain. | It enlarges the similarity-score numerator, lowering total gain.
Correct Answer: It enlarges the similarity-score denominator, lowering total gain.

Question Text: The similarity score used in XGBoost gain and the optimal leaf weight w both involve the gradients in a leaf. How do they differ mathematically in their treatment of the gradient sum?
Options (A|B|C|D): Similarity squares the gradient sum, while the leaf weight uses it raw. | The leaf weight squares the gradient sum, while similarity uses it raw. | The leaf weight sums squared gradients, similarity uses the raw sum. | Similarity sums the squared gradients, while the weight uses the raw sum.
Correct Answer: Similarity squares the gradient sum, while the leaf weight uses it raw.

Question Text: During post-pruning of an XGBoost tree, what condition must a branch's split gain satisfy for that branch to be kept rather than removed?
Options (A|B|C|D): Its gain must equal the ratio of gamma to the tree depth. | Its gain must be strictly less than the gamma hyperparameter. | Its gain must be strictly greater than the gamma hyperparameter. | Its gain must equal the product of gamma and the tree depth.
Correct Answer: Its gain must be strictly greater than the gamma hyperparameter.

Question Text: In a regression task trained with Mean Squared Error loss, how does the Hessian H behave inside the XGBoost gain calculation?
Options (A|B|C|D): It equals 1 for every observation, simplifying the gain denominator. | It equals the absolute error value, simplifying the gain numerator. | It equals the feature variance value, reshaping the gain numerator. | It changes with each prediction, reshaping the gain denominator.
Correct Answer: It equals 1 for every observation, simplifying the gain denominator.

Question Text: Why is XGBoost's gain calculation considered more broadly applicable across loss functions than the Gini impurity used in classic decision trees?
Options (A|B|C|D): It uses a first-order Taylor expansion of any differentiable loss. | It uses feature-variance analysis of any differentiable loss. | It uses a second-order Taylor expansion of any differentiable loss. | It uses categorical frequency counts of any differentiable loss.
Correct Answer: It uses a second-order Taylor expansion of any differentiable loss.

Question Text: Suppose h(x) = x^2 * g(x). If g(1) = 4 and g'(1) = 2, what is the value of h'(1)?
For a nonzero differentiable function f(x), which expression gives the derivative of y = 1 / f(x)?
At which x-coordinates does the function f(x) = x / (x^2 + 1) have a horizontal tangent line?
Options (A|B|C|D): At x = 1 and x = -1 | At x = 1 and x = 0 | At x = 2 and x = -2 | At x = 0 and x = -1
Correct Answer: At x = 1 and x = -1

Question Text: How does the derivative of a product f(x)g(x) compare with the simple product of the two derivatives f'(x)g'(x)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It can be obtained using only the quotient rule instead. | It can be obtained using only the power rule instead. | It also includes terms built from the original functions. | It excludes any terms built from the original functions.
Correct Answer: It also includes terms built from the original functions.

Question Text: When the power rule is applied once to every term of a polynomial P(x) of degree n (with n >= 1), how does the polynomial's degree change?
Options (A|B|C|D): The degree drops straight to zero. | The degree decreases by exactly one. | The degree increases by exactly one. | The degree stays exactly the same.
Correct Answer: The degree decreases by exactly one.

Question Text: When XGBoost evaluates candidate splits using the gain formula, what role does the gamma parameter play?
Options (A|B|C|D): It rescales leaf weights to curb overfitting at deep tree levels. | It rescales the Hessian values to stabilize gradients in the loss. | It rescales the learning rate to slow the gradient-descent steps. | It sets a minimum gain threshold required to add a new node.
Correct Answer: It sets a minimum gain threshold required to add a new node.

Question Text: How does raising the L2 regularization parameter lambda affect the gain computed for a candidate split?
Options (A|B|C|D): It steadies the gain by capping the maximum leaf weight. | It raises the gain by inflating the squared numerator. | It lowers the gain by enlarging the denominator values. | It offsets the gain by canceling the influence of gamma.
Correct Answer: It lowers the gain by enlarging the denominator values.

Question Text: When computing gain for a feature that contains missing values, how does XGBoost decide the default direction for those missing entries?
Options (A|B|C|D): It imputes the missing values with the feature mean before scoring any gain. | It routes missing values to whichever branch already holds more instances. | It scores gain for sending all nulls left, then right, keeping the better. | It drops the missing rows and computes gain from only the known values.
Correct Answer: It scores gain for sending all nulls left, then right, keeping the better.

Question Text: Why does the XGBoost gain formula use second-order gradients (Hessians) rather than relying on first-order gradients alone?
Options (A|B|C|D): It lets the algorithm approximate the loss via a Taylor expansion. | It keeps the algorithm from reaching the global minimum too early. | It lets the algorithm derive feature-importance metrics more easily. | It makes the algorithm invariant to the scale of the input features.
Correct Answer: It lets the algorithm approximate the loss via a Taylor expansion.

Question Text: In XGBoost tree construction, what happens when a candidate split produces a negative gain value?
Options (A|B|C|D): The split is accepted if its child nodes later yield a high positive gain. | The split is discarded because the complexity penalty exceeds the loss reduction. | The split is kept but the resulting leaf weights are reset to neutral. | The split is adjusted by tuning the learning rate until the gain hits zero.
Correct Answer: The split is discarded because the complexity penalty exceeds the loss reduction.

Question Text: What is the 102nd derivative of f(x) = sin(x)?
What is the first derivative of the composite function y = sin(cos(x))?
Options (A|B|C|D): y' = -sin(x) * cos(cos(x)) | y' = -cos(x) * sin(sin(x)) | y' = -sin(x) * sin(sin(x)) | y' = -cos(x) * cos(cos(x))
Correct Answer: y' = -sin(x) * cos(cos(x))

Question Text: For f(x) = sin(x) and g(x) = cos(x), how do f'(x) and g'(x) compare at x = pi/4?
Options (A|B|C|D): They have equal magnitude but opposite signs. | They have equal magnitude and the same sign. | They are reciprocals of each other there. | They are both exactly equal to zero there.
Correct Answer: They have equal magnitude but opposite signs.

Question Text: At which x-value in [0, pi] does f(x) = sin(x) + cos(x) have a slope of zero?
Options (A|B|C|D): The slope is zero at x = pi/4. | The slope is zero at x = pi/2. | The slope is zero at x = pi/6. | The slope is zero at x = pi/3.
Correct Answer: The slope is zero at x = pi/4.

Question Text: What is the concavity of f(x) = cos(x) on the interval (0, pi/2)?
Options (A|B|C|D): Concave down, because f''(x) is more than zero there. | Concave up, because f''(x) is less than zero there. | Concave up, because f''(x) is more than zero there. | Concave down, because f''(x) is less than zero there.
Correct Answer: Concave down, because f''(x) is less than zero there.

Question Text: If you decrease the max_depth parameter in an XGBoost model, holding predictive goals fixed, how does this typically change the optimal number of boosting rounds?
Options (A|B|C|D): Fewer boosting rounds are needed, since shallower trees capture less per round. | More boosting rounds are needed, since shallower trees capture less per round. | More boosting rounds are needed, since shallower trees overfit each round. | Fewer boosting rounds are needed, since deeper signal is already captured.
Correct Answer: More boosting rounds are needed, since shallower trees capture less per round.

Question Text: In bias-variance terms, how does constraining the depth of each tree primarily affect an XGBoost ensemble?
Options (A|B|C|D): It lowers each learner's variance but raises the ensemble's bias. | It raises each learner's bias but lowers the ensemble's variance. | It raises each learner's variance but lowers the ensemble's bias. | It lowers each learner's bias but raises the ensemble's variance.
Correct Answer: It raises each learner's bias but lowers the ensemble's variance.

Question Text: When XGBoost builds trees under a strict depth limit, what effect does this have on how the second-order (gradient/Hessian) objective is optimized?
Options (A|B|C|D): It blocks the model from partitioning the gradient space into fine regions. | It blocks the model from computing any Hessian values at leaf nodes. | It forces the model to favor training speed over predictive accuracy. | It forces the model to drop the regularization terms while searching splits.
Correct Answer: It blocks the model from partitioning the gradient space into fine regions.

Question Text: How does limiting trees with max_depth differ from pruning splits via the gamma (min_split_loss) parameter in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): It imposes a structural cap on path length regardless of any node's gain. | It imposes a penalty on total leaf weight applied during the pruning step. | It imposes a statistical cap on leaf purity regardless of any node's gain. | It imposes a penalty on total leaf count applied during the pruning step.
Correct Answer: It imposes a structural cap on path length regardless of any node's gain.

Question Text: What does the max_depth parameter control about feature interactions modeled within a single XGBoost tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It sets the maximum count of distinct features allowed in the whole model. | It sets a floor on the variance explained by each individual split node. | It sets a ceiling on the order of feature interactions one tree can model. | It sets the minimum importance weight assigned to every input feature.
Correct Answer: It sets a ceiling on the order of feature interactions one tree can model.

Question Text: If x^2 + y^2 = 1, find the second derivative d^2y/dx^2 expressed solely in terms of y.
Using implicit differentiation, find the slope of the tangent line to the curve y^3 + xy - x^2 = 3 at the point (2, 1).
Options (A|B|C|D): The slope is 3/5. | The slope is 2/3. | The slope is 1/5. | The slope is 4/3.
Correct Answer: The slope is 3/5.

Question Text: Using logarithmic differentiation, which expression equals the derivative of y = x^sin(x) for x > 0?
Options (A|B|C|D): x^sin(x) [sin(x)·ln(x) + cos(x)/x] | x^sin(x) [sin(x)·ln(x) - cos(x)/x] | x^sin(x) [cos(x)·ln(x) - sin(x)/x] | x^sin(x) [cos(x)·ln(x) + sin(x)/x]
Correct Answer: x^sin(x) [cos(x)·ln(x) + sin(x)/x]

Question Text: Suppose f(x) = x^3 + x. Using the inverse-function derivative rule, find the value of (f^-1)'(2).
In the XGBoost objective, what is the primary role of the gamma parameter during tree construction?
Options (A|B|C|D): It smooths the Hessian values relied on during the split-finding step. | It penalizes complexity in proportion to the tree's total number of leaves. | It normalizes the output weights assigned to each leaf of the tree. | It rescales the loss-function gradients used when evaluating each split.
Correct Answer: It penalizes complexity in proportion to the tree's total number of leaves.

Question Text: Why does XGBoost prune trees bottom-up after growing them, rather than stopping a branch early when a split looks unhelpful?
Options (A|B|C|D): So the computed feature-importance scores stay consistent and stable. | So the learning rate is applied consistently across every tree level. | So the maximum-depth constraint is enforced strictly at each node. | So a beneficial split deeper in the tree is not discarded prematurely.
Correct Answer: So a beneficial split deeper in the tree is not discarded prematurely.

Question Text: How does raising the gamma parameter affect an XGBoost model's bias-variance tradeoff?
Options (A|B|C|D): A larger gamma yields a more complex model with deeper branches. | A larger gamma yields a sharper model with lower local bias. | A larger gamma yields a more conservative model with lower variance. | A larger gamma yields a more flexible model with higher variance.
Correct Answer: A larger gamma yields a more conservative model with lower variance.

Question Text: Conceptually, how does pruning via gamma differ from L2 regularization via the lambda parameter in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Gamma governs the learning rate, while lambda governs feature selection. | Gamma governs structural complexity, while lambda governs leaf magnitude. | Gamma governs feature selection, while lambda governs the learning rate. | Gamma governs leaf magnitude, while lambda governs structural complexity.
Correct Answer: Gamma governs structural complexity, while lambda governs leaf magnitude.

Question Text: During XGBoost's pruning phase, what must hold for a split to be kept in the tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): The split's computed gain must be smaller than the chosen learning rate. | The split's computed gain must be added onto the total leaf weight. | The split's computed gain must be strictly greater than the gamma value. | The split's computed gain must be exactly equal to the gamma value.
Correct Answer: The split's computed gain must be strictly greater than the gamma value.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: In the XGBoost optimal leaf-weight formula w = -G / (H + lambda), how does the L2 term lambda relate to the Hessian sum H?
Options (A|B|C|D): It joins the numerator, weakening the gradient sum's effect on weight size. | It joins the denominator, strengthening the gradient sum's effect on weight size. | It joins the numerator, strengthening the gradient sum's effect on weight size. | It joins the denominator, weakening the gradient sum's effect on weight size.
Correct Answer: It joins the denominator, weakening the gradient sum's effect on weight size.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: With lambda held constant, where is its relative shrinkage of a leaf's weight most pronounced?
Options (A|B|C|D): Only in regions where the Hessian sum exceeds lambda. | In regions where the leaf's Hessian sum is very small. | In regions where the leaf's Hessian sum is very large. | Equally in every region, regardless of the Hessian sum.
Correct Answer: In regions where the leaf's Hessian sum is very small.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: What best distinguishes the regularizing effect of lambda from that of the learning rate (eta) in XGBoost?
Options (A|B|C|D): Lambda scales the whole tree's contribution; eta shrinks each leaf's weight via its Hessian sum. | Lambda shrinks each leaf's weight via its Hessian sum; eta scales the whole tree's contribution. | Lambda cuts the number of leaf nodes per tree; eta shrinks the loss-function gradients. | Lambda shrinks the loss-function gradients; eta cuts the number of leaf nodes per tree.
Correct Answer: Lambda shrinks each leaf's weight via its Hessian sum; eta scales the whole tree's contribution.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: From a bias-variance view, how does increasing lambda change the model's behavior during additive boosting?
Options (A|B|C|D): It lowers variance by enlarging the maximum correction each new leaf can add. | It lowers variance by limiting the maximum correction each new leaf can add. | It lowers bias by enlarging the maximum correction each new leaf can add. | It lowers bias by limiting the maximum correction each new leaf can add.
Correct Answer: It lowers variance by limiting the maximum correction each new leaf can add.

Topic (Sub-Lesson): How Lambda Shrinks Leaf Output Values
Question Text: As lambda approaches infinity in XGBoost, what is the limiting behavior of the leaf outputs and the overall model?
Options (A|B|C|D): Leaf outputs approach zero, so the model converges to its initial prediction. | Leaf outputs approach zero, so the model converges to the mean of the labels. | Leaf outputs approach one, so the model converges to the mean of the labels. | Leaf outputs approach one, so the model converges to its initial prediction.
Correct Answer: Leaf outputs approach zero, so the model converges to its initial prediction.

Question Text: A closed cylindrical can must hold a fixed volume. Which relationship between its height h and radius r minimizes the total surface area?
Options (A|B|C|D): The height equals twice the radius (h = 2r). | The radius equals three times the height (r = 3h). | The radius equals twice the height (r = 2h). | The height equals the radius exactly (h = r).
Correct Answer: The height equals twice the radius (h = 2r).

Question Text: A ladder of fixed length L leans against a vertical wall, and its base slides away from the wall at a constant rate. How does the angular rate dθ/dt (θ measured from the ground) behave as a function of the height y of the top?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is inversely related to the height y of the top. | It is directly proportional to the height y of the top. | It depends only on the fixed ladder length L. | It stays constant for the entire sliding motion.
Correct Answer: It is inversely related to the height y of the top.

Question Text: When finding the absolute maximum of a continuous function on a closed interval [a, b], why must the endpoints be evaluated in addition to the critical points?
Options (A|B|C|D): Because it locates where the second derivative is zero. | Because a global extremum can occur at a boundary point. | Because the Mean Value Theorem requires the endpoint values. | Because it confirms the function stays continuous on [a, b].
Correct Answer: Because a global extremum can occur at a boundary point.

Question Text: For an ideal gas held at constant temperature (PV = k), the volume V decreases at a constant rate. How does the rate of change of pressure, dP/dt, relate to the volume?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is directly proportional to the square of the volume. | It is inversely proportional to the cube of the volume. | It is directly proportional to the cube of the volume. | It is inversely proportional to the square of the volume.
Correct Answer: It is inversely proportional to the square of the volume.

Question Text: A cost is modeled by C(x) = a·x + b/x with constants a, b > 0 and x > 0. At the value of x that minimizes C(x), what is true of the two terms?
Options (A|B|C|D): The term a·x is twice the value of b/x. | The term a·x equals the square of b/x. | The term a·x equals the term b/x exactly. | The term a·x is half the value of b/x.
Correct Answer: The term a·x equals the term b/x exactly.

Question Text: In an XGBoost binary classifier, the raw output is a sum of leaf values across all trees. How is this raw score converted into a final class probability?
Options (A|B|C|D): It is divided by the standard deviation of the current loss. | It is passed through a ReLU to clip away any negative values. | It is passed through a sigmoid to map it into the range (0, 1). | It is divided by the total tree count to average the class weights.
Correct Answer: It is passed through a sigmoid to map it into the range (0, 1).

Question Text: During tree construction, XGBoost uses the second-order derivative (Hessian) of the loss in addition to the gradient. What does the Hessian provide?
Options (A|B|C|D): A pruning signal that drops branches not aiding accuracy. | A second-order approximation used to set optimal leaf weights. | A variance penalty that removes high-error training samples. | A speedup that cuts the number of splits needed per tree.
Correct Answer: A second-order approximation used to set optimal leaf weights.

Question Text: In the XGBoost split-gain formula, how does the complexity parameter gamma affect the growth of a classification tree?
Options (A|B|C|D): It adds a fixed penalty on the sum of squared leaf weights. | It sets the maximum depth allowed for any single tree. | It sets the minimum gain a split must exceed to be kept. | It acts as a learning-rate multiplier that curbs overfitting.
Correct Answer: It sets the minimum gain a split must exceed to be kept.

Question Text: When training an XGBoost classifier on a heavily imbalanced dataset, what does the scale_pos_weight hyperparameter primarily do?
Options (A|B|C|D): It lowers the learning rate applied to the majority class only. | It scales the positive class's gradients to balance class influence. | It deepens the trees so the minority class is captured fully. | It drops majority class samples until the class ratio is equal.
Correct Answer: It scales the positive class's gradients to balance class influence.

Question Text: How does XGBoost's structure change when moving from binary classification to multi-class classification?
Options (A|B|C|D): It swaps logistic loss for a multi-dimensional exponential loss. | It builds one tree per class on each boosting iteration. | It builds one tree per forest to get a joint probability. | It swaps gradient descent for a genetic-algorithm search.
Correct Answer: It builds one tree per class on each boosting iteration.

Question Text: XGBoost's additive model works in log-odds space. If the initial predicted probability is 0.5, what is the corresponding initial raw (log-odds) score?
Options (A|B|C|D): A log-odds value of 0.0. | A log-odds value of 0.5. | A log-odds value of -1.0. | A log-odds value of 1.0.
Correct Answer: A log-odds value of 0.0.

Question Text: In binary classification with labels in {0, 1}, an initial predicted probability of 0.5 is used. How does this affect the first set of residuals (label minus prediction)?
Options (A|B|C|D): It makes the very first gradient step mathematically undefined. | It yields symmetric residuals of +0.5 or -0.5 for every sample. | It forces the first tree to ignore the global mean of the labels. | It yields positive residuals only for the majority class samples.
Correct Answer: It yields symmetric residuals of +0.5 or -0.5 for every sample.

Question Text: On a heavily imbalanced dataset, why might a practitioner set the initial probability to the training set's mean (base rate) instead of the default 0.5?
Options (A|B|C|D): It avoids applying the logistic (sigmoid) transformation entirely. | It stops the model from overfitting the minority class labels. | It removes the need to compute the second-order Hessian terms. | It starts nearer the optimum, so training converges faster.
Correct Answer: It starts nearer the optimum, so training converges faster.

Question Text: In XGBoost classification, the model accumulates raw additive scores in log-odds space. What is the primary role of the sigmoid function applied to those scores?
Options (A|B|C|D): It maps the raw additive scores into a valid probability range. | It dampens the effect of outliers on the final class label. | It stabilizes the variance of the gradients across the trees. | It rescales the leaf weights so they each stay within [0, 1].
Correct Answer: It maps the raw additive scores into a valid probability range.

Question Text: When the true class distribution is heavily skewed, what is a direct consequence of keeping the default initial prediction of 0.5?
Options (A|B|C|D): The learning rate must be raised just to gain any accuracy. | The first tree has to correct for a large initial bias. | The Hessian values are forced to stay constant during training. | The final model becomes unable to output low probabilities.
Correct Answer: The first tree has to correct for a large initial bias.
